aaa-rupture.title: Abdominal Aortic Aneurysm (AAA)

cellular.title: Vessel Wall Integrity
cellular.content: Chronic high pressure (HTN) and smoking damage the aortic endothelium, causing the arterial wall to weaken and dilate. If the wall reaches its breaking point, it ruptures, leading to rapid exsanguination into the abdominal cavity.

left: Pulsatile Abdominal Mass
Abdominal Bruit
Back/Flank Pain (Stable)
Incidental Imaging Finding
right: Sudden, Ripping Back Pain
Hypovolemic Shock (Low BP, High HR)
Ecchymosis (Groin/Scrotum)
Decreased Hematocrit/Hemoglobin

medications.name: Sodium Nitroprusside
medications.action: Emergency BP control

pearls: Hourly urine output must be >30mL to ensure renal perfusion
Monitor peripheral pulses distal to graft post-op
Report sudden severe pain immediately - sign of graft leak
diagnostics: Expect abdominal ultrasound or CT angiography to be ordered
Monitor serial hemoglobin and hematocrit trends
Expect type and crossmatch for blood products
Monitor continuous vital signs on telemetry
Expect coagulation studies to be drawn
Monitor urine output hourly
management: Maintain bed rest as ordered
Keep environment calm to reduce anxiety and BP spikes
Maintain large-bore IV access as ordered
Avoid straining or Valsalva maneuver
Follow blood pressure parameters ordered
Prepare for possible emergency surgery as directed
nursingActions: Monitor vital signs every 15 minutes during acute phase
Report sudden severe back or abdominal pain immediately
Monitor peripheral pulses (pedal, popliteal) and mark with pen
Measure and document abdominal girth
Monitor urine output hourly (report < 30 mL/hr)
Report signs of hypovolemic shock (tachycardia, hypotension, pallor)

mi-management.title: Myocardial Infarction Management

cellular.title: Coronary Occlusion
cellular.content: A ruptured atherosclerotic plaque triggers a thrombotic cascade that completely blocks blood flow to the myocardium. Ischemia leads to irreversible cell death within 20-40 minutes if reperfusion isn't achieved.

left: Heavy/Crushing Chest Pain
Pain radiating to jaw or left arm
Diaphoresis & Nausea
Feeling of Impending Doom
right: ST-Elevation (STEMI)
New S3 Heart Sound (HF)
Crackles in Lungs
Ominous Bradycardia/Heart Block

medications.name: Aspirin
medications.action: Prevents clot expansion

medications.name: Nitroglycerin
medications.action: Reduces Preload/Afterload

pearls: NPO until swallow screen (if stroke signs coexist)
Avoid NSAIDs - they increase mortality risk post-MI
Semi-Fowler's position to reduce heart workload
diagnostics: Expect 12-lead ECG to be ordered STAT
Monitor cardiac biomarkers (troponin) trends
Expect chest X-ray to be ordered
Monitor continuous cardiac telemetry
Expect CBC and metabolic panel to be drawn
Monitor continuous pulse oximetry
management: Position in semi-Fowler's to reduce cardiac workload
Administer oxygen as ordered
Maintain IV access as ordered
Keep patient on bed rest during acute phase
Maintain NPO or clear liquids as ordered
Administer prescribed medications (aspirin, nitroglycerin) per orders
Reduce anxiety and provide calm environment
nursingActions: Monitor vital signs every 15 minutes during acute phase
Assess and document chest pain characteristics (PQRST)
Report any changes in pain, rhythm, or hemodynamics immediately
Monitor intake and output
Document response to medication administration
Provide emotional support and reduce anxiety
Report signs of heart failure (crackles, S3, JVD)

hf-advanced.title: Advanced Heart Failure

cellular.title: Remodeling & Failure
cellular.content: Chronic overload leads to ventricular remodeling. Reduced Ejection Fraction (Systolic) involves thin, weak walls, while Preserved EF (Diastolic) involves thick, stiff walls that cannot fill properly.

left: Lungs: Crackles & Frothy Sputum
Dyspnea & Orthopnea
Paroxysmal Nocturnal Dyspnea
right: Systemic: JVD & Edema
Hepatomegaly & Ascites
Weight Gain (>3lb in 2 days)

medications.name: Furosemide
medications.action: Reduces Preload

medications.name: ACE Inhibitors
medications.action: Blocks RAAS

pearls: Daily weights are the most sensitive indicator of fluid status
Sodium restriction (<3g/day) is vital
Avoid NSAIDs as they cause fluid retention
diagnostics: Expect BNP or NT-proBNP levels to be ordered
Monitor daily weights (most sensitive indicator)
Expect chest X-ray to assess pulmonary congestion
Monitor continuous pulse oximetry
Expect echocardiogram to be ordered
Monitor intake and output strictly
Expect basic metabolic panel (electrolytes, renal function)
management: Position in high-Fowler's for breathing ease
Administer oxygen as ordered
Enforce sodium restriction (< 2-3 g/day) as ordered
Enforce fluid restriction as ordered
Administer diuretics and cardiac medications as prescribed
Maintain bed rest during acute exacerbation
Apply compression stockings as ordered
nursingActions: Weigh patient daily at same time on same scale
Monitor vital signs and oxygen saturation regularly
Assess lung sounds for crackles every shift
Monitor edema (peripheral and sacral) and document
Report weight gain > 2 lbs in 24 hours or 3 lbs in 2 days
Measure and document intake and output
Educate patient on sodium and fluid restrictions
Report increasing dyspnea or orthopnea immediately

dysrhythmias.title: Lethal Dysrhythmias

cellular.title: Electrical Instability
cellular.content: Disruption in the heart's electrical conduction system. VT/VF lead to zero cardiac output. Atrial Fibrillation increases the risk of mural thrombi and embolic stroke.

left: A-Fib: Irregularly irregular pulse
Palpitations
Dizziness
Shortness of breath
right: VT/VF: Pulselessness
Apnea
Loss of consciousness
Bradycardia < 40

medications.name: Amiodarone
medications.action: Prolongs action potential

medications.name: Adenosine
medications.action: Restarts AV node

pearls: A-Fib: Priority is rate control and anticoagulation
VFib = Defib (Immediate)
Check a pulse for at least 5 but no more than 10 seconds
diagnostics: Monitor continuous cardiac telemetry
Expect 12-lead ECG to be ordered
Monitor electrolyte levels (K+, Mg2+, Ca2+)
Expect troponin if ischemia suspected
Monitor digoxin levels if applicable
Expect thyroid function tests if indicated
management: Maintain continuous cardiac monitoring
Maintain IV access as ordered
Administer antiarrhythmic medications as prescribed
Keep emergency/code cart accessible
Follow prescribed electrolyte replacement protocols
Maintain oxygen delivery as ordered
nursingActions: Monitor heart rate and rhythm continuously
Report irregular rhythms or rate changes immediately
Assess pulse quality (rate, regularity, strength)
Monitor for signs of decreased cardiac output (dizziness, syncope, pallor)
Report palpitations or chest pain immediately
Document rhythm strips and report changes
Check pulse for at least 5-10 seconds for pulselessness

pe-recognition.title: Pulmonary Embolism (PE)

cellular.title: V/Q Mismatch
cellular.content: A blockage in the pulmonary artery, usually from a DVT. This creates a 'dead space' where ventilation occurs but no perfusion (gas exchange) happens, leading to acute hypoxemia.

left: Sudden Dyspnea
Pleuritic Chest Pain
Tachypnea
Tachycardia
right: Hemoptysis
Hypotension (Obstructive Shock)
Right Heart Strain (JVD)
Feeling of Impending Doom

medications.name: Heparin
medications.action: Prevents clot growth

medications.name: Alteplase
medications.action: Dissolves existing PE

pearls: DVT prophylaxis (Enoxaparin/SCDs) is the best prevention
Position: High-Fowler's immediately
CT Angiography is the gold standard for diagnosis
diagnostics: Expect CT angiography (gold standard) to be ordered
Monitor D-dimer levels
Monitor oxygen saturation continuously
Expect ABG to be drawn
Expect ECG to assess right heart strain
Expect coagulation studies (aPTT for heparin monitoring)
management: Position in high-Fowler's to improve breathing
Administer oxygen as ordered
Maintain IV access for anticoagulation therapy
Maintain bed rest during acute phase as ordered
Apply sequential compression devices (SCDs) to unaffected leg
Administer anticoagulants as prescribed
nursingActions: Monitor respiratory status and oxygen saturation continuously
Report sudden dyspnea, chest pain, or hemoptysis immediately
Assess for signs of right heart failure (JVD, peripheral edema)
Monitor for bleeding complications from anticoagulation
Report signs of hemodynamic instability (hypotension, tachycardia)
Educate on DVT prevention (early ambulation, hydration)
Document response to oxygen therapy and anticoagulation

infective-endocarditis.title: Infective Endocarditis

cellular.title: Valve Infection & Vegetation
cellular.content: Microbial infection of heart valves/endocardium. Vegetation (platelet-fibrin-microbe mass) forms on damaged valves. Most common organisms: Strep viridans (native valves), Staph aureus (IV drug users, prosthetic valves). Duke criteria used for diagnosis.

left: Fever
New/Changing Heart Murmur
Janeway Lesions (painless palms/soles)
Osler Nodes (painful fingertips)
right: Splinter Hemorrhages (nails)
Roth Spots (retina)
Splenomegaly
Embolic Events (stroke, PE)

medications.name: Vancomycin + Gentamicin
medications.action: Broad-spectrum coverage for endocarditis

medications.name: Nafcillin
medications.action: Targets MSSA endocarditis

pearls: Blood cultures x3 from different sites before starting antibiotics
PICC line placement for long-term outpatient IV therapy
Dental prophylaxis required for patients with prosthetic valves
Monitor for embolic complications (stroke, splenic infarct, renal infarct)
diagnostics: Vital signs with temperature trending
Auscultation for new or changing murmur
Blood culture collection as ordered
Petechiae and skin assessment
Monitor for signs of embolic events
management: Administer IV antibiotics as ordered
Monitor temperature trends
Report new heart murmur to provider
Maintain strict hand hygiene
Ensure IV access site care
nursingActions: Assess for splinter hemorrhages in nails
Monitor temperature q4h
Report fever spikes to RN/physician
Educate on dental hygiene importance
Observe for signs of embolic complications (stroke, PE)

peripheral-artery-disease.title: Peripheral Artery Disease (PAD)

cellular.title: Atherosclerotic Narrowing
cellular.content: Atherosclerotic narrowing of peripheral arteries reduces blood flow to extremities. Progression: intermittent claudication → rest pain → critical limb ischemia → tissue loss. Ankle-Brachial Index (ABI) < 0.9 is diagnostic.

left: Intermittent Claudication (calf pain with walking, relieved by rest)
Pallor on Elevation
Dependent Rubor
Hair Loss on Affected Leg
right: Thick Toenails
Cool Extremities
Absent/Diminished Pedal Pulses
Non-healing Wounds on Toes/Feet

medications.name: Cilostazol
medications.action: Vasodilation and antiplatelet effect, improves walking distance

medications.name: Aspirin + Statin
medications.action: Reduces cardiovascular events and atherosclerotic progression

pearls: Supervised walking program is the most important non-surgical intervention
Buerger's exercises improve collateral circulation
Position legs in DEPENDENT position (NOT elevated like venous disease)
Smoking cessation is critical - single most modifiable risk factor
diagnostics: Pedal pulse assessment (dorsalis pedis, posterior tibial)
Capillary refill time in toes
Ankle-brachial index measurement as ordered
Skin color and temperature assessment of extremities
management: Position legs in dependent position (NOT elevated)
Encourage supervised walking program as tolerated
Protect feet from injury with proper footwear
Assist with smoking cessation resources
Administer prescribed medications
nursingActions: Assess pedal pulses and skin color every shift
Monitor for signs of worsening ischemia (rest pain, non-healing wounds)
Educate patient on foot care and protection
Report absent pulses or new skin breakdown immediately
Encourage smoking cessation
Teach Buerger's exercises as prescribed

aortic-dissection.title: Aortic Dissection

cellular.title: Aortic Intimal Tear
cellular.content: Tear in aortic intima allows blood to enter between layers of the aortic wall, creating a false lumen. Stanford Type A (ascending aorta - surgical emergency) vs Type B (descending aorta - medical management). Major risk factors: uncontrolled HTN, Marfan syndrome, bicuspid aortic valve.

left: Sudden Tearing/Ripping Chest Pain Radiating to Back
Unequal BP in Arms (>20mmHg difference)
Widened Mediastinum on CXR
Aortic Regurgitation Murmur
right: Hypertension (most common)
Pulse Deficits
Neurological Deficits (if carotid involved)
Malperfusion Syndrome (renal, mesenteric)

medications.name: Esmolol
medications.action: Reduces heart rate and aortic shear stress

medications.name: Nitroprusside
medications.action: Reduces afterload and aortic wall stress

pearls: Control HR before BP (target HR <60 bpm)
Type A (ascending) = emergency surgery
BP must be checked in BOTH arms - unequal pressures are diagnostic
Morphine for pain management (also reduces sympathetic drive)
diagnostics: Blood pressure measurement in both arms
Heart rate and rhythm monitoring
Pain assessment (location, character, radiation)
Monitoring for pulse deficits in extremities
management: Keep patient on strict bedrest
Administer IV medications as ordered for BP and HR control
Maintain quiet environment to reduce stimulation
Prepare for possible emergency surgery if Type A
Report any neurological changes immediately
nursingActions: Monitor BP in both arms and report >20mmHg difference
Assess pain level and administer analgesics as ordered
Report any changes in neurological status
Monitor for signs of malperfusion (renal, mesenteric)
Keep patient calm and reduce anxiety
Maintain IV access with large-bore catheter

carotid-endarterectomy.title: Carotid Endarterectomy Post-Op Care

cellular.title: Carotid Plaque Removal
cellular.content: Surgical removal of atherosclerotic plaque from the carotid artery to restore blood flow and reduce stroke risk. Indicated for symptomatic stenosis >70% or asymptomatic stenosis >80%. Patch angioplasty or primary closure techniques used.

left: Monitor Neuro Status q1h (NIHSS)
CN VII Injury (facial droop)
CN X Injury (hoarseness, dysphagia)
CN XII Injury (tongue deviation)
right: Neck Hematoma (airway emergency)
Hypotension or Hypertension
Headache/Seizures (hyperperfusion syndrome)
Signs of Stroke (new deficits)

medications.name: Aspirin + Clopidogrel
medications.action: Prevents thrombosis at surgical site

medications.name: Labetalol
medications.action: Post-op BP control

pearls: Keep BP within 20% of baseline (too low = thrombosis, too high = hemorrhage)
Monitor for hyperperfusion syndrome (headache, seizures, focal deficits)
Keep emergency airway equipment at bedside (hematoma can compress trachea)
Assess cranial nerves frequently - injury may be temporary or permanent
diagnostics: Neurological checks (level of consciousness, facial symmetry, speech, grip strength)
Blood pressure monitoring (maintain within 20% of baseline)
Neck assessment for swelling or hematoma
Cranial nerve assessment (facial droop, tongue deviation, hoarseness)
management: Maintain BP within ordered parameters
Keep head of bed elevated 30 degrees
Apply ice to surgical site as ordered
Report neurological changes immediately
Keep emergency airway equipment at bedside
nursingActions: Perform neuro checks every 1-2 hours post-op
Monitor for neck swelling or hematoma (airway compromise)
Assess for hoarseness or difficulty swallowing
Report any new neurological deficits immediately
Monitor surgical site for bleeding
Assess cranial nerves frequently

cardiovascular-rpn.title: Cardiovascular System Basics (RPN)

cellular.title: Heart Anatomy and the Cardiac Cycle
cellular.content:
The heart is a four-chambered muscular pump divided into right and left sides by the interventricular septum. The right side receives deoxygenated blood from the systemic circulation via the superior and inferior vena cava into the right atrium, passes it through the tricuspid valve into the right ventricle, and ejects it through the pulmonic valve into the pulmonary arteries for gas exchange. The left side receives oxygenated blood from the pulmonary veins into the left atrium, passes it through the mitral (bicuspid) valve into the left ventricle, and ejects it through the aortic valve into systemic circulation.

The Cardiac Cycle consists of two main phases:
1. Systole (Contraction): The ventricles contract, ejecting blood. The AV valves (tricuspid and mitral) close, producing S1 ('lub'). Pressure in the ventricles exceeds arterial pressure, opening the semilunar valves (aortic and pulmonic).
2. Diastole (Relaxation): The ventricles relax and fill with blood. The semilunar valves close, producing S2 ('dub'). The AV valves open as ventricular pressure drops below atrial pressure, allowing passive filling followed by atrial contraction (atrial kick contributes ~20-30% of ventricular filling).

Cardiac Output (CO) = Heart Rate (HR) × Stroke Volume (SV). Normal CO is 4-8 L/min. Stroke volume is influenced by Preload (volume stretching the ventricle: Frank-Starling mechanism), Afterload (resistance the ventricle must overcome to eject blood), and Contractility (force of myocardial contraction).

Coronary Arteries: The left main coronary artery branches into the Left Anterior Descending (LAD: supplies anterior wall, septum) and the Left Circumflex (LCx: supplies lateral and posterior walls). The Right Coronary Artery (RCA) supplies the right ventricle, inferior wall, and SA/AV nodes in most people. Coronary perfusion occurs primarily during diastole: tachycardia reduces diastolic filling time and can compromise coronary perfusion.

Common Cardiovascular Conditions:
• Hypertension: Sustained BP ≥130/80 mmHg. Chronic elevation increases afterload, leading to left ventricular hypertrophy and eventual heart failure. Often called the 'silent killer' because it is frequently asymptomatic until organ damage occurs.
• Heart Failure: The heart cannot pump enough blood to meet metabolic demands. Left-sided failure causes pulmonary congestion (dyspnea, crackles, orthopnea). Right-sided failure causes systemic congestion (JVD, peripheral edema, hepatomegaly).
• Angina Pectoris: Chest pain caused by myocardial ischemia (oxygen demand exceeds supply). Stable angina is predictable with exertion and relieved by rest/nitroglycerin. Unstable angina occurs at rest and is a medical emergency (ACS).
• Myocardial Infarction (MI): Complete blockage of a coronary artery leading to myocardial cell death. Classic presentation: crushing substernal chest pain radiating to left arm/jaw, diaphoresis, nausea. Women and diabetics may present atypically (fatigue, back pain, indigestion).

left: Normal heart rate 60-100 bpm
Regular rhythm with equal pulses bilaterally
Blood pressure within normal range (systolic <130, diastolic <80)
S1 and S2 heart sounds heard clearly
Capillary refill <3 seconds
No jugular venous distension
No peripheral edema
right: Hypertension: BP ≥130/80 sustained: assess for headache, visual changes, epistaxis
Tachycardia >100 bpm or Bradycardia <60 bpm: assess hemodynamic stability
Irregular pulse: may indicate atrial fibrillation or other dysrhythmia
S3 gallop: indicates ventricular failure and volume overload
Chest pain with diaphoresis: possible MI, activate emergency protocol
JVD and peripheral edema: right-sided heart failure
Dyspnea, orthopnea, crackles: left-sided heart failure with pulmonary congestion

medications.name: Nitroglycerin
medications.action: Relaxes vascular smooth muscle by releasing nitric oxide, causing venous dilation (reduces preload) and coronary artery dilation (improves myocardial oxygen supply). Used for angina relief and acute MI management

medications.name: Metoprolol
medications.action: Blocks beta-1 adrenergic receptors in the heart, reducing heart rate, contractility, and myocardial oxygen demand. Decreases blood pressure by reducing cardiac output and suppressing renin release

medications.name: Lisinopril
medications.action: Inhibits angiotensin-converting enzyme, preventing conversion of angiotensin I to angiotensin II. This causes vasodilation (reduces afterload), decreases aldosterone secretion (reduces sodium and water retention), and prevents cardiac remodeling

pearls: The coronary arteries fill during diastole, not systole: this is why tachycardia is dangerous in cardiac patients: less diastolic time = less coronary perfusion = more ischemia.
Heart failure is NOT a disease: it is a syndrome. Always look for the underlying cause: hypertension, CAD, valvular disease, cardiomyopathy.
Angina pain should resolve within 5 minutes of rest or nitroglycerin. Pain lasting >20 minutes suggests MI: activate emergency response.
Daily weights are the single most important monitoring tool for heart failure patients. Weight gain of >2 lbs/day or >5 lbs/week indicates fluid retention: report immediately.
Atypical MI presentations (no chest pain) are common in women, elderly, and diabetic patients. Look for unexplained fatigue, dyspnea, nausea, jaw/back pain.
diagnostics: Vital signs with blood pressure in both arms
Heart sounds auscultation (S1, S2, murmurs)
Peripheral pulse assessment (rate, rhythm, quality)
Edema assessment (pitting, location)
Skin color and temperature of extremities
management: Administer cardiac medications as ordered at correct times
Monitor vital signs and report abnormalities
Encourage compliance with prescribed diet (low sodium, low fat)
Assist with activity as tolerated per orders
Report chest pain or new symptoms immediately
nursingActions: Monitor vital signs including apical pulse before cardiac medications
Assess peripheral circulation (pulses, color, temperature, edema)
Report abnormal heart sounds or rhythms to RN/provider
Educate on low-sodium, heart-healthy diet
Monitor daily weight for fluid retention
Report chest pain, dyspnea, or palpitations immediately

cardiovascular-rn.title: Advanced Cardiovascular Nursing (RN)

cellular.title: Hemodynamic Monitoring and Advanced Cardiac Pathophysiology
cellular.content:
Advanced cardiovascular nursing requires understanding hemodynamic principles at the cellular and systemic levels. The heart functions as a demand-responsive pump whose performance is determined by four key hemodynamic variables:

1. Preload: The volume of blood stretching the ventricle at end-diastole. Measured as Central Venous Pressure (CVP, normal 2-6 mmHg) for right heart and Pulmonary Artery Wedge Pressure (PAWP/PCWP, normal 8-12 mmHg) for left heart. The Frank-Starling mechanism states that increased preload (stretch) increases force of contraction: UP TO A POINT. Beyond optimal stretch, the sarcomeres become overstretched and contractile force decreases (the failing heart operates on the descending limb of the Starling curve).

2. Afterload: The resistance the ventricle must overcome to eject blood. Measured as Systemic Vascular Resistance (SVR, normal 800-1200 dynes/sec/cm⁻⁵) for the left ventricle and Pulmonary Vascular Resistance (PVR, normal 150-250 dynes/sec/cm⁻⁵) for the right ventricle. Increased afterload = increased myocardial oxygen demand = decreased cardiac output.

3. Contractility: The intrinsic force of myocardial contraction independent of preload and afterload. Enhanced by sympathetic stimulation (catecholamines), digitalis, and inotropes. Decreased by hypoxia, acidosis, electrolyte imbalances, and myocardial damage.

4. Heart Rate: Directly affects cardiac output (CO = HR × SV). Tachycardia initially compensates for decreased stroke volume but ultimately reduces diastolic filling time and coronary perfusion.

Pulmonary Artery (Swan-Ganz) Catheter Monitoring:
• CVP/RAP (Right Atrial Pressure): Reflects right ventricular preload. Elevated in right heart failure, fluid overload, pulmonary hypertension, cardiac tamponade.
• PA Pressures (Normal: Systolic 20-30 / Diastolic 8-15 mmHg): Elevated in left heart failure, mitral stenosis, pulmonary hypertension, COPD.
• PAWP/PCWP (Normal 8-12 mmHg): Reflects left ventricular preload. Elevated in left heart failure (>18 = pulmonary edema). Low in hypovolemia.
• Cardiac Output/Cardiac Index: CO normal 4-8 L/min. CI (CO/BSA) normal 2.5-4.0 L/min/m². Decreased in heart failure, cardiogenic shock.
• Mixed Venous Oxygen Saturation (SvO2, normal 60-80%): Reflects the balance between oxygen delivery and consumption. Low SvO2 (<60%) indicates increased extraction (shock, anemia, increased metabolic demand).

Post-MI Nursing Priorities:
• 12-lead ECG interpretation: ST elevation (STEMI) indicates transmural injury. ST depression and T-wave inversion indicate ischemia/subendocardial injury.
• Serial troponin monitoring: Troponin I/T rises within 3-6 hours, peaks at 12-24 hours, remains elevated 7-14 days.
• Complication surveillance: Heart failure (S3, crackles), cardiogenic shock (hypotension, tachycardia, altered mental status), papillary muscle rupture (new systolic murmur), ventricular septal rupture, cardiac tamponade (Beck's triad), lethal dysrhythmias.
• Activity progression: Bed rest for first 12-24 hours, gradual ambulation with continuous telemetry monitoring.

Cardiogenic Shock in the context of MI:
• Most common cause of death in hospitalized MI patients
• Occurs when >40% of left ventricular myocardium is damaged
• Hemodynamic profile: Low CO/CI, elevated PAWP (>18), elevated SVR (compensatory vasoconstriction), low SvO2
• Emergent PCI is the treatment of choice to restore perfusion

left: Normal hemodynamic parameters: CVP 2-6, PAWP 8-12, CO 4-8 L/min, SVR 800-1200
Normal ECG: NSR, no ST changes, normal PR/QRS/QT intervals
Troponin negative, BNP <100 pg/mL
Adequate urine output >0.5 mL/kg/hr
Warm, dry skin with normal capillary refill <3 seconds
Alert and oriented, no signs of decreased perfusion
right: ST-elevation in contiguous leads: STEMI requiring emergent catheterization
Elevated troponin trending upward: active myocardial necrosis
New S3 gallop with bilateral crackles: acute heart failure/pulmonary edema
Hypotension (MAP <65) with tachycardia and altered mental status: cardiogenic shock
PAWP >18 with low CI <2.2: left ventricular failure with pulmonary congestion
New holosystolic murmur post-MI: papillary muscle rupture or VSD
SvO2 <60%: inadequate oxygen delivery, tissue hypoperfusion

medications.name: Dobutamine
medications.action: Stimulates beta-1 receptors in the myocardium, increasing contractility (positive inotrope) and mildly increasing heart rate. Increases cardiac output without significantly increasing SVR. Also has mild beta-2 effects causing peripheral vasodilation

medications.name: Heparin (Unfractionated)
medications.action: Binds to antithrombin III, potentiating its ability to inactivate thrombin (Factor IIa) and Factor Xa by 1000-fold. Prevents clot propagation and new clot formation in ACS. Does NOT dissolve existing clots: the body's fibrinolytic system does that

medications.name: Clopidogrel (Plavix)
medications.action: Irreversibly blocks the P2Y12 ADP receptor on platelets, preventing platelet aggregation for the life of the platelet (7-10 days). Used in combination with aspirin (dual antiplatelet therapy: DAPT) for ACS and post-PCI/stent placement

pearls: Hemodynamic profile recognition: Cardiogenic shock = High PAWP + Low CO + High SVR. Hypovolemic shock = Low PAWP + Low CO + High SVR. Septic shock (warm) = Low PAWP + High CO + Low SVR. Learn these patterns: they guide treatment.

Post-MI complications by timeline: Hours 0-24: Dysrhythmias (most common cause of death before hospitalization). Days 1-3: Heart failure, cardiogenic shock. Days 3-5: Papillary muscle rupture, VSD. Days 5-14: Ventricular wall rupture (cardiac tamponade). Weeks 2-8: Dressler syndrome (pericarditis).

SvO2 is the 'canary in the coal mine' for tissue perfusion. A dropping SvO2 often precedes clinical signs of shock. Normal SvO2 (60-80%) means oxygen delivery is meeting demand. Below 60% means tissues are extracting more oxygen = delivery is inadequate.

The Starling curve explains why 'more fluid is not always better.' A failing heart operates on the flat or descending portion of the curve: additional volume increases preload but does NOT increase cardiac output, instead causing pulmonary edema.
Always check SVR before giving vasopressors. If SVR is already elevated (>1400), adding a vasopressor may worsen cardiac output by increasing afterload. Consider afterload reduction instead.
diagnostics: 12-lead ECG interpretation
Cardiac biomarkers (troponin, BNP/NT-proBNP, CK-MB)
Hemodynamic monitoring (CVP, arterial line, PA catheter if placed)
Echocardiography findings correlation
Continuous telemetry monitoring and rhythm interpretation
Chest X-ray for cardiac silhouette and pulmonary congestion
management: Titrate vasoactive medications per protocol (nitroglycerin, dobutamine)
Manage fluid balance based on hemodynamic parameters
Implement heart failure pathways (diuresis, afterload reduction)
Coordinate care for cardiac catheterization or surgical intervention
Initiate anticoagulation protocols as ordered
nursingActions: Interpret cardiac rhythms and identify life-threatening arrhythmias
Perform hemodynamic calculations (CI, SVR, MAP) as applicable
Titrate vasoactive drips per protocol and monitor response
Assess for signs of decompensation (JVD, crackles, S3 gallop, peripheral edema)
Manage post-cardiac procedure care (sheath removal, hemostasis)
Coordinate rapid response for acute cardiovascular deterioration

cardiovascular-np.title: NP Cardiovascular: Advanced Management

cellular.title: Advanced Heart Failure Pharmacology and ECG Interpretation
cellular.content:
The Nurse Practitioner must master advanced heart failure classification, guideline-directed medical therapy (GDMT), and ECG interpretation at a diagnostic level.

Heart Failure Classification and Staging:
• ACC/AHA Stages: Stage A (at risk, no structural disease), Stage B (structural disease, no symptoms), Stage C (structural disease with symptoms), Stage D (refractory, requiring advanced therapies: LVAD, transplant, palliative care).
• NYHA Functional Classification: Class I (no limitation), Class II (mild limitation: comfortable at rest, symptomatic with ordinary activity), Class III (marked limitation: comfortable at rest, symptomatic with less than ordinary activity), Class IV (unable to carry out any physical activity without discomfort, symptoms at rest).
• Ejection Fraction Categories: HFrEF (EF ≤40%: systolic failure, reduced contractility), HFmrEF (EF 41-49%: mid-range), HFpEF (EF ≥50%: diastolic failure, impaired relaxation/filling).

Guideline-Directed Medical Therapy (GDMT) for HFrEF: The Four Pillars:
1. RAAS Inhibition: ACEi/ARB or preferably ARNI (sacubitril/valsartan). ARNI inhibits neprilysin (which degrades natriuretic peptides) + blocks angiotensin II receptors. PARADIGM-HF trial showed 20% reduction in cardiovascular death vs. enalapril.
2. Beta-Blocker: Carvedilol, metoprolol succinate, or bisoprolol. Start low, go slow. Only these three have mortality benefit in HF.
3. Mineralocorticoid Receptor Antagonist (MRA): Spironolactone or eplerenone. RALES trial showed 30% mortality reduction. Monitor potassium closely: risk of hyperkalemia especially with ACEi/ARB.
4. SGLT2 Inhibitor: Dapagliflozin or empagliflozin. DAPA-HF and EMPEROR-Reduced trials showed benefit regardless of diabetes status. Mechanism in HF: osmotic diuresis, reduced preload, anti-inflammatory and anti-fibrotic effects.

Additional HFrEF Therapies:
• Hydralazine + Isosorbide Dinitrate: For African American patients with NYHA III-IV already on optimal GDMT, or patients who cannot tolerate ACEi/ARB/ARNI.
• Ivabradine: Selective If current inhibitor that reduces heart rate without affecting BP or contractility. For patients in sinus rhythm with HR ≥70 on maximally tolerated beta-blocker.
• Digoxin: Positive inotrope (inhibits Na+/K+ ATPase) that reduces hospitalizations but does NOT reduce mortality. Narrow therapeutic index (0.5-2.0 ng/mL, target 0.5-0.9). Toxicity: visual changes (yellow-green halos), GI symptoms, bradycardia, heart block.

ECG Interpretation Mastery:
• Systematic Approach: Rate → Rhythm → Axis → Intervals (PR, QRS, QT) → ST segment/T-wave → Overall impression
• Axis Determination: Normal axis (leads I and aVF both positive). LAD (I positive, aVF negative: think LVH, LAFB). RAD (I negative, aVF positive: think RVH, LPFB, PE).
• Bundle Branch Blocks: RBBB (rsR' in V1, wide S in I/V6: 'MaRRoW'). LBBB (broad notched R in I/V6, QS in V1: 'WiLLiaM'). New LBBB + chest pain = STEMI equivalent.
• ST-Segment Changes: Elevation = acute injury (transmural ischemia). Depression = ischemia or reciprocal changes. Hyperacute T-waves (tall, peaked, symmetric) may be earliest sign of MI.
• QT Prolongation: QTc >500 ms = high risk for Torsades de Pointes. Common culprits: antiarrhythmics (amiodarone, sotalol), antibiotics (fluoroquinolones, azithromycin), antipsychotics, electrolyte imbalances (hypoK, hypoMg, hypoCa).

riskFactors: Modifiable: hypertension, dyslipidemia, diabetes, obesity, smoking, sedentary lifestyle, excessive alcohol
Non-modifiable: age, sex, family history, genetic predisposition
Emerging: elevated hsCRP, elevated Lp(a), obstructive sleep apnea, chronic kidney disease
ASCVD 10-year risk calculation determines treatment intensity

left: Normal ECG: Regular rate 60-100, PR 0.12-0.20, QRS <0.12, QTc <440 (male)/<460 (female)
Normal EF 55-70% on echocardiography
BNP <100 pg/mL or NT-proBNP <300 pg/mL
Normal hemodynamic profile: CI 2.5-4.0, PAWP 8-12, SVR 800-1200
Compensated heart failure: stable weight, no dyspnea at rest, functional capacity maintained
right: BNP >400 pg/mL with progressive dyspnea: decompensated heart failure requiring aggressive diuresis
New LBBB with chest pain: STEMI equivalent requiring emergent catheterization
EF <25% with NYHA IV symptoms: Stage D HF, evaluate for LVAD or transplant
QTc >500 ms: discontinue offending medications, monitor on telemetry, correct electrolytes
Digoxin level >2.0 ng/mL with visual changes and bradycardia: toxicity, administer Digibind
Cardiorenal syndrome: Rising creatinine with aggressive diuresis: balance decongestion with renal preservation

medications.name: Sacubitril/Valsartan (Entresto)
medications.action: Dual mechanism: Sacubitril inhibits neprilysin, preventing degradation of natriuretic peptides (ANP, BNP), which promote vasodilation, natriuresis, and diuresis. Valsartan blocks AT1 receptors, preventing vasoconstriction and aldosterone release. Net effect: reduced preload and afterload, decreased cardiac remodeling, improved outcomes in HFrEF

medications.name: Spironolactone
medications.action: Competitively blocks aldosterone receptors in the collecting duct, reducing sodium reabsorption and potassium excretion. In heart failure, aldosterone contributes to myocardial fibrosis, ventricular remodeling, and endothelial dysfunction: spironolactone blocks these deleterious effects independent of its diuretic action

medications.name: Amiodarone
medications.action: Blocks potassium channels (prolongs repolarization and refractory period), sodium channels, calcium channels, and beta-adrenergic receptors. This multi-channel blockade makes it effective against both atrial and ventricular dysrhythmias. Extremely long half-life (40-55 days) due to extensive tissue distribution

pearls: The Four Pillars of HFrEF GDMT (ARNI + BB + MRA + SGLT2i) should be initiated and uptitrated to target doses as quickly as tolerated. Each pillar independently reduces mortality: getting all four on board is critical.
New LBBB + chest pain = STEMI equivalent. Do NOT wait for troponin to come back: activate the cath lab. The LBBB makes ST-segment analysis unreliable, so clinical presentation drives the decision.

BNP and NT-proBNP: BNP is cleared by neprilysin, so levels are UNRELIABLE in patients on ARNI (sacubitril inhibits neprilysin, causing BNP accumulation). Use NT-proBNP instead for monitoring patients on Entresto.

Heart failure with preserved ejection fraction (HFpEF) has no proven mortality-reducing therapy except SGLT2 inhibitors (EMPEROR-Preserved trial). Treatment focuses on managing congestion, controlling comorbidities (hypertension, obesity, AFib), and exercise.

When initiating beta-blockers in heart failure, the patient may initially feel WORSE due to reduced contractility. Educate: 'The medication needs time to work: improvement in heart function occurs over weeks to months. Do not stop taking it because you feel worse initially.'
diagnostics: Order 12-lead ECG with systematic interpretation (rate, rhythm, axis, intervals, ST/T changes)
Order echocardiography for structural and functional assessment (EF, wall motion, valves)
Order cardiac biomarkers: troponin (MI), BNP/NT-proBNP (heart failure), D-dimer (PE/DVT)
Order lipid panel with calculated ASCVD risk score
Order stress testing (exercise or pharmacologic) for stable angina evaluation
Order cardiac catheterization for acute coronary syndrome or high-risk findings
management: Prescribe evidence-based GDMT for HFrEF (ARNI/ACEi/ARB + beta-blocker + MRA + SGLT2i)
Prescribe antiplatelet therapy (DAPT post-PCI, aspirin for secondary prevention)
Prescribe statin therapy based on ASCVD risk (high-intensity for ACS/known ASCVD)
Order anticoagulation for atrial fibrillation using CHA2DS2-VASc score
Manage hypertensive emergencies with IV antihypertensives and 25% MAP reduction in first hour
nursingActions: Perform comprehensive cardiovascular examination including JVP, PMI, S3/S4, murmur characterization
Interpret ECG findings and correlate with clinical presentation
Calculate and trend hemodynamic parameters
Evaluate medication efficacy and adverse effects at each visit
Counsel on lifestyle modifications (diet, exercise, smoking cessation, weight management)
Coordinate referrals to cardiology, cardiac rehab, and electrophysiology as indicated

chf-basics.title: Congestive Heart Failure

cellular.title: Heart Failure Pathophysiology: Left vs Right, Systolic vs Diastolic
cellular.content:
Heart failure (HF) is a clinical syndrome in which the heart is unable to pump sufficient blood to meet the metabolic demands of the body, OR can only do so at elevated filling pressures. It represents the final common pathway of virtually all cardiac diseases.

Left-Sided Heart Failure (most common):
The left ventricle fails to adequately eject blood into systemic circulation. Blood backs up into the left atrium and pulmonary veins, increasing pulmonary capillary hydrostatic pressure. When this pressure exceeds plasma oncotic pressure (~25 mmHg), fluid transudates into the pulmonary interstitium and alveoli, causing pulmonary edema.
• Pathophysiology: LV dysfunction → increased LV end-diastolic pressure → increased LA pressure → increased pulmonary venous pressure → pulmonary congestion → impaired gas exchange → hypoxemia
• Clinical Manifestations: Dyspnea (hallmark), orthopnea (dyspnea when lying flat: fluid redistributes to lungs), paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea (PND: sudden awakening with severe dyspnea 2-3 hours after falling asleep), bibasilar crackles, S3 gallop (volume overload: ventricular gallop), frothy pink-tinged sputum (severe pulmonary edema), tachycardia, fatigue

Right-Sided Heart Failure:
Usually occurs secondary to left-sided failure (most common cause). The right ventricle fails, causing blood to back up into the systemic venous circulation.
• Pathophysiology: RV dysfunction → increased RV end-diastolic pressure → increased RA pressure → systemic venous congestion → peripheral edema, hepatic congestion, ascites
• Primary RV failure causes: Pulmonary hypertension, cor pulmonale (from COPD), right ventricular MI, PE
• Clinical Manifestations: Jugular venous distension (JVD), hepatomegaly and hepatojugular reflux, dependent peripheral edema (pedal edema when upright, sacral edema when supine), ascites, weight gain, anorexia and nausea (hepatic/GI congestion)

Systolic vs Diastolic Dysfunction:
• Systolic Failure (HFrEF: EF ≤40%): The ventricle is dilated and weak, unable to contract forcefully enough to eject adequate blood volume. The heart becomes a 'baggy pump.' Think: Contractility problem. Causes: Post-MI damage, dilated cardiomyopathy, chronic volume overload.
• Diastolic Failure (HFpEF: EF ≥50%): The ventricle is stiff and non-compliant, unable to relax and fill adequately during diastole. The EF appears normal because the ventricle ejects a normal percentage of a reduced volume. The heart becomes a 'stiff box.' Think: Filling problem. Causes: Chronic hypertension (concentric LVH), hypertrophic cardiomyopathy, aortic stenosis.

NYHA Functional Classification:
• Class I: No limitation of physical activity. Ordinary activity does not cause symptoms.
• Class II: Slight limitation. Comfortable at rest. Ordinary activity causes fatigue, dyspnea, or palpitations.
• Class III: Marked limitation. Comfortable at rest. Less than ordinary activity causes symptoms.
• Class IV: Unable to carry out any physical activity without discomfort. Symptoms at rest.

Key Laboratory Values:
• BNP (B-type Natriuretic Peptide): Released from ventricular myocytes in response to stretching (volume overload). BNP <100 pg/mL = HF unlikely. BNP >400 pg/mL = HF likely. BNP 100-400 = indeterminate (consider other causes of dyspnea).
• NT-proBNP: Inactive fragment with longer half-life. More sensitive but affected by renal function. Age-stratified cutoffs: <50 years: >450 pg/mL, 50-75 years: >900, >75 years: >1800.
• Troponin: May be mildly elevated in acute decompensation due to subendocardial ischemia from increased wall stress.

Neurohormonal Compensation (Initially helpful, ultimately harmful):
1. Sympathetic Nervous System activation: Increases HR and contractility but also increases afterload and myocardial oxygen demand.
2. RAAS activation: Sodium and water retention increases preload but worsens congestion. Angiotensin II causes vasoconstriction (increased afterload) and promotes cardiac remodeling (fibrosis).
3. Natriuretic peptide release: Promotes vasodilation and natriuresis (beneficial counterregulatory response).

left: Stable compensated HF: Baseline dyspnea on exertion only
Controlled weight (no gain >2 lbs/day)
BNP at patient's baseline level
Adequate urine output on current diuretic regimen
Able to perform ADLs without significant limitation
No orthopnea (sleeps flat or with one pillow)
right: Acute pulmonary edema: Severe dyspnea, frothy pink sputum, SpO2 dropping, crackles throughout: sit upright, oxygen, IV furosemide STAT
New S3 gallop: indicates volume overload and ventricular dysfunction
Weight gain >3 lbs in 2 days or >5 lbs in 1 week: fluid retention, needs diuretic adjustment
JVD, hepatomegaly, and 3+ pitting edema: right-sided failure with systemic congestion
BNP >400 rising from baseline: decompensation, evaluate for triggers (dietary indiscretion, medication non-compliance, infection, arrhythmia)
Orthopnea requiring 3+ pillows or sleeping in recliner: worsening left-sided failure
PND episodes waking patient from sleep: fluid redistribution from dependent areas to pulmonary vasculature in supine position

medications.name: Furosemide (Lasix)
medications.action: Inhibits the sodium-potassium-2-chloride (Na+/K+/2Cl-) cotransporter in the thick ascending loop of Henle, preventing reabsorption of approximately 25% of filtered sodium. This creates a powerful diuresis, reducing intravascular volume and preload. IV onset 5 minutes, PO onset 30-60 minutes

medications.name: Carvedilol
medications.action: Blocks beta-1 receptors (reduces heart rate and contractility), beta-2 receptors (bronchial smooth muscle), and alpha-1 receptors (causes vasodilation, reducing afterload). In heart failure, chronic beta-blockade reverses deleterious neurohormonal activation, reduces cardiac remodeling, and improves EF over time. Has antioxidant properties that may provide additional cardioprotection

medications.name: Digoxin
medications.action: Inhibits the sodium-potassium ATPase pump in cardiac myocytes, increasing intracellular sodium, which then increases intracellular calcium via the sodium-calcium exchanger, enhancing contractility. Also increases vagal tone (parasympathetic), slowing conduction through the AV node (useful in atrial fibrillation). At toxic levels, increases automaticity and can cause life-threatening dysrhythmias

pearls: LEFT-sided failure = LUNGS (pulmonary symptoms: dyspnea, crackles, orthopnea, PND). RIGHT-sided failure = REST of body (systemic symptoms: JVD, edema, hepatomegaly, ascites). Memory aid: Left = Lungs, Right = Rest.
Daily weights are MORE RELIABLE than I&O for assessing fluid status. Weigh at the same time each day, same scale, same clothing. 1 liter of fluid = 1 kilogram (2.2 lbs) of weight.

The most common cause of right-sided heart failure is LEFT-sided heart failure. Chronic left-sided failure increases pulmonary pressures, which the right ventricle must pump against, eventually leading to RV failure.

BNP is a 'rule-out' test for heart failure. BNP <100 = dyspnea is NOT from HF (look for other causes: COPD, PE, pneumonia). BNP >400 = dyspnea IS from HF. BNP 100-400 = indeterminate, need additional workup.

The 'HF Zone System' for patient education: Green Zone (all clear) = no symptoms, stable weight. Yellow Zone (caution) = weight gain >2 lbs/day, increased dyspnea, more pillows needed: call provider. Red Zone (emergency) = severe dyspnea at rest, unrelieved by sitting up, unable to sleep: call 911.
diagnostics: Daily weight monitoring (1 kg gain = 1L fluid retention)
Vital signs with blood pressure assessment
Lung sounds auscultation (crackles indicate pulmonary congestion)
Peripheral edema assessment (pitting edema grading)
Jugular vein distension assessment
Intake and output monitoring
management: Administer diuretics as ordered and monitor output
Restrict sodium intake as ordered (typically 2g/day)
Restrict fluids as ordered (typically 1.5-2L/day)
Elevate head of bed to facilitate breathing
Administer oxygen as ordered
nursingActions: Weigh patient daily at same time, same scale, same clothing
Monitor strict intake and output
Assess breath sounds for crackles (pulmonary edema)
Monitor for peripheral edema and JVD
Educate patient on daily weight monitoring at home (report 2-3 lb gain in 1 day or 5 lb in 1 week)
Administer medications as ordered and monitor for adverse effects

mi-acute.title: Acute Myocardial Infarction

cellular.title: Coronary Occlusion and Myocardial Cell Death
cellular.content:
Acute Myocardial Infarction (AMI) occurs when a coronary artery is abruptly occluded, interrupting blood flow to the myocardium. The underlying mechanism almost always involves rupture of an atherosclerotic plaque, exposing the lipid core and subendothelial collagen to flowing blood, which triggers the coagulation cascade and platelet aggregation, forming an occlusive thrombus.

Ischemic Cascade (Time is Myocardium):
• 0-20 minutes: Reversible ischemia. Cells switch from aerobic to anaerobic metabolism. ATP production drops from 36 to 2 ATP per glucose molecule. Lactic acid accumulates, causing chest pain. ECG shows T-wave changes.
• 20-40 minutes: Irreversible injury begins. Cell membranes lose integrity, intracellular enzymes leak (troponin, CK-MB). Necrosis starts in the subendocardium (area most vulnerable to ischemia) and progresses transmurally toward the epicardium ('wavefront phenomenon').
• >6 hours: Extensive transmural necrosis. Complete infarction of the at-risk myocardium.

STEMI vs NSTEMI:
• STEMI (ST-Elevation MI): Complete coronary occlusion causing transmural ischemia/necrosis. ECG shows ST-elevation ≥1 mm in ≥2 contiguous leads (or new LBBB). Requires emergent reperfusion (PCI within 90 minutes of first medical contact, or fibrinolytics within 30 minutes if PCI not available within 120 minutes).
• NSTEMI (Non-ST-Elevation MI): Partial coronary occlusion or complete occlusion with collateral flow. Troponin elevated but ECG shows ST-depression, T-wave inversion, or may be non-diagnostic. Managed medically initially with antiplatelet, anticoagulant, and anti-ischemic therapy, with catheterization within 24-72 hours.

Localization by ECG Leads:
• Anterior Wall (LAD): Leads V1-V4. Largest MI, worst prognosis. Risk of cardiogenic shock.
• Lateral Wall (LCx): Leads I, aVL, V5-V6.
• Inferior Wall (RCA): Leads II, III, aVF. Check right-sided leads (V4R) for RV involvement. Avoid nitroglycerin and morphine in RV infarct (preload-dependent).
• Posterior Wall: Reciprocal ST-depression in V1-V3 (tall R-waves, ST-depression). Confirm with posterior leads V7-V9.

Biomarkers:
• Troponin I/T: Gold standard. Most sensitive and specific. Rises 3-6 hours, peaks 12-24 hours, remains elevated 7-14 days. High-sensitivity troponin (hs-cTn) can detect MI within 1-3 hours.
• CK-MB: Rises 4-6 hours, peaks 12-24 hours, returns to normal in 48-72 hours. Useful for detecting reinfarction (because it normalizes faster than troponin).

Percutaneous Coronary Intervention (PCI):
• Primary PCI: Emergent catheterization with balloon angioplasty ± stent placement for STEMI. Door-to-balloon time goal <90 minutes.
• Drug-Eluting Stent (DES): Coated with antiproliferative medication to prevent restenosis. Requires DAPT (aspirin + P2Y12 inhibitor) for minimum 12 months: premature discontinuation = stent thrombosis.
• Bare Metal Stent (BMS): No drug coating. Higher restenosis rate but shorter DAPT requirement (1-3 months).

MONA-B Protocol (initial MI management):
• M - Morphine (for pain unrelieved by nitroglycerin: use cautiously, may cause hypotension)
• O - Oxygen (only if SpO2 <94%: routine oxygen in normoxic patients may increase infarct size)
• N - Nitroglycerin (reduces preload and coronary vasodilation: contraindicated in RV infarct, hypotension, PDE5 inhibitor use)
• A - Aspirin (162-325 mg chewed immediately: irreversible COX-1 inhibition preventing thromboxane A2 production)
• B - Beta-blocker (reduces heart rate, BP, and myocardial oxygen demand: avoid in acute decompensated HF, bradycardia, hypotension)

left: Typical anginal chest pain relieved by rest and nitroglycerin within 5 minutes
Normal ECG with no ST changes during pain-free intervals
Normal serial troponins
Hemodynamically stable with adequate perfusion
right: Crushing substernal chest pain lasting >20 minutes unrelieved by nitroglycerin: activate STEMI alert
ST-elevation in contiguous leads: emergent cardiac catheterization required
Troponin rising on serial draws: active myocardial necrosis
Diaphoresis, nausea, sense of impending doom: sympathetic response to ischemia
New dysrhythmias (PVCs, VTach, heart block): ischemia-induced electrical instability
Hypotension with tachycardia and altered mentation: cardiogenic shock, call rapid response
Atypical presentation in women/elderly/diabetics: epigastric pain, jaw pain, fatigue, dyspnea without chest pain

medications.name: Aspirin
medications.action: Irreversibly acetylates cyclooxygenase-1 (COX-1) in platelets, blocking production of thromboxane A2 (TXA2), a potent platelet aggregator and vasoconstrictor. Since platelets lack nuclei and cannot synthesize new COX, inhibition lasts the life of the platelet (7-10 days). In MI, aspirin prevents further thrombus growth on the ruptured plaque

medications.name: Alteplase (tPA)
medications.action: Recombinant tissue plasminogen activator that binds to fibrin in thrombus and converts plasminogen to plasmin, which enzymatically dissolves the fibrin clot. Used when PCI is not available within 120 minutes of first medical contact for STEMI

medications.name: Ticagrelor (Brilinta)
medications.action: Reversibly binds and inhibits the P2Y12 ADP receptor on platelets, preventing ADP-mediated platelet activation and aggregation. Unlike clopidogrel, does not require hepatic activation (consistent antiplatelet effect regardless of CYP2C19 polymorphisms) and has faster onset/offset of action

pearls: Time is Myocardium: Every 30-minute delay in reperfusion increases mortality. Door-to-balloon <90 minutes for PCI. Door-to-needle <30 minutes for fibrinolytics. The first hour after symptom onset is the 'golden hour': most myocardium can be salvaged.

Right Ventricular MI: If inferior STEMI (ST-elevation in II, III, aVF), ALWAYS check right-sided ECG (V4R). RV infarct is preload-dependent: the failing RV needs volume to maintain output. AVOID nitroglycerin, morphine, and diuretics (all reduce preload). Treatment: IV fluid bolus to maintain preload.

Reperfusion dysrhythmias are a GOOD sign! When a coronary artery is reopened (by PCI or fibrinolytics), the reperfused myocardium often generates dysrhythmias (PVCs, AIVR, brief runs of VTach). These usually resolve spontaneously and indicate successful reperfusion.

Post-MI medications for EVERY patient (unless contraindicated): 'ABCDE': Aspirin/Antiplatelet, Beta-blocker, Cholesterol (statin: high-intensity), DAPT (dual antiplatelet if stent), ACEI/ARB (prevent remodeling).

Morphine in MI: Use with CAUTION. While it relieves pain, morphine may worsen outcomes in NSTEMI (increased mortality in some studies). Use ONLY if pain is unrelieved by nitroglycerin. Current guidelines have de-emphasized morphine: some institutions have moved to 'ONA-B' instead of 'MONA-B.'
diagnostics: 12-lead ECG monitoring for ST changes (as reported)
Pain assessment using PQRST
Vital signs monitoring continuously
Oxygen saturation monitoring
Monitor for signs of cardiogenic shock
management: Administer MONA protocol as ordered (Morphine, Oxygen, Nitroglycerin, Aspirin)
Maintain bed rest during acute phase
Establish IV access
Prepare for cardiac catheterization as ordered
Monitor for arrhythmias on telemetry
nursingActions: Administer aspirin and nitroglycerin as ordered (hold NTG if SBP < 90)
Monitor continuous telemetry for arrhythmias
Assess chest pain characteristics and response to treatment
Monitor vital signs frequently (every 5-15 minutes during acute phase)
Keep patient calm and minimize oxygen demand
Report unrelieved chest pain after 3 nitroglycerin doses

hypertension-management.title: Hypertension Management

cellular.title: Hypertension Pathophysiology and Classification
cellular.content:
Hypertension is defined as a sustained systolic blood pressure ≥130 mmHg and/or diastolic blood pressure ≥80 mmHg (2017 ACC/AHA guidelines). It is the most prevalent modifiable risk factor for cardiovascular disease, affecting approximately 46% of US adults.

Blood Pressure Classification (ACC/AHA 2017):
• Normal: SBP <120 AND DBP <80
• Elevated: SBP 120-129 AND DBP <80
• Stage 1 Hypertension: SBP 130-139 OR DBP 80-89
• Stage 2 Hypertension: SBP ≥140 OR DBP ≥90
• Hypertensive Crisis: SBP >180 AND/OR DBP >120

Pathophysiology:
95% of hypertension is Primary (Essential): no identifiable cause. It results from complex interactions between genetic predisposition and environmental factors that increase peripheral vascular resistance and/or cardiac output.

Mechanisms contributing to sustained hypertension:
1. RAAS Overactivation: Angiotensin II causes direct vasoconstriction, stimulates aldosterone release (sodium/water retention), promotes vascular smooth muscle hypertrophy, and stimulates sympathetic nervous system activity.
2. Sympathetic Nervous System Overactivity: Increases heart rate, contractility, and vascular tone through catecholamine release.
3. Endothelial Dysfunction: Reduced nitric oxide (vasodilator) production and increased endothelin-1 (vasoconstrictor) production shift the balance toward vasoconstriction.
4. Sodium Retention: Excess dietary sodium increases intravascular volume and sensitizes vascular smooth muscle to vasoconstrictors.
5. Vascular Remodeling: Chronic pressure elevation causes hypertrophy of vascular smooth muscle, increasing wall thickness and narrowing the lumen: this creates a self-perpetuating cycle of elevated resistance.

Secondary Hypertension (5%): Always consider in:
• Young patients (<30 years) with severe hypertension
• Resistant hypertension (uncontrolled on 3+ medications including a diuretic)
• Abrupt onset of hypertension or sudden worsening
Common causes: Renal artery stenosis (renovascular HTN), pheochromocytoma (episodic HTN with headache, sweating, palpitations: excess catecholamines), primary aldosteronism (Conn syndrome: hypokalemia + HTN), coarctation of the aorta (upper extremity HTN with lower extremity hypotension), Cushing syndrome, thyroid disorders, obstructive sleep apnea.

Target Organ Damage from Chronic Hypertension:
• Heart: Left ventricular hypertrophy → diastolic dysfunction → heart failure. Coronary artery disease and MI.
• Brain: Stroke (ischemic and hemorrhagic), vascular dementia, hypertensive encephalopathy.
• Kidneys: Hypertensive nephrosclerosis → chronic kidney disease → ESRD. Proteinuria is early sign.
• Eyes: Hypertensive retinopathy (arteriolar narrowing, AV nicking, cotton-wool spots, flame hemorrhages, papilledema in severe cases).
• Vasculature: Aortic dissection, peripheral artery disease, aortic aneurysm.

Hypertensive Crisis:
• Hypertensive Urgency: SBP >180 and/or DBP >120 WITHOUT acute target organ damage. Reduce BP gradually over 24-48 hours with oral medications. Goal: 25% reduction in first few hours, then to <160/100 over 24-48 hours.
• Hypertensive Emergency: SBP >180 and/or DBP >120 WITH acute target organ damage (encephalopathy, stroke, MI, aortic dissection, acute kidney injury, pulmonary edema). Requires IV medications in ICU setting. Reduce MAP by no more than 25% in first hour (except in aortic dissection: goal SBP <120 within 20 minutes).

Lifestyle Modifications (each independently reduces SBP by 4-11 mmHg):
• DASH diet (rich in fruits, vegetables, low-fat dairy, low saturated fat)
• Sodium restriction (<2,300 mg/day, ideally <1,500 mg/day): ~5 mmHg reduction
• Regular aerobic exercise (150 min/week moderate intensity): ~5-8 mmHg
• Weight loss (if BMI >25): ~1 mmHg per kg lost
• Moderate alcohol intake (≤2 drinks/day men, ≤1 drink/day women): ~4 mmHg
• Smoking cessation: Does not directly lower BP but dramatically reduces CV risk

left: Blood pressure within target range (<130/80 for most patients)
No evidence of target organ damage on exam
Clear lung sounds, no S3/S4
Normal fundoscopic exam
Normal renal function and urinalysis
No headache, visual changes, or neurologic deficits
right: SBP >180 and/or DBP >120 with headache, visual changes, chest pain: hypertensive emergency, immediate IV therapy needed
New onset proteinuria or rising creatinine: hypertensive nephropathy
Flame hemorrhages and papilledema on fundoscopy: grade III/IV hypertensive retinopathy
LVH on ECG (increased voltage, strain pattern): chronic pressure overload
New focal neurologic deficits with severe hypertension: hypertensive encephalopathy or stroke
Sudden severe tearing back pain with hypertension: aortic dissection, emergent BP reduction

medications.name: Amlodipine
medications.action: Blocks L-type voltage-gated calcium channels in vascular smooth muscle, inhibiting calcium influx and causing arterial vasodilation. Reduces peripheral vascular resistance (afterload) without significant effects on cardiac conduction (unlike non-dihydropyridines like verapamil/diltiazem). Long half-life allows once-daily dosing

medications.name: Hydrochlorothiazide (HCTZ)
medications.action: Inhibits the sodium-chloride cotransporter in the distal convoluted tubule, preventing sodium reabsorption and producing a modest diuresis. The long-term antihypertensive effect is primarily from vasodilation (mechanism not fully understood: possibly reduced vascular smooth muscle calcium sensitivity). Enhances calcium reabsorption in the DCT

medications.name: Nitroprusside (Nipride)
medications.action: Releases nitric oxide (NO) directly, causing relaxation of both arterial and venous smooth muscle. Potent, rapid-acting vasodilator that reduces both preload and afterload. Onset within seconds, duration 1-10 minutes after discontinuation. Used for hypertensive emergencies requiring precise, minute-to-minute BP control

pearls: Hypertension is the 'silent killer': most patients are asymptomatic until target organ damage occurs. Regular screening and compliance monitoring are essential.

First-line agents: ACEi/ARB, CCB, or thiazide diuretic. Initial drug selection based on patient characteristics: ACEi/ARB for diabetics, CKD, HF, post-MI. CCB or thiazide for elderly and Black patients. Combination therapy is often needed to reach target.

In hypertensive emergency, do NOT lower BP too fast! Dropping MAP >25% in the first hour risks cerebral, coronary, or renal ischemia. The autoregulatory curve has shifted rightward in chronic hypertension: the brain/kidneys need higher pressures to perfuse. Exception: Aortic dissection (SBP <120 within 20 minutes).
The white coat effect can cause falsely elevated readings. Confirm with ambulatory BP monitoring (ABPM) or home BP monitoring before diagnosing hypertension and starting medications.

Resistant hypertension (uncontrolled on 3+ drugs including diuretic): Screen for secondary causes (renal artery stenosis, primary aldosteronism, pheochromocytoma, OSA). Adding spironolactone as 4th agent is most effective per PATHWAY-2 trial.
diagnostics: Blood pressure measurement (correct cuff size, proper technique)
Assess for target organ damage signs (headache, visual changes, chest pain)
Monitor for orthostatic hypotension with position changes
Assess medication compliance
management: Administer antihypertensive medications as ordered
Encourage DASH diet (low sodium, high potassium)
Encourage regular exercise as tolerated
Educate on medication compliance
Report hypertensive crisis (SBP > 180 or DBP > 120) immediately
nursingActions: Measure blood pressure using correct technique (proper cuff size, arm at heart level)
Monitor for orthostatic hypotension (measure BP lying, sitting, standing)
Educate on DASH diet and sodium restriction
Teach patient to avoid abruptly stopping antihypertensive medications
Report persistent elevated BP or symptoms of hypertensive emergency
Monitor for medication side effects (dizziness, bradycardia, electrolyte changes)

cardiac-monitoring.title: Cardiac Monitoring and Telemetry

cellular.title: Cardiac Conduction System and Rhythm Interpretation
cellular.content:
The heart's electrical conduction system generates and propagates impulses that coordinate cardiac contraction. Understanding this system is fundamental to rhythm interpretation.

Conduction Pathway:
1. SA Node (Sinoatrial Node): Primary pacemaker located in the right atrium near the SVC junction. Intrinsic rate: 60-100 bpm. Generates the impulse that initiates each heartbeat.
2. Internodal Pathways: Conduct the impulse through the atria, causing atrial depolarization (P-wave on ECG).
3. AV Node (Atrioventricular Node): Located at the junction of the atria and ventricles in the interatrial septum. Slows conduction (0.12-0.20 second delay = PR interval) to allow ventricular filling after atrial contraction. Intrinsic rate: 40-60 bpm (junctional rhythm if SA node fails).
4. Bundle of His: Penetrates the fibrous skeleton of the heart, connecting the AV node to the ventricles.
5. Bundle Branches: Right and Left bundle branches course along the interventricular septum. The left bundle divides into the left anterior fascicle and left posterior fascicle.
6. Purkinje Fibers: Terminal network that rapidly conducts impulses throughout the ventricular myocardium, causing synchronized ventricular depolarization (QRS complex). Intrinsic rate: 20-40 bpm (ventricular escape rhythm).

ECG Components:
• P-wave: Atrial depolarization. Should be upright in lead II, one P before each QRS.
• PR Interval: Time from onset of atrial depolarization to onset of ventricular depolarization. Normal: 0.12-0.20 seconds (3-5 small boxes). Prolonged = first-degree AV block.
• QRS Complex: Ventricular depolarization. Normal: <0.12 seconds (3 small boxes). Wide QRS = bundle branch block or ventricular origin.
• ST Segment: Represents early ventricular repolarization. Should be isoelectric (at baseline). Elevation = acute injury/STEMI. Depression = ischemia.
• T-wave: Ventricular repolarization. Should be upright in most leads. Inversion may indicate ischemia. Tall, peaked = hyperkalemia.
• QT Interval: Total ventricular depolarization and repolarization. Corrected QT (QTc) should be <440 ms (male) or <460 ms (female). Prolonged QTc >500 ms = risk of Torsades de Pointes.

Normal Sinus Rhythm (NSR) Criteria:
• Rate: 60-100 bpm
• Rhythm: Regular (R-R intervals equal)
• P-waves: Present, upright in lead II, one P before each QRS
• PR interval: 0.12-0.20 seconds, consistent
• QRS: <0.12 seconds

Common Arrhythmias (Basic Recognition):
• Sinus Bradycardia: Rate <60, regular rhythm, normal P-QRS-T morphology. May be normal in athletes. Treat only if symptomatic (atropine, pacing).
• Sinus Tachycardia: Rate >100, regular rhythm, normal P-QRS-T. Always a response to something (pain, fever, anxiety, hypovolemia, HF). Treat the underlying cause, not the rhythm.
• Premature Atrial Contractions (PACs): Early beat with abnormal P-wave followed by normal QRS. Usually benign.
• Premature Ventricular Contractions (PVCs): Wide, bizarre QRS without preceding P-wave. Isolated PVCs are usually benign. Concern when: >6/minute, multifocal, couplets/triplets, R-on-T phenomenon (PVC landing on the T-wave can trigger VTach/VFib).
• Atrial Fibrillation: Irregularly irregular rhythm with NO discernible P-waves (fibrillatory baseline). Rate may be controlled (60-100) or uncontrolled (>100). Major risk: blood stasis in atria → thrombus formation → stroke.
• Ventricular Tachycardia: ≥3 consecutive PVCs at rate >100. Wide QRS, regular rhythm. May be pulseless (treat as cardiac arrest with defibrillation) or pulsed (synchronized cardioversion or antiarrhythmics).
• Ventricular Fibrillation: Chaotic, disorganized ventricular electrical activity with no effective cardiac output. Always pulseless: immediate CPR and defibrillation required.

left: Normal sinus rhythm on telemetry: rate 60-100, regular, normal P-QRS-T
PR interval 0.12-0.20 seconds, consistent
QRS <0.12 seconds
No ectopy (PVCs, PACs)
Patient asymptomatic with stable hemodynamics
right: New atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response (>150 bpm): rate control with diltiazem or beta-blocker
Sustained VTach with pulse: prepare for synchronized cardioversion, amiodarone
VFib or pulseless VTach: immediate CPR and defibrillation, activate code blue
Complete heart block (third-degree AV block): transcutaneous pacing, prepare for transvenous pacing
ST-elevation on monitoring: obtain 12-lead ECG immediately, activate STEMI protocol
New prolonged QTc >500 ms: review medications, check Mg2+ and K+, risk of Torsades
Asystole: CPR, epinephrine, identify and treat reversible causes (H's and T's)

medications.name: Atropine
medications.action: Blocks acetylcholine at muscarinic receptors in the SA and AV nodes, reducing parasympathetic (vagal) tone. This increases SA node firing rate and improves AV nodal conduction. First-line pharmacologic treatment for symptomatic bradycardia

medications.name: Adenosine
medications.action: Slows conduction through the AV node by activating potassium channels and inhibiting calcium channels, causing brief AV nodal block. This breaks re-entrant circuits that use the AV node as one limb of the circuit (SVT/AVNRT/AVRT). Effect lasts only 10-15 seconds due to rapid cellular uptake and metabolism

pearls: Systematic rhythm interpretation: 1) Is there a pulse? (Pulseless = ACLS protocol) 2) Rate? (Tachy vs Brady) 3) Rhythm regular or irregular? 4) P-waves present? (One P per QRS?) 5) PR interval? 6) QRS width? This approach prevents missing critical findings.

Sinus tachycardia is NEVER treated with cardioversion. It is always a compensatory response: treat the underlying cause (fever, pain, hypovolemia, anxiety, PE, HF). Cardioverting sinus tach removes the heart's compensation and can cause cardiovascular collapse.

Artifact vs. real rhythm: Always check the patient first. If the monitor shows VFib but the patient is sitting up talking to you, it's artifact. Check lead placement, loose electrodes, patient movement, and electrical interference before treating a rhythm.

The most important rhythm in ACLS is the one you can identify as NOT normal. You don't need to name every rhythm perfectly: you need to recognize when something is wrong, assess for a pulse, and initiate appropriate treatment (CPR/defib for pulseless, medication/cardioversion for pulsed unstable, medications for pulsed stable).

Telemetry alarm fatigue is a leading safety concern. Ensure alarm parameters are individualized to each patient and respond to every alarm: even false alarms require acknowledgment. The one alarm you ignore may be the real event.
diagnostics: Continuous telemetry monitoring
Lead placement verification (correct electrode position)
Heart rate and rhythm assessment
Identify lethal arrhythmias (VFib, VTach, asystole)
Monitor for artifact vs. true arrhythmia
management: Report abnormal rhythms to RN/provider immediately
Maintain proper lead placement and skin preparation
Change electrodes every 24-48 hours or when signal is poor
Ensure alarms are set appropriately and never turned off
Report lethal arrhythmias and call for help immediately
nursingActions: Verify correct lead placement at beginning of each shift
Monitor telemetry continuously and respond to alarms
Differentiate artifact from true arrhythmia (check patient first)
Report any new arrhythmia immediately
Ensure alarm limits are set appropriately for each patient
Document rhythm strips per facility policy

cardiac-rhythm-rn.title: Cardiac Rhythm Interpretation (RN Level)

cellular.title: Advanced Dysrhythmia Recognition and Management
cellular.content:
Advanced rhythm interpretation requires understanding the electrophysiologic mechanisms behind dysrhythmias and their clinical significance.

Mechanisms of Dysrhythmias:
1. Automaticity: Enhanced or abnormal automaticity in cells outside the SA node. Example: Ectopic atrial tachycardia, accelerated junctional rhythm.
2. Re-entry: An electrical impulse travels in a circular pathway, continuously re-excites tissue. Requires: unidirectional block in one pathway and slow conduction in an alternative pathway. Most common mechanism for: SVT (AVNRT), atrial flutter, VTach.
3. Triggered Activity: Afterdepolarizations that reach threshold and generate premature beats. Early afterdepolarizations (EADs) occur during repolarization: mechanism for Torsades de Pointes. Delayed afterdepolarizations (DADs) occur after repolarization: mechanism for digoxin toxicity arrhythmias.

Atrial Fibrillation (AFib):
• Multiple chaotic re-entrant circuits in the atria firing at 350-600/min. AV node acts as a filter, allowing only some impulses through (irregularly irregular ventricular response).
• No organized P-waves: irregular fibrillatory baseline.
• CHA₂DS₂-VASc Score: Determines stroke risk and need for anticoagulation. C=CHF(1), H=HTN(1), A₂=Age≥75(2), D=DM(1), S₂=Stroke/TIA(2), V=Vascular disease(1), A=Age 65-74(1), Sc=Sex female(1). Score ≥2 (males) or ≥3 (females) = anticoagulation recommended.
• Rate Control: Target HR <110 at rest. Agents: Beta-blockers (metoprolol), CCBs (diltiazem/verapamil), digoxin (adjunct only). Avoid CCBs in HFrEF.
• Rhythm Control: Cardioversion (electrical or pharmacologic). Must rule out LAA thrombus (TEE) or anticoagulate for ≥3 weeks before elective cardioversion.

Atrial Flutter:
• Single macro-re-entrant circuit in the right atrium (typical flutter: counterclockwise around the tricuspid annulus).
• Sawtooth flutter waves at ~300/min, most commonly with 2:1 conduction → ventricular rate ~150 bpm.
• Hallmark: Regular tachycardia at ~150 bpm. Think flutter until proven otherwise.
• Catheter ablation of the cavotricuspid isthmus is curative for typical flutter.

Supraventricular Tachycardia (SVT):
• Regular narrow-complex tachycardia, rate 150-250 bpm.
• Most common type: AVNRT (AV Nodal Re-entrant Tachycardia): re-entry circuit within or near the AV node.
• Treatment: Vagal maneuvers first (Valsalva, carotid massage: NOT both carotids simultaneously, cold water face immersion). If unsuccessful: Adenosine 6mg → 12mg → 12mg. If unstable: Synchronized cardioversion.

Ventricular Tachycardia (VTach):
• ≥3 consecutive ventricular beats at >100 bpm. Wide QRS (>0.12 sec), typically regular.
• Monomorphic VTach: Uniform QRS morphology: usually from a scar-related re-entry circuit (post-MI).
• Polymorphic VTach: Varying QRS morphology. If QT is prolonged = Torsades de Pointes (treat with IV magnesium, overdrive pacing). If QT normal = treat as ischemia.
• Sustained VTach (>30 sec or causing hemodynamic compromise): Pulseless → defibrillation. Pulsed + unstable → synchronized cardioversion. Pulsed + stable → amiodarone or procainamide.

Ventricular Fibrillation (VFib):
• Chaotic ventricular electrical activity with no organized depolarization. No cardiac output. Lethal without immediate intervention.
• Treatment: Immediate high-quality CPR + defibrillation (NOT synchronized: chaotic rhythm cannot be synchronized). Energy: Biphasic 120-200J initial, max energy for subsequent shocks.
• Epinephrine 1mg IV/IO every 3-5 minutes. Amiodarone 300mg IV first dose, 150mg second dose for refractory VFib/pVT.

Heart Blocks:
• First-Degree: Prolonged PR interval (>0.20 sec) but all impulses conducted. Usually benign. No treatment needed.
• Second-Degree Type I (Wenckebach/Mobitz I): Progressive PR prolongation until a beat is dropped. 'Longer, longer, longer, DROP: that's a Wenckebach.' Usually benign, located at AV node level. Responds to atropine.
• Second-Degree Type II (Mobitz II): Sudden dropped beats WITHOUT progressive PR prolongation. PR interval is constant for conducted beats. Located below the AV node (His-Purkinje system). HIGH RISK of progressing to third-degree block. Requires pacing.
• Third-Degree (Complete): No atrial impulses conduct to ventricles. P-waves and QRS complexes are completely independent (AV dissociation). Atrial rate > ventricular rate. Ventricular escape rhythm may be junctional (40-60, narrow QRS) or ventricular (20-40, wide QRS). Requires permanent pacemaker.

Pacemaker Rhythms:
• Pacemaker spikes: Vertical lines before P-waves (atrial pacing), QRS (ventricular pacing), or both (dual-chamber).
• Expected findings: Consistent capture (each spike followed by a complex), appropriate sensing (pacemaker inhibits when native rhythm present).
• Failure to capture: Spike present but no subsequent depolarization. Causes: lead displacement, battery depletion, elevated threshold.
• Failure to sense: Pacemaker fires inappropriately (doesn't recognize native beats). Risk of R-on-T and VFib.

riskFactors: Drug toxicity (digoxin, beta-blockers, calcium channel blockers)

left: Normal sinus rhythm with appropriate rate for clinical situation
Stable hemodynamics with any rhythm (MAP >65, adequate perfusion)
Controlled atrial fibrillation with ventricular rate 60-110
Functioning pacemaker with consistent capture and sensing
right: New-onset atrial fibrillation with RVR >150: rate control, evaluate for cardioversion, assess CHA₂DS₂-VASc for anticoagulation
Monomorphic VTach with pulse: amiodarone IV, prepare for cardioversion if hemodynamics worsen
Torsades de Pointes: IV magnesium 2g bolus, correct QT-prolonging factors, overdrive pacing
Mobitz Type II block: transcutaneous pacing on standby, anticipate permanent pacemaker
Pacemaker failure to capture: reposition patient, increase output, check connections, notify provider STAT
Sustained wide-complex tachycardia at 150 bpm: assume VTach until proven otherwise, do NOT give adenosine

medications.name: Amiodarone
medications.action: Predominantly blocks potassium channels (prolongs action potential duration and refractory period) but also has sodium-channel blocking (Class I), calcium-channel blocking (Class IV), and beta-blocking (Class II) properties. This multi-channel blockade makes it effective against both supraventricular and ventricular arrhythmias. Long half-life (40-55 days)

medications.name: Diltiazem
medications.action: Blocks L-type calcium channels in the AV node, slowing conduction velocity and increasing refractory period. Also reduces heart rate by slowing SA node automaticity. Used primarily for rate control in atrial fibrillation and atrial flutter. Has mild negative inotropic effect

medications.name: Magnesium Sulfate
medications.action: Stabilizes cardiac cell membranes by regulating potassium and calcium channel function. In Torsades de Pointes specifically, magnesium suppresses early afterdepolarizations (EADs) that trigger the arrhythmia by blocking L-type calcium channels during the prolonged repolarization phase. Also essential cofactor for Na+/K+ ATPase (affects all membrane potentials)

pearls: Wide-complex tachycardia = VTach until proven otherwise. Treating VTach as SVT-with-aberrancy is far more dangerous than treating SVT as VTach. When in doubt, treat as VTach.
AFib with a ventricular rate of exactly 150? Think: Is this actually atrial FLUTTER with 2:1 block? Increase paper speed to 50 mm/sec or use vagal maneuvers/adenosine to unmask flutter waves.

Mobitz Type II is the dangerous second-degree block (risk of complete block → asystole). 'Longer, longer, longer, DROP = Wenckebach' (Type I: relatively benign). 'Some P's don't get through, same PR when they do = Mobitz II' (dangerous: needs pacing).
The 'Ugly Five': rhythms requiring immediate intervention: VFib (defib), Pulseless VTach (defib), Asystole (CPR + epi), PEA (CPR + epi + fix cause), and Torsades (Mg2+ + overdrive pacing).

When a patient with a pacemaker develops bradycardia, think failure to CAPTURE (spikes without complexes) or failure to PACE (no spikes when expected). Place a magnet over the pacemaker to switch to asynchronous mode (VOO/DOO): this tests the pacemaker's ability to fire and capture without being inhibited by sensing.
diagnostics: Systematic ECG interpretation (rate, rhythm, P waves, PR interval, QRS width, ST segment)
Continuous telemetry with alarm management
Electrolyte panel (K+, Mg2+, Ca2+) correlation with rhythm
Drug levels when applicable (digoxin, antiarrhythmic levels)
12-lead ECG for new-onset arrhythmias
management: Implement ACLS protocols for life-threatening arrhythmias
Administer antiarrhythmic medications per protocol
Prepare for synchronized cardioversion or defibrillation as ordered
Correct underlying electrolyte imbalances
Initiate transcutaneous pacing for symptomatic bradycardia
nursingActions: Identify and classify arrhythmias using systematic approach
Differentiate stable vs unstable arrhythmias (hemodynamic assessment)
Administer antiarrhythmic medications and monitor for adverse effects
Prepare emergency equipment (defibrillator, pacing pads, airway supplies)
Monitor electrolytes and correlate with rhythm changes
Document rhythm changes and interventions with rhythm strips

cardiac-auscultation-rn.title: Cardiac Auscultation: Heart Sounds and Murmurs

cellular.title: Hemodynamics of Heart Sounds and Valvular Pathology
cellular.content:
Heart sounds are produced by the closure of valves and the turbulence of blood flow. Understanding the hemodynamic events that produce each sound is essential for clinical assessment.

Normal Heart Sounds:
• S1 ('Lub'): Closure of the AV valves (mitral and tricuspid) at the BEGINNING of systole. Best heard at the apex (mitral area: 5th ICS, left MCL). S1 marks the onset of ventricular contraction.
