Brugada ECG patterns are classified into three types: Type 1 (diagnostic) shows coved ST elevation ≥2 mm followed by a negative T wave in V1-V3. Type 2 shows saddleback ST elevation ≥2 mm with positive or biphasic T wave. Type 3 shows either coved or saddleback morphology with <1 mm ST elevation. Only Type 1 is diagnostic; Types 2 and 3 require provocative testing with sodium channel blockers (ajmaline, procainamide, or flecainide) to unmask a Type 1 pattern.

riskFactors: SCN5A mutation carriers (autosomal dominant with variable penetrance, 20-30% of cases)
Male sex - 8:1 male predominance due to higher testosterone-mediated Ito density in male epicardium
Southeast Asian descent - highest prevalence (1 in 1000 in Thailand, leading cause of sudden death in young men)
Family history of sudden cardiac death before age 45
Febrile illness - fever augments sodium channel dysfunction and can unmask Type 1 ECG pattern
Medications that block sodium channels (Class IA/IC antiarrhythmics, tricyclic antidepressants, lithium, cocaine)
Vagal predominance - symptoms occur at rest or during sleep when vagal tone is highest

left: Type 1 (coved) Brugada ECG pattern: ST elevation ≥2 mm with coved morphology and negative T wave in V1-V3
Type 2 (saddleback) pattern: saddleback ST elevation ≥2 mm with positive or biphasic T wave in V1-V3
Normal baseline ECG with dynamic/intermittent ST changes (concealed Brugada)
Positive drug provocation: conversion from Type 2/3 to Type 1 pattern with ajmaline or procainamide
right: Polymorphic VT/VF (typically nocturnal or at rest)
Unexplained syncope (especially during sleep or at rest, without prodrome)
Agonal respirations during sleep (witnessed nocturnal events)
Sudden cardiac arrest - often first presentation in previously asymptomatic individuals

medications.name: Quinidine
medications.action: Blocks the transient outward potassium current (Ito) that drives the pathological transmural voltage gradient in Brugada syndrome. By reducing Ito, quinidine restores the epicardial action potential dome and eliminates the substrate for phase 2 reentry. Also blocks INa and IKr, prolonging both QRS and QT intervals.

medications.name: Isoproterenol
medications.action: Increases L-type calcium current (ICa,L) via beta-1 stimulation and cAMP-mediated phosphorylation of calcium channels. The enhanced ICa,L counterbalances the dominant Ito, restoring the epicardial action potential dome and suppressing phase 2 reentry. Also increases heart rate, reducing vagal arrhythmogenic substrate.

pearls: Brugada Type 1 ECG can be unmasked by fever, sodium channel blockers, vagotonic states, and hypokalemia - always check ECG during febrile illness in known carriers
ICD is the ONLY proven therapy to prevent sudden cardiac death in Brugada - no antiarrhythmic drug has shown mortality benefit in RCTs
Brugada ECG mimickers: right bundle branch block, early repolarization, anterior STEMI - always correlate with clinical context and consider V1-V3 lead repositioning
diagnostics: Order resting 12-lead ECG with right precordial leads V1-V3 positioned in higher intercostal spaces (2nd and 3rd) if standard placement is non-diagnostic
Order sodium channel blocker provocation test (ajmaline 1 mg/kg IV over 5 min or procainamide 10 mg/kg IV over 20 min) if Type 2 or 3 pattern is present
Order genetic testing for SCN5A and related genes (CACNA1C, CACNB2, SCN1B) for confirmed Brugada
Order electrophysiology study (EPS) with programmed ventricular stimulation for risk stratification in asymptomatic patients
Order cascade genetic screening for first-degree family members of confirmed cases
Order Holter or event monitor to document spontaneous arrhythmias
management: Prescribe ICD implantation for survivors of cardiac arrest or documented sustained VT/VF (Class I indication)
Prescribe ICD for patients with spontaneous Type 1 ECG pattern AND history of syncope (Class IIa)
Order electrophysiology study for risk stratification in asymptomatic patients with spontaneous Type 1 pattern
Prescribe quinidine for ICD-ineligible patients or as adjunct for electrical storm (restores epicardial action potential dome by blocking Ito)
Prescribe isoproterenol infusion for acute management of electrical storm in Brugada (increases ICa,L, counteracting Ito dominance)
Order catheter ablation of the right ventricular outflow tract epicardium for recurrent VF despite ICD and quinidine
Prescribe fever management protocol: aggressive antipyretic therapy with acetaminophen for any febrile episode
nursingActions: Order comprehensive medication review - discontinue all sodium channel blocking drugs (consult Brugadadrugs.org database)
Prescribe ICD with defibrillation threshold testing for indicated patients
Order genetic counseling and cascade screening for first-degree relatives
Prescribe aggressive fever management protocol (acetaminophen standing order for temperature >38°C)
Evaluate syncope episodes with detailed history: nocturnal events, rest-associated = higher risk
Order risk stratification with EPS for asymptomatic patients with drug-induced Type 1 only
Prescribe quinidine 300-600 mg PO daily in divided doses for patients with recurrent ICD shocks

long-qt-syndrome-np.title: Long QT Syndrome (NP)

cellular.title: Repolarization Channelopathy & Torsades de Pointes
cellular.content:
Long QT syndrome (LQTS) encompasses a group of inherited and acquired disorders characterized by delayed ventricular repolarization, manifesting as prolonged QTc interval on ECG and predisposing to torsades de pointes (TdP), a polymorphic ventricular tachycardia that can degenerate into ventricular fibrillation and sudden cardiac death.

At the cellular level, the QT interval represents the duration of the ventricular action potential (phases 0 through 3). Normal repolarization requires a precise balance between inward depolarizing currents that prolong the action potential (late INa, ICa,L) and outward repolarizing currents that shorten it (IKr, IKs, IK1). LQTS results from either gain-of-function mutations in depolarizing channels (increased INa in LQT3 via SCN5A) or loss-of-function mutations in repolarizing channels (reduced IKs in LQT1 via KCNQ1, reduced IKr in LQT2 via KCNH2/hERG).

Acquired LQTS (drug-induced) most commonly involves blockade of the hERG potassium channel (IKr), which has an unusually large inner vestibule that accommodates many structurally diverse drug molecules. When IKr is blocked, repolarization is prolonged, and early afterdepolarizations (EADs) can develop during phase 2 or 3 of the action potential. EADs occur when L-type calcium channels recover from inactivation during the prolonged plateau phase and reactivate, generating a secondary depolarization. If this EAD reaches threshold, it triggers a premature action potential.

Torsades de pointes arises from the interaction of EADs with transmural dispersion of repolarization. M-cells (mid-myocardial cells) have longer action potential duration than epicardial or endocardial cells. When repolarization is further prolonged, the dispersion between layers increases, creating a functional reentrant substrate. An EAD-triggered premature beat encounters heterogeneous refractoriness, initiating the characteristic twisting QRS morphology around the isoelectric baseline.

riskFactors: Congenital LQTS gene mutations - LQT1 (KCNQ1, 40-55%), LQT2 (KCNH2/hERG, 30-45%), LQT3 (SCN5A, 5-10%)

QTc-prolonging medications - antiarrhythmics (sotalol, dofetilide, amiodarone), antibiotics (fluoroquinolones, azithromycin, TMP-SMX), antipsychotics (haloperidol, ziprasidone), antiemetics (ondansetron, droperidol)
Hypokalemia - reduces IKr current by altering channel rectification properties
Hypomagnesemia - impairs repolarizing K+ channel function and enhances L-type Ca2+ current
Female sex - 2:1 female predominance for drug-induced LQTS due to lower baseline IKr density (estrogen effect)
Bradycardia - slower heart rates prolong repolarization and increase EAD susceptibility (pause-dependent TdP)
Structural heart disease with reduced ejection fraction - heterogeneous repolarization substrate

left: Prolonged QTc interval on ECG (>470 ms males, >480 ms females)
T-wave morphology changes (bifid T waves in LQT2, late-onset T waves in LQT3)
QTc >500 ms = high risk for torsades de pointes
U wave prominence indicating prolonged repolarization
right: Torsades de pointes: polymorphic VT with QRS complexes twisting around isoelectric baseline, often preceded by short-long-short sequence
Syncope (often exercise-triggered in LQT1, auditory startle in LQT2, sleep/rest in LQT3)
Seizure-like episodes (misdiagnosed as epilepsy - always obtain ECG in young patients with seizures)
Sudden cardiac arrest/death - may be first presentation

medications.name: Magnesium Sulfate
medications.action: Suppresses early afterdepolarizations (EADs) by blocking L-type calcium channel reactivation during the prolonged plateau phase. Also enhances Na+/K+-ATPase activity, stabilizing resting membrane potential. Reduces transmural dispersion of repolarization. Does NOT shorten the QTc interval but prevents the triggered activity that initiates TdP.

medications.name: Nadolol
medications.action: Blocks beta-1 and beta-2 adrenergic receptors, reducing sympathetic stimulation that triggers EADs and arrhythmias in congenital LQTS. Beta-blockade reduces cAMP-mediated phosphorylation of L-type calcium channels, decreasing ICa,L and suppressing EAD formation. Also reduces heart rate variability and prevents catecholamine surges during exercise or emotional stress.

pearls: QTc >500 ms is the critical threshold - risk of TdP increases exponentially. Discontinue offending drugs, correct electrolytes, and consider overdrive pacing.
Torsades treatment is NOT the same as monomorphic VT: do NOT give amiodarone or procainamide (both prolong QT further). First-line: IV magnesium. Second-line: overdrive pacing or isoproterenol.
Genotype-trigger correlations: LQT1 = exercise (especially swimming), LQT2 = auditory stimuli (alarm clocks, phones), LQT3 = sleep/rest. Counsel patients on genotype-specific avoidance strategies.
diagnostics: Order 12-lead ECG and calculate QTc using Bazett formula (QTc = QT/√RR) - prolonged if >470 ms (males) or >480 ms (females)
Order comprehensive metabolic panel with magnesium (hypokalemia and hypomagnesemia lower TdP threshold)
Order complete medication reconciliation using CredibleMeds QTdrugs database (crediblemeds.org)
Order genetic testing for confirmed or suspected congenital LQTS (targeted panel: KCNQ1, KCNH2, SCN5A)
Order serial ECG monitoring when initiating any QTc-prolonging medication (baseline, 2-4 hours post-dose, and steady state)
Order Holter monitoring to assess QTc dynamics, T-wave morphology, and pause-dependent QT prolongation
management: Prescribe IV magnesium sulfate 2 g over 10-20 minutes as first-line treatment for torsades de pointes (stabilizes myocardial cell membranes and suppresses EADs)
Order immediate discontinuation of all QTc-prolonging medications
Prescribe isoproterenol infusion or order overdrive temporary pacing at 90-110 bpm for pause-dependent TdP (shortens QT by accelerating repolarization)
Prescribe IV potassium replacement to maintain K+ >4.0 mEq/L (higher target than standard to maximize IKr function)
Prescribe ICD implantation for congenital LQTS with prior cardiac arrest, recurrent syncope on beta-blockers, or QTc >500 ms with symptoms
Prescribe nadolol or propranolol for congenital LQT1 and LQT2 (first-line chronic therapy - reduces sympathetic triggers for EADs)
Prescribe mexiletine for LQT3 (late sodium channel blocker that shortens action potential duration)
nursingActions: Order baseline ECG and QTc calculation before prescribing any QTc-prolonging medication
Prescribe QTc monitoring protocol: hold drug and notify if QTc >500 ms or QTc increases >60 ms from baseline
Order aggressive electrolyte replacement: potassium target >4.0 mEq/L, magnesium target >2.0 mg/dL
Prescribe IV magnesium sulfate 2 g stat for any patient with torsades de pointes
Order continuous telemetry for all patients on high-risk QTc-prolonging medications (dofetilide, sotalol loading requires 72-hour inpatient monitoring)
Evaluate all new prescriptions against CredibleMeds database for QTc-prolonging potential before ordering
Prescribe genotype-specific therapy: beta-blockers for LQT1/LQT2, mexiletine for LQT3

tetralogy-of-fallot.title: Tetralogy of Fallot

cellular.title: Right Ventricular Outflow Tract Obstruction
cellular.content: Tetralogy of Fallot (TOF) is the most common cyanotic congenital heart defect, caused by anterior and cephalad deviation of the infundibular septum during embryonic development. This single developmental error produces four anatomical defects: (1) Pulmonary stenosis  -  narrowing of the right ventricular outflow tract (RVOT) restricts blood flow to the lungs, reducing oxygenation. The degree of stenosis determines clinical severity. (2) Ventricular septal defect (VSD)  -  a large, nonrestrictive defect allows communication between ventricles. (3) Overriding aorta  -  the aortic root shifts rightward, straddling the VSD and receiving blood from both ventricles. (4) Right ventricular hypertrophy  -  develops secondarily as the right ventricle works against the pulmonary obstruction. Hemodynamically, pulmonary stenosis increases right-sided pressures, causing deoxygenated blood to shunt right-to-left through the VSD into the overriding aorta and systemic circulation. This results in systemic desaturation and cyanosis. During hypercyanotic ('Tet') spells, infundibular spasm acutely worsens RVOT obstruction, dramatically increasing the right-to-left shunt and causing profound cyanosis, hypoxia, and metabolic acidosis. Without intervention, severe spells can lead to seizures, stroke, or death.

riskFactors: Maternal rubella infection in first trimester
Family history of congenital heart disease
Fetal exposure to phenytoin, retinoic acid, or other teratogens

left: Cyanosis (worsens with crying/feeding)
Systolic Ejection Murmur (RVOT obstruction)
Clubbing of Fingers/Toes (chronic hypoxia)
Squatting Behavior in Older Children
right: Hypercyanotic (Tet) Spells
Irritability and Inconsolable Crying
Boot-Shaped Heart on CXR
Polycythemia (compensatory)

medications.name: Morphine Sulfate
medications.action: Relaxes infundibular spasm and reduces systemic venous return during Tet spells

medications.name: Propranolol
medications.action: Reduces infundibular spasm and heart rate, stabilizing RVOT dynamics

medications.name: Prostaglandin E1 (Alprostadil)
medications.action: Maintains ductus arteriosus patency to allow pulmonary blood flow in severe cases

pearls: Knee-chest position is the FIRST nursing action during a Tet spell  -  it increases systemic vascular resistance, which reduces right-to-left shunting and forces blood through the pulmonary valve
Squatting in older, unrepaired children is the self-taught equivalent of knee-chest  -  children instinctively assume this position to relieve cyanosis
Boot-shaped heart on CXR is classic: concavity at the pulmonary artery segment with upturned apex from RV hypertrophy
Do NOT administer digoxin during Tet spells  -  increased contractility worsens infundibular obstruction
diagnostics: Expect echocardiogram to confirm the four anatomical defects (gold standard)
Monitor pulse oximetry continuously (baseline saturations typically 75-85%)
Expect chest X-ray showing 'boot-shaped heart' (coeur en sabot) from RV hypertrophy
Expect ECG showing right axis deviation and right ventricular hypertrophy
Monitor hemoglobin and hematocrit (compensatory polycythemia develops)
Expect cardiac catheterization pre-operatively to define anatomy
Monitor arterial blood gases during Tet spells
management: Place infant in knee-chest position during hypercyanotic (Tet) spells
Administer oxygen as ordered during Tet spells (supplemental, not curative)
Administer morphine as prescribed to reduce infundibular spasm during spells
Administer IV phenylephrine as prescribed to increase SVR during severe spells
Provide small, frequent, high-calorie feedings with rest periods
Cluster nursing care to minimize oxygen consumption
Prepare family for surgical repair (complete intracardiac repair typically at 3-6 months)
Maintain IV access as ordered
nursingActions: Monitor for hypercyanotic (Tet) spells: sudden deep cyanosis, irritability, inconsolable crying, hyperpnea
Position infant in knee-chest position immediately during Tet spells and notify provider
Monitor oxygen saturation trends and report declining baseline
Assess feeding tolerance (diaphoresis, tachypnea, fatigue during feeds indicate cardiac stress)
Monitor for signs of polycythemia complications (thrombosis, stroke)
Maintain adequate hydration to prevent hemoconcentration and sickling of polycythemic blood
Educate parents on recognizing Tet spells at home and knee-chest positioning
Monitor post-operative complications: bleeding, arrhythmias, residual VSD, pulmonary regurgitation

copd-exacerbation.title: COPD: Airway & Oxygenation

cellular.title: Air Trapping
cellular.content: Chronic inflammation and alveolar destruction lead to air trapping and impaired gas exchange. Excessive mucus production and bronchospasm cause frequent exacerbations.

left: Barrel Chest
Productive Cough
Pursed-Lip Breathing
Clubbing
right: Respiratory Acidosis
Hypoxemic Drive Failure
Confusion/CO2 Narcosis
Silent Chest (Emergency)

medications.name: Albuterol
medications.action: Bronchodilation

medications.name: Ipratropium
medications.action: Blocks bronchoconstriction

pearls: Keep O2 sats between 88-92% for chronic retainers
Avoid high-flow oxygen which can suppress respiratory drive
Position: High-Fowler's or Tripod
diagnostics: Monitor oxygen saturation continuously (target 88-92%)
Expect ABG to be drawn to assess CO2 retention
Expect chest X-ray to be ordered
Monitor sputum color, amount, and consistency
Expect CBC and metabolic panel
Expect pulmonary function tests when stable
management: Position in high-Fowler's or tripod position
Administer low-flow oxygen as ordered (1-2 L/min)
Administer bronchodilators and steroids as prescribed
Encourage pursed-lip breathing
Maintain adequate hydration to thin secretions
Assist with energy conservation techniques
nursingActions: Monitor respiratory rate, depth, and oxygen saturation frequently
Report O2 sat < 88% or signs of CO2 narcosis immediately
Assess breath sounds every shift and report changes
Monitor for signs of respiratory distress (accessory muscle use, nasal flaring)
Encourage and assist with incentive spirometry
Document sputum characteristics
Educate on smoking cessation and inhaler technique

asthma-emergency.title: Asthma & Status Asthmaticus

cellular.title: Airway Hyperreactivity
cellular.content: Chronic inflammatory disorder. During an attack, bronchoconstriction and airway edema severely limit airflow. Status Asthmaticus is an unresponsive medical emergency.

left: Expiratory Wheezing
Chest Tightness
Accessory Muscle Use
Tachypnea
right: Silent Chest
Paradoxical Breathing
Inability to speak in sentences
Respiratory Acidosis

medications.name: Albuterol
medications.action: Rapid Bronchodilation

medications.name: Methylprednisolone
medications.action: Reduces airway inflammation

pearls: Silent chest = imminent respiratory arrest
Peak Flow Meter helps track control
Avoid beta-blockers in asthma patients
diagnostics: Monitor peak expiratory flow rate (PEFR) trends
Monitor oxygen saturation continuously
Expect ABG to be drawn in severe exacerbation
Expect chest X-ray to rule out pneumonia
Monitor respiratory rate and effort closely
Expect CBC if infection suspected
management: Position in high-Fowler's or tripod for breathing ease
Administer oxygen as ordered
Administer bronchodilators and steroids as prescribed
Maintain calm environment to reduce anxiety
Ensure IV access as ordered
Keep intubation equipment accessible during severe attacks
nursingActions: Monitor respiratory status continuously (rate, effort, SpO2)
Report silent chest or inability to speak immediately
Assess breath sounds before and after treatments
Monitor peak flow readings and document trends
Educate on proper inhaler technique and spacer use
Report worsening dyspnea or accessory muscle use
Provide reassurance to reduce anxiety

peds-respiratory.title: Pediatric Respiratory: RSV & Croup

cellular.title: Airway Narrowing
cellular.content: Infant airways are much smaller and more prone to obstruction from inflammation and mucus. RSV (Bronchiolitis) affects the lower airways, while Croup (Laryngotracheobronchitis) affects the upper airway.

left: RSV: Wheezing/Crackles
Copious Secretions
Nasal Flaring
Retractions
right: Croup: Barking Cough
Inspiratory Stridor
Steeple Sign on X-ray
Agitation

medications.name: Ribavirin
medications.action: Inhibits viral replication

medications.name: Dexamethasone
medications.action: Reduces upper airway edema

pearls: RSV: Suctioning before feeding is the priority
Croup: Cool mist or cold air can help relieve symptoms
Monitor for dehydration due to poor feeding
diagnostics: Expect nasal wash for RSV testing
Monitor oxygen saturation continuously
Expect chest X-ray (steeple sign for croup)
Monitor respiratory rate and effort closely
Expect CBC if bacterial infection suspected
Monitor hydration status (mucous membranes, fontanelle)
management: Maintain humidified oxygen as ordered
Suction nares with bulb syringe before feeding (RSV)
Position with head elevated for breathing ease
Provide cool mist or cold air for croup as ordered
Maintain IV fluids if oral intake is inadequate
Maintain droplet/contact precautions for RSV
nursingActions: Monitor respiratory rate, effort, and oxygen saturation continuously
Report signs of respiratory distress (retractions, nasal flaring, grunting)
Assess hydration status and feeding tolerance
Suction as needed before feedings
Monitor for signs of dehydration (decreased wet diapers, dry mucous membranes)
Provide small, frequent feedings as tolerated
Educate parents on hand hygiene and infection prevention

epiglottitis-peds.title: Epiglottitis: Airway Emergency

cellular.title: Supraglottic Inflammation
cellular.content: A life-threatening bacterial infection (often H. influenzae type b) causing sudden, massive swelling of the epiglottis, which can completely occlude the airway within minutes.

left: High Fever
The 4 Ds: Drooling, Dysphagia, Dysphonia, Distress
Inspiratory Stridor
Tripod Position
right: Absent Cough
Agitation
Cyanosis
Total Airway Obstruction

medications.name: Ceftriaxone
medications.action: Treats underlying infection

pearls: NEVER examine the throat with a tongue depressor (triggers laryngospasm)
Keep the child calm (avoid crying)
Prepare for emergency intubation/tracheostomy
diagnostics: Expect lateral neck X-ray (thumbprint sign) to be ordered
Monitor oxygen saturation continuously
Expect blood cultures after airway is secured
Monitor respiratory effort and airway patency closely
Expect CBC with differential to be drawn
management: NEVER examine the throat with tongue depressor
Keep child calm and in position of comfort (sitting upright)
Have emergency airway equipment at bedside (intubation/trach tray)
Maintain oxygen delivery as ordered
Administer antibiotics AFTER airway is secured as prescribed
Allow parent to stay to reduce child's anxiety
nursingActions: Monitor for signs of complete airway obstruction (stridor, cyanosis)
Keep child as calm as possible (avoid crying/agitation)
Report worsening stridor or respiratory distress immediately
Maintain NPO status
Do NOT attempt throat examination or cultures until airway secured
Stay with the child at all times during acute phase
Document respiratory status and interventions frequently

ards-management.title: ARDS Management

cellular.title: Diffuse Alveolar Damage
cellular.content: Acute Respiratory Distress Syndrome involves diffuse alveolar damage from inflammatory mediators. Increased alveolar-capillary permeability causes non-cardiogenic pulmonary edema. Bilateral infiltrates on CXR. Severity by PaO2/FiO2 ratio: <300 (mild), <200 (moderate), <100 (severe).

left: Acute Onset Dyspnea
Refractory Hypoxemia (not responding to O2)
Bilateral Infiltrates on CXR ('white-out')
Decreased Lung Compliance
right: Tachypnea
Accessory Muscle Use
Cyanosis
Respiratory Failure Requiring Intubation

medications.name: Cisatracurium
medications.action: Eliminates patient-ventilator dyssynchrony in severe ARDS

medications.name: Dexamethasone
medications.action: Reduces inflammatory response in select cases

pearls: Lung-protective ventilation: Tidal volume 6mL/kg IDEAL body weight, Plateau pressure <30 cmH2O
Prone positioning improves V/Q matching and reduces mortality
Conservative fluid strategy (keep patient dry)
Address the underlying cause (sepsis, aspiration, pneumonia)
diagnostics: Oxygen saturation monitoring (SpO2)
Respiratory rate and effort assessment
Breath sounds auscultation
Monitor for worsening respiratory distress
Observe ventilator alarms and settings as reported
management: Maintain positioning as ordered (prone positioning if indicated)
Assist with oral care every 2 hours
Report changes in oxygen saturation promptly
Maintain head of bed elevation as ordered
Monitor fluid balance (conservative fluid strategy)
nursingActions: Monitor respiratory status continuously
Maintain HOB at 30-45 degrees
Perform oral care with chlorhexidine every 2 hours
Report increasing oxygen requirements to RN/physician
Assist with repositioning and prone positioning as ordered
Monitor intake and output strictly

active-tb.title: Active Tuberculosis (TB)

cellular.title: Acid-Fast Bacillus Infection
cellular.content: Mycobacterium tuberculosis is an acid-fast bacillus transmitted via airborne droplets. Infects alveolar macrophages and forms caseating granulomas. Primary infection is often latent (90-95%). Reactivation occurs in immunocompromised states (HIV, malnutrition, immunosuppressive therapy).

riskFactors: Close contact with active TB case

left: Chronic Productive Cough >3 Weeks
Hemoptysis
Night Sweats
Unintentional Weight Loss
right: Low-Grade Fever
Cavitary Lesions on CXR (upper lobes)
Positive Sputum AFB Smear
Fatigue and Malaise

medications.name: RIPE Therapy
medications.action: Rifampin, Isoniazid, Pyrazinamide, Ethambutol for initial 2 months

medications.name: Pyridoxine (Vitamin B6)
medications.action: Prevents INH-induced peripheral neuropathy

pearls: Airborne precautions: N95 respirator for staff, negative pressure room for patient
3 consecutive negative sputum AFB cultures needed to discontinue airborne isolation
Rifampin turns body fluids orange/red (tears, urine, sweat) - warn patient
Monitor hepatic function regularly (LFTs) - hepatotoxicity is major risk
diagnostics: Sputum specimen collection as ordered (3 specimens)
Temperature monitoring
Weight monitoring
Respiratory assessment (cough character, sputum production)
management: Implement airborne precautions (negative pressure room, N95 for staff)
Administer RIPE therapy medications as ordered
Ensure directly observed therapy (DOT) compliance
Report medication side effects promptly
Maintain isolation until 3 negative sputum cultures
nursingActions: Wear N95 respirator when entering patient room
Collect sputum specimens early morning as ordered
Monitor for hepatotoxicity symptoms (jaundice, dark urine, RUQ pain)
Educate patient on covering cough and proper tissue disposal
Teach patient that Rifampin turns body fluids orange/red
Monitor nutritional status and encourage adequate intake

osa-management.title: Obstructive Sleep Apnea (OSA)

cellular.title: Upper Airway Collapse
cellular.content: Repetitive upper airway collapse during sleep causes intermittent hypoxia and sleep fragmentation. Increased sympathetic nervous system activity leads to HTN, cardiac arrhythmias, and metabolic syndrome. Apnea-Hypopnea Index (AHI) >5 events/hour is diagnostic.

left: Loud Snoring
Witnessed Apneic Episodes
Excessive Daytime Sleepiness (Epworth Scale)
Morning Headache
right: Nocturia
Obesity (BMI >30)
Large Neck Circumference (>17in men, >16in women)
Cognitive Impairment/Mood Changes

medications.name: CPAP
medications.action: Pneumatic splint keeps airway open during sleep

medications.name: Modafinil
medications.action: Reduces residual daytime sleepiness despite CPAP use

pearls: Weight loss can be curative in obese patients
Polysomnography (sleep study) is the gold standard for diagnosis
CPAP compliance is the key challenge - educate on proper use
Lateral sleeping position reduces airway collapse
Avoid alcohol and sedatives before bed (relax airway muscles)
diagnostics: Epworth Sleepiness Scale screening
Neck circumference measurement
BMI calculation
Oxygen saturation monitoring during sleep
Assess for morning headaches
management: Encourage weight loss for overweight patients
Assist with CPAP education and proper mask fitting
Advise lateral sleeping position
Educate on avoiding alcohol and sedatives before bed
Report excessive daytime sleepiness to provider
nursingActions: Educate patient on proper CPAP use and maintenance
Assess compliance with CPAP therapy
Monitor for complications of untreated OSA (hypertension, arrhythmias)
Teach importance of weight loss
Report signs of sleep deprivation (excessive drowsiness, irritability)
Encourage follow-up sleep studies as ordered

respiratory-rpn.title: Respiratory System Basics (RPN)

cellular.title: Lung Anatomy and Oxygenation Fundamentals
cellular.content:
The respiratory system's primary function is gas exchange: delivering oxygen (O2) to the blood and removing carbon dioxide (CO2). Understanding the anatomy and physiology of respiration is foundational to recognizing and managing respiratory conditions.

Anatomy:
• Upper Airway: Nose, pharynx, larynx. Warms, humidifies, and filters inspired air. The epiglottis protects the airway during swallowing.
• Lower Airway: Trachea (divides at the carina into right and left main bronchi), bronchi, bronchioles, terminal bronchioles, respiratory bronchioles, alveolar ducts, and alveoli.
• Right Lung: 3 lobes (upper, middle, lower). Right mainstem bronchus is wider, shorter, and more vertical than left: aspiration and endotracheal tube malposition preferentially go to the RIGHT lung.
• Left Lung: 2 lobes (upper, lower). Smaller due to cardiac notch.
• Alveoli: ~300 million thin-walled sacs where gas exchange occurs. Lined with Type I pneumocytes (gas exchange: flat, thin cells comprising 95% of alveolar surface) and Type II pneumocytes (produce surfactant: reduces surface tension, prevents alveolar collapse).
• Respiratory Membrane: The barrier between alveolar air and pulmonary capillary blood. Consists of alveolar epithelium, basement membranes, and capillary endothelium. Total thickness ~0.5 micrometers: thin enough for efficient diffusion.

Physiology of Gas Exchange:
• Ventilation: Movement of air in and out of the lungs. Driven by pressure gradients created by diaphragm and intercostal muscle contraction (inspiration) and passive elastic recoil (expiration).
• Diffusion: Movement of O2 and CO2 across the respiratory membrane down their concentration gradients. O2 moves from alveoli (PaO2 ~100 mmHg) to pulmonary capillaries (PvO2 ~40 mmHg). CO2 moves from capillaries (PvCO2 ~46 mmHg) to alveoli (PACO2 ~40 mmHg).
• Perfusion: Blood flow through the pulmonary capillaries. Must match ventilation for efficient gas exchange (V/Q matching).
• Oxygen Transport: 97% bound to hemoglobin (oxyhemoglobin), 3% dissolved in plasma (measured as PaO2). Oxygen-Hemoglobin Dissociation Curve describes the relationship between PaO2 and hemoglobin saturation.

Normal Values:
• Respiratory Rate: 12-20 breaths/min (adult)
• SpO2 (Pulse Oximetry): 95-100% (normal), 90-94% (hypoxemia: supplemental O2 needed), <90% (severe hypoxemia: emergent intervention)
• PaO2: 80-100 mmHg (arterial blood gas)
• PaCO2: 35-45 mmHg
• pH: 7.35-7.45

Common Respiratory Conditions:
• Pneumonia: Infection of lung parenchyma causing alveolar inflammation and consolidation (fluid-filled alveoli). Presents with fever, productive cough, dyspnea, crackles on auscultation. Consolidation impairs gas exchange → hypoxemia.
• Asthma: Chronic inflammatory airway disease characterized by reversible bronchoconstriction, airway edema, and excessive mucus production. Triggers include allergens, cold air, exercise, infections. Wheezing, dyspnea, chest tightness, cough (especially nocturnal). Treated with bronchodilators (rescue) and inhaled corticosteroids (controller).
• COPD: Chronic, progressive, irreversible airflow obstruction. Includes emphysema (alveolar destruction, air trapping, barrel chest) and chronic bronchitis (chronic productive cough, excessive mucus). Most commonly caused by smoking. Patients are often chronically hypoxemic with compensated respiratory acidosis. Oxygen must be used carefully: high-flow O2 may suppress hypoxic drive.

left: Respiratory rate 12-20 breaths/min, regular pattern
SpO2 95-100% on room air
Clear and equal bilateral breath sounds
Symmetrical chest expansion
No use of accessory muscles
No cough or sputum production
Unlabored breathing, speaks in full sentences
right: Tachypnea >24 and SpO2 <90%: administer supplemental oxygen, assess for cause
Wheezing with accessory muscle use: bronchospasm, administer bronchodilator
Crackles with fever and productive cough: pneumonia, obtain chest X-ray and sputum culture
Stridor (high-pitched inspiratory sound): upper airway obstruction, EMERGENCY
Barrel chest with prolonged expiratory phase: COPD, use low-flow oxygen (1-2 L/min)
Absent breath sounds unilaterally: pneumothorax or large pleural effusion, emergent evaluation
Cyanosis (central): severe hypoxemia, immediate intervention needed

medications.name: Albuterol (Ventolin)
medications.action: Selectively stimulates beta-2 adrenergic receptors in bronchial smooth muscle, causing rapid bronchodilation by relaxing airway smooth muscle and inhibiting mast cell mediator release. Onset of action 5-15 minutes via inhaler, duration 4-6 hours. Used as rescue medication for acute bronchospasm

medications.name: Fluticasone (Flovent)
medications.action: Reduces airway inflammation by suppressing inflammatory gene transcription, decreasing production of cytokines, chemokines, and inflammatory mediators. Reduces airway edema, mucus production, and bronchial hyperresponsiveness. Does NOT provide acute bronchodilation: this is a CONTROLLER medication for long-term management

medications.name: Amoxicillin
medications.action: Inhibits bacterial cell wall synthesis by binding to penicillin-binding proteins (PBPs), disrupting peptidoglycan cross-linking. Bactericidal against common community-acquired pneumonia pathogens including Streptococcus pneumoniae, Haemophilus influenzae, and Moraxella catarrhalis. First-line outpatient treatment for community-acquired pneumonia in otherwise healthy adults

pearls: The right mainstem bronchus is wider, shorter, and more vertical than the left. This means aspirated objects and misplaced endotracheal tubes preferentially go RIGHT. After intubation, listen to BOTH lungs: absent left-sided breath sounds suggests right mainstem intubation.

SpO2 and PaO2 are related but not the same. SpO2 90% ≈ PaO2 60 mmHg (the 'cliff' on the oxyhemoglobin dissociation curve). Above this point, small changes in PaO2 cause minimal SpO2 changes. Below this point, small decreases in PaO2 cause DRAMATIC drops in SpO2.

In COPD patients, be cautious with high-flow oxygen. Some COPD patients rely on hypoxic drive for respiratory stimulation (their chemoreceptors have adapted to chronic hypercapnia and no longer respond to CO2). High-flow O2 may remove their drive to breathe. Target SpO2 88-92% (not 95-100%).

Asthma = REVERSIBLE airway obstruction. COPD = IRREVERSIBLE airway obstruction. This distinction is fundamental. Asthma patients should have normal PFTs between exacerbations. COPD patients have persistent airflow limitation.

Early signs of respiratory distress: Tachypnea, accessory muscle use, nasal flaring, tripod positioning, speaking in short sentences. LATE signs: Bradypnea (ominous: respiratory fatigue), cyanosis, altered mental status, silent chest (no air movement: impending respiratory arrest).
diagnostics: Respiratory rate and pattern assessment
Oxygen saturation via pulse oximetry
Breath sounds auscultation (bilateral comparison)
Cough assessment (productive/nonproductive, sputum characteristics)
Skin color assessment (cyanosis)
management: Administer oxygen as ordered and monitor SpO2
Position patient for optimal breathing (high Fowler's or tripod)
Encourage deep breathing and coughing exercises
Assist with incentive spirometry as ordered
Report respiratory distress immediately
nursingActions: Assess respiratory status every shift (rate, depth, effort, SpO2)
Auscultate breath sounds and compare bilaterally
Administer oxygen as ordered and monitor response
Position for optimal ventilation (HOB elevated)
Report changes in respiratory status to RN/physician
Encourage use of incentive spirometer and deep breathing exercises

respiratory-rn.title: Advanced Respiratory Nursing (RN)

cellular.title: Ventilator Management, ARDS, and Respiratory Failure
cellular.content:
Advanced respiratory nursing requires understanding of mechanical ventilation, acute respiratory distress syndrome, chest tube management, and the pathophysiology of respiratory failure.

Respiratory Failure Classification:
• Type I (Hypoxemic): PaO2 <60 mmHg on room air with normal or low PaCO2. Caused by V/Q mismatch, shunt, or diffusion impairment. Examples: Pneumonia, ARDS, PE, pulmonary edema.
• Type II (Hypercapnic): PaCO2 >50 mmHg with respiratory acidosis (pH <7.35). Caused by inadequate alveolar ventilation. Examples: COPD exacerbation, neuromuscular disease (GBS, myasthenia gravis), drug overdose (opioid-induced respiratory depression), severe asthma.
• Type III (Perioperative): Atelectasis-related respiratory failure in surgical patients.
• Type IV (Shock): Respiratory failure from hemodynamic instability causing respiratory muscle fatigue.

Mechanical Ventilation:
Indications: Apnea, respiratory failure unresponsive to non-invasive support, GCS ≤8 (airway protection), impending respiratory arrest.

Ventilator Modes:
• Volume-Controlled (VC/AC): Delivers a set tidal volume with each breath. Pressure varies based on compliance. Risk: Barotrauma if compliance decreases (pressure increases to deliver set volume).
• Pressure-Controlled (PC): Delivers breaths to a set pressure. Volume varies based on compliance. Advantage: Limits peak airway pressure. Disadvantage: Tidal volume may decrease if compliance worsens.
• Pressure Support (PSV): Patient-triggered mode that augments spontaneous breaths with set pressure. Used for weaning. Patient controls rate, inspiratory time, and tidal volume.
• SIMV (Synchronized Intermittent Mandatory Ventilation): Delivers set number of mandatory breaths synchronized with patient effort; patient can breathe spontaneously between mandatory breaths. Used for weaning.

Key Ventilator Settings:
• FiO2: Fraction of inspired oxygen (21-100%). Start at 100% and titrate down. Goal: Lowest FiO2 to maintain PaO2 >60 or SpO2 >92%. FiO2 >60% for prolonged periods → oxygen toxicity (alveolar damage, absorption atelectasis).
• Tidal Volume (VT): Volume per breath. Standard: 6-8 mL/kg ideal body weight. ARDS: 4-6 mL/kg IBW (lung-protective ventilation).
• PEEP (Positive End-Expiratory Pressure): Maintains positive pressure at end-expiration to prevent alveolar collapse and improve oxygenation. Normal: 5 cmH2O. ARDS: May need 10-20+ cmH2O. Higher PEEP recruits collapsed alveoli, improving gas exchange.
• Respiratory Rate: Typically 12-20/min. Adjusted to achieve target PaCO2.
• Peak Inspiratory Pressure: Should be <35-40 cmH2O to minimize barotrauma risk.
• Plateau Pressure: Reflects alveolar pressure during an inspiratory hold. Should be <30 cmH2O (especially in ARDS: higher pressures cause ventilator-induced lung injury).

ARDS (Acute Respiratory Distress Syndrome):
Berlin Criteria for Diagnosis:
1. Timing: Within 1 week of known clinical insult or new/worsening respiratory symptoms
2. Imaging: Bilateral opacities on CXR not fully explained by effusions, atelectasis, or nodules
3. Origin: Respiratory failure not fully explained by cardiac failure or fluid overload
4. Oxygenation (PaO2/FiO2 ratio on PEEP ≥5):
   - Mild: 200-300
   - Moderate: 100-200
   - Severe: <100

ARDS Pathophysiology:
1. Triggering event (sepsis, pneumonia, aspiration, trauma, pancreatitis, transfusion)
2. Diffuse alveolar damage: Inflammatory mediators damage the alveolar-capillary membrane
3. Exudative phase: Protein-rich fluid floods alveoli, inactivating surfactant → alveolar collapse, refractory hypoxemia
4. Proliferative phase: Fibroblast proliferation, organization of exudate
5. Fibrotic phase: Pulmonary fibrosis (survivors may have permanent lung impairment)

ARDS Management:
• Lung-protective ventilation: Low VT (4-6 mL/kg IBW), Plateau pressure <30 cmH2O, Permissive hypercapnia (allow PaCO2 to rise rather than increase VT/pressures)
• Higher PEEP strategy to recruit collapsed alveoli
• Prone positioning: Improves V/Q matching by redistributing perfusion to previously collapsed dorsal lung regions. Indicated for PaO2/FiO2 <150. Requires ≥16 hours/day prone. PROSEVA trial showed 28-day mortality reduction from 32.8% to 16%.
• Conservative fluid management: Minimize pulmonary edema: use diuretics judiciously
• Neuromuscular blockade: May be considered in early severe ARDS to improve ventilator synchrony and reduce oxygen consumption

Chest Tubes:
• Indications: Pneumothorax, hemothorax, pleural effusion, empyema, post-thoracic surgery.
• Water-Seal Drainage System: Three-chamber system: Collection chamber (measures drainage), Water-seal chamber (one-way valve preventing air from entering pleural space; continuous bubbling = air leak; tidaling = normal), Suction control chamber (-20 cmH2O continuous gentle bubbling).
• Nursing management: Assess drainage (type, amount, color every 1-2 hours), maintain water seal at all times, keep system below chest level, never clamp unless ordered, assess for subcutaneous emphysema, monitor respiratory status, tape all connections, keep clamps and occlusive dressing at bedside for emergencies.

left: Patient breathing comfortably on ventilator, synchronous with set parameters
ABG showing adequate oxygenation (PaO2 >60) and ventilation (PaCO2 35-45)
Plateau pressure <30 cmH2O on protective ventilation
Chest tube: Tidaling in water-seal chamber, no continuous air leak, appropriate drainage
right: High peak pressures with coughing/biting: consider sedation, assess for obstruction, pneumothorax
PaO2/FiO2 <200 with bilateral infiltrates: ARDS, initiate lung-protective ventilation and consider prone positioning
Sudden loss of tidal volume with high-pressure alarm: disconnect/kink, pneumothorax, mucus plug, or ETT displacement
Continuous bubbling in water-seal chamber: persistent air leak, assess chest tube connections and notify provider
Subcutaneous emphysema tracking from chest tube site: possible chest tube malposition or large bronchopleural fistula
Ventilator auto-PEEP (breath stacking): increase expiratory time, reduce rate, consider bronchodilator
Massive bloody drainage >200 mL/hr from chest tube: hemothorax, prepare for autotransfusion or surgery

medications.name: Propofol
medications.action: Enhances GABA-A receptor activity, providing sedation, anxiolysis, and amnesia for mechanically ventilated patients. Rapid onset (30-60 seconds) and short duration (5-10 minutes after discontinuation): ideal for daily sedation vacations and neurologic assessments. No analgesic properties: must combine with opioid for pain

medications.name: Cisatracurium
medications.action: Competitively blocks acetylcholine at the nicotinic neuromuscular junction, preventing muscle contraction. Used in severe ARDS to improve chest wall compliance, reduce oxygen consumption, prevent ventilator dyssynchrony, and facilitate lung-protective ventilation. Undergoes Hofmann degradation (organ-independent metabolism): preferred in renal/hepatic impairment

pearls: Lung-protective ventilation saves lives in ARDS: Low tidal volume (4-6 mL/kg IBW), plateau pressure <30 cmH2O, permissive hypercapnia (tolerate PaCO2 up to 55-60 if pH >7.25). The ARDSNet trial showed 22% mortality reduction with low-VT strategy.

Prone positioning in ARDS improves oxygenation by redistributing perfusion to ventral (now non-dependent) regions that remain better aerated, improving V/Q matching. Proning must be done for ≥16 hours/day to show mortality benefit. Complications: facial edema, pressure injuries, accidental extubation, line displacement: requires dedicated prone team.

Chest tube troubleshooting: Continuous bubbling in water-seal = air leak. Check connections first (clamp tubing close to patient: if bubbling stops, leak is at patient's chest; if bubbling continues, leak is in the system). Sudden cessation of tidaling = tube obstruction (kinked, clotted) or lung has fully re-expanded.

Ventilator alarms: HIGH PRESSURE = obstruction (secretions, kinking, biting ETT, bronchospasm, pneumothorax, fighting ventilator). LOW PRESSURE = disconnection or leak (check circuit, cuff leak, chest tube bronchopleural fistula). Always go to the PATIENT first, not the ventilator.

The most dangerous time on a ventilator is extubation. Criteria for extubation readiness: Able to protect airway (gag/cough), adequate mentation (follows commands), FiO2 ≤40%, PEEP ≤5, spontaneous breathing trial passed (RSBI <105: ratio of RR to VT in liters), hemodynamically stable, no active sepsis or pending procedures. Keep reintubation equipment at bedside for 24-48 hours post-extubation.
diagnostics: ABG analysis and interpretation
Chest X-ray correlation with clinical findings
Continuous SpO2 and EtCO2 monitoring
Peak flow measurements for asthma
Sputum culture and sensitivity results
CT pulmonary angiography for PE evaluation
management: Titrate oxygen therapy to maintain target SpO2 (88-92% for COPD, >94% for most others)
Initiate and manage non-invasive ventilation (BiPAP/CPAP) per protocol
Administer nebulized bronchodilators and corticosteroids per protocol
Prepare for intubation if non-invasive measures fail
Manage chest tube systems and troubleshoot complications
nursingActions: Interpret ABG results and correlate with clinical presentation
Manage oxygen delivery systems (NC, face mask, Venturi, NRB, HFNC)
Assess and manage patients on non-invasive ventilation
Monitor chest tube function (drainage, air leak, tidaling)
Administer respiratory medications and assess response
Prepare for rapid sequence intubation when respiratory failure is imminent

respiratory-np.title: NP Respiratory: Advanced Management

cellular.title: PFT Interpretation and Advanced ARDS Management
cellular.content:
The Nurse Practitioner must master pulmonary function test interpretation, advanced ventilator management, and evidence-based ARDS protocols.

Pulmonary Function Tests (PFTs):
• Spirometry: Measures airflow and lung volumes during forced breathing maneuvers.
  - FVC (Forced Vital Capacity): Total volume exhaled during a forced expiration. Reduced in restrictive disease.
  - FEV1 (Forced Expiratory Volume in 1 second): Volume exhaled in the first second. Reduced in both obstructive and restrictive disease.
  - FEV1/FVC Ratio: KEY diagnostic ratio. Normal ≥70% (or ≥LLN). <70% = Obstructive pattern (air trapping prevents rapid exhalation). Normal or elevated = Restrictive pattern (lungs can't fill, but what fills empties quickly).
  - Bronchodilator Response: ≥12% AND ≥200 mL improvement in FEV1 post-bronchodilator = significant reversibility (supports asthma diagnosis). Minimal/no reversibility supports COPD.

• Lung Volumes (measured by body plethysmography or gas dilution):
  - TLC (Total Lung Capacity): Increased in obstructive disease (hyperinflation/air trapping). Decreased in restrictive disease.
  - RV (Residual Volume): Air remaining after maximal expiration. Increased in obstructive disease (air trapping). Decreased in restrictive.
  - FRC (Functional Residual Capacity): Volume at end-expiration during tidal breathing. Increased in emphysema.

• DLCO (Diffusion Capacity for Carbon Monoxide): Measures gas transfer across the alveolar-capillary membrane.
  - Decreased: Emphysema (alveolar destruction reduces surface area), pulmonary fibrosis (thickened membrane impairs diffusion), pulmonary vascular disease, anemia.
  - Increased: Polycythemia, pulmonary hemorrhage (hemoglobin in alveoli absorbs CO), asthma (during acute bronchospasm: air trapping increases contact time), left-to-right shunts (increased pulmonary blood flow).
  - Normal: Asthma (between attacks), chronic bronchitis (airway disease, not parenchymal), chest wall disease.

PFT Pattern Recognition:
• Obstructive: FEV1/FVC <70%, ↓FEV1, ↑TLC, ↑RV. Diseases: COPD, asthma, bronchiectasis, cystic fibrosis.
• Restrictive: FEV1/FVC normal or ↑, ↓FVC, ↓TLC. Diseases: Pulmonary fibrosis, sarcoidosis, obesity, neuromuscular disease, chest wall deformity, pleural disease.
• Mixed: Both obstructive and restrictive patterns. FEV1/FVC <70% AND ↓TLC.

GOLD Classification for COPD Severity (by FEV1 % predicted, post-bronchodilator):
• GOLD 1 (Mild): FEV1 ≥80%
• GOLD 2 (Moderate): FEV1 50-79%
• GOLD 3 (Severe): FEV1 30-49%
• GOLD 4 (Very Severe): FEV1 <30%

Advanced Ventilator Management:
• Driving Pressure: Plateau pressure minus PEEP. Should be <15 cmH2O. Emerging evidence suggests driving pressure is the strongest predictor of mortality in ARDS (Amato et al., NEJM 2015). Optimizing driving pressure may be more important than targeting specific VT or plateau pressure.
• Transpulmonary Pressure Monitoring: Using esophageal balloon to estimate pleural pressure and calculate true transpulmonary pressure. Allows individualized PEEP titration based on actual lung mechanics rather than empiric tables.
• Recruitment Maneuvers: Brief application of sustained high pressure (e.g., 40 cmH2O for 40 seconds) to open collapsed alveoli. Controversial: ART trial showed increased mortality with aggressive recruitment maneuvers. Use with caution.
• Airway Pressure Release Ventilation (APRV): Time-cycled pressure mode that spends most time at high pressure (P-high) with brief releases to low pressure (P-low) for CO2 elimination. May improve oxygenation in ARDS. Allows spontaneous breathing throughout. Controversial and center-dependent.
• ECMO for Severe ARDS: VV-ECMO (veno-venous) provides gas exchange support when conventional ventilation fails. EOLIA trial: Trend toward mortality benefit but did not reach significance (35% vs 46%). Consider when PaO2/FiO2 <80 despite optimized ventilator management and prone positioning, or uncompensated respiratory acidosis with pH <7.20.

riskFactors: Chronic obstructive pulmonary disease with frequent exacerbations

left: PFTs showing normal spirometry: FEV1/FVC >70%, FEV1 >80% predicted
Normal DLCO (>80% predicted)
Adequate oxygenation on current support with improving PaO2/FiO2 ratio
Driving pressure <15 cmH2O on lung-protective ventilation
right: FEV1/FVC <70% with minimal bronchodilator response: COPD confirmed, classify by GOLD stage
FEV1/FVC normal but TLC <80% predicted: restrictive pattern, evaluate for ILD, neuromuscular disease
DLCO <60% with restrictive PFTs: suggests pulmonary fibrosis, obtain HRCT
PaO2/FiO2 <80 despite optimized ventilation and proning: evaluate for VV-ECMO candidacy
Driving pressure >15 cmH2O: adjust PEEP or reduce VT to optimize
Plateau pressure >30 cmH2O: immediate reduction in VT or pressure settings to prevent VILI

medications.name: Inhaled Nitric Oxide (iNO)
medications.action: Endogenously produced vasodilator that, when inhaled, selectively dilates pulmonary vasculature adjacent to ventilated alveoli, improving V/Q matching. Reduces pulmonary artery pressure and right ventricular afterload. Rapidly inactivated by hemoglobin when it enters the bloodstream: so NO systemic vasodilation occurs (unlike IV vasodilators)

medications.name: Surfactant (Beractant/Calfactant)
medications.action: Replaces deficient or dysfunctional surfactant in the alveoli. Surfactant reduces alveolar surface tension at the air-liquid interface, preventing alveolar collapse during expiration (atelectasis). Composed primarily of phosphatidylcholine (DPPC) and surfactant proteins (SP-A, SP-B, SP-C, SP-D). SP-B is essential: its deficiency is lethal

pearls: PFT interpretation algorithm: 1) Look at FEV1/FVC ratio first. <70% = Obstructive. Normal = check TLC. TLC low = Restrictive. TLC normal = normal spirometry. 2) If obstructive, check bronchodilator response: Reversible = Asthma. Irreversible = COPD. 3) Check DLCO: Low DLCO + obstruction = Emphysema. Normal DLCO + obstruction = Chronic bronchitis or Asthma.

Driving pressure (Plateau - PEEP) is emerging as the most important predictor of outcomes in ARDS. Aim for <15 cmH2O. When adjusting ventilator, consider whether changes improve or worsen driving pressure rather than focusing on individual VT or PEEP targets.

The 'open lung' approach in ARDS: Use enough PEEP to keep recruitable alveoli open (prevent cyclical opening/closing that causes shear injury), but not so much that overdistension occurs. Balance is key: there is no one-size-fits-all PEEP. Consider transpulmonary pressure monitoring for individualized PEEP titration.

ARDS management is primarily SUPPORTIVE: there is no pharmacologic therapy proven to reduce ARDS mortality. What works: lung-protective ventilation, prone positioning, conservative fluid management, treating the underlying cause. What doesn't work (despite theoretical rationale): surfactant, inhaled NO, steroids (controversial: may help in some subgroups), beta-agonists, statins.

VV-ECMO indications for ARDS: PaO2/FiO2 <80 for >6 hours despite optimization, OR pH <7.25 with PaCO2 >60 for >6 hours. Contraindications: Irreversible underlying condition, severe comorbidities, prolonged mechanical ventilation (>7-10 days: lung recovery less likely). ECMO is a BRIDGE: to recovery, to transplant, or to decision-making. It is not a destination therapy.
diagnostics: Order and interpret ABGs with acid-base analysis
Order pulmonary function tests (FEV1, FVC, FEV1/FVC ratio, DLCO)
Order CT chest for parenchymal evaluation
Order D-dimer and CTPA for PE evaluation
Order sputum cultures with antibiotic sensitivities
Order bronchoscopy for diagnostic evaluation when indicated
Order sleep study (polysomnography) for suspected OSA
management: Prescribe stepwise asthma therapy per GINA guidelines
Prescribe COPD medications per GOLD guidelines (LABA + LAMA ± ICS)
Prescribe antibiotics for pneumonia based on severity scoring (CURB-65, PSI)
Order ventilator settings and adjustments for mechanically ventilated patients
Prescribe systemic corticosteroids for acute exacerbations
Order pulmonary rehabilitation referral for chronic lung disease
nursingActions: Perform comprehensive respiratory examination (inspection, palpation, percussion, auscultation)
Interpret PFTs and classify severity of obstructive vs restrictive disease
Interpret ABGs using systematic approach (pH, PaCO2, HCO3, compensation, oxygenation)
Evaluate medication regimen efficacy and adjust as needed
Assess inhaler technique and provide education
Coordinate multidisciplinary care (respiratory therapy, pulmonology, palliative care)

asthma-copd.title: Asthma and COPD

cellular.title: Obstructive Airway Disease Pathophysiology
cellular.content:
Asthma and COPD are the two most common obstructive airway diseases. While both cause airflow limitation, their pathophysiology, clinical course, and management differ significantly.

Asthma Pathophysiology:
Asthma is a chronic inflammatory disorder of the airways characterized by three key processes:
1. Bronchoconstriction: Airway smooth muscle contraction triggered by allergens, irritants, cold air, exercise, or infections. Mediated by acetylcholine (parasympathetic), histamine (mast cells), and leukotrienes (inflammatory cells).
2. Airway Inflammation: Chronic eosinophilic inflammation with CD4+ T-helper lymphocytes, mast cells, and eosinophils. Release of inflammatory mediators causes mucosal edema, increased vascular permeability, and mucus hypersecretion.
3. Airway Remodeling: Chronic inflammation leads to structural changes: subepithelial fibrosis, smooth muscle hypertrophy, mucous gland hyperplasia, and neovascularization. These changes may lead to partially irreversible airflow limitation over time.

Asthma Classification (Stepwise):
• Intermittent: Symptoms ≤2 days/week, nighttime symptoms ≤2x/month, FEV1 >80%, no interference with activity. Treatment: SABA PRN only.
• Mild Persistent: Symptoms >2 days/week, nighttime 3-4x/month, FEV1 >80%, minor limitation. Step 2: Low-dose ICS.
• Moderate Persistent: Daily symptoms, nighttime >1x/week, FEV1 60-80%, some limitation. Step 3-4: Medium-dose ICS + LABA.
• Severe Persistent: Symptoms throughout day, nighttime nightly, FEV1 <60%, extreme limitation. Step 5-6: High-dose ICS + LABA ± oral steroids, biologics.

COPD Pathophysiology:
COPD encompasses two overlapping conditions:
1. Chronic Bronchitis ('Blue Bloater'): Chronic inflammation of bronchial mucosa → excess mucus production → chronic productive cough (≥3 months/year for ≥2 consecutive years). Airway narrowing from inflammation and mucus. Hypoxemia develops early; patients often appear cyanotic and edematous.
2. Emphysema ('Pink Puffer'): Destruction of alveolar walls by proteases (especially neutrophil elastase) exceeding antiprotease (alpha-1 antitrypsin) defenses. Results in loss of elastic recoil, air trapping, hyperinflation, and decreased gas exchange surface area. Patients work hard to breathe (pursed lip breathing), maintaining oxygenation until late: appear thin and pink but dyspneic.

Alpha-1 Antitrypsin Deficiency: Genetic cause of emphysema. Uninhibited neutrophil elastase destroys alveolar walls. Suspect in young patients (<45) with emphysema, especially non-smokers, or with basilar-predominant emphysema.

Trigger Management:
• Asthma triggers: Allergens (dust mites, pet dander, mold, pollen), cold air, exercise, respiratory infections, GERD, medications (beta-blockers, aspirin/NSAIDs: aspirin-exacerbated respiratory disease), emotional stress, strong odors.
• COPD exacerbation triggers: Respiratory infections (most common: viral 50-70%, bacterial 30-50%), air pollution, cold weather, medication non-compliance, heart failure exacerbation.

Exacerbation Management:
• Asthma Exacerbation: SABA (albuterol) every 20 min x3, systemic corticosteroids (prednisone 40-60 mg/day x5-7 days or methylprednisolone IV), ipratropium bromide nebulizer, oxygen to maintain SpO2 >92%. Severe/life-threatening: Add IV magnesium sulfate (2g over 20 min: bronchodilator via calcium channel antagonism in smooth muscle), consider epinephrine SC/IM, BiPAP, intubation if impending respiratory arrest.
• COPD Exacerbation: Short-acting bronchodilators (albuterol + ipratropium), systemic corticosteroids (prednisone 40 mg x5 days: REDUCE trial showed 5 days equivalent to 14), antibiotics if increased sputum purulence (azithromycin, amoxicillin-clavulanate, or doxycycline), controlled oxygen (target SpO2 88-92%), BiPAP for hypercapnic respiratory failure.

left: Well-controlled asthma: Symptoms ≤2 days/week, nighttime ≤2x/month, SABA use ≤2 days/week, no activity limitation
Stable COPD: On maintenance inhalers, SpO2 88-92% on home O2, stable exercise tolerance
Normal breath sounds between exacerbations (asthma)
Peak flow at personal best or >80% of predicted
right: Expiratory wheezing with prolonged expiratory phase: bronchospasm, administer bronchodilator immediately
Silent chest in known asthmatic: OMINOUS: No air movement, impending respiratory arrest, emergent intubation
Accessory muscle use, tripod positioning, speaking in single words: severe exacerbation
Peak flow <50% of personal best: severe exacerbation, needs systemic steroids and continuous nebulization
Paradoxical breathing (chest retracts during inspiration): respiratory muscle fatigue, prepare for intubation
Worsening hypercapnia on ABG despite treatment: impending respiratory failure, BiPAP or intubation

medications.name: Ipratropium Bromide (Atrovent)
medications.action: Blocks acetylcholine at muscarinic M3 receptors in bronchial smooth muscle, preventing parasympathetically-mediated bronchoconstriction. Onset 15-30 minutes, peak 1-2 hours, duration 4-6 hours. Also reduces mucus secretion. Does NOT cross blood-brain barrier (quaternary ammonium compound)

medications.name: Prednisone
medications.action: Potent anti-inflammatory that suppresses inflammatory gene expression, reduces inflammatory cell recruitment and activation, decreases mucus production, and restores beta-receptor sensitivity (upregulates beta-2 receptors that become downregulated during severe inflammation). Onset of action 4-6 hours (even IV steroids take hours to work: not an acute bronchodilator)

medications.name: Tiotropium (Spiriva)
medications.action: Selectively and persistently blocks M3 muscarinic receptors in bronchial smooth muscle, providing sustained bronchodilation for 24 hours (once-daily dosing). Dissociates very slowly from M3 receptors (half-life at receptor ~35 hours). First-line maintenance therapy for COPD. Also approved for asthma as add-on therapy in Step 4-5

pearls: Asthma = REVERSIBLE obstruction. COPD = IRREVERSIBLE obstruction. However, they can coexist (Asthma-COPD Overlap: ACO). ACO features: significant bronchodilator reversibility + persistent airflow limitation + history of asthma and/or atopy.

A 'silent chest' in a severe asthma attack is an OMINOUS sign: it means there is so little air movement that wheezing cannot be generated. This is NOT improvement. It indicates impending respiratory arrest and requires immediate intervention (epinephrine, intubation preparation).

Step-up therapy in asthma: If symptoms are not controlled at the current step for >2-4 weeks, step up. If well-controlled for ≥3 months, consider stepping down. Always assess inhaler technique and adherence before stepping up: poor technique and non-adherence are the most common causes of 'uncontrolled' asthma.

COPD is the only chronic disease where the primary diagnostic test (spirometry) is also the staging tool. GOLD stage is based on post-bronchodilator FEV1 % predicted. But symptoms (CAT score or mMRC dyspnea scale) and exacerbation history determine the treatment GROUP (A, B, E in GOLD 2023).

Never give beta-blockers to asthma patients (even beta-1 selective: selectivity is dose-dependent). Beta-blockers block beta-2 receptors in bronchial smooth muscle, causing bronchospasm. In contrast, beta-blockers are generally safe and beneficial in COPD patients (cardioselective beta-1 blockers like metoprolol/bisoprolol).
diagnostics: Peak flow meter readings
Oxygen saturation monitoring
Breath sounds assessment (wheezing, diminished sounds)
Respiratory rate and effort assessment
Sputum assessment (color, amount, consistency)
management: Administer bronchodilators as ordered (SABA first for acute exacerbation)
Administer oxygen as ordered (COPD: 1-2 L/min, target SpO2 88-92%)
Position in high Fowler's or tripod position for breathing ease
Encourage smoking cessation
Administer corticosteroids as ordered for exacerbations
nursingActions: Assess respiratory status and breath sounds frequently
Administer medications via MDI or nebulizer as ordered
Monitor oxygen therapy (COPD patients: low-flow to avoid suppressing hypoxic drive)
Educate on inhaler technique and medication sequence (bronchodilator before corticosteroid)
Teach pursed-lip breathing for COPD patients
Report worsening respiratory distress immediately

pneumonia-basics.title: Pneumonia: Classification and Management

cellular.title: Pneumonia Pathophysiology and Classification
cellular.content:
Pneumonia is an acute infection of the lung parenchyma that causes inflammation and consolidation of the alveoli. When pathogens overwhelm the pulmonary defense mechanisms (mucociliary clearance, alveolar macrophages, secretory IgA), they colonize the lower respiratory tract and trigger an inflammatory response.

Pathophysiology:
1. Pathogens reach the alveoli via aspiration of oropharyngeal flora (most common), inhalation of aerosolized organisms, or hematogenous spread.
2. Alveolar macrophages recognize and attempt to phagocytize the pathogen.
3. If the pathogen burden overwhelms local defenses, inflammatory mediators (TNF-α, IL-1, IL-8) are released, recruiting neutrophils.
4. Neutrophil influx, fibrin deposition, and edema fluid fill the alveolar space: CONSOLIDATION.
5. Consolidated alveoli cannot participate in gas exchange → V/Q mismatch → Hypoxemia.
6. Inflammatory exudate is visible on CXR as infiltrate/opacity.

Classification:
• Community-Acquired Pneumonia (CAP): Develops outside of hospital or within 48 hours of admission. Most common pathogens: Streptococcus pneumoniae (#1 overall), Haemophilus influenzae, Mycoplasma pneumoniae ('walking pneumonia': atypical, common in young adults), Chlamydophila pneumoniae, respiratory viruses (influenza, RSV, SARS-CoV-2).
• Hospital-Acquired Pneumonia (HAP): Develops ≥48 hours after hospital admission, not incubating at time of admission. Pathogens: MRSA, Pseudomonas aeruginosa, other gram-negative rods (Klebsiella, Acinetobacter), Enterobacteriaceae. Broader antibiotic coverage required.
• Ventilator-Associated Pneumonia (VAP): Develops ≥48 hours after endotracheal intubation. Most serious form. Pathogens similar to HAP plus additional MDR organisms. Prevention: VAP bundle (HOB ≥30-45°, daily sedation vacation, DVT prophylaxis, PUD prophylaxis, daily assessment of extubation readiness, oral care with chlorhexidine).
• Aspiration Pneumonia: Results from aspiration of oropharyngeal or gastric contents. Risk factors: Altered consciousness, dysphagia, GERD, NG tube, impaired gag reflex. Often involves anaerobic organisms. Typically affects dependent lung segments (posterior segments of upper lobes, superior segments of lower lobes when supine; basal segments when upright).

CURB-65 Severity Score (Determines disposition for CAP):
• C: Confusion (new-onset mental confusion)
• U: Uremia (BUN >20 mg/dL or >7 mmol/L)
• R: Respiratory rate ≥30/min
• B: Blood pressure (SBP <90 or DBP ≤60)
• 65: Age ≥65 years
Score: 0-1 = Outpatient treatment. 2 = Consider short hospitalization or closely supervised outpatient. 3-5 = Hospitalize; score 4-5 consider ICU.

Diagnostic Workup:
• CXR: Lobar consolidation (typical bacterial), bilateral interstitial infiltrates (atypical or viral), cavitation (TB, Staphylococcus, Klebsiella, anaerobes).
• Blood cultures: x2 sets before antibiotics (positive in ~10-20% of hospitalized CAP).
• Sputum culture and Gram stain: Before antibiotics if possible. Deep cough specimen: sample must have >25 WBCs and <10 epithelial cells per LPF to be adequate (not just saliva).
• Procalcitonin: Bacterial biomarker: helps distinguish bacterial (elevated) from viral (low) pneumonia. May guide antibiotic initiation and duration.
• Legionella and pneumococcal urinary antigens: In severe CAP.
• CBC, BMP, lactate, ABG if indicated.

riskFactors: Aspiration risk (dysphagia, decreased LOC, NG tube)

left: Clear bilateral breath sounds
Afebrile with normal WBC count
Productive cough clearing with deep breathing and coughing exercises
Improving oxygenation with decreasing O2 requirements
Adequate oral intake and hydration
right: Fever >38.3°C with productive purulent cough, crackles, and new infiltrate on CXR: pneumonia, initiate antibiotics
Severe sepsis from pneumonia: Tachycardia, hypotension, altered mental status, lactate >2: activate sepsis protocol
Pleural effusion (dullness to percussion, decreased breath sounds at base): may need thoracentesis to rule out empyema
CURB-65 ≥3: ICU admission criteria met, aggressive management needed
No improvement after 48-72 hours of appropriate antibiotics: consider resistant organism, empyema, or alternative diagnosis
Elderly/immunocompromised with vague symptoms (confusion, falls, decreased appetite) without classic pneumonia symptoms: atypical presentation, CXR needed

medications.name: Ceftriaxone
medications.action: Inhibits bacterial cell wall synthesis by binding to PBPs and disrupting peptidoglycan cross-linking. Broad-spectrum coverage of gram-positive (S. pneumoniae) and gram-negative organisms (H. influenzae, Klebsiella, E. coli). Long half-life allows once-daily dosing (24 hours). Does NOT cover atypical organisms: combine with azithromycin for CAP

medications.name: Azithromycin
medications.action: Binds to the 50S ribosomal subunit, inhibiting bacterial protein synthesis. Bacteriostatic (at standard doses) against a broad spectrum including atypical organisms (Mycoplasma, Chlamydophila, Legionella) and gram-positives. Excellent tissue penetration: concentrates in lung tissue at levels 10-100x serum levels. Also has anti-inflammatory properties beneficial in chronic lung disease

pearls: The #1 causative organism for CAP across all age groups is Streptococcus pneumoniae. Prevention: Pneumococcal vaccines (PCV20 for all adults ≥65 or younger with risk factors).

VAP prevention bundle (evidence-based, reduces VAP by 50%+): HOB elevation ≥30-45°, daily sedation vacation + readiness-to-extubate assessment, DVT prophylaxis, PUD prophylaxis, oral care with chlorhexidine (controversial in recent studies but still widely used). The most effective intervention is reducing the duration of intubation.

Elderly patients with pneumonia may NOT present with typical symptoms (fever, cough, pleuritic pain). Instead, they may present with CONFUSION as the primary symptom: acute mental status change in an elderly patient should prompt CXR and urinalysis.

Aspiration pneumonia is NOT the same as aspiration pneumonitis. Pneumonitis = chemical inflammation from gastric acid (sterile initially: antibiotics not needed unless secondary infection develops). Pneumonia = bacterial infection from aspiration of colonized oropharyngeal secretions (antibiotics needed).

De-escalation of antibiotics: Once culture and sensitivity results return (48-72 hours), narrow antibiotic coverage to the identified organism. This reduces antibiotic resistance development, C. difficile risk, cost, and side effects. Broad-spectrum → Narrow-spectrum based on cultures is a quality metric.
diagnostics: Respiratory assessment (breath sounds: crackles, rhonchi)
Temperature monitoring
Oxygen saturation monitoring
Sputum characteristics assessment (color, amount, odor)
Monitor for signs of sepsis
management: Administer antibiotics as ordered
Encourage deep breathing, coughing, and incentive spirometry
Maintain adequate hydration
Administer oxygen therapy as ordered
Position in semi-Fowler's to optimize breathing
nursingActions: Monitor respiratory status (breath sounds, SpO2, respiratory effort)
Administer antibiotics as ordered and on time
Encourage incentive spirometry use 10 times every hour while awake
Monitor temperature and report persistent fever
Maintain adequate hydration to thin secretions
Assess for complications (sepsis, respiratory failure, pleural effusion)

abg-basics.title: ABG Interpretation Basics

cellular.title: Acid-Base Physiology and ABG Analysis
cellular.content:
Arterial blood gas (ABG) analysis provides critical information about a patient's acid-base status, oxygenation, and ventilation. Understanding acid-base physiology at the cellular level is essential for accurate interpretation.

Normal ABG Values:
• pH: 7.35-7.45 (reflects the overall acid-base balance)
• PaCO2: 35-45 mmHg (respiratory component: regulated by the lungs)
• HCO3-: 22-26 mEq/L (metabolic component: regulated by the kidneys)
• PaO2: 80-100 mmHg (oxygenation: not part of acid-base analysis but critical)
• SaO2: 95-100% (oxygen saturation of arterial hemoglobin)
• Base Excess/Deficit: -2 to +2 mEq/L (reflects metabolic component)

Acid-Base Physiology:
• Acids are produced continuously by cellular metabolism: CO2 (volatile acid: exhaled by lungs, ~15,000 mmol/day) and non-volatile acids (lactic acid, ketoacids, phosphoric acid: excreted by kidneys, ~50-100 mEq/day).
• Buffer Systems: Bicarbonate buffer (HCO3-/H2CO3: most important extracellular buffer), phosphate buffer, protein buffer (hemoglobin: most important intracellular buffer).
• Henderson-Hasselbalch Equation: pH = 6.1 + log([HCO3-] / [0.03 × PaCO2]). The ratio of HCO3- to PaCO2 determines pH, not their absolute values.

Step-by-Step ABG Interpretation:

Step 1: Look at the pH
• pH <7.35 = Acidosis (too much acid / too little base)
• pH >7.45 = Alkalosis (too much base / too little acid)
• pH 7.35-7.45 = Normal (but may be compensated: check other values)

Step 2: Look at the PaCO2 (Respiratory Component)
• PaCO2 >45 = Respiratory acidosis (hypoventilation: retaining CO2)
• PaCO2 <35 = Respiratory alkalosis (hyperventilation: blowing off CO2)

Step 3: Look at the HCO3- (Metabolic Component)
• HCO3- <22 = Metabolic acidosis (loss of bicarbonate or accumulation of acid)
• HCO3- >26 = Metabolic alkalosis (gain of bicarbonate or loss of acid)

Step 4: Match: Which component explains the pH?
• If pH is acidotic and PaCO2 is elevated → Respiratory acidosis
• If pH is acidotic and HCO3- is low → Metabolic acidosis
• If pH is alkalotic and PaCO2 is low → Respiratory alkalosis
• If pH is alkalotic and HCO3- is high → Metabolic alkalosis

Step 5: Is there Compensation?
• The body attempts to normalize pH through the opposite system:
  - Respiratory disorders → Kidneys compensate (takes 24-48 hours for full renal compensation)
  - Metabolic disorders → Lungs compensate (begins within minutes: rapid but limited)
• Uncompensated: pH is abnormal, one value is abnormal, the other is normal
• Partially Compensated: pH is abnormal, BOTH PaCO2 and HCO3- are abnormal
• Fully Compensated: pH is NORMAL (7.35-7.45), but both PaCO2 and HCO3- are abnormal. Look at which side of 7.40 the pH falls to determine the primary disorder.

ROME Mnemonic:
R-espiratory = O-pposite: In respiratory disorders, PaCO2 and pH move in OPPOSITE directions (↑CO2 = ↓pH)
M-etabolic = E-qual: In metabolic disorders, HCO3- and pH move in the SAME direction (↑HCO3- = ↑pH)

Common Clinical Scenarios:
• Respiratory Acidosis: COPD exacerbation, opioid overdose, neuromuscular disease, severe asthma, pneumonia. Treatment: Improve ventilation.
• Respiratory Alkalosis: Anxiety/hyperventilation, pain, PE, early sepsis, salicylate toxicity, high-altitude. Treatment: Treat underlying cause.
• Metabolic Acidosis: DKA, lactic acidosis (shock, sepsis), renal failure, diarrhea, salicylate toxicity. Treatment: Treat underlying cause; bicarbonate if pH <7.1.
• Metabolic Alkalosis: Vomiting, NG suction (loss of HCl), diuretic use (contraction alkalosis), excessive antacid intake. Treatment: Fluid and electrolyte replacement.

left: ABG within normal limits: pH 7.35-7.45, PaCO2 35-45, HCO3 22-26, PaO2 80-100
Adequate oxygenation on current therapy
Base excess within normal range (-2 to +2)
right: pH <7.20: severe acidosis, risk of cardiac arrest, evaluate for bicarbonate administration
pH >7.55: severe alkalosis, risk of seizures and arrhythmias
PaCO2 >60 with pH <7.30: respiratory failure, prepare for mechanical ventilation
PaO2 <60 (or SpO2 <90%): severe hypoxemia, increase supplemental oxygen and investigate cause
Uncompensated metabolic acidosis with large base deficit: consider DKA, lactic acidosis, sepsis, toxin ingestion
Rising PaCO2 in asthmatic: ominous sign of respiratory muscle fatigue and impending arrest

medications.name: Sodium Bicarbonate (NaHCO3)
medications.action: Directly provides bicarbonate ions (HCO3-) to buffer excess hydrogen ions (H+), raising blood pH. Each mEq of bicarbonate neutralizes one mEq of hydrogen ion. Used as a temporizing measure in severe metabolic acidosis while the underlying cause is treated

pearls: ROME: Respiratory = Opposite (pH and PaCO2 go in opposite directions), Metabolic = Equal (pH and HCO3 go in the same direction). This simple mnemonic helps identify the primary disorder.

In a fully compensated ABG (pH is normal), look at which side of 7.40 the pH falls: pH 7.35-7.39 suggests the primary disorder is acidosis (the body is slightly favoring the acid side despite compensation). pH 7.41-7.45 suggests the primary disorder is alkalosis. The body NEVER overcompensates.

A rising PaCO2 in an asthma patient is an OMINOUS sign. Asthma patients normally hyperventilate during exacerbations (respiratory alkalosis from increased work of breathing). A 'normalizing' or rising PaCO2 indicates respiratory muscle fatigue and impending respiratory failure: prepare for intubation.

Metabolic acidosis with a normal anion gap (hyperchloremic) = loss of bicarbonate (diarrhea, renal tubular acidosis, early renal failure). Metabolic acidosis with an elevated anion gap = accumulation of unmeasured acids. Remember MUDPILES: Methanol, Uremia, DKA, Propylene glycol, Isoniazid/Iron, Lactic acidosis, Ethylene glycol, Salicylates.

Compensation is NEVER complete for metabolic acidosis: the lungs cannot blow off enough CO2 to fully normalize pH in severe metabolic acidosis. Kussmaul respirations (deep, rapid breathing) represent maximal respiratory compensation for metabolic acidosis.
diagnostics: pH assessment (7.35-7.45 normal range)
PaCO2 assessment (35-45 mmHg normal)
HCO3 assessment (22-26 mEq/L normal)
PaO2 assessment (80-100 mmHg normal)
SaO2 assessment (>95% normal)
management: Report critical ABG values immediately to RN/physician
Monitor respiratory status in conjunction with ABG results
Apply ROME method (Respiratory Opposite, Metabolic Equal)
Assess for compensation (full vs. partial)
Correlate ABG findings with patient clinical status
nursingActions: Understand normal ABG values (pH 7.35-7.45, PaCO2 35-45, HCO3 22-26)
Apply ROME method for interpretation (Respiratory = Opposite of pH, Metabolic = Equal to pH)
Report critical values immediately
Assist with ABG specimen collection (apply pressure 5 min post-draw, 10 min if anticoagulated)
Place specimen on ice and transport to lab within 30 minutes
Document FiO2 and ventilator settings at time of draw

abg-interpretation-rn.title: Advanced ABG Interpretation (RN)

cellular.title: Mixed Acid-Base Disorders and Anion Gap Analysis
cellular.content:
Advanced ABG interpretation requires the ability to identify mixed disorders (two or more primary acid-base disturbances occurring simultaneously) and calculate the anion gap to narrow the differential diagnosis of metabolic acidosis.

Anion Gap (AG):
The anion gap represents unmeasured anions in the blood. It helps classify metabolic acidosis into two categories.

Formula: AG = Na+ - (Cl- + HCO3-)
Normal: 8-12 mEq/L (some labs use 12 ± 4 depending on albumin correction)

• Elevated Anion Gap Metabolic Acidosis (AGMA): Accumulation of unmeasured acids. Each acid provides an H+ (which consumes HCO3-) and an unmeasured anion (which increases the gap). MUDPILES: Methanol, Uremia, DKA (beta-hydroxybutyrate), Propylene glycol, Isoniazid/Iron, Lactic acidosis (shock, sepsis, ischemia), Ethylene glycol, Salicylates.

• Non-Anion Gap (Normal AG) Metabolic Acidosis (NAGMA): Loss of bicarbonate OR gain of chloride (hyperchloremic). Causes: Diarrhea (loss of HCO3-rich intestinal fluid), Renal Tubular Acidosis, early renal failure, excessive NS infusion (dilutional acidosis), acetazolamide, ureteral diversion.
