This reaction can occur after infusion of as little as 10-15 mL of incompatible blood, which is why the first 15 minutes of any transfusion are critically monitored.

riskFactors: Clerical or identification errors (most common cause)
Failure to follow two-person verification protocol
Mislabeled blood specimens sent to the blood bank
Patient unable to state name (unconscious, confused, nonverbal)
Multiple patients with similar names on the same unit
Previous alloimmunization from prior transfusions or pregnancies

left: Fever and Chills (often first sign)
Flank or Low Back Pain
Chest Tightness
Anxiety or Sense of Impending Doom
right: Hypotension and Tachycardia
Dark or Cola-Colored Urine
Bleeding from IV Sites (DIC)
Flushing or Burning at IV Site

medications.name: Normal Saline (0.9% NaCl)
medications.action: Maintains renal perfusion and promotes urinary excretion of free hemoglobin to prevent acute kidney injury

medications.name: Epinephrine
medications.action: Increases blood pressure and cardiac output through alpha and beta adrenergic stimulation

pearls: AHTR can be fatal after as little as 10-15 mL of incompatible blood - this is why the first 15 minutes of every transfusion are critically important
The most common cause is human error (wrong patient, mislabeled specimen) - meticulous two-person bedside verification prevents this
Flank pain and dark urine together during a transfusion strongly suggest hemolysis - stop the transfusion immediately
Never discard the blood bag or tubing after a suspected reaction - the blood bank needs them for investigation
Free hemoglobin in the blood is toxic to the kidneys - aggressive IV normal saline helps flush it out and protect renal function
diagnostics: Observe for fever, chills, and rigors shortly after transfusion begins
Monitor for flank or low back pain (renal capsule distension)
Observe urine for dark red, brown, or cola-colored appearance
Monitor vital signs frequently (watch for hypotension and tachycardia)
Report any chest tightness or feeling of impending doom
Observe for signs of bleeding from IV sites (DIC development)
Monitor urine output closely (report oliguria or anuria)
management: Stop the transfusion IMMEDIATELY - do not infuse any more blood
Keep the IV line open with normal saline using new tubing
Notify the provider and blood bank immediately
Return the blood bag and tubing to the blood bank for investigation
Send post-reaction blood and urine samples as ordered
Maintain aggressive IV fluid resuscitation as prescribed to protect kidneys
Prepare for possible transfer to a higher level of care
nursingActions: Stop the transfusion at the first sign of a reaction
Disconnect the blood product tubing and connect new NS tubing to the IV catheter
Obtain vital signs immediately and compare to baseline
Report findings to the charge nurse and prescribing provider
Save the blood bag, tubing, and attached labels - do not discard
Collect blood and urine specimens as ordered by the provider
Document the reaction including time of onset, signs observed, and interventions
Monitor urine output hourly and report decreased output

anaphylactic-transfusion-rpn.title: Anaphylactic Transfusion Reaction

cellular.title: IgA Deficiency and Anti-IgA Antibody Response
cellular.content:
Anaphylactic transfusion reactions are rare but life-threatening type I hypersensitivity responses that occur within seconds to minutes of starting a transfusion. The most well-characterized mechanism involves patients with selective IgA deficiency who have developed anti-IgA antibodies from prior transfusion or pregnancy exposure. When these patients receive blood products containing IgA (present in plasma), the anti-IgA antibodies trigger massive mast cell and basophil degranulation.

This degranulation releases histamine, leukotrienes, and prostaglandins, causing severe bronchospasm, laryngeal edema, profound vasodilation, and increased vascular permeability. The result is a rapid-onset anaphylactic response with airway compromise and circulatory collapse. Unlike allergic (urticarial) reactions which are localized and mild, anaphylactic reactions are systemic and can be fatal without immediate intervention.

Selective IgA deficiency is the most common primary immunodeficiency, affecting approximately 1 in 300 to 1 in 700 individuals. However, only a small percentage of IgA-deficient patients develop clinically significant anti-IgA antibodies. For patients with known IgA deficiency, blood products must be obtained from IgA-deficient donors or the plasma must be washed to remove IgA-containing plasma.

riskFactors: Known IgA deficiency with anti-IgA antibodies
History of prior anaphylactic transfusion reaction
History of severe allergic reactions to blood products

left: Bronchospasm and Wheezing
Stridor (Upper Airway Obstruction)
Urticaria and Flushing
Angioedema (Face, Lips, Tongue)
right: Severe Hypotension (Shock)
Tachycardia
Loss of Consciousness
Abdominal Cramping and Nausea

medications.name: Epinephrine (Adrenaline)
medications.action: Alpha-1 effects cause vasoconstriction (raises BP); Beta-1 effects increase heart rate and contractility; Beta-2 effects cause bronchodilation and reduce mast cell degranulation

medications.name: Diphenhydramine (Benadryl)
medications.action: Blocks histamine H1 receptors to reduce urticaria, pruritus, and flushing

medications.name: Methylprednisolone (Solu-Medrol)
medications.action: Reduces late-phase inflammatory response and helps prevent biphasic anaphylaxis recurrence

pearls: Anaphylaxis from a transfusion occurs within SECONDS to MINUTES - if respiratory distress or hypotension occurs immediately after starting blood, think anaphylaxis
Epinephrine is the FIRST-LINE treatment for anaphylaxis - do not delay by giving antihistamines first; epinephrine saves lives
Patients with known IgA deficiency must receive washed RBCs or blood products from IgA-deficient donors
Unlike mild allergic reactions (urticaria only), anaphylaxis involves the airway and/or cardiovascular system - this distinction determines the urgency of response
After an anaphylactic transfusion reaction, the patient should be tested for IgA deficiency and anti-IgA antibodies to guide future transfusion safety
diagnostics: Observe for rapid onset of respiratory distress within seconds to minutes of starting transfusion
Monitor for wheezing, stridor, or inability to speak
Observe for facial or tongue swelling (angioedema)
Monitor blood pressure for sudden severe hypotension
Observe for widespread urticaria, flushing, or erythema
Monitor oxygen saturation continuously
Report any throat tightness or difficulty swallowing immediately
management: Stop the transfusion IMMEDIATELY at the first sign of anaphylaxis
Call for emergency assistance (Code Blue or Rapid Response)
Administer epinephrine as prescribed (first-line treatment for anaphylaxis)
Maintain airway - prepare for possible intubation
Administer high-flow oxygen via non-rebreather mask
Position patient supine with legs elevated if hypotensive (unless respiratory distress)
Establish additional IV access for fluid resuscitation
nursingActions: Stop the transfusion and disconnect blood product tubing immediately
Activate emergency response team
Administer epinephrine IM as ordered (anterolateral thigh)
Apply high-flow oxygen and prepare airway equipment (suction, intubation supplies)
Obtain vital signs every 2-5 minutes during acute phase
Establish a second large-bore IV for fluid and medication administration
Document the reaction including onset time, signs, interventions, and response
After stabilization, notify the blood bank and save the blood bag for investigation

febrile-transfusion-rpn.title: Febrile Nonhemolytic Transfusion Reaction

cellular.title: Cytokine Accumulation and Antibody-Mediated WBC Response
cellular.content:
Febrile nonhemolytic transfusion reactions (FNHTRs) are the most common type of transfusion reaction, occurring in approximately 1-3% of RBC transfusions and up to 30% of platelet transfusions. Two primary mechanisms are involved: (1) Recipient antibodies against donor white blood cell antigens (HLA antibodies) - the recipient's immune system recognizes donor leukocytes as foreign and mounts a febrile response through cytokine release. This is most common in patients who have been previously transfused or who are multiparous. (2) Cytokine accumulation - during blood product storage, donor white blood cells release cytokines (IL-1, IL-6, TNF-alpha) into the product. When transfused, these preformed cytokines act on the hypothalamus to produce fever.

The fever is defined as a temperature rise of 1 degree Celsius or greater above baseline, occurring during or within 4 hours after transfusion completion. While FNHTRs are not life-threatening, they are clinically significant because fever is also an early sign of the far more dangerous acute hemolytic transfusion reaction and bacterial contamination. Therefore, any fever during a transfusion must be treated as a potential emergency until a hemolytic reaction is ruled out.

Prevention strategies include leukoreduction (filtering out donor WBCs from blood products before storage or at the bedside) and premedication with acetaminophen. Leukoreduction has significantly reduced the incidence of FNHTRs and is now standard practice in many blood banks.

riskFactors: History of previous febrile transfusion reactions
Multiple prior transfusions (alloimmunization to HLA antigens)
Multiparous women (exposure to fetal HLA antigens)
Prolonged storage of blood products (increased cytokine accumulation)
Immunocompromised patients with heightened inflammatory responses

left: Fever (1-2 Degree Celsius Rise)
Chills and Rigors
Headache
General Malaise
right: Tachycardia (Mild)
Flushing
Muscle Aches
Nausea

medications.name: Acetaminophen (Tylenol)
medications.action: Inhibits prostaglandin synthesis in the CNS to reduce fever and provide mild pain relief

medications.name: Meperidine (Demerol)
medications.action: Reduces severe rigors by suppressing the shivering response through opioid receptor agonism

pearls: FNHTR is a diagnosis of exclusion - you must ALWAYS rule out acute hemolytic reaction and bacterial contamination first before attributing fever to a benign febrile reaction
Leukoreduction filters remove donor white blood cells and have dramatically reduced FNHTR rates - this is now standard practice in many institutions
A temperature rise of 1 degree Celsius or more above baseline during or within 4 hours of transfusion defines an FNHTR
Premedication with acetaminophen (and sometimes diphenhydramine) is recommended for patients who have had two or more febrile reactions to reduce recurrence
Platelet transfusions cause more febrile reactions than RBC transfusions because platelets are stored at room temperature, allowing more cytokine accumulation
diagnostics: Monitor temperature before, during, and after transfusion
Observe for chills, rigors, or shaking during or shortly after transfusion
Compare current temperature to pre-transfusion baseline
Monitor for headache and general malaise
Report any fever of 1 degree Celsius or more above baseline
Observe for other signs that may indicate a more serious reaction (back pain, dark urine)
Monitor vital signs at prescribed intervals throughout transfusion
management: Stop the transfusion at the onset of fever or rigors
Keep the IV line open with normal saline
Notify the provider and blood bank of the reaction
Administer antipyretics (acetaminophen) as prescribed
Send blood and urine specimens as ordered to rule out hemolytic reaction
Provide comfort measures (light blankets for chills, tepid compresses for fever)
Resume transfusion only if hemolytic reaction is ruled out and provider orders continuation
nursingActions: Obtain baseline temperature within 30 minutes before starting transfusion
Monitor temperature at 15 minutes, 30 minutes, and hourly during transfusion
Stop the transfusion immediately if fever develops and notify the provider
Administer prescribed acetaminophen for fever management
Save the blood bag and tubing for blood bank investigation
Document pre-transfusion baseline vitals and all changes observed
Educate the patient that premedication may be ordered for future transfusions
Request leukoreduced products for future transfusions as ordered

kawasaki-critical.title: Kawasaki Disease: Vasculitis

cellular.title: Arterial Inflammation
cellular.content: A systemic vasculitis affecting small/medium arteries. The major danger is coronary artery damage, where inflammation leads to dilation and potential aneurysm formation.

left: Fever > 5 days (Unresponsive)
Strawberry Tongue
Conjunctival Redness
Swollen Palms/Soles
right: Coronary Artery Aneurysm
Peeling Skin (Late sign)
Irritability
Recurring Fever

medications.name: IVIG
medications.action: Systemic anti-inflammatory

medications.name: Aspirin (High Dose)
medications.action: Prevents Coronary Clots

pearls: Need baseline and follow-up echoes
Postpone live vaccines for 11 months post IVIG
Monitor for MI symptoms even in children
diagnostics: Expect echocardiogram to be ordered (baseline and follow-up)
Monitor CBC, ESR, CRP trends
Expect liver function tests to be drawn
Monitor platelet count (thrombocytosis is expected)
Expect urinalysis to be ordered
Monitor temperature trends closely
management: Administer IVIG and aspirin as prescribed
Maintain IV access as ordered
Provide comfort measures for irritable child
Offer soft foods and fluids for oral comfort
Avoid live vaccines for 11 months after IVIG
Maintain cardiac monitoring as ordered
nursingActions: Monitor temperature every 4 hours
Assess skin changes daily and document
Monitor for signs of cardiac complications (tachycardia, murmur)
Report recurring fever after IVIG immediately
Provide gentle skin care (avoid rubbing peeling areas)
Monitor intake and output
Provide emotional support to parents
Assess extremities for swelling and color changes

congenital-heart.title: Congenital Heart Defects

cellular.title: Structural Anomalies
cellular.content: Heart defects present at birth. Classified into Acyanotic (Left-to-Right shunt, increased lung flow) and Cyanotic (Right-to-Left shunt, decreased systemic oxygenation).

left: Acyanotic: Murmur, HF signs
Tachypnea
Poor feeding
Weight gain (fluid)
right: Cyanotic: Tetrology of Fallot
Cyanosis (Blue-ish skin)
Clubbing
Squatting (Tet spells)

medications.name: Indomethacin
medications.action: Closes Patent Ductus Arteriosus

pearls: Cyanotic defects require immediate surgical or prostaglandin intervention
Polycythemia is a compensatory response to chronic hypoxia
Monitor for congestive heart failure in acyanotic defects
diagnostics: Expect echocardiogram to be ordered
Monitor oxygen saturation pre- and post-ductal
Expect chest X-ray to be ordered
Monitor growth parameters (poor weight gain)
Expect CBC and metabolic panel
Monitor continuous cardiac monitoring
management: Position in knee-chest position during Tet spells as instructed
Administer oxygen as ordered
Provide small, frequent feedings to reduce cardiac workload
Maintain calm environment and minimize crying
Follow fluid restriction or supplementation orders
Maintain normothermia
nursingActions: Monitor oxygen saturation and report cyanotic episodes
Assess feeding tolerance and weight gain trends
Report signs of heart failure (tachypnea, diaphoresis with feeding)
Monitor for Tet spells and respond with knee-chest position
Document intake and output including feeding amounts
Report signs of polycythemia (clubbing, plethora)
Provide family education and emotional support

pyloric-intussusception.title: Pyloric Stenosis & Intussusception

cellular.title: Mechanical Obstruction
cellular.content: Pyloric: Hypertrophy of pyloric sphincter blocks gastric emptying. Intussusception: Bowel telescopes into itself, cutting off blood supply.

riskFactors: Pyloric stenosis: male sex (4:1), firstborn, family history

left: Pyloric: Projectile Vomiting
Olive-shaped mass
Intuss: Colicky Pain
Sausage-shaped mass
right: Intuss: Currant Jelly Stool
Dehydration (Sunken fontanelle)
Metabolic Alkalosis (Pyloric)
Shock symptoms

medications.name: IV Fluids
medications.action: Rehydration

pearls: Pyloric: Vomiting is non-bilious
Intussusception: Air enema is diagnostic and often curative
Monitor for passage of normal stool
diagnostics: Expect abdominal ultrasound for pyloric stenosis (thickened pylorus)
Expect abdominal X-ray for intussusception
Monitor electrolytes (metabolic alkalosis in pyloric stenosis from vomiting)
Expect air or barium enema for intussusception (diagnostic AND therapeutic)
Monitor hydration status (fontanelle, mucous membranes, urine output)
Expect stool testing for occult blood (currant jelly stools)
management: Maintain NPO status and IV fluids as prescribed
Correct electrolyte imbalances before surgery (pyloric stenosis)
Position upright after feeding to reduce vomiting (pyloric)
Prepare for pyloromyotomy (pyloric stenosis surgical repair)
Monitor for passage of normal stool after air enema (intussusception)
Maintain NG tube if ordered for decompression
nursingActions: Monitor intake and output strictly (assess for dehydration)
Weigh diapers for accurate output measurement in infants
Assess fontanelle for dehydration (sunken) or increased ICP (bulging)
Report projectile vomiting or currant jelly stools immediately
Monitor for return of bowel function after intervention
Educate parents on post-operative feeding schedule (gradual reintroduction)

adhd-basics.title: ADHD

cellular.title: Neurodevelopmental Dysregulation
cellular.content: Neurodevelopmental disorder. Dysregulation of dopamine and norepinephrine in prefrontal cortex. Three presentations: predominantly inattentive, predominantly hyperactive-impulsive, combined. Symptoms must be present before age 12 in 2+ settings.

riskFactors: Premature birth or low birth weight

left: Difficulty sustaining attention
Fidgeting
Difficulty waiting turn
right: Excessive talking
Disorganization
Losing things frequently

medications.name: Methylphenidate
medications.action: Increases dopamine and norepinephrine in prefrontal cortex

medications.name: Amphetamine Salts
medications.action: Increases dopamine and norepinephrine release

medications.name: Atomoxetine
medications.action: Selective norepinephrine reuptake inhibitor

pearls: Monitor growth (height and weight) - stimulants may suppress appetite and growth
Take stimulants in the morning to minimize insomnia
Drug holidays during school breaks may be considered
Behavioral therapy should accompany medication
diagnostics: Expect behavioral rating scales (Conners, Vanderbilt) from parents and teachers
Monitor growth parameters (height and weight) on stimulant therapy
Expect cardiovascular screening (HR, BP, ECG) before starting stimulants
Monitor academic performance reports
Expect developmental and psychological testing
Monitor for comorbid conditions (anxiety, depression, learning disabilities)
management: Administer stimulant medications in the morning as prescribed
Implement behavioral management strategies alongside medication
Establish consistent routines and structure
Monitor growth and appetite regularly
Consider drug holidays during school breaks if appropriate
Ensure regular follow-up for medication adjustment
nursingActions: Monitor height and weight at each visit (growth suppression risk)
Assess heart rate and blood pressure before and during stimulant therapy
Report decreased appetite or weight loss to provider
Educate parents on medication administration timing
Assess for sleep difficulties and provide sleep hygiene education
Screen for comorbid mood and anxiety disorders
Monitor school performance and behavioral reports

separation-anxiety.title: Separation Anxiety

cellular.title: Developmental vs Pathological
cellular.content: Normal developmental stage peaking 10-18 months. Pathological when excessive/persistent beyond age, causing significant distress/impairment. Related to attachment theory - child fears loss of caregiver.

riskFactors: Recent loss or change (divorce, move, death)

left: Excessive distress when separated
Worry about harm to caregiver
Refusal to go to school/sleep alone
right: Nightmares about separation
Somatic complaints (stomachache/headache)
Clinginess

medications.name: CBT
medications.action: First-line treatment - cognitive behavioral therapy

medications.name: Fluoxetine
medications.action: Reduces anxiety symptoms for severe cases

pearls: Establish consistent routines
Practice brief separations and gradually increase
Avoid prolonged goodbyes
Validate feelings without reinforcing avoidance
Normal vs pathological depends on age and severity
diagnostics: Expect clinical interview and behavioral observation
Monitor severity of symptoms and functional impairment
Expect anxiety screening tools (SCARED, Screen for Anxiety)
Assess for comorbid conditions (depression, other anxiety disorders)
Expect developmental history review
management: Implement gradual exposure to separations (systematic desensitization)
Establish consistent routines and predictable schedules
Avoid reinforcing avoidance behaviors
Validate feelings without prolonging goodbyes
Consider CBT referral for persistent symptoms
Administer SSRI only for severe refractory cases as prescribed
nursingActions: Assess developmental appropriateness of anxiety level
Provide calm, consistent approach during hospital separations
Educate parents on age-appropriate separation strategies
Report escalating anxiety or functional impairment to provider
Encourage parents to practice brief separations gradually
Monitor for somatic complaints (stomachache, headache) as anxiety manifestations

separation-anxiety-rn.title: Separation Anxiety Disorder: RN Clinical Decision-Making

cellular.title: Attachment Theory & Neurobiological Basis
cellular.content: Separation anxiety disorder (SAD) involves pathological activation of the attachment system beyond developmental norms. John Bowlby's attachment theory explains how disrupted caregiver-child bonds create internal working models of insecurity that persist into later childhood. Neurobiologically, the amygdala serves as the fear-processing center and becomes hyperactivated in SAD, triggering the hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal (HPA) axis. This results in excessive cortisol release, maintaining a state of chronic hyperarousal. The prefrontal cortex, responsible for fear extinction and emotional regulation, shows reduced connectivity with the amygdala in anxious children, impairing their ability to modulate fear responses. Developmental neurobiology research demonstrates that the critical period for attachment formation (6-24 months) coincides with rapid amygdala maturation, and adverse experiences during this window can alter neural circuitry governing threat detection and safety signaling. Insecure attachment patterns (anxious-ambivalent) are strongly associated with later development of SAD, as the child internalizes unpredictable caregiver availability as a template for future relationships.

riskFactors: Family history of anxiety disorders or panic disorder
Traumatic separation experience (hospitalization, parental deployment, divorce)
Comorbid generalized anxiety or social anxiety disorder
Maternal anxiety disorder (modeling and genetic transmission)
Recent life transition (school change, relocation, new sibling)

left: Excessive distress disproportionate to developmental stage
Persistent worry about losing attachment figure to illness/death/disaster
Refusal to attend school or sleep away from caregiver
Repeated somatic complaints on school mornings (stomachache, nausea, headache)
right: Nightmares with separation themes
Panic-like symptoms during actual or anticipated separation (tachycardia, hyperventilation)
Social withdrawal and declining peer relationships
Regression to earlier developmental behaviors (thumb-sucking, bedwetting)

medications.name: Cognitive Behavioral Therapy (CBT)
medications.action: Restructures maladaptive cognitions about separation danger and builds coping skills through graduated exposure

medications.name: Fluoxetine
medications.action: Increases serotonergic transmission in amygdala-prefrontal circuits, reducing fear responsivity and improving emotional regulation

medications.name: Sertraline
medications.action: Selective serotonin reuptake inhibition reducing anxiety circuit hyperactivation

pearls: SCARED screening tool: score ≥25 indicates clinically significant anxiety warranting intervention
Accommodation by parents (allowing avoidance) maintains and worsens the disorder - parent coaching is essential
School refusal is a behavioral emergency requiring rapid intervention to prevent chronic non-attendance
Comorbidity is the rule, not the exception: screen for depression, GAD, and social anxiety
CBT + SSRI combination is superior to either treatment alone for moderate-severe cases
diagnostics: Expect SCARED (Screen for Child Anxiety Related Disorders) assessment tool to be administered
Monitor functional impairment across domains (school attendance, peer relationships, sleep)
Expect clinical interview assessing DSM-5 criteria (excessive worry about separation lasting 4+ weeks in children)
Assess for somatic complaints pattern (Monday morning stomachaches, headaches before school)
Expect developmental history and attachment quality assessment
Monitor for comorbid conditions (depression, school refusal, selective mutism)
management: Implement evidence-based CBT with graduated exposure hierarchy
Develop systematic desensitization plan (rank feared separations from least to most anxiety-provoking)
Coach parents on extinction of reassurance-seeking behaviors
Establish transitional objects and coping strategies (breathing techniques, cognitive restructuring)
Collaborate with school for accommodation plan during treatment
Administer SSRIs only for severe refractory cases as prescribed (fluoxetine or sertraline first-line)
nursingActions: Conduct comprehensive anxiety assessment using validated screening tools (SCARED score ≥25 indicates clinical anxiety)
Assess attachment quality through observation of parent-child interaction patterns
Monitor for school refusal and declining academic performance
Educate parents on the difference between accommodation (reinforcing avoidance) and supportive limit-setting
Report escalating avoidance behaviors or emergence of panic symptoms to provider
Screen for suicidal ideation in adolescents with severe anxiety and comorbid depression
Coordinate with school counselor for behavioral intervention plan
Document frequency, duration, and triggers of separation distress episodes

separation-anxiety-np.title: Separation Anxiety Disorder: NP Advanced Psychopharmacology & Neuroscience

cellular.title: Molecular Neurobiology of Pathological Anxiety
cellular.content: At the molecular level, separation anxiety disorder involves dysregulation of the corticotropin-releasing factor (CRF) system, which serves as the primary mediator of the stress response. CRF neurons in the central nucleus of the amygdala (CeA) project to the paraventricular nucleus of the hypothalamus, activating the HPA axis and stimulating adrenocorticotropic hormone (ACTH) release from the anterior pituitary, which drives cortisol production from the adrenal cortex. In pathological anxiety, CRF receptor type 1 (CRF1) upregulation in the amygdala creates a feed-forward loop of heightened threat sensitivity. Glucocorticoid receptor (GR) polymorphisms, particularly the BclI and N363S variants, alter negative feedback sensitivity of the HPA axis, predisposing to chronic cortisol elevation and anxiety vulnerability. Epigenetic mechanisms play a critical role: early-life stress induces hypermethylation of the NR3C1 gene (encoding the glucocorticoid receptor) promoter region, reducing GR expression in the hippocampus and impairing cortisol negative feedback. This mirrors findings from rodent models where maternal separation alters DNA methylation patterns at stress-regulatory genes, producing lasting anxiety phenotypes. Neuroimaging studies using functional MRI reveal amygdala hyperreactivity to separation-related stimuli and reduced prefrontal cortical activation during emotion regulation tasks in children with SAD. Structural MRI shows increased amygdala volume and altered white matter tract integrity in the uncinate fasciculus (the primary connection between the prefrontal cortex and amygdala) in anxious youth. The serotonergic system is central to treatment: the 5-HTTLPR polymorphism (short allele) in the serotonin transporter gene (SLC6A4) is associated with increased amygdala reactivity and anxiety vulnerability, particularly in the context of environmental adversity (gene-environment interaction).

riskFactors: 5-HTTLPR short allele carrier status (serotonin transporter polymorphism)
Glucocorticoid receptor polymorphisms (BclI, N363S) affecting HPA axis regulation
Epigenetic modifications from early adverse experiences (NR3C1 hypermethylation)
Behavioral inhibition temperament (high amygdala reactivity phenotype)
CRF1 receptor overexpression in amygdala circuits
Adverse childhood experiences during critical attachment period (6-24 months)
Reduced prefrontal-amygdala functional connectivity on neuroimaging

left: Amygdala hyperreactivity to separation cues (neuroimaging finding)
HPA axis dysregulation with elevated basal cortisol
Persistent avoidance behavior causing significant functional impairment (school refusal, social isolation)
Somatic symptom clusters temporally linked to separation (GI distress, headache, chest tightness)
right: Panic-like autonomic activation during separation (tachycardia, diaphoresis, hyperventilation)
Sleep architecture disruption with separation-themed nightmares
Cognitive distortions: catastrophic thinking about caregiver safety
Developmental regression under stress (enuresis, selective mutism, clinginess beyond developmental stage)

medications.name: Fluoxetine
medications.action: Blocks serotonin reuptake transporter (SERT/SLC6A4), increasing synaptic 5-HT concentration. Downstream effects include desensitization of 5-HT1A autoreceptors, enhanced serotonergic neurotransmission in amygdala-prefrontal circuits, and reduced CRF release from the central amygdala

medications.name: Sertraline
medications.action: Potent SERT inhibition with mild dopamine transporter (DAT) reuptake inhibition at higher doses, providing both anxiolytic and mild activating effects

medications.name: Buspirone
medications.action: Partial agonist at presynaptic 5-HT1A autoreceptors (reducing serotonin firing initially) and postsynaptic 5-HT1A receptors in hippocampus and prefrontal cortex, modulating anxiety circuits without GABA-ergic sedation

pearls: Pharmacogenomics: CYP2D6 poor metabolizers may need 50% lower SSRI starting doses; ultra-rapid metabolizers may need higher doses or alternative agents
5-HTTLPR short allele carriers show greater amygdala reactivity but also greater treatment response to combined CBT + SSRI
CAMS trial evidence hierarchy: CBT + sertraline (80.7%) > CBT alone (59.7%) ≈ sertraline alone (54.9%) > placebo (23.7%)
Epigenetic changes from early stress are potentially reversible with effective treatment (reduced NR3C1 methylation after successful CBT)
Monitor for SSRI discontinuation syndrome if stopping abruptly (except fluoxetine due to long half-life) - taper over 2-4 weeks
diagnostics: Expect comprehensive DSM-5 diagnostic assessment (Criterion A: developmentally inappropriate excessive anxiety regarding separation, with 3+ symptoms for ≥4 weeks in children)
Administer SCARED multidimensional anxiety scale with parent and child report versions
Expect assessment of functional impairment using Children's Global Assessment Scale (CGAS)
Evaluate for differential diagnoses: generalized anxiety disorder, social anxiety disorder, specific phobia, PTSD, adjustment disorder
Assess attachment quality using validated measures (Strange Situation paradigm for younger children)
Screen for comorbid major depressive disorder, ADHD, and OCD
Expect thyroid function evaluation to rule out endocrine contribution to anxiety symptoms
management: Prescribe first-line SSRI therapy (fluoxetine 5-10 mg initial dose, titrate to 20-40 mg over 4-6 weeks based on response)
Consider sertraline as alternative (25 mg initial, titrate to 50-200 mg) based on CAMS trial evidence
Implement combination CBT + pharmacotherapy for moderate-severe cases (CAMS trial demonstrated 80.7% response rate for combination vs 59.7% CBT alone vs 54.9% sertraline alone)
Monitor for SSRI activation syndrome in first 2 weeks (paradoxical agitation, insomnia, disinhibition)
Consider augmentation with buspirone (5-HT1A partial agonist) for partial SSRI responders
Evaluate benzodiazepine short-term bridge therapy only for acute crisis situations (avoid chronic use due to dependence risk and cognitive effects in developing brain)
Develop pharmacogenomic-informed prescribing approach considering CYP2D6 and CYP2C19 metabolizer status for SSRI selection and dosing
nursingActions: Conduct advanced psychopharmacological assessment including medication history, prior SSRI trials, and adverse effects
Monitor for FDA black box warning signs: suicidal ideation, behavioral activation, and agitation in first 4-6 weeks of SSRI therapy
Assess CYP2D6/CYP2C19 metabolizer status implications for drug selection and dosing
Evaluate for serotonin syndrome risk if multiple serotonergic agents prescribed (mental status changes, autonomic instability, neuromuscular hyperactivity)
Monitor treatment response using standardized outcome measures (SCARED score reduction ≥50%)
Educate family on neurobiological basis of anxiety to reduce stigma and improve treatment adherence
Coordinate multidisciplinary care (prescriber, therapist, school) for integrated treatment plan
Document detailed psychiatric review of systems at each follow-up visit

lead-poisoning.title: Lead Poisoning

cellular.title: Heavy Metal Toxicity
cellular.content: Lead interferes with heme synthesis (inhibits ALA dehydratase and ferrochelatase), disrupts calcium signaling in neurons, causes oxidative damage. Children absorb more lead than adults and are more vulnerable due to developing brain.

riskFactors: Living in pre-1978 housing with lead-based paint

left: Developmental delays
Irritability
Abdominal pain (lead colic)
right: Constipation
Lead lines on gums (Burton lines)
Wrist/foot drop
Encephalopathy (severe)

medications.name: Succimer (DMSA)
medications.action: Binds lead for renal excretion

medications.name: CaEDTA
medications.action: Chelates lead for severe cases/encephalopathy

pearls: Screen at ages 1 and 2
Most common source: old paint in pre-1978 housing
Blood lead level >5 mcg/dL requires investigation
Hand washing before eating reduces ingestion
Wet mopping removes lead dust (dry sweeping spreads it)
diagnostics: Expect venous blood lead level (BLL) for screening and confirmation
Monitor CBC (microcytic anemia, basophilic stippling)
Expect free erythrocyte protoporphyrin level
Monitor renal function during chelation therapy
Expect abdominal X-ray if recent ingestion suspected (lead is radiopaque)
Expect developmental screening assessment
management: Remove child from lead exposure source immediately
Ensure adequate nutrition (calcium, iron, vitamin C reduce absorption)
Administer chelation therapy as prescribed for elevated levels
Maintain adequate hydration during chelation
Implement lead abatement in the home environment
Follow up with serial blood lead levels
nursingActions: Screen blood lead levels at ages 1 and 2 per guidelines
Educate parents on lead sources and prevention (wet mopping, hand washing)
Report elevated blood lead levels to public health authorities
Monitor for developmental delays and neurobehavioral changes
Assess home environment for lead exposure sources
Educate on high-calcium, high-iron diet to reduce lead absorption

varicella.title: Varicella (Chickenpox)

cellular.title: Varicella-Zoster Virus (VZV)
cellular.content: Varicella is caused by the Varicella-Zoster Virus (VZV), a member of the herpesvirus family. The virus enters through the respiratory tract and conjunctivae, replicates in regional lymph nodes, and spreads via primary viremia to the liver and spleen. Secondary viremia seeds the skin, producing the characteristic vesicular rash. The virus then becomes latent in dorsal root ganglia and can reactivate later in life as herpes zoster (shingles). Transmission occurs via airborne droplets and direct contact with vesicular fluid. The incubation period is 10-21 days, and the patient is contagious from 1-2 days before rash onset until all lesions have crusted over.

left: Vesicular rash in successive 'crops' at different stages (macules, papules, vesicles, crusts)
Low-grade fever preceding rash onset
Intense pruritus (itching) of lesions
Rash starts on trunk and spreads to face and extremities (centripetal distribution)
Malaise, irritability, and decreased appetite
right: Lesions appear in 'crops': all stages present simultaneously
Airborne AND Contact Precautions required (negative pressure room)
Highly contagious: contagious 1-2 days before rash until ALL lesions crusted
Immunocompromised children at risk for severe/disseminated disease
Complications: Secondary bacterial skin infection, pneumonia, encephalitis

medications.name: Acyclovir (Zovirax)
medications.action: Inhibits viral DNA polymerase, preventing VZV replication and reducing severity and duration of illness

medications.name: Calamine Lotion
medications.action: Provides cooling effect on skin to relieve itching and discomfort from vesicular lesions

pearls: NEVER give aspirin to children with varicella: risk of Reye's syndrome (acute hepatic encephalopathy). Use acetaminophen for fever.
Isolate patient until ALL lesions have crusted over (typically 5-7 days after rash onset). Airborne + Contact precautions in hospital.
Varicella vaccine (Varivax) is a live attenuated vaccine given at 12-15 months and 4-6 years. Contraindicated in immunocompromised and pregnant patients.
Exposed susceptible contacts should receive varicella vaccine within 72 hours or VZIG (varicella-zoster immune globulin) if immunocompromised.
diagnostics: Visual assessment of rash characteristics (crops in different stages)
Temperature monitoring
Assess for secondary bacterial infection of lesions
Monitor respiratory status in immunocompromised patients
management: Implement airborne AND contact precautions
Isolate until ALL lesions have crusted over
Keep fingernails short to prevent scratching and secondary infection
Administer antihistamines as ordered for pruritus
Administer acyclovir as ordered for high-risk patients
nursingActions: Implement airborne and contact precautions
Assess rash progression (macule → papule → vesicle → crust)
Maintain skin care to prevent secondary infection
Apply calamine lotion as ordered for itching
Monitor for complications (pneumonia, encephalitis)
Verify immunity status of healthcare workers before entering room

dehydration-peds.title: Pediatric Dehydration

cellular.title: Fluid Volume Deficit in Children
cellular.content: Pediatric dehydration occurs when fluid output exceeds intake, most commonly from gastroenteritis (vomiting and diarrhea). Children are at higher risk than adults due to their higher body surface area-to-weight ratio, higher metabolic rate, immature renal concentrating ability, and greater percentage of total body water. Dehydration is classified by percentage of body weight loss: Mild (3-5%), Moderate (6-9%), and Severe (≥10%). The type of dehydration (isotonic, hypotonic, or hypertonic) depends on the relative loss of water versus electrolytes. Isotonic dehydration (equal loss of water and sodium) is most common. Prompt assessment and rehydration are critical as severe dehydration can rapidly progress to hypovolemic shock in pediatric patients.

riskFactors: Infants and young children (higher body water percentage)

left: Sunken anterior fontanelle in infants (reliable early indicator)
Decreased or absent tears when crying
Poor skin turgor (tenting of skin on abdomen)
Dry mucous membranes (lips, tongue, oral mucosa)
Decreased urine output (fewer wet diapers: <4-6/day in infants)
right: Tachycardia (early compensatory sign of volume depletion)
Prolonged capillary refill time (>2 seconds)
Lethargy, irritability, or altered mental status (moderate-severe)
Hypotension (LATE sign: indicates severe/decompensated dehydration)
Sunken eyes, cool/mottled extremities in severe dehydration

medications.name: Oral Rehydration Therapy (ORT): Pedialyte
medications.action: Provides balanced glucose-electrolyte solution that utilizes intestinal sodium-glucose cotransporter to maximize fluid and electrolyte absorption

medications.name: IV Normal Saline (0.9% NaCl) Bolus
medications.action: Rapidly expands intravascular volume to restore tissue perfusion and correct hypovolemic state

pearls: Monitor urine output closely: best indicator of rehydration success. Goal: >1 mL/kg/hr in children, 4-6 wet diapers/day in infants.
Daily weights are the most accurate measure of fluid status: weigh at same time, same scale, same clothing. A 1kg weight loss = approximately 1L fluid deficit.
Assess anterior fontanelle in infants: sunken fontanelle indicates significant dehydration. Also reassess with rehydration (should normalize).
Teach parents ORT technique: small frequent sips (teaspoon/syringe), not large volumes at once. Vomiting does NOT mean ORT has failed: wait 15-20 minutes and restart slowly.
diagnostics: Anterior fontanelle assessment in infants (sunken = dehydration)
Skin turgor assessment (tenting)
Mucous membrane assessment (dry)
Urine output monitoring (wet diapers count)
Daily weight measurement
management: Administer oral rehydration solution (Pedialyte) in small frequent amounts
Administer IV fluids as ordered for severe dehydration
Continue breastfeeding if applicable
Resume age-appropriate diet as tolerated
Avoid juice, soda, and sports drinks
nursingActions: Monitor strict intake and output (count wet diapers)
Weigh daily at same time, same scale, same clothing
Assess anterior fontanelle in infants (sunken indicates dehydration)
Administer oral rehydration in small frequent sips (5mL every 1-2 minutes)
Monitor for signs of worsening dehydration (tachycardia, lethargy, hypotension)
Report decreased urine output or altered mental status immediately

hip-dysplasia.title: Developmental Dysplasia of the Hip (DDH)

cellular.title: Abnormal Hip Joint Development
cellular.content: Developmental dysplasia of the hip encompasses a spectrum of abnormalities where the femoral head and acetabulum fail to develop a stable relationship. The acetabulum may be shallow (dysplasia), the femoral head may be partially displaced (subluxation), or completely displaced from the acetabulum (dislocation). During fetal development, mechanical forces (breech positioning) or ligamentous laxity (maternal estrogen exposure) prevent normal acetabular deepening, leading to an unstable hip joint that may progress to complete dislocation if untreated.

riskFactors: Female sex (4:1 ratio due to estrogen-induced ligament laxity)
Swaddling with legs extended and adducted
Left hip involvement (more common due to intrauterine positioning)

left: Positive Ortolani 'Clunk'
Asymmetric Thigh/Gluteal Folds
Limited Hip Abduction
Apparent Leg Length Discrepancy
right: Positive Barlow Sign (Hip Dislocates)
Galeazzi Sign (Unequal Knee Heights)
Trendelenburg Gait (Older Child)
Waddling Gait with Bilateral DDH

medications.name: Acetaminophen
medications.action: Pain relief post-procedure

pearls: Ortolani reduces a DISLOCATED hip (clunk IN), Barlow DISLOCATES an unstable hip (clunk OUT) - think 'Ortolani = Out to In'
Never swaddle infants with legs straight and adducted - always allow hips to flex and abduct naturally ('frog-leg' position)
Pavlik harness avascular necrosis risk increases if hip is forced into extreme abduction - harness should allow some movement
diagnostics: Perform Ortolani maneuver (reduces dislocated hip - palpable 'clunk')
Perform Barlow maneuver (dislocates unstable hip)
Expect hip ultrasound for infants <4-6 months
Expect X-ray for infants >4-6 months (ossification centers visible)
Assess for asymmetric gluteal/thigh folds
Monitor for leg length discrepancy (Galeazzi sign)
Assess for limited hip abduction
management: Apply Pavlik harness as ordered for infants <6 months
Maintain hip flexion and abduction position
Closed reduction under anesthesia if harness fails
Open surgical reduction for late-diagnosed cases
Spica cast application post-reduction as ordered
Follow-up imaging to confirm reduction
nursingActions: Teach parents proper Pavlik harness application and care
Ensure harness is worn 23 hours/day as ordered
Check skin under harness straps for breakdown every diaper change
Assess neurovascular status of lower extremities
Teach safe swaddling (hips flexed and abducted - 'hip-healthy' swaddling)
Provide car seat modification education
Monitor growth and development milestones

foreign-body-aspiration.title: Foreign Body Aspiration

cellular.title: Airway Obstruction Emergency
cellular.content: Foreign body aspiration (FBA) occurs when an object is inhaled into the airway, causing partial or complete obstruction of the tracheobronchial tree. It is most common in children aged 6 months to 3 years due to their tendency to explore objects orally, incomplete dentition (poor chewing ability), immature swallowing coordination, and small airway diameter. The most commonly aspirated items include round foods (grapes, hot dogs, nuts, popcorn, hard candy), small toys/toy parts, coins, and buttons/batteries. The right main bronchus is more commonly affected due to its wider diameter and more vertical orientation. FBA is a leading cause of accidental death in children under 5 years. Prompt recognition and intervention are life-saving, as complete obstruction can lead to respiratory arrest within minutes.

riskFactors: Small objects (coins, buttons, beads, small toys)
Round foods (grapes, hot dogs, nuts, popcorn)

left: Sudden onset of choking, gagging, or coughing episode
Stridor (high-pitched inspiratory sound indicating upper airway obstruction)
Unilateral wheezing or decreased breath sounds on affected side
Respiratory distress: nasal flaring, intercostal/subcostal retractions, tachypnea
Cyanosis (late sign indicating severe hypoxia)
right: Witnessed choking event with sudden onset (key history finding)
Partial obstruction: forceful coughing, able to vocalize: encourage coughing, do NOT intervene
Complete obstruction: unable to cough, cry, or breathe: IMMEDIATE intervention required
Right main bronchus most common site (wider, more vertical angle)
High-risk items: Grapes, hot dogs, nuts, popcorn, coins, small toy parts, button batteries

medications.name: Back Blows + Chest Thrusts (Infant <1 year)
medications.action: Creates artificial cough by generating increased intrathoracic pressure to expel the foreign body from the airway

medications.name: Abdominal Thrusts: Heimlich Maneuver (Child >1 year)
medications.action: Rapid inward and upward abdominal compression creates forceful upward airflow to dislodge and expel the foreign body from the airway

pearls: Prevention is KEY: Cut grapes and hot dogs lengthwise (not round coins), avoid nuts/popcorn/hard candy for children <4 years, choose age-appropriate toys (no small parts), supervise meals.
Do NOT perform blind finger sweeps: may push object deeper into airway. Only remove visible objects from the mouth.
Age-appropriate intervention: Infants <1 year = back blows + chest thrusts. Children >1 year = abdominal thrusts (Heimlich maneuver). NEVER use Heimlich on infants.
After any choking event, child should be evaluated by healthcare provider even if object was expelled: assess for retained fragments, airway trauma, or aspiration pneumonia.
diagnostics: Assess respiratory status (stridor, wheezing, coughing)
Monitor oxygen saturation
Observe for asymmetric breath sounds
Assess for signs of complete airway obstruction (inability to speak, cyanosis)
management: For partial obstruction: encourage coughing, do NOT perform blind finger sweeps
For complete obstruction: back blows and chest thrusts for infants; abdominal thrusts (Heimlich) for children >1 year
Maintain airway and administer oxygen as ordered
Prepare for bronchoscopy for object removal
Monitor post-removal for laryngeal edema
nursingActions: Assess respiratory status continuously
Keep emergency equipment at bedside (suction, oxygen)
Educate parents on age-appropriate food preparation (cut grapes lengthwise, no nuts < 4 years)
Teach parents choking rescue techniques
Report sudden onset coughing, gagging, or wheezing in a previously well child
Supervise young children during meals and play

androgen-insensitivity-rpn.title: Androgen Insensitivity Syndrome: RPN Basics

cellular.title: Androgen Receptor Dysfunction
cellular.content: Androgen Insensitivity Syndrome (AIS) is a condition in which a person who is genetically male (46,XY karyotype) is unable to respond to androgens (male hormones such as testosterone and dihydrotestosterone). Because the body's cells cannot use these hormones, the person develops with a female or partially female external appearance despite having male chromosomes. The testes produce testosterone normally, but the cells lack functioning androgen receptors, so the masculinizing effects of testosterone do not occur. The testes also produce Müllerian Inhibiting Substance (MIS), which causes the internal female reproductive structures (uterus, fallopian tubes, upper vagina) to regress during fetal development. As a result, individuals with complete AIS are born appearing phenotypically female but have no uterus or fallopian tubes. Undescended testes may be located in the abdomen or inguinal canal and may present as inguinal masses in a phenotypically female newborn.

riskFactors: Family history of AIS (X-linked recessive inheritance pattern)
Maternal carrier status (mother carries the gene mutation on X chromosome)
Phenotypically female infant with bilateral inguinal hernias or masses
History of undescended testes in phenotypically female child

left: Phenotypically female newborn with inguinal masses (may contain testes)
Primary amenorrhea in adolescent (no menstrual periods)
Female external genitalia with blind-ending vaginal pouch
Absent uterus and fallopian tubes on imaging
right: Normal female breast development at puberty (testosterone aromatized to estrogen)
Sparse or absent pubic and axillary hair
Bilateral inguinal hernias in a phenotypically female infant
Tall stature for assigned sex

medications.name: Estrogen Replacement Therapy
medications.action: Provides estrogen supplementation after gonadectomy to maintain bone density, cardiovascular health, and secondary sexual characteristics

medications.name: Calcium and Vitamin D Supplementation
medications.action: Supports bone mineral density maintenance in conjunction with estrogen replacement

pearls: AIS is an X-linked condition: a phenotypically female person has a 46,XY karyotype but cannot respond to androgens
Inguinal hernias in a phenotypically female infant should raise suspicion for AIS and prompt further investigation
Primary amenorrhea in an adolescent with female appearance but absent uterus is a key presentation
Sensitive, family-centered communication is essential: use preferred terms and respect privacy
Psychosocial support and referral to counseling are critical components of care
diagnostics: Expect karyotype analysis to be ordered (will show 46,XY)
Monitor for inguinal masses in phenotypically female newborns
Expect hormone levels to be drawn (testosterone may be elevated)
Expect pelvic ultrasound to assess for absence of uterus and ovaries
Monitor growth and pubertal development milestones
management: Provide supportive and sensitive communication with family
Maintain confidentiality and use family-preferred language
Support psychosocial needs of patient and family
Reinforce information provided by the healthcare team regarding diagnosis
Assist with referrals to genetics, endocrinology, and psychology as ordered
Provide age-appropriate education to the patient
nursingActions: Use sensitive, non-judgmental language when discussing the condition with family
Respect patient and family privacy regarding the diagnosis
Document using family-preferred terms and pronouns
Assess family coping and refer to support services as needed
Monitor for signs of psychological distress in the patient (especially adolescents)
Educate family on the importance of follow-up appointments with specialists
Report any signs of inguinal herniation in phenotypically female infants to provider
Provide emotional support during diagnostic workup and disclosure

androgen-insensitivity.title: Androgen Insensitivity Syndrome: RN Pathophysiology & Clinical Management

cellular.title: Androgen Receptor Dysfunction & Sexual Differentiation
cellular.content: Androgen Insensitivity Syndrome (AIS) results from mutations in the androgen receptor (AR) gene located on the X chromosome. This X-linked recessive condition causes partial or complete inability of target cells to respond to testosterone and dihydrotestosterone (DHT), despite normal or elevated production of these hormones by the testes. During embryonic development, the SRY gene on the Y chromosome directs gonadal differentiation into testes, which then produce two critical hormones: testosterone (and its more potent derivative DHT) for masculinization of external genitalia, and Müllerian Inhibiting Substance (MIS, also called Anti-Müllerian Hormone or AMH) for regression of the Müllerian duct structures. In complete AIS (CAIS), the androgen receptor is entirely non-functional. Testosterone and DHT are produced normally by the fetal testes, but target tissues cannot respond. Consequently, external genital masculinization fails entirely, and the individual develops phenotypically female external genitalia. However, because MIS/AMH production and its receptor function are intact, the Müllerian ducts still regress. This results in the absence of a uterus, fallopian tubes, and the upper two-thirds of the vagina. The Wolffian duct structures (epididymis, vas deferens, seminal vesicles) also fail to develop because their differentiation requires local androgen action. The net result is a 46,XY individual with female external genitalia, a blind-ending vaginal pouch, absent uterus, and intra-abdominal or inguinal testes. At puberty, the testes produce testosterone which is aromatized to estrogen via the aromatase enzyme (CYP19A1), driving breast development and a female body habitus. However, pubic and axillary hair growth is minimal or absent because hair follicle androgen receptors are non-functional. In partial AIS (PAIS), the androgen receptor retains some function, resulting in ambiguous genitalia at birth with variable degrees of virilization depending on the residual receptor activity. The spectrum ranges from predominantly female phenotype with mild clitoromegaly to predominantly male phenotype with hypospadias and micropenis.

riskFactors: X-linked recessive inheritance (AR gene mutation on Xq11-12)
Maternal carrier status (50% of 46,XY offspring will be affected)
Family history of AIS, primary amenorrhea, or inguinal hernias in phenotypic females
Consanguinity (increases likelihood of homozygous or compound heterozygous mutations)

left: Complete AIS: phenotypically female with 46,XY karyotype
Primary amenorrhea at expected pubertal age
Female external genitalia with blind-ending vaginal pouch
Absent uterus and fallopian tubes confirmed on imaging
right: Normal female breast development at puberty (aromatization of testosterone to estrogen)
Sparse or absent pubic and axillary hair (androgen receptor dysfunction)
Inguinal hernias containing testes in phenotypic female infant or child
Elevated testosterone and LH levels with female phenotype

medications.name: Estradiol (Oral or Transdermal)
medications.action: Replaces endogenous estrogen production lost after gonadectomy, maintaining bone mineral density, cardiovascular protection, breast tissue, and female secondary sexual characteristics

medications.name: Vaginal Dilators
medications.action: Gradual mechanical dilation of the blind-ending vaginal pouch to achieve functional vaginal length for sexual intercourse

pearls: Complete AIS presents as a phenotypically female individual with 46,XY karyotype, absent uterus, and inguinal or abdominal testes
Breast development occurs normally at puberty due to aromatization of testicular testosterone to estrogen
Gonadal malignancy risk in CAIS is approximately 2-5% and increases with age; gonadectomy timing is individualized
Partial AIS presents with ambiguous genitalia and requires careful evaluation for gender assignment
Psychosocial support is paramount: disclosure should be age-appropriate, patient-centered, and involve mental health professionals
diagnostics: Expect karyotype analysis (reveals 46,XY despite female phenotype)
Expect serum testosterone levels (normal male range or elevated due to lack of negative feedback)
Expect serum LH levels (elevated due to impaired androgen-mediated negative feedback on hypothalamus)
Expect pelvic ultrasound or MRI (absent uterus, may identify intra-abdominal or inguinal gonads)
Expect AMH/MIS levels (normal male range, confirming presence of functional testicular tissue)
Monitor estradiol levels (produced via aromatization of testosterone)
Expect genetic testing for AR gene mutations to confirm diagnosis and classify as complete vs partial
management: Coordinate multidisciplinary team approach (endocrinology, genetics, psychology, gynecology, surgery)
Support patient-centered gender identity: individuals with CAIS are raised female and typically identify as female
Manage gonadectomy decision-making: discuss timing, risks of gonadal malignancy vs benefits of endogenous hormone production
Initiate estrogen replacement therapy after gonadectomy to maintain bone density and cardiovascular health
Address vaginal length adequacy: vaginal dilation therapy is first-line; surgical vaginoplasty only if dilation fails
Provide fertility counseling: individuals with CAIS are infertile (no uterus, no oocytes)
Ensure ongoing bone density monitoring (DEXA scans) given gonadectomy and estrogen dependence
nursingActions: Assess patient and family understanding of the diagnosis and provide education using clear, sensitive language
Monitor for signs of gonadal malignancy (abdominal pain, palpable mass, hormonal changes) in patients who retain gonads
Educate patient on estrogen replacement therapy adherence and side effects after gonadectomy
Screen for psychological distress, depression, and anxiety related to diagnosis disclosure
Coordinate specialist referrals and ensure continuity of care across disciplines
Educate adolescent patients on the condition in an age-appropriate, supportive manner
Assess bone health indicators and reinforce importance of DEXA scan follow-up
Facilitate connection with peer support groups and advocacy organizations (e.g., AIS-DSD Support Group)

androgen-insensitivity-np.title: Androgen Insensitivity Syndrome: NP Advanced Molecular Endocrinology & DSD Management

cellular.title: Molecular Genetics of Androgen Receptor Dysfunction
cellular.content: Androgen Insensitivity Syndrome arises from loss-of-function mutations in the androgen receptor (AR) gene, located on the long arm of the X chromosome at Xq11-12. The AR gene spans over 90 kb and contains 8 exons encoding a 919-amino acid nuclear receptor protein. The AR protein belongs to the nuclear receptor superfamily and contains four functional domains: the N-terminal transactivation domain (NTD, exon 1), the DNA-binding domain (DBD, exons 2-3) containing two zinc finger motifs that bind androgen response elements (AREs) in target gene promoters, the hinge region (exon 4) containing the nuclear localization signal, and the C-terminal ligand-binding domain (LBD, exons 4-8) that binds testosterone and DHT. Over 1,000 distinct AR mutations have been catalogued in the Androgen Receptor Gene Mutations Database. These mutations can be classified by their functional consequence: complete loss of ligand binding (LBD missense mutations disrupting the hydrophobic ligand-binding pocket), impaired DNA binding (DBD mutations affecting zinc finger coordination or ARE recognition), defective nuclear translocation (hinge region mutations), and impaired coactivator recruitment (mutations affecting the AF-1 and AF-2 transactivation surfaces that interact with p160 coactivators such as SRC-1, SRC-2/TIF2, and SRC-3). In CAIS, the AR is completely non-functional, whereas in PAIS, residual receptor activity ranges from minimal to substantial, explaining the clinical spectrum. The molecular mechanism of normal androgen signaling involves testosterone diffusing into the cell and either binding directly to the AR or being converted to the more potent DHT by 5-alpha reductase type 2 in genital skin. Upon ligand binding, the AR undergoes conformational change, dissociates from heat shock proteins (HSP90, HSP70), homodimerizes, and translocates to the nucleus where it binds AREs and recruits the transcriptional machinery to activate androgen-responsive genes. In AIS, this signaling cascade is disrupted at various points depending on the specific mutation. The testosterone-to-estrogen conversion pathway remains intact: aromatase (CYP19A1) in peripheral tissues converts testosterone to estradiol, which acts through functional estrogen receptors to drive breast development and fat distribution. The hypothalamic-pituitary-gonadal axis is affected because testosterone cannot exert negative feedback on GnRH and LH secretion through the dysfunctional AR, resulting in elevated LH, elevated testosterone, and elevated estradiol levels. The ratio of testosterone to DHT and the absolute testosterone level can help distinguish AIS from 5-alpha reductase deficiency, another important cause of 46,XY DSD.

riskFactors: AR gene mutations on Xq11-12 (over 1,000 documented mutations)
X-linked recessive inheritance: carrier mothers have 50% chance of affected 46,XY offspring
De novo AR mutations (approximately 30% of CAIS cases have no family history)
Ligand-binding domain mutations (most common in CAIS, disrupting testosterone/DHT binding)
DNA-binding domain mutations (disrupting zinc finger-ARE interaction)
Polyglutamine repeat expansion in exon 1 (CAG repeat length inversely correlates with AR transactivation efficiency)
Family history of primary amenorrhea, inguinal hernias in phenotypic females, or infertility in 46,XY individuals

left: CAIS: 46,XY with completely female external genitalia and phenotype
Primary amenorrhea with normal breast development but absent uterus
Elevated testosterone in female range presentation (AR-mediated negative feedback failure)
Elevated LH with normal FSH (selective androgen feedback disruption)
right: PAIS: ambiguous genitalia ranging from predominantly female to predominantly male phenotype
Testosterone-to-DHT ratio normal (distinguishes from 5-alpha reductase deficiency where ratio >20:1)
Absent Wolffian structures (epididymis, vas deferens, seminal vesicles) due to local androgen insensitivity
Gonadal histopathology: Sertoli cell-only tubules, Leydig cell hyperplasia, risk of intratubular germ cell neoplasia

medications.name: Transdermal Estradiol
medications.action: Bypasses hepatic first-pass metabolism, providing physiologic estradiol levels to maintain bone mineral density, cardiovascular protection, breast tissue maintenance, and prevention of menopausal symptoms after gonadectomy

medications.name: Oral Estradiol
medications.action: Oral 17-beta estradiol undergoes hepatic first-pass metabolism, increasing sex hormone-binding globulin (SHBG) and coagulation factor production, providing systemic estrogen replacement

medications.name: Alendronate
medications.action: Inhibits farnesyl pyrophosphate synthase in the mevalonate pathway within osteoclasts, inducing osteoclast apoptosis and reducing bone resorption to prevent osteoporosis

pearls: AR gene mutations are classified by affected functional domain: LBD mutations (most common in CAIS) disrupt ligand binding, DBD mutations impair ARE recognition, and NTD mutations affect coactivator recruitment

Gonadal malignancy risk differs dramatically by AIS type: CAIS 2-5% lifetime (low, can delay gonadectomy to allow natural puberty) vs PAIS with intra-abdominal gonads up to 15-50% (high, early gonadectomy recommended)

Differentiate AIS from 5-alpha reductase deficiency: both present as 46,XY DSD but testosterone-to-DHT ratio is normal in AIS and markedly elevated (>20:1) in 5-ARD; 5-ARD patients virilize at puberty while CAIS patients do not
2006 Chicago Consensus replaced outdated terminology: 'testicular feminization' is no longer used; correct term is 'complete androgen insensitivity syndrome' classified under 46,XY DSD

Pharmacogenomic consideration: CAG repeat length in AR exon 1 inversely correlates with receptor transactivation efficiency; longer repeats associated with reduced androgen sensitivity even in the general population
diagnostics: Expect karyotype: 46,XY in phenotypically female individual
Expect serum testosterone: normal male or elevated range (lack of AR-mediated negative feedback)
Expect serum LH: elevated (impaired androgen negative feedback on hypothalamus)
Expect serum DHT and testosterone-to-DHT ratio (to differentiate from 5-alpha reductase deficiency: in AIS, ratio is normal; in 5-ARD, ratio is elevated >20:1)
Expect serum AMH/MIS: normal male range (confirms functional Sertoli cells and testicular tissue)
Expect serum estradiol: elevated for 46,XY due to peripheral aromatization
Expect pelvic MRI: absent uterus, may identify gonadal location (inguinal canal or intra-abdominal)
Expect AR gene sequencing to identify specific mutation and classify complete vs partial AIS
Expect gonadal biopsy histopathology if malignancy risk stratification needed (Sertoli cell nodules, intratubular germ cell neoplasia)
management: Order AR gene sequencing for definitive molecular diagnosis and mutation classification
Stratify gonadal malignancy risk: CAIS lifetime risk 2-5% (low), PAIS with intra-abdominal gonads up to 15-50% (high) - individualize gonadectomy timing
For CAIS: consider delaying gonadectomy until after puberty to allow spontaneous breast development and pubertal maturation from endogenous estrogen
Prescribe estrogen replacement post-gonadectomy: transdermal estradiol 50-100 mcg/day or oral estradiol 1-2 mg/day, titrated to maintain premenopausal estradiol levels
No progestogen required (no uterus present)
Order baseline and serial DEXA scans for bone mineral density monitoring (every 2 years post-gonadectomy)
Refer for vaginal dilation therapy (Frank method) when patient is psychologically ready; reserve McIndoe vaginoplasty or Vecchietti procedure for dilation failure
Classify within DSD framework: AIS falls under 46,XY DSD (disorders of androgen action) per 2006 Chicago Consensus/2016 Updated Guidelines
Provide genetic counseling: carrier testing for female relatives, preimplantation genetic diagnosis options for carrier mothers
nursingActions: Conduct comprehensive endocrine assessment including AR gene mutation classification and genotype-phenotype correlation
Monitor gonadal malignancy surveillance: annual tumor markers (AFP, beta-hCG, LDH) and imaging if gonads retained
Assess estrogen replacement therapy compliance and efficacy (symptom control, estradiol levels, bone density)
Evaluate for osteoporosis risk factors and ensure DEXA monitoring adherence
Screen for psychological comorbidities: depression, anxiety, body image concerns, sexual identity distress using validated instruments
Coordinate multidisciplinary DSD team care (endocrinology, genetics, gynecology, psychology, surgery, ethics)
Educate patient on fertility options and limitations: egg donation with gestational carrier is the only biological parenting option
Facilitate informed consent process for gonadectomy: discuss malignancy risk, hormone implications, irreversibility, and timing options
Document using patient-preferred terminology and ensure all team members are informed of communication preferences

newborn-assessment.title: Newborn Assessment and APGAR

cellular.title: Transition to Extrauterine Life
cellular.content: At birth, the newborn undergoes critical physiological transitions: lungs inflate and begin gas exchange, fetal circulatory shunts close (ductus arteriosus, foramen ovale), and thermoregulation becomes self-dependent. The APGAR score is assessed at 1 and 5 minutes: Appearance (skin color), Pulse (heart rate), Grimace (reflex irritability), Activity (muscle tone), and Respirations (respiratory effort). Each parameter scored 0-2, total 0-10. Score of 7-10 is normal, 4-6 requires intervention, <4 requires resuscitation.

left: Normal HR: 120-160 bpm
Normal RR: 30-60 breaths/min
Normal Weight: 2500-4000g (5.5-8.8 lbs)
right: Moro Reflex: startle response, arms extend
Rooting Reflex: turns toward stimulus on cheek
Sucking Reflex: sucks when palate stimulated
Babinski Reflex: toes fan out (normal in newborns)

medications.name: Erythromycin Eye Ointment
medications.action: Prevents ophthalmia neonatorum (gonorrheal/chlamydial conjunctivitis)

medications.name: Vitamin K (Phytonadione)
medications.action: Prevents hemorrhagic disease of the newborn

pearls: Palmar grasp reflex: infant grips finger placed in palm: disappears by 3-4 months
Acrocyanosis (blue hands/feet) is normal in first 24-48 hours; central cyanosis is NOT normal
Head circumference (33-35cm) should be measured and compared to chest circumference (30.5-33cm)
diagnostics: Perform APGAR scoring at 1 and 5 minutes
Monitor vital signs (HR, RR, temp) within first hour and per protocol
Measure and document weight, length, head circumference, chest circumference
Expect newborn metabolic screening (PKU, thyroid, sickle cell)
Monitor blood glucose if risk factors present (maternal diabetes, LGA/SGA)
Expect hearing screening before discharge
management: Dry newborn immediately and place skin-to-skin on mother
Apply cap to prevent heat loss from head
Administer Erythromycin eye ointment within 1 hour of birth
Administer Vitamin K IM within 1 hour of birth
Clamp umbilical cord and monitor for bleeding
Initiate breastfeeding within first hour if possible
nursingActions: Perform head-to-toe newborn assessment systematically
Verify ID bands match between mother and infant
Assess all newborn reflexes (Moro, rooting, sucking, Babinski, grasp)
Monitor for signs of respiratory distress (grunting, flaring, retractions)
Check umbilical cord stump for bleeding and signs of infection
Monitor first void and meconium passage
Educate parents on safe sleep practices (back to sleep, firm surface)

neonatal-thermoreg.title: Thermoregulation in Neonates

cellular.title: Brown Fat Metabolism
cellular.content: Neonates are highly vulnerable to cold stress due to their large body surface area relative to mass, thin skin, and limited subcutaneous fat. They cannot shiver; instead, they rely on non-shivering thermogenesis via brown fat (brown adipose tissue) located between the scapulae, around the kidneys, and along the great vessels. Brown fat metabolism generates heat through uncoupled oxidative phosphorylation but rapidly depletes oxygen and glucose reserves. Cold stress triggers a cascade: increased oxygen consumption, hypoglycemia, metabolic acidosis, and potentially pulmonary vasoconstriction with respiratory distress.

riskFactors: Premature birth (large body surface area to mass ratio)

left: Cool skin to touch (especially extremities)
Mottled or pale skin color
Lethargy and poor feeding
Weak cry and decreased activity
right: Hypoglycemia (jitteriness, tremors)
Tachypnea (increased O2 demand)
Metabolic Acidosis
Peripheral vasoconstriction (acrocyanosis)

medications.name: Dextrose 10% IV
medications.action: Corrects hypoglycemia secondary to cold stress

pearls: Neutral thermal environment (NTE): ambient temperature where the neonate expends the least energy to maintain normal body temp
Skin-to-skin contact (kangaroo care) is the most effective warming intervention for stable newborns
Dry the newborn immediately after birth and place a cap on the head: the head is the largest heat-loss surface
diagnostics: Monitor axillary temperature every 30-60 minutes during rewarming
Monitor blood glucose (cold stress depletes glycogen stores)
Expect ABG if respiratory distress develops (metabolic acidosis)
Expect CBC if sepsis suspected as cause of temperature instability
Monitor oxygen saturation (cold stress increases O2 consumption)
Monitor weight (for fluid status assessment)
management: Dry newborn immediately and remove wet linens
Place cap on head (largest heat-loss surface)
Initiate skin-to-skin contact (kangaroo care) for stable newborns
Use radiant warmer or incubator for unstable neonates
Warm slowly (0.5°C per hour) to prevent apnea
Maintain neutral thermal environment (NTE)
nursingActions: Monitor temperature every 15-30 minutes during active rewarming
Monitor blood glucose during and after cold stress episode
Assess for signs of cold stress cascade (tachypnea, hypoglycemia, acidosis)
Keep delivery room warm (minimum 77°F / 25°C)
Minimize exposure during assessments (uncover only what is needed)
Report persistent hypothermia (may indicate sepsis)
Educate parents on keeping infant warm (hats, swaddling, skin-to-skin)

neonatal-feeding.title: Neonatal Feeding

cellular.title: Nutritional Requirements
cellular.content: Neonates require approximately 100-120 kcal/kg/day for adequate growth. Breastmilk is the gold standard, providing optimal nutrition, immunoglobulins, and promoting GI maturation. Formula (iron-fortified, 20 kcal/oz) is an appropriate alternative. Newborns lose 5-7% of birth weight in the first week (up to 10% for breastfed infants) and should regain birth weight by 10-14 days. Feeding cues progress from early (rooting, lip smacking, hand-to-mouth) to late (crying: a late hunger cue). Adequate intake is assessed by monitoring output: 6-8 wet diapers and 3-4 stools per day by day 4-5 of life.

left: Early Feeding Cues: rooting, lip smacking
Hand-to-mouth movements
Sucking on fingers/fists
Quiet alert state
right: Inadequate Intake: <6 wet diapers/day
Weight loss >10% of birth weight
Persistent jaundice
Lethargy and poor suck

medications.name: Iron-Fortified Formula
medications.action: Provides complete nutrition when breastfeeding is not possible

pearls: Feed on demand, not on a rigid schedule: typically every 2-3 hours for breastfed, every 3-4 hours for formula-fed
Burp infant after every 1-2 oz (formula) or when switching breasts to prevent regurgitation
Weight gain of approximately 15-30g (0.5-1 oz) per day is expected after initial loss
diagnostics: Monitor daily weight (expect regain of birth weight by 10-14 days)
Count wet diapers (6-8/day by day 4-5 indicates adequate intake)
Count stools (3-4/day for breastfed, frequency varies for formula-fed)
Monitor blood glucose if at risk (preterm, SGA, LGA, maternal diabetes)
Expect bilirubin screening if jaundice develops or poor feeding
Assess feeding tolerance (vomiting, distension, residuals in preterm)
management: Feed on demand for breastfed (every 2-3 hours) and formula-fed (every 3-4 hours)
Burp infant after every 1-2 oz or when switching breasts
Mix formula exactly as directed (never dilute or concentrate)
Position upright for 30 minutes after feeding to reduce reflux
Provide small, frequent feedings for poor feeders
Supplement with expressed breast milk or formula if weight loss > 10%
nursingActions: Assess feeding readiness cues (rooting, lip smacking, hand-to-mouth)
Monitor weight gain pattern (expect 15-30g/day after initial loss)
Educate parents on feeding techniques and proper formula preparation
Report poor feeding, excessive spitting up, or weight loss > 10%
Assess for signs of aspiration during feeding (choking, cyanosis, desaturation)
Document feeding amounts, frequency, and tolerance at each feeding

neonatal-jaundice-basics.title: Neonatal Jaundice

cellular.title: Bilirubin Metabolism
cellular.content: Jaundice results from elevated unconjugated (indirect) bilirubin in the blood. Newborns are predisposed due to high RBC volume with shorter lifespan (70-90 days vs 120 days), immature liver conjugation enzymes (glucuronyl transferase), and increased enterohepatic circulation. Physiological jaundice appears after 24 hours of life and peaks at days 3-5, resolving by 1-2 weeks. Pathological jaundice appears within the first 24 hours and rises rapidly: causes include ABO/Rh incompatibility, sepsis, and excessive hemolysis. Bilirubin follows a cephalocaudal progression: face first, then trunk, then extremities as levels rise.

left: Yellowing of skin (cephalocaudal)
Scleral icterus (yellow eyes)
Dark amber urine
Lethargy and poor feeding
right: Bilirubin >12 mg/dL (physiological concern)
Jaundice within 24 hours (PATHOLOGICAL)
High-pitched cry (kernicterus risk)
Opisthotonus (severe kernicterus)

medications.name: Phototherapy (Bili Lights)
medications.action: Converts unconjugated bilirubin into water-soluble form for excretion via urine and stool

pearls: Jaundice in the first 24 hours is ALWAYS pathological: notify provider immediately
Risk factors: ABO incompatibility, Rh incompatibility, breastfeeding jaundice (insufficient intake), prematurity, bruising from birth trauma
Press on the skin over a bony prominence (forehead, sternum) and observe the underlying color for blanch jaundice assessment
diagnostics: Expect transcutaneous or serum bilirubin level to be ordered
Monitor total and direct bilirubin levels (unconjugated is concerning)
Expect blood type and Coombs test (for ABO/Rh incompatibility)
Monitor bilirubin trend on nomogram (Bhutani chart)
Expect CBC and reticulocyte count if hemolysis suspected
Assess jaundice progression (cephalocaudal: face → trunk → extremities)
management: Increase feeding frequency (every 2-3 hours) to promote bilirubin excretion
Initiate phototherapy as ordered (maximize skin exposure, diaper only)
Protect eyes with opaque shields during phototherapy
Supplement with formula if breastfeeding insufficient and bilirubin rising
Monitor hydration closely during phototherapy (increases insensible water loss)
Report bilirubin within first 24 hours of life immediately (always pathological)
nursingActions: Assess skin color using blanch technique over bony prominences
Monitor bilirubin levels and report rising trends
Ensure eye shields are properly positioned during phototherapy
Monitor temperature during phototherapy (risk of hyperthermia)
Assess hydration status (wet diapers, mucous membranes, fontanelle)
Monitor stool output (phototherapy increases loose green stools)
Educate parents on jaundice, phototherapy purpose, and signs to report

neonatal-respiratory-distress.title: Neonatal Respiratory Distress Syndrome

cellular.title: Surfactant Deficiency
cellular.content: Respiratory Distress Syndrome (RDS) results from insufficient pulmonary surfactant in premature lungs. Surfactant, produced by Type II pneumocytes (which do not mature until approximately 35 weeks gestation), reduces alveolar surface tension to prevent collapse during expiration.
