Without adequate surfactant, alveoli collapse with each breath (atelectasis), leading to decreased lung compliance, ventilation-perfusion (V/Q) mismatch, hypoxemia, and increased work of breathing. The resulting hypoxia and acidosis further damage the alveolar epithelium, creating a cycle of worsening respiratory failure. Hyaline membranes form from protein-rich exudate lining the alveoli.

left: Nasal flaring
Expiratory grunting (auto-PEEP attempt)
Intercostal and subcostal retractions
Tachypnea (respiratory rate >60/min)
right: Central cyanosis
Suprasternal retractions (severe)
Silverman-Anderson score for severity
Ground-glass appearance on chest X-ray

medications.name: Beractant (Survanta)
medications.action: Replaces deficient surfactant to reduce alveolar surface tension and prevent collapse

medications.name: Caffeine Citrate
medications.action: Stimulates respiratory center to prevent apnea of prematurity

medications.name: Dexamethasone (Prenatal)
medications.action: Given to the mother before preterm delivery to accelerate fetal lung maturity

pearls: Position prone or side-lying to optimize ventilation and oxygenation
Minimal stimulation and clustered care to reduce oxygen consumption
Monitor ABGs and pulse oximetry: target SpO2 88-95% in preterm infants to avoid retinopathy of prematurity
Prenatal betamethasone to the mother is the BEST prevention if preterm delivery is anticipated
diagnostics: Expect chest X-ray (ground-glass appearance, air bronchograms)
Monitor oxygen saturation continuously
Expect ABG to assess oxygenation and acid-base status
Monitor Silverman-Anderson score for respiratory distress severity
Expect blood glucose monitoring (respiratory distress increases glucose consumption)
Monitor respiratory rate and effort continuously
management: Position prone or side-lying to optimize ventilation
Maintain oxygen therapy as ordered (target SpO2 88-95% in preterm)
Minimize stimulation and cluster nursing care
Administer surfactant via endotracheal tube as prescribed
Maintain neutral thermal environment to reduce oxygen consumption
Maintain IV fluids as ordered (oral feeding often contraindicated)
nursingActions: Monitor respiratory rate, effort, and oxygen saturation continuously
Report signs of worsening distress (increasing retractions, grunting, cyanosis)
Assess for complications (pneumothorax: sudden deterioration, unequal chest rise)
Monitor blood glucose and temperature frequently
Minimize handling to reduce oxygen consumption
Suction only as needed (excessive suctioning increases oxygen demand)
Document respiratory assessment findings and interventions

neonatal-sepsis.title: Neonatal Sepsis Recognition

cellular.title: Immature Immune Response
cellular.content: Neonates have an immature immune system characterized by decreased neutrophil storage pools (limited ability to mount a sustained response), poor opsonization (decreased complement activity), low immunoglobulin levels (primarily relying on maternal IgG transferred in the third trimester: premature infants have even less), and immature T-cell function. Early-onset sepsis (<72 hours) results from vertical transmission during labor/delivery (Group B Streptococcus, E. coli are most common). Late-onset sepsis (>72 hours) is typically nosocomial, acquired from the hospital environment (Coagulase-negative Staph, Klebsiella, Candida), especially in infants with central lines or prolonged NICU stays.

left: Temperature instability (hypothermia MORE common than fever)
Poor feeding and weak suck
Lethargy and decreased activity
Apnea and bradycardia episodes
right: Glucose instability (hypo- or hyperglycemia)
Mottled or pale skin color
Abdominal distension
"Just not looking right": subtle behavioral changes

medications.name: Ampicillin + Gentamicin
medications.action: Broad-spectrum coverage for early-onset sepsis (GBS and E. coli)

medications.name: Vancomycin + Cefotaxime
medications.action: Coverage for late-onset nosocomial organisms (CoNS, gram-negatives)

pearls: ALWAYS obtain blood culture BEFORE starting antibiotics: but do NOT delay treatment waiting for results
GBS prophylaxis (intrapartum penicillin to GBS-positive mothers) has dramatically reduced early-onset sepsis
Hypothermia is a more common sign of neonatal sepsis than fever: do not dismiss low temperatures
CBC with differential: look for leukopenia, bandemia (left shift), and thrombocytopenia as sepsis markers
diagnostics: Expect blood culture to be drawn BEFORE antibiotics started
Expect CBC with differential (look for leukopenia, bandemia, thrombocytopenia)
Monitor temperature frequently (hypothermia is more common than fever)
Expect CRP levels to be ordered
Monitor blood glucose (instability is a sepsis sign)
Expect chest X-ray if respiratory symptoms present
management: Administer prescribed antibiotics promptly after cultures obtained
Maintain IV fluids as ordered
Maintain neutral thermal environment
Support respiratory function as needed (oxygen, positioning)
Maintain strict hand hygiene and infection control precautions
Prepare for possible lumbar puncture if meningitis suspected
nursingActions: Monitor vital signs frequently (temperature, HR, RR, BP)
Assess for subtle signs of sepsis (lethargy, poor feeding, color changes)
Report temperature instability (hypothermia OR fever) immediately
Monitor feeding tolerance and report poor feeding
Maintain strict aseptic technique with all invasive lines
Document behavioral changes ("not looking right" is a valid concern)
Monitor for apnea and bradycardia episodes

hyperbilirubinemia.title: Hyperbilirubinemia & Phototherapy

cellular.title: Bilirubin Metabolism
cellular.content: Bilirubin is produced from the breakdown of heme in red blood cells. Unconjugated (indirect) bilirubin is lipid-soluble and can cross the blood-brain barrier (BBB), depositing in the basal ganglia and brainstem nuclei to cause kernicterus (bilirubin encephalopathy): a devastating, irreversible neurological injury. The liver conjugates bilirubin (making it water-soluble) for excretion in bile and stool. Neonates are vulnerable because of: immature hepatic conjugation enzymes (glucuronyl transferase), increased RBC turnover (shorter RBC lifespan of 70-90 days vs 120 days in adults), and increased enterohepatic circulation (gut bacteria that break down bilirubin are not yet established).

left: Jaundice progressing cephalocaudal (face → trunk → extremities)
Scleral icterus (yellow discoloration of eyes)
Dark amber urine
Poor feeding and sleepiness
right: High-pitched cry (early kernicterus)
Hypotonia progressing to hypertonia
Opisthotonus (arched back: severe kernicterus)
Seizures and hearing loss (late kernicterus)

medications.name: Phototherapy
medications.action: Blue-green light isomerizes unconjugated bilirubin in the skin into water-soluble forms (lumirubin) that can be excreted without liver conjugation

medications.name: IVIG (Intravenous Immunoglobulin)
medications.action: Reduces hemolysis in isoimmune hemolytic disease (ABO/Rh incompatibility) by blocking antibody receptors

medications.name: Phenobarbital
medications.action: Induces hepatic glucuronyl transferase to increase bilirubin conjugation

pearls: ALWAYS protect eyes during phototherapy with opaque eye shields: check positioning frequently
Monitor for bronze baby syndrome (grayish-brown skin discoloration) which occurs with conjugated hyperbilirubinemia under phototherapy
Increase feeding frequency (every 2-3 hours) to promote bilirubin excretion through stool
Jaundice within the first 24 hours is ALWAYS pathological: never dismiss as physiological
diagnostics: Expect total and direct bilirubin levels to be ordered
Expect blood type and Direct Coombs test for hemolytic disease
Monitor bilirubin levels per Bhutani nomogram for treatment thresholds
Expect CBC and reticulocyte count if hemolysis suspected
Expect G6PD screening in at-risk populations
Monitor hydration status indicators (weight, wet diapers, fontanelle)
management: Initiate phototherapy as ordered (blue-green light, maximize skin exposure)
Protect eyes with opaque shields during phototherapy at all times
Increase feeding frequency to promote bilirubin excretion through stool
Remove all clothing except diaper during phototherapy
Monitor for bronze baby syndrome (contraindicated with conjugated hyperbilirubinemia)
Prepare for exchange transfusion if bilirubin reaches critical levels
nursingActions: Assess skin color systematically using blanch technique
Monitor bilirubin levels and report rising trends to provider
Ensure eye shields remain properly positioned (check frequently)
Monitor temperature during phototherapy (risk of overheating)
Assess hydration status (wet diapers, mucous membranes, fontanelle)
Increase fluid intake to compensate for insensible losses during phototherapy
Educate parents on jaundice progression, phototherapy, and when to seek care

nec-necrotizing.title: Necrotizing Enterocolitis (NEC)

cellular.title: Intestinal Necrosis Pathology
cellular.content: Necrotizing enterocolitis (NEC) is the most common GI emergency in premature neonates. It results from intestinal ischemia leading to mucosal necrosis and subsequent bacterial invasion of the bowel wall. The pathogenesis involves three key factors: an immature intestinal barrier (decreased mucus production, tight junction immaturity, and reduced IgA), abnormal bacterial colonization of the gut, and ischemia-reperfusion injury. Formula feeding significantly increases NEC risk compared to breast milk, which provides IgA, lactoferrin, oligosaccharides, and growth factors that protect the immature gut. Gas-producing bacteria invade the necrotic bowel wall, creating pneumatosis intestinalis (air in the intestinal wall): the pathognomonic radiographic finding.

left: Abdominal distension and tenderness
Bilious (green) emesis
Bloody or guaiac-positive stools
Feeding intolerance with increased residuals
right: Pneumatosis intestinalis on abdominal X-ray
Portal venous gas (ominous sign)
Pneumoperitoneum (perforation: surgical emergency)
Systemic signs: apnea, lethargy, temperature instability

medications.name: NPO + OG Decompression
medications.action: Complete bowel rest to halt intestinal workload and prevent further mucosal injury; OG tube decompresses distended bowel

medications.name: Ampicillin + Gentamicin + Metronidazole
medications.action: Broad-spectrum coverage: Ampicillin (gram-positive), Gentamicin (gram-negative), Metronidazole (anaerobes)

medications.name: TPN (Total Parenteral Nutrition)
medications.action: Provides complete nutrition (amino acids, dextrose, lipids, vitamins, minerals) while the bowel is resting

pearls: Breast milk is the single most PROTECTIVE factor against NEC: encourage maternal breast milk for all preterm infants
Bell Staging: Stage I (suspected: nonspecific signs), Stage II (definite: pneumatosis), Stage III (advanced: perforation, shock)
Surgical consult is required for pneumoperitoneum (free air) indicating bowel perforation
Serial abdominal X-rays and girth measurements are essential for monitoring progression
diagnostics: Expect serial abdominal X-rays (pneumatosis intestinalis is pathognomonic)
Monitor abdominal girth measurements every 4-8 hours
Expect CBC with differential and blood cultures
Monitor stool for occult blood (guaiac testing)
Expect CRP and serial metabolic panels
Monitor for portal venous gas on X-ray (ominous sign)
management: Maintain NPO status and bowel rest as ordered
Maintain NG/OG tube for decompression as ordered
Administer prescribed antibiotics (triple therapy)
Maintain TPN for nutrition during bowel rest
Use breast milk when feeds resume (most protective factor)
Prepare for surgical consultation if pneumoperitoneum develops
nursingActions: Measure and document abdominal girth at consistent location
Report abdominal distension, bilious emesis, or bloody stools immediately
Monitor vital signs for systemic deterioration (apnea, lethargy, temp instability)
Handle abdomen gently during assessments and diaper changes
Monitor for signs of perforation (sudden deterioration, abdominal rigidity)
Maintain strict aseptic technique with all lines and procedures
Promote breast milk feedings when restarting enteral nutrition

necrotizing-enterocolitis-basics-rpn.title: Necrotizing Enterocolitis (NEC) - RPN Basics

cellular.title: What Is NEC?
cellular.content: Necrotizing enterocolitis (NEC) is a serious condition in premature newborns where part of the intestinal wall becomes damaged and begins to die (necrosis). The immature gut lining in preterm infants is fragile and vulnerable to injury. When the bowel wall is damaged, bacteria can invade the tissue, causing inflammation, infection, and potentially perforation (a hole in the intestine). NEC is most common in very low birth weight infants and is the most common gastrointestinal emergency in the NICU. Formula-fed preterm infants are at significantly higher risk than those receiving breast milk. The RPN role focuses on early recognition of warning signs, accurate documentation, and prompt reporting to the RN or provider.

left: Abdominal distension (increasing girth)
Bilious (green) emesis or aspirates
Bloody or mucoid stools
Feeding intolerance with increased gastric residuals
right: Abdominal wall discoloration (redness or blue tinge)
Apnea and bradycardia episodes
Temperature instability (hypothermia)
Lethargy and decreased activity

medications.name: NPO + Gastric Decompression
medications.action: Complete bowel rest stops intestinal workload and prevents further damage to the injured gut

medications.name: IV Antibiotics (as prescribed)
medications.action: Treats bacterial invasion of the damaged bowel wall

pearls: Breast milk is the best protection against NEC: always encourage and support breast milk use for preterm infants
Increasing abdominal girth + feeding intolerance + bloody stools = report immediately (classic NEC triad)
Measure abdominal girth consistently at the same anatomical landmark each time
Gentle abdominal handling is critical: avoid unnecessary pressure on a distended abdomen
diagnostics: Monitor and document abdominal girth measurements as directed
Test stools for occult blood (guaiac test) as ordered
Report any bloody or mucoid stools immediately
Observe feeding tolerance and document residuals accurately
management: Maintain NPO (nothing by mouth) status as ordered
Maintain OG/NG tube for gastric decompression as directed
Administer IV fluids and TPN as prescribed
Use breast milk when enteral feeds are restarted per orders
Handle the abdomen very gently during care and diaper changes
nursingActions: Measure abdominal girth at the same location each time and document
Report abdominal distension, discoloration, or tenderness immediately
Report bilious (green) vomiting or bloody stools to the RN/provider
Monitor vital signs and report temperature instability, apnea, or lethargy
Maintain strict hand hygiene and aseptic technique with all lines
Document feeding amounts, residuals, and tolerance accurately

meconium-aspiration-basics-rpn.title: Meconium Aspiration Syndrome - RPN Basics

cellular.title: What Is Meconium Aspiration?
cellular.content: Meconium aspiration syndrome (MAS) occurs when a newborn breathes in (aspirates) amniotic fluid that contains meconium (the baby's first stool). Meconium is normally passed after birth, but when a fetus is stressed (usually from lack of oxygen), it may pass meconium before delivery. If the baby gasps or takes breaths while meconium is in the amniotic fluid, the thick, sticky substance can block the airways and irritate the lungs. This causes difficulty breathing, poor oxygen levels, and potentially serious complications. MAS is more common in post-term infants (born after 42 weeks) and in babies who experienced fetal distress during labor. The RPN role focuses on recognizing the signs of respiratory distress, supporting airway management, and promptly reporting changes to the RN or provider.

left: Meconium-stained amniotic fluid (green/yellow-green)
Respiratory distress shortly after birth
Tachypnea (breathing fast, > 60 breaths/min)
Nasal flaring and grunting
right: Chest retractions (intercostal, subcostal)
Cyanosis (blue discoloration)
Barrel-shaped chest (from air trapping)
Meconium staining visible on skin or nails

medications.name: Supplemental Oxygen
medications.action: Provides additional oxygen to maintain adequate saturation levels

medications.name: Exogenous Surfactant (if ordered)
medications.action: Replaces surfactant that has been inactivated by meconium in the lungs

pearls: Green or yellow-green amniotic fluid means meconium is present: alert the team immediately
Thick meconium carries a higher aspiration risk than thin meconium
Sudden respiratory deterioration in a MAS baby may mean pneumothorax: report immediately
Minimal handling and a quiet environment help reduce the infant's oxygen demand
Post-term infants are at highest risk for meconium aspiration
diagnostics: Monitor oxygen saturation continuously as directed
Observe and document respiratory rate, effort, and breath sounds
Report any worsening respiratory distress immediately
Note color changes (cyanosis, pallor, mottling)
management: Maintain airway positioning (slight neck extension, sniffing position)
Provide supplemental oxygen as prescribed
Minimize stimulation and unnecessary handling
Keep the infant warm (neutral thermal environment)
Support respiratory equipment setup as directed by the RN/RT
nursingActions: Monitor respiratory status continuously (rate, effort, oxygen saturation)
Report nasal flaring, grunting, retractions, or cyanosis immediately
Report any sudden worsening of breathing (may indicate pneumothorax)
Note meconium staining on skin, nails, or umbilical cord and document
Keep suctioning equipment at bedside and ready
Maintain a calm, quiet environment to minimize oxygen consumption
Document all respiratory assessments and interventions accurately

meconium-ileus-rpn.title: Meconium Ileus - RPN Basics

cellular.title: What Is Meconium Ileus?
cellular.content: Meconium ileus is a bowel obstruction in a newborn caused by abnormally thick, sticky meconium that becomes impacted in the ileum (the last part of the small intestine). In a healthy newborn, meconium is passed within the first 24-48 hours of life. In meconium ileus, the meconium is too thick and viscous to move through the bowel, creating a blockage. This condition is strongly associated with cystic fibrosis (CF): approximately 80-90% of infants with meconium ileus are later diagnosed with CF. The thick secretions characteristic of CF affect the pancreas and intestinal glands, producing abnormally viscous meconium. The RPN role focuses on recognizing the signs of bowel obstruction in the newborn, monitoring for complications, and reporting findings promptly.

riskFactors: Failure to pass meconium within 24-48 hours

left: Failure to pass meconium within 24-48 hours
Abdominal distension (progressive)
Bilious (green) vomiting
Palpable doughy mass in right lower abdomen
right: No meconium stool despite stimulation
Loops of distended bowel visible through thin abdominal wall
Feeding intolerance
Dehydration signs if obstruction is prolonged

medications.name: Gastrografin Enema (therapeutic)
medications.action: Draws fluid into the bowel to loosen and break up the impacted meconium plug

medications.name: IV Fluids
medications.action: Maintains hydration and electrolyte balance while infant is NPO

pearls: Failure to pass meconium within 24-48 hours is a red flag: report immediately
Meconium ileus is strongly linked to cystic fibrosis: sweat chloride testing will likely be ordered
Bilious (green) vomiting in a newborn always indicates possible bowel obstruction
Monitor hydration carefully if Gastrografin enema is given: it causes significant fluid shifts
diagnostics: Monitor and document passage of meconium (timing and characteristics)
Report failure to pass meconium within 24-48 hours of birth
Monitor abdominal girth and document any increase
Observe for signs of bowel obstruction (vomiting, distension)
management: Maintain NPO status as ordered for bowel obstruction
Maintain NG/OG tube for gastric decompression as directed
Administer IV fluids as prescribed
Assist with therapeutic enemas (Gastrografin) as ordered
Prepare infant and family for possible surgical consultation
nursingActions: Report failure to pass meconium within expected timeframe
Monitor and document abdominal girth at consistent landmarks
Report bilious vomiting or increasing abdominal distension
Monitor vital signs and report any signs of deterioration
Monitor hydration status (wet diapers, skin turgor, fontanelle)
Support parents and provide simple explanations about the condition
Document all findings accurately including stool output

meconium-ileus.title: Meconium Ileus - RN Level

cellular.title: Pathophysiology of Meconium Impaction
cellular.content: Meconium ileus is a neonatal intestinal obstruction caused by inspissated (thickened, dehydrated) meconium impacted in the terminal ileum. The pathophysiology is directly linked to abnormal exocrine gland secretions, most commonly due to cystic fibrosis (CF). In CF, mutations in the cystic fibrosis transmembrane conductance regulator (CFTR) gene produce dysfunctional chloride channels in epithelial cells. This results in decreased chloride and water secretion into the intestinal lumen, producing viscous, protein-rich, dehydrated meconium that cannot transit the bowel normally. The impacted meconium creates a mechanical obstruction in the distal ileum, with proximal bowel dilation, increased intraluminal pressure, and potential vascular compromise of the bowel wall. Meconium ileus is classified as simple (uncomplicated obstruction) or complex (with associated volvulus, atresia, perforation, or meconium peritonitis from prenatal perforation). Complex meconium ileus has significantly higher morbidity. The microcolon (unused colon) is a characteristic radiographic finding, as meconium never reached the large intestine. Approximately 80-90% of infants presenting with meconium ileus have underlying CF, making sweat chloride testing and genetic analysis essential components of the diagnostic workup. Pancreatic enzyme insufficiency contributes to the abnormal meconium composition through inadequate digestive enzyme secretion.

riskFactors: Autosomal recessive inheritance pattern for CF
Known CFTR gene mutations in family

left: Failure to pass meconium within 24-48 hours of birth
Progressive abdominal distension
Bilious (green) vomiting
Palpable doughy or rope-like mass in right lower quadrant
right: Ground-glass appearance on abdominal X-ray (Neuhauser sign)
Microcolon on contrast enema
Absence of air-fluid levels (meconium too thick)
Complex: volvulus, perforation, meconium peritonitis with calcifications

medications.name: Gastrografin (Diatrizoate) Enema
medications.action: Draws fluid into the intestinal lumen osmotically, hydrating and loosening inspissated meconium to relieve obstruction without surgery

medications.name: Pancrelipase (Creon)
medications.action: Replaces deficient pancreatic enzymes (lipase, protease, amylase) to improve fat and protein digestion in confirmed CF

medications.name: N-Acetylcysteine (Mucomyst)
medications.action: May be used as rectal irrigation or orally to thin viscous meconium secretions

pearls: Meconium ileus is the presenting sign of CF in approximately 15-20% of CF patients
Simple vs complex classification determines management: simple may respond to enema therapy, complex requires surgery
Ground-glass appearance (Neuhauser sign) on X-ray represents air mixed with thick meconium in the terminal ileum
Microcolon indicates the colon has never been used (meconium never reached it)
Aggressive hydration is critical during Gastrografin enema: the hyperosmolar solution can cause dangerous fluid shifts
All infants with meconium ileus require CF workup regardless of newborn screening results
diagnostics: Expect abdominal X-ray (dilated bowel loops, ground-glass appearance in right lower quadrant, absence of air-fluid levels)
Expect contrast enema showing microcolon (unused colon)
Expect sweat chloride test to evaluate for cystic fibrosis
Expect genetic testing for CFTR mutations
Monitor electrolytes closely (risk of dehydration and imbalance)
Expect CBC and inflammatory markers if perforation suspected
Monitor abdominal girth serially and document trends
management: Initiate NPO and gastric decompression with NG/OG tube
Administer IV fluids and correct electrolyte imbalances
Therapeutic Gastrografin enema under fluoroscopy (simple meconium ileus)
Surgical intervention for complex cases (volvulus, perforation, atresia)
Begin pancreatic enzyme replacement when enteral feeds resume (if CF confirmed)
Initiate multidisciplinary CF care team consultation
Gradual refeeding with breast milk or appropriate formula after obstruction resolves
nursingActions: Perform serial abdominal assessments (girth, auscultation, palpation)
Maintain accurate intake and output documentation
Monitor for signs of perforation (sudden distension, rigidity, hemodynamic instability)
Administer IV fluids and electrolyte replacements as prescribed
Monitor for dehydration during and after Gastrografin enema
Prepare family for sweat chloride testing and potential CF diagnosis
Coordinate care with neonatology, pediatric surgery, and CF team
Educate parents on CF implications and long-term management needs

meconium-ileus-np.title: Meconium Ileus - NP Advanced Pathophysiology

cellular.title: CFTR Molecular Pathology & Intestinal Obstruction Mechanisms
cellular.content:
Meconium ileus represents the earliest clinical manifestation of cystic fibrosis, occurring in approximately 15-20% of CF neonates. The molecular basis lies in mutations of the CFTR gene on chromosome 7q31.2, which encodes a cyclic AMP-regulated chloride channel expressed on the apical membrane of epithelial cells throughout the body. Over 2,000 CFTR mutations have been identified, classified into six functional classes: Class I (no protein synthesis, e.g., G542X), Class II (misfolding and proteasomal degradation, e.g., F508del  -  the most common mutation worldwide, present in approximately 70% of CF alleles), Class III (gating defects, e.g., G551D), Class IV (reduced conductance), Class V (reduced synthesis), and Class VI (accelerated turnover). The severity of meconium ileus correlates with the degree of CFTR dysfunction and resultant pancreatic insufficiency.

In the intestinal epithelium, dysfunctional CFTR channels impair chloride and bicarbonate secretion into the intestinal lumen. This reduces water content of luminal secretions, creating a dehydrated, viscous mucosal environment. Simultaneously, pancreatic duct CFTR dysfunction leads to inspissation of pancreatic secretions, progressive acinar destruction, and exocrine pancreatic insufficiency (present in 85-90% of CF patients). The absence of pancreatic digestive enzymes (lipase, trypsin, chymotrypsin) results in incomplete digestion of swallowed amniotic fluid proteins and cellular debris that compose meconium. The combination of dehydrated intestinal secretions and undigested protein produces abnormally viscous, tenacious meconium that impacts in the terminal ileum where the lumen narrows and water reabsorption is maximal.

Complex meconium ileus occurs when the obstruction leads to secondary complications: volvulus (twisting of the meconium-laden bowel on its mesentery, causing ischemia), intestinal atresia (from prenatal vascular compromise), perforation with meconium peritonitis (sterile chemical peritonitis from prenatal rupture, often producing characteristic peritoneal calcifications visible on X-ray), or giant meconium pseudocyst formation. The distinction between simple and complex meconium ileus is critical for management decisions.

The microcolon seen on contrast enema is a functional finding: the colon appears small because it has never been distended by intestinal contents. This differentiates meconium ileus from other causes of neonatal low obstruction such as Hirschsprung disease (which shows a transition zone) or meconium plug syndrome (which typically responds to simple rectal stimulation and is less commonly associated with CF).

riskFactors: Homozygous or compound heterozygous CFTR mutations (especially Class I-III)
Family history of CF or meconium ileus
Modifier genes affecting meconium ileus susceptibility (e.g., CFM1 locus on chromosome 19q13)

left: Failure to pass meconium within 24-48 hours
Progressive abdominal distension with visible bowel loops
Bilious emesis (obstruction proximal to ampulla of Vater)
Palpable doughy mass in RLQ (inspissated meconium)
right: Complex: signs of volvulus (acute abdomen, hemodynamic instability, bloody stools)
Peritoneal calcifications on X-ray (prenatal perforation and meconium peritonitis)
Microcolon on contrast study (functional disuse)
Distal intestinal obstruction syndrome (DIOS) in later life (recurrent meconium ileus equivalent)

medications.name: Gastrografin (Diatrizoate Meglumine/Sodium)
medications.action: Osmolarity of 1900 mOsm/L draws fluid into intestinal lumen, hydrating inspissated meconium and promoting evacuation through osmotic and detergent (wetting agent) effects

medications.name: Pancrelipase (Creon/Zenpep)
medications.action: Provides exogenous lipase, protease, and amylase to compensate for pancreatic exocrine insufficiency in CF

medications.name: Elexacaftor/Tezacaftor/Ivacaftor (Trikafta)
medications.action: Elexacaftor and tezacaftor are CFTR correctors (facilitate F508del protein folding and trafficking to cell surface); ivacaftor is a CFTR potentiator (increases open probability of the channel at the cell surface)

medications.name: Fat-Soluble Vitamins (A, D, E, K)
medications.action: Replaces fat-soluble vitamins malabsorbed due to pancreatic insufficiency and bile salt abnormalities in CF

pearls: F508del is the most common CFTR mutation worldwide: present in ~70% of CF alleles, with Class II dysfunction (protein misfolding and degradation)
Simple vs complex classification is the primary determinant of surgical vs non-surgical management
Neuhauser sign (ground-glass RLQ opacity) reflects air trapped within thick meconium: pathognomonic for meconium ileus
Peritoneal calcifications indicate prenatal perforation with sterile meconium peritonitis
All infants with meconium ileus require comprehensive CF evaluation regardless of newborn screening results: false negatives occur
Distal intestinal obstruction syndrome (DIOS) is the older-child equivalent of meconium ileus and may recur throughout life
CFTR modulator therapy has revolutionized CF care: genotype-directed therapy should be planned from diagnosis
Genetic counseling is essential: 25% recurrence risk with each pregnancy for carrier parents
diagnostics: Abdominal X-ray: dilated small bowel loops, ground-glass opacity (Neuhauser sign) in RLQ, absence of air-fluid levels, peritoneal calcifications if prenatal perforation occurred
Contrast enema: microcolon with possible reflux of contrast into dilated terminal ileum showing meconium pellets
Sweat chloride test (gold standard for CF diagnosis): elevated chloride > 60 mmol/L is diagnostic
CFTR genotyping for specific mutation identification (guides CFTR modulator eligibility)
Immunoreactive trypsinogen (IRT) from newborn screening (may be elevated)
Fecal elastase-1 to assess pancreatic exocrine function (low levels confirm insufficiency)
Prenatal ultrasound findings: echogenic bowel, bowel dilation, peritoneal calcifications
Serial electrolytes, renal function, and acid-base monitoring
management: Simple meconium ileus: therapeutic Gastrografin enema under fluoroscopic guidance with concurrent aggressive IV hydration
N-acetylcysteine rectal irrigations as adjunct mucolytic therapy
Complex meconium ileus: surgical exploration with enterotomy and irrigation, resection of atretic or necrotic segments, primary anastomosis or temporary enterostomy
Postoperative management: gradual refeeding, pancreatic enzyme replacement therapy (PERT) with all feeds
Initiate CFTR modulator therapy evaluation based on genotype (ivacaftor for gating mutations, elexacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor for F508del)
Fat-soluble vitamin supplementation (A, D, E, K) for pancreatic insufficiency
Pulmonary surveillance initiation and comprehensive CF center enrollment
Genetic counseling for parents regarding recurrence risk (25% for autosomal recessive)
nursingActions: Coordinate multidisciplinary CF team involvement (pulmonology, gastroenterology, nutrition, genetics, social work)
Monitor fluid and electrolyte status meticulously during Gastrografin therapy
Assess for postoperative complications (anastomotic leak, short bowel syndrome, adhesive obstruction)
Educate family on CF disease process, PERT administration, and long-term management
Initiate newborn pulmonary care protocols (airway clearance, infection prevention)
Coordinate sweat chloride testing and genetic counseling
Screen for associated CF complications (meconium peritonitis, distal intestinal obstruction syndrome in future)
Monitor nutritional status and growth parameters closely

fetal-monitoring-advanced.title: Fetal Monitoring: Late & Variable Decels

cellular.title: Uteroplacental Insufficiency
cellular.content:
Late Decelerations: Placental insufficiency causes fetal hypoxia after the contraction peak. Reflex vasoconstriction preserves blood flow to vital organs but indicates acidosis if persistent. 
Variable Decelerations: Umbilical cord compression obstructs blood flow, causing rapid drops in FHR.

left: Late: Decel starts after contraction peak
Variable: Abrupt 'V' or 'W' shape
Loss of Variability (Hypoxia)
Fetal Tachycardia (Infection/Hypoxia)
right: Interventions: Turn to Left Lateral
Stop Pitocin (Oxytocin)
IV Fluid Bolus
O2 via non-rebreather

medications.name: Terbutaline
medications.action: Relaxes uterus

medications.name: Magnesium Sulfate
medications.action: Vasodilation/CNS depression

pearls: VEAL CHOP: Variable-Cord, Early-Head, Accel-Ok, Late-Placenta
Amnioinfusion can relieve variable decels (cord compression)
Acidemia: Umbilical artery pH < 7.20 indicates fetal compromise
diagnostics: Order fetal scalp electrode for internal monitoring when external tracing inadequate
Order fetal scalp pH or lactate sampling for Category II tracings
Order biophysical profile (BPP) for antepartum surveillance
Order contraction stress test (CST) for high-risk pregnancies
Order umbilical artery Doppler velocimetry for IUGR assessment
Order amniocentesis for fetal lung maturity (L/S ratio, PG) if early delivery anticipated
Order cord blood gases immediately after delivery (arterial and venous)
Order Kleihauer-Betke test if fetal-maternal hemorrhage suspected
management: Prescribe Terbutaline 0.25mg SQ for acute tachysystole with non-reassuring FHR
Order amnioinfusion for recurrent variable decelerations (cord compression)
Discontinue Oxytocin immediately for late decelerations or tachysystole
Prescribe Magnesium Sulfate for neuroprotection if delivery < 32 weeks anticipated
Order emergent cesarean delivery for Category III tracings unresponsive to resuscitation
Prescribe Betamethasone 12mg IM x2 doses for fetal lung maturity (24-34 weeks)
Order operative vaginal delivery (vacuum/forceps) if appropriate and expedient
Consult maternal-fetal medicine for complex or refractory patterns
nursingActions: Interpret FHR tracings using NICHD terminology (baseline, variability, accelerations, decelerations)
Perform intrauterine resuscitation maneuvers (left lateral, O2, IV bolus, stop oxytocin)
Monitor contraction frequency, duration, and resting tone
Differentiate Category I, II, and III tracings and escalate appropriately
Perform amnioinfusion per protocol and monitor for complications
Calculate Bishop Score to assess cervical readiness for induction
Monitor for tachysystole (> 5 contractions in 10 minutes averaged over 30 min)
Coordinate with neonatal team for anticipated complications at delivery

prenatal-basics.title: Prenatal Care Essentials

cellular.title: Embryonic & Fetal Development
cellular.content: Prenatal care ensures optimal maternal and fetal outcomes through scheduled assessments. Visit schedule: Monthly until 28 weeks, biweekly 28-36 weeks, weekly 36 weeks until delivery. Key assessments include GTPAL (Gravida, Term, Preterm, Abortions, Living), fundal height (should equal gestational age in cm at 20-36 weeks), and fetal heart tones (normal 110-160 bpm). Folic acid (400-800 mcg daily) prevents neural tube defects and should begin before conception.

left: Vaginal Bleeding (any trimester)
Severe Persistent Headache
Epigastric Pain (liver distention)
Visual Changes (blurring, scotomata)
right: Decreased Fetal Movement (<10 kicks/2hrs)
Sudden Severe Edema (face/hands)
Rupture of Membranes before 37 weeks
Fever >100.4°F / Signs of Infection

medications.name: Prenatal Vitamins with Folic Acid
medications.action: Prevents neural tube defects and supports fetal development

medications.name: Rho(D) Immune Globulin (RhoGAM)
medications.action: Prevents Rh sensitization in Rh-negative mothers

pearls: GTPAL: Gravida = total pregnancies; Para = deliveries past 20 weeks (T-P-A-L)
Nagele's Rule: LMP first day minus 3 months plus 7 days = EDD
Fundal height at umbilicus = approximately 20 weeks gestation
diagnostics: Expect blood type and Rh factor to be drawn at first visit
Expect routine prenatal labs (CBC, urinalysis, rubella titer, STI screening)
Monitor fundal height at each visit after 20 weeks
Expect glucose challenge test at 24-28 weeks
Monitor fetal heart tones (normal 110-160 bpm) at each visit
Expect Group B Strep (GBS) culture at 35-37 weeks
management: Ensure folic acid supplementation (400-800 mcg daily)
Educate on warning signs to report (bleeding, headache, visual changes, decreased fetal movement)
Position in left lateral to improve placental perfusion
Maintain adequate nutrition and prenatal vitamin intake
Follow prescribed visit schedule (monthly, biweekly, then weekly)
Administer RhoGAM at 28 weeks for Rh-negative mothers as prescribed
nursingActions: Assess vital signs and weight at each prenatal visit
Monitor and document fundal height measurements
Assess fetal heart tones and document
Screen for domestic violence and depression at each visit
Educate on fetal kick counts (10 kicks in 2 hours)
Report any vaginal bleeding, headaches, or visual changes immediately
Document GTPAL accurately

labor-stages.title: Stages of Labor

cellular.title: Cervical Changes & Uterine Contractions
cellular.content: Labor progresses through four stages driven by coordinated uterine contractions. Stage 1: Cervical effacement and dilation (0-10cm) with three phases: Latent (0-6cm, mild contractions q5-30min), Active (6-8cm, moderate contractions q3-5min), and Transition (8-10cm, strong contractions q2-3min lasting 60-90sec). Stage 2: Complete dilation to delivery of the infant. Stage 3: Delivery of the placenta (5-30 minutes). Stage 4: Recovery period (first 1-2 hours postpartum) with close monitoring for hemorrhage.

left: Latent: Mild contractions, talkative
Active: Increased pain, focused breathing
Transition: Intense pressure, nausea, irritability
Stage 2: Urge to push, crowning
right: Stage 3: Gush of blood, cord lengthening
Stage 4: Fundal firmness check q15min
Abnormal: Late decelerations on FHR
Abnormal: Meconium-stained amniotic fluid

medications.name: Oxytocin (Pitocin)
medications.action: Stimulates uterine contractions for induction/augmentation

medications.name: Fentanyl/Epidural
medications.action: Pain management during labor

pearls: Transition phase is the shortest but most intense; provide continuous support
Monitor FHR continuously during active labor and Stage 2
Stage 4 hemorrhage risk highest: assess fundal tone and lochia q15min x1hr
diagnostics: Monitor cervical dilation and effacement during vaginal exams
Monitor fetal heart rate continuously during active labor
Monitor contraction pattern (frequency, duration, intensity)
Expect amniotic fluid assessment (color, amount, odor) at rupture
Monitor maternal vital signs every 30-60 minutes in labor
Expect GBS status to be verified for antibiotic prophylaxis
management: Provide comfort measures appropriate to each stage
Encourage ambulation and position changes during early labor
Maintain hydration with IV fluids or clear liquids as ordered
Support pushing efforts during Stage 2 (open glottis technique)
Monitor for hemorrhage during Stage 4 (fundal checks q15min)
Keep bladder empty to allow uterine descent
nursingActions: Assess cervical progress and document stage of labor
Monitor fetal heart rate pattern and report non-reassuring findings
Time contractions and document frequency, duration, and intensity
Provide emotional support and coaching throughout labor
Assess for signs of complications (prolapsed cord, meconium, hemorrhage)
Perform fundal assessment and lochia checks every 15 minutes in Stage 4
Monitor maternal vital signs and intake/output

postpartum-basics.title: Postpartum Assessment

cellular.title: Uterine Involution & Recovery
cellular.content: After delivery, the uterus undergoes involution: returning to its pre-pregnant state over approximately 6 weeks. The fundus should be firm, midline, and at or below the umbilicus immediately postpartum, descending approximately 1 cm (one fingerbreadth) per day. The BUBBLE-HE mnemonic guides systematic postpartum assessment: Breasts (engorgement, nipple integrity), Uterus (fundal height, firmness), Bladder (distention, voiding), Bowel (function, hemorrhoids), Lochia (type, amount, odor), Episiotomy/incision (REEDA: Redness, Edema, Ecchymosis, Discharge, Approximation), Homan sign (calf tenderness for DVT), and Emotions (bonding, mood changes, postpartum depression screening).

left: Boggy Uterus (atony)
Lochia Rubra (days 1-3, dark red)
Lochia Serosa (days 4-10, pink/brown)
Lochia Alba (day 10+, yellowish-white)
right: Fundus above umbilicus or deviated
Foul-smelling lochia (infection)
Heavy bleeding soaking pad in <1 hour
Positive Homan sign (DVT risk)

medications.name: Methylergonovine (Methergine)
medications.action: Sustained uterine contraction to control postpartum hemorrhage

medications.name: Oxytocin (Pitocin)
medications.action: Stimulates uterine contractions postpartum to prevent hemorrhage

pearls: A boggy uterus is the #1 cause of postpartum hemorrhage: massage the fundus firmly
Bladder distention displaces the uterus and prevents contraction
Lochia should never contain large clots (>golf ball size) or have a foul odor
diagnostics: Monitor hemoglobin and hematocrit if excessive blood loss
Monitor vital signs every 15 minutes x 1 hour, then per protocol
Assess fundal height and firmness at regular intervals
Monitor lochia (type, amount, odor, clots)
Monitor voiding pattern (bladder distention check)
Screen for postpartum depression using Edinburgh Postnatal Depression Scale
management: Massage boggy uterus firmly to promote contraction
Have patient void if bladder distended (displaces uterus)
Apply ice pack to perineum for comfort as ordered
Administer prescribed uterotonics for uterine atony
Encourage early ambulation with assistance for DVT prevention
Support breastfeeding initiation within first hour if desired
nursingActions: Perform BUBBLE-HE assessment systematically
Check fundal firmness and position every 15 minutes in Stage 4
Assess lochia for amount, color, and odor (foul odor = infection)
Monitor for signs of hemorrhage (boggy uterus, heavy bleeding, tachycardia)
Assess episiotomy/incision using REEDA scale
Assess for Homan sign and DVT risk factors
Screen for postpartum mood changes and provide emotional support

breastfeeding-basics.title: Breastfeeding and Lactation

cellular.title: Lactation Physiology
cellular.content: Prolactin stimulates milk production, while oxytocin triggers the let-down reflex (milk ejection). Colostrum is produced in the first 2-5 days: it is rich in immunoglobulins (IgA), protein, and provides passive immunity. Transitional milk appears days 5-14, followed by mature milk. Proper latch requires the infant's mouth to cover the entire areola (not just the nipple), with lips flanged outward, audible swallowing, and rhythmic suck-swallow-breathe pattern. Engorgement occurs when milk supply exceeds demand, causing breast swelling and pain.

left: Proper Latch: wide mouth, flanged lips
Audible swallowing during feeds
Softened breasts after feeding
Infant content between feedings
right: 6-8 wet diapers/day by day 4
3-4 yellow seedy stools/day (breastfed)
Steady weight gain after initial loss
Urate crystals (brick dust) normal first 2 days

medications.name: Lanolin Cream
medications.action: Protects and heals cracked nipples

pearls: Feed on demand, typically every 2-3 hours (8-12 times per day) in the newborn period
Contraindications to breastfeeding: HIV (in developed countries), active herpes on breast, certain medications, active substance abuse
Cabbage leaves and cold compresses help relieve engorgement; warm compresses before feeding aid let-down
diagnostics: Monitor infant weight daily (expect 5-7% loss in first week, regain by 10-14 days)
Count wet diapers (expect 6-8/day by day 4-5)
Count stools (expect 3-4 yellow seedy stools/day for breastfed infants)
Assess latch quality using breastfeeding assessment tools
Monitor for signs of dehydration (dry mucous membranes, sunken fontanelle)
Monitor bilirubin if jaundice develops
management: Feed on demand every 2-3 hours (8-12 times per day)
Apply lanolin cream to nipples after each feeding
Use warm compresses before feeding to promote let-down
Use cold compresses or cabbage leaves for engorgement
Position infant properly (tummy to tummy, nose to nipple)
Ensure infant mouth covers entire areola, not just nipple
nursingActions: Assess latch and positioning at each feeding
Assess for signs of adequate intake (swallowing sounds, softened breasts)
Monitor infant weight and output daily
Educate mother on feeding cues (rooting, lip smacking = early; crying = late)
Report cracked or bleeding nipples and assess for infection (mastitis)
Provide emotional support and lactation consultation referral
Educate on contraindications to breastfeeding (HIV, active herpes on breast)

placenta-previa-abruption.title: Placenta Previa vs Abruption

cellular.title: Placental Pathology
cellular.content: Placenta Previa occurs when the placenta abnormally implants over the cervical os (marginal, partial, or complete), obstructing the birth canal and causing painless hemorrhage as the cervix dilates. Placental Abruption is the premature separation of a normally implanted placenta from the uterine wall, causing retroplacental hemorrhage. The bleeding may be revealed (visible) or concealed (trapped behind the placenta), making abruption particularly dangerous as blood loss can be underestimated. Abruption triggers a cascade of coagulopathy and can lead to DIC.

riskFactors: Placenta previa: previous C-section, multiple gestation, advanced maternal age, smoking, prior previa
Abruption: hypertension (most common cause), cocaine use, trauma, short umbilical cord, rapid decompression of uterus

left: Painless bright red vaginal bleeding
Soft, non-tender, relaxed uterus
Normal FHR initially
Bleeding occurs in 3rd trimester
Diagnosed by ultrasound
right: Painful dark red vaginal bleeding
Rigid, board-like abdomen
Fetal distress with late decelerations
DIC risk with coagulopathy
Concealed hemorrhage possible

medications.name: Terbutaline
medications.action: Relaxes uterine smooth muscle to stop contractions

medications.name: Betamethasone
medications.action: Accelerates fetal lung maturity by stimulating surfactant production

medications.name: RhoGAM
medications.action: Prevents Rh sensitization in Rh-negative mothers exposed to Rh-positive fetal blood

pearls: NEVER perform a vaginal exam with suspected Placenta Previa: may trigger massive hemorrhage
Abruption is a surgical emergency if severe: prepare for emergent C-section
Monitor for Couvelaire uterus (blood infiltrates myometrium, uterus appears purple/bruised)
Abruption risk factors: HTN, cocaine use, abdominal trauma, prior abruption
diagnostics: Expect ultrasound for placenta previa diagnosis (location of placenta)
Monitor continuous fetal heart rate for signs of distress
Expect CBC, type and crossmatch, and coagulation studies to be ordered
Monitor Kleihauer-Betke test for fetal-maternal hemorrhage
Monitor maternal vital signs every 5-15 minutes during active bleeding
Expect fibrinogen level if DIC suspected (abruption)
management: NEVER perform vaginal exam with suspected placenta previa
Maintain bed rest and left lateral position as ordered
Maintain IV access with large-bore catheter
Prepare for emergent cesarean section if hemorrhage is severe
Administer Betamethasone as prescribed for fetal lung maturity if preterm
Administer RhoGAM as prescribed for Rh-negative mothers
nursingActions: Count and weigh pads to estimate blood loss
Monitor fetal heart rate continuously during bleeding episode
Report any increase in bleeding, pain, or change in fetal status immediately
Monitor maternal vital signs frequently for signs of shock
Maintain strict bed rest and calm environment
Prepare for emergency delivery and have neonatal team on standby
Monitor for signs of DIC in abruption (oozing from IV sites, petechiae)

postpartum-hemorrhage.title: Postpartum Hemorrhage

cellular.title: Hemorrhage Pathophysiology
cellular.content: Postpartum hemorrhage (PPH) is defined as blood loss >500mL after vaginal delivery or >1000mL after cesarean section. The 4 T's framework identifies the causes: Tone (uterine atony accounts for 80% of PPH: the uterus fails to contract and compress spiral arteries at the placental site), Tissue (retained placental fragments prevent uterine contraction), Trauma (lacerations of the cervix, vagina, or perineum), and Thrombin (coagulopathy disorders such as DIC, von Willebrand disease). The uterus receives 500-700mL/min of blood flow at term, making rapid intervention critical.

left: Boggy, soft uterus on palpation
Fundus above expected level
Steady trickle or gush of blood
Blood loss >500mL vaginal delivery
right: Tachycardia (early compensatory sign)
Hypotension (late sign: already significant loss)
Altered mental status
Cool, clammy skin with pallor

medications.name: Oxytocin (Pitocin)
medications.action: Stimulates uterine contraction to compress bleeding vessels

medications.name: Methylergonovine (Methergine)
medications.action: Sustained uterine contraction

medications.name: Carboprost (Hemabate)
medications.action: Powerful uterine contraction

medications.name: Misoprostol (Cytotec)
medications.action: Uterine contraction via multiple routes (PO, SL, PR)

pearls: Fundal massage is the FIRST intervention for uterine atony: firm, circular massage on the fundus
Quantitative blood loss (QBL) measurement is more accurate than visual estimation
Early activation of massive transfusion protocol (1:1:1 ratio of PRBCs:FFP:Platelets)
Two large-bore IVs and type & crossmatch should be done proactively for high-risk patients
diagnostics: Monitor estimated blood loss (weigh pads and chux)
Expect CBC and coagulation studies to be ordered
Expect type and crossmatch if transfusion anticipated
Monitor vital signs every 5-15 minutes during active hemorrhage
Assess fundal firmness and position frequently
Monitor urine output hourly via Foley catheter
management: Perform fundal massage immediately for boggy uterus (first-line intervention)
Have patient void (full bladder prevents uterine contraction)
Administer uterotonics as prescribed (Pitocin first-line)
Maintain two large-bore IV lines for fluid resuscitation
Apply bimanual compression if fundal massage ineffective
Prepare for blood transfusion as ordered
nursingActions: Massage fundus firmly using circular motions until firm
Monitor and document blood loss quantitatively (weigh pads)
Report continued bleeding or boggy uterus despite massage
Monitor vital signs every 5 minutes during active hemorrhage
Assess for signs of shock (tachycardia, hypotension, pallor, confusion)
Maintain IV access and administer fluids/blood products as ordered
Support patient emotionally and keep family informed

gestational-diabetes.title: Gestational Diabetes Management

cellular.title: Insulin Resistance Mechanism
cellular.content: During pregnancy, placental hormones (human placental lactogen/hPL, cortisol, progesterone, and growth hormone) create progressive insulin resistance to ensure adequate glucose delivery to the fetus. In gestational diabetes (GDM), maternal pancreatic beta cells cannot compensate with sufficient insulin production, leading to maternal hyperglycemia. Per the Pedersen hypothesis, maternal hyperglycemia crosses the placenta, stimulating fetal hyperinsulinemia, which drives excessive fetal growth (macrosomia), increased oxygen consumption, and potential fetal hypoxia. After delivery, the neonate loses the maternal glucose supply but continues producing excess insulin, causing neonatal hypoglycemia.

left: Fetal macrosomia (>4000g)
Polyhydramnios (excess amniotic fluid)
Recurrent urinary tract infections
Polyuria and polydipsia
right: Shoulder dystocia risk during delivery
Neonatal hypoglycemia after birth
Neonatal hyperbilirubinemia
Increased risk of preeclampsia

medications.name: Insulin
medications.action: Directly lowers blood glucose; does not cross the placenta

medications.name: Glyburide
medications.action: Stimulates pancreatic insulin secretion

medications.name: Metformin
medications.action: Decreases hepatic glucose production and increases insulin sensitivity

pearls: Screen ALL pregnant women at 24-28 weeks with 1-hour glucose challenge test (50g glucose, threshold ≥130-140 mg/dL)
If 1-hour screen is positive, confirm with 3-hour GTT (100g glucose)
Postpartum: recheck glucose at 6-12 weeks: 50% of GDM patients develop Type 2 DM within 10 years
Diet and exercise are FIRST-LINE treatment; pharmacotherapy added if glucose targets not met
diagnostics: Expect 1-hour glucose challenge test at 24-28 weeks (50g glucose)
Expect 3-hour GTT if 1-hour screen positive (100g glucose)
Monitor fasting and postprandial blood glucose levels
Expect ultrasound for fetal growth assessment
Expect non-stress test (NST) in third trimester for fetal well-being
Monitor HbA1c for overall glucose control
management: Follow prescribed diet (carbohydrate-controlled, 3 meals + 2-3 snacks)
Encourage regular moderate exercise as tolerated
Administer insulin as prescribed if diet and exercise are insufficient
Monitor blood glucose 4 times daily (fasting and 1-2 hours post-meal)
Follow prescribed monitoring schedule for fetal well-being
Prepare for neonatal glucose monitoring after delivery
nursingActions: Teach blood glucose self-monitoring technique
Educate on dietary modifications (carbohydrate counting, portion control)
Monitor for signs of hypoglycemia and hyperglycemia
Assess fetal movement patterns (kick counts)
Monitor for signs of complications (preeclampsia, macrosomia, polyhydramnios)
Educate on importance of postpartum glucose screening (6-12 weeks)
Report blood glucose consistently above target to provider

fetal-monitoring-rn.title: Fetal Heart Rate Monitoring

cellular.title: Autonomic Regulation
cellular.content: The fetal heart rate (FHR) is regulated by the autonomic nervous system. The sympathetic nervous system increases heart rate, while the parasympathetic (vagus nerve) decreases it. Normal baseline is 110-160 bpm. Variability reflects intact CNS function: absent (no fluctuation: ominous), minimal (<5 bpm), moderate (6-25 bpm: reassuring, indicates intact neurological pathway), and marked (>25 bpm). Accelerations (≥15 bpm above baseline for ≥15 seconds) are reassuring and indicate fetal well-being. Decelerations are classified as Early (head compression, mirrors contractions), Late (uteroplacental insufficiency, begins after contraction peak), and Variable (cord compression, abrupt onset/offset with varying shape).

left: Category I (Normal): baseline 110-160, moderate variability, no late/variable decels
Accelerations present (reactive NST)
Early decelerations (benign: head compression)
Moderate variability (most reassuring sign)
right: Category II (Indeterminate): minimal variability, recurrent variable decels
Category III (Abnormal): absent variability with recurrent late decels
Sinusoidal pattern (severe fetal anemia)
Prolonged deceleration >2 minutes

medications.name: Terbutaline
medications.action: Intrauterine resuscitation: relaxes uterus to improve placental perfusion

medications.name: Oxytocin (Pitocin)
medications.action: Stimulates uterine contractions for labor induction/augmentation

pearls: Late decelerations = uteroplacental insufficiency → turn off Pitocin, position left lateral, O2, IV fluid bolus
Variable decelerations = cord compression → reposition mother (knee-chest, Trendelenburg), amnioinfusion may help
Moderate variability is the SINGLE MOST reassuring sign of fetal well-being
Category III tracing requires immediate intervention: prepare for emergent delivery
diagnostics: Anticipate continuous electronic fetal monitoring for high-risk patients
Prepare for fetal scalp electrode placement if external monitoring inadequate
Anticipate non-stress test (NST) and biophysical profile (BPP) orders
Monitor contraction pattern with tocodynamometer
Prepare for umbilical cord blood gas collection at delivery
Anticipate amnioinfusion supplies for recurrent variable decelerations
management: Administer IV fluid bolus for non-reassuring FHR patterns as ordered
Discontinue Oxytocin infusion immediately for late decelerations
Administer Terbutaline as ordered for uterine tachysystole
Prepare for emergent cesarean delivery for Category III tracings
Position patient in left lateral, knee-chest, or Trendelenburg as appropriate
Administer oxygen via non-rebreather mask at 10 L/min for fetal distress
nursingActions: Interpret and document FHR baseline, variability, accelerations, and decelerations
Perform intrauterine resuscitation for non-reassuring patterns (reposition, O2, fluids, stop Pitocin)
Report Category II patterns that do not improve with interventions
Report Category III patterns immediately and prepare for delivery
Monitor contraction frequency and resting tone
Document all interventions and fetal responses
Communicate FHR findings using SBAR to provider

postpartum-depression-care.title: Postpartum Depression: Screening, Pathophysiology, and Management

cellular.title: Neurobiological and Hormonal Basis of Postpartum Mood Disorders
cellular.content:
Postpartum depression (PPD) is a major depressive episode that occurs within the first year after childbirth (most commonly onset within 4-6 weeks postpartum), affecting approximately 10-20% of new mothers and increasingly recognized in fathers/partners (8-10%). It is critically distinct from 'baby blues' (which affects 50-80% of new mothers, self-resolves within 2 weeks, and involves mild mood lability, tearfulness, and irritability) and from postpartum psychosis (a psychiatric emergency occurring in 0.1-0.2% of deliveries, typically within 48-72 hours, involving hallucinations, delusions, disorganized behavior, and high risk of infanticide/suicide).

The pathophysiology of PPD involves a complex interplay of hormonal, neurobiological, psychological, and social factors:

1. Hormonal cascade: During pregnancy, estrogen and progesterone levels rise progressively to 200-300x their non-pregnant levels by the third trimester. These hormones have profound effects on brain neurotransmitter systems: estrogen upregulates serotonin receptor expression, increases tryptophan hydroxylase (the rate-limiting enzyme in serotonin synthesis), and inhibits monoamine oxidase (the enzyme that breaks down serotonin, norepinephrine, and dopamine). Progesterone's metabolite, allopregnanolone, is a potent positive allosteric modulator of GABA-A receptors, producing anxiolytic and calming effects similar to benzodiazepines. After delivery of the placenta, estrogen and progesterone levels plummet by >90% within 48 hours: the most rapid, dramatic hormonal withdrawal the human body ever experiences. This sudden loss of serotonergic support and GABAergic modulation creates a neurochemical vulnerability to depression in susceptible individuals.

2. HPA axis dysregulation: Pregnancy involves progressive activation of the hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal (HPA) axis, with cortisol levels 2-3x normal by term (driven by placental CRH production). After delivery, the abrupt withdrawal of placental CRH suppresses the maternal HPA axis, which requires weeks to recalibrate. This transient adrenal insufficiency may contribute to fatigue, apathy, and stress intolerance. Women who develop PPD show persistent HPA axis dysregulation with blunted cortisol responses to stress.

3. Neuroinflammation: Pregnancy involves a shift from Th1 (pro-inflammatory) to Th2 (anti-inflammatory) immune responses to prevent rejection of the fetus. After delivery, the immune system rapidly rebalances with a pro-inflammatory rebound: elevated IL-6, IL-1β, TNF-α, and C-reactive protein. These inflammatory cytokines cross the blood-brain barrier and activate microglia (brain-resident immune cells), which release additional neuroinflammatory mediators. Neuroinflammation reduces serotonin availability by activating the kynurenine pathway (tryptophan is shunted toward kynurenine instead of serotonin synthesis) and damages hippocampal neurogenesis.

4. Oxytocin system disruption: Oxytocin, released during breastfeeding and skin-to-skin contact, normally promotes maternal bonding, reduces anxiety, and dampens HPA axis stress responses. Women with PPD show lower oxytocin levels and impaired oxytocin receptor sensitivity, disrupting the reward and bonding circuit between mother and infant.

5. Psychosocial factors: Sleep deprivation (profound: newborns wake every 2-3 hours), loss of autonomy and identity, relationship strain, social isolation, history of trauma or abuse, unplanned pregnancy, lack of partner support, and socioeconomic stress all interact with biological vulnerability to precipitate PPD.

Risk factors: Prior history of depression or PPD (strongest predictor: 25-50% recurrence risk), depression during pregnancy, family history of mood disorders, history of premenstrual dysphoric disorder (PMDD), inadequate social support, stressful life events during pregnancy or postpartum, complications during delivery, NICU admission of infant, difficulty breastfeeding, and thyroid dysfunction (screen TSH in all women with PPD symptoms).

left: Persistent depressed mood or pervasive sadness lasting >2 weeks postpartum (beyond the typical baby blues period)
Anhedonia: loss of interest or pleasure in activities that were previously enjoyed, including caring for the baby
Excessive guilt or feelings of worthlessness as a mother ('I'm a terrible mother,' 'My baby would be better off without me')
Difficulty bonding with the infant: may feel detached, ambivalent, or resentful toward the baby (this is deeply distressing to the mother and she may be reluctant to disclose it)
Sleep disturbance beyond what is expected with a newborn: inability to sleep even when the baby is sleeping (insomnia despite exhaustion is a hallmark)
Edinburgh Postnatal Depression Scale (EPDS) score ≥13 (validated screening tool: should be administered at 4-6 week postpartum visit, and again at 3 and 6 months)
right: Severe anxiety or panic attacks: may manifest as hypervigilance about the baby's safety (intrusive thoughts of harm coming to the baby, which are ego-dystonic and cause extreme distress)
Suicidal ideation: ALWAYS screen directly: 'Have you had thoughts of harming yourself?' Suicide is a leading cause of maternal death in the first year postpartum

Thoughts of harming the baby (must differentiate intrusive ego-dystonic thoughts of PPD: 'What if I accidentally drop the baby?': from ego-syntonic delusions of postpartum psychosis: 'God told me to sacrifice the baby')
Appetite changes (significant weight loss or gain beyond normal postpartum changes)
Psychomotor agitation or retardation (restless pacing or conversely, difficulty getting out of bed)
Labs: TSH (hypothyroidism mimics PPD: screen all symptomatic women), CBC (anemia worsens fatigue and mood), iron studies (iron deficiency common postpartum and contributes to depression)

medications.name: Sertraline (Zoloft)
medications.action: Sertraline selectively inhibits the serotonin transporter (SERT) protein on presynaptic neurons. SERT normally reuptakes serotonin (5-HT) from the synaptic cleft back into the presynaptic terminal, terminating serotonergic signaling. By blocking SERT, sertraline increases the concentration and duration of serotonin in the synaptic cleft, enhancing transmission at post-synaptic 5-HT receptors. Over 2-4 weeks, this leads to downstream neuroplastic changes: desensitization of presynaptic 5-HT1A autoreceptors (which normally provide negative feedback to reduce serotonin firing), increased BDNF (brain-derived neurotrophic factor) expression promoting hippocampal neurogenesis, and normalization of HPA axis hyperactivity. Sertraline is the first-line SSRI for PPD because it has the LOWEST transfer into breast milk of all SSRIs (relative infant dose <2%), making it the safest choice for breastfeeding mothers

medications.name: Brexanolone (Zulresso)
medications.action: Brexanolone is a synthetic form of allopregnanolone: the neurosteroid metabolite of progesterone that normally acts as a potent positive allosteric modulator of GABA-A receptors. During pregnancy, allopregnanolone levels rise progressively alongside progesterone, enhancing GABAergic inhibitory tone in the brain. After delivery, the abrupt loss of allopregnanolone contributes to PPD in susceptible women whose GABA-A receptors have become adapted to high neurosteroid levels and cannot quickly readjust to the withdrawal. Brexanolone restores this GABAergic signaling by binding to a unique site on the GABA-A receptor (the neurosteroid binding site, distinct from the benzodiazepine site), enhancing chloride ion conductance and restoring inhibitory neurotransmission. Unlike SSRIs which take weeks, brexanolone can produce rapid improvement in depressive symptoms within 24-48 hours because it directly addresses the hormonal mechanism of PPD

medications.name: Zuranolone (Zurzuvae)
medications.action: Zuranolone is an oral neuroactive steroid that, like brexanolone, acts as a positive allosteric modulator of GABA-A receptors at the neurosteroid binding site. It restores the GABAergic tone lost after the precipitous postpartum decline in allopregnanolone. The oral formulation makes it far more accessible than IV brexanolone. It is taken as a 50mg oral capsule once daily in the evening for 14 days. Clinical trials showed significant improvement in depressive symptoms as early as day 3, with sustained improvement through day 45 (4 weeks after completing the 14-day course). The rapid onset and short treatment course differentiate it dramatically from traditional antidepressants

pearls: Screen ALL postpartum patients with the Edinburgh Postnatal Depression Scale (EPDS) at the 4-6 week visit, 3-month visit, and 6-month visit. An EPDS score ≥10 warrants further assessment; ≥13 is consistent with probable depression. Question #10 specifically asks about self-harm: ANY positive response requires immediate safety assessment and intervention.

Differentiate baby blues vs. PPD vs. postpartum psychosis: Baby blues (onset days 3-5, resolves by 2 weeks, mild tearfulness and mood lability, NO treatment needed beyond support). PPD (onset typically 4-6 weeks, persists >2 weeks, significant functional impairment, requires treatment). Postpartum psychosis (onset 48-72 hours, psychiatric emergency: hallucinations, delusions, disorganized behavior, high risk of harm to self or infant: requires IMMEDIATE psychiatric hospitalization, mood stabilizers, and antipsychotics).

Breastfeeding and antidepressants: Untreated PPD is MORE harmful to the infant (impaired bonding, developmental delays, insecure attachment) than SSRI exposure through breast milk. Sertraline and paroxetine have the lowest breast milk transfer. The benefits of treating maternal depression overwhelmingly outweigh the minimal risks of infant SSRI exposure. NEVER advise a mother to choose between breastfeeding and medication: she can do both.

Safety planning is essential: Assess for suicidal/homicidal ideation at every visit. Ask directly: many mothers are afraid to disclose intrusive thoughts of harming their baby because they fear the baby will be taken away. Normalize these fears while emphasizing they are symptoms of illness, not character flaws. Ensure a safety plan is in place: remove access to lethal means, identify support persons, provide crisis numbers.

PPD affects the entire family. Partners report higher rates of depression (8-10%). Infants of mothers with untreated PPD show impaired cognitive and emotional development, insecure attachment, and behavioral problems. Treating maternal PPD improves outcomes for the entire family unit.

Non-pharmacologic interventions are first-line for mild PPD and adjunctive for moderate-severe: Cognitive behavioral therapy (CBT), interpersonal therapy (IPT), exercise (30 minutes of moderate aerobic activity 3-5x/week has antidepressant efficacy comparable to SSRIs for mild-moderate depression), social support groups, adequate sleep (enlist partner/family for nighttime feeding shifts), omega-3 fatty acid supplementation (EPA/DHA: modest evidence for mood improvement).
diagnostics: Edinburgh Postnatal Depression Scale (EPDS) screening
PHQ-9 depression screening
Assess for bonding difficulties with infant
Monitor for sleep and appetite disturbances
Assess for suicidal ideation or thoughts of harming infant
management: Administer prescribed antidepressants as ordered (SSRIs safe for breastfeeding)
Provide emotional support and therapeutic communication
Encourage involvement of support system
Report suicidal ideation or thoughts of infant harm immediately
Facilitate referral to mental health provider
nursingActions: Screen all postpartum patients using EPDS at follow-up visits
Assess maternal-infant bonding during interactions
Provide non-judgmental emotional support
Educate patient and family that PPD is a medical condition, not a character flaw
Report any suicidal ideation or thoughts of harming infant immediately
Encourage self-care activities (sleep, nutrition, exercise when cleared)

vitals-assessment.title: Vital Signs and Physical Assessment

cellular.title: Physiological Baselines
cellular.content: Vital signs reflect the body's basic physiological functions. Temperature, pulse, respirations, blood pressure, and oxygen saturation provide a window into cardiovascular, respiratory, and neurological status. Changes from baseline are more clinically significant than isolated readings.

left: Temperature (oral 36.5-37.5°C, rectal is most accurate, temporal for screening)
Pulse (radial most common, apical for cardiac meds, 60-100 adult normal)
right: Respirations (12-20 adult, count for full 30sec, note depth/pattern)
BP (auscultatory gap awareness, cuff size matters - too small reads high)
SpO2 (>95% normal, <90% critical)

medications.name: Acetaminophen
medications.action: Reduces fever

pearls: Always compare to baseline
Orthostatic vitals (lying/sitting/standing)
Pain is the 5th vital sign
Document and report trends
diagnostics: Expect baseline vital signs to be ordered on admission
Monitor trends in vital sign values over time
Expect pulse oximetry to be continuously monitored
Monitor orthostatic vital signs as ordered
Expect temperature monitoring route to be specified by provider
Monitor capillary blood glucose if indicated
management: Position patient appropriately for accurate readings
Ensure proper equipment sizing (BP cuff width)
Maintain calm environment before measurements
Follow facility protocol for vital sign frequency
Report abnormal values per facility parameters
Document trends and notify provider of significant changes
nursingActions: Obtain full set of vital signs at beginning of each shift
Report vital signs outside normal parameters to charge nurse/provider
Compare current values to patient baseline
Document vital signs accurately and timely
Assess pain as the 5th vital sign with each assessment
Recheck abnormal vital signs within 15-30 minutes
Perform orthostatic vital signs for patients with dizziness or syncope

wound-care-basics.title: Wound Care and Dressing Changes

cellular.title: Wound Healing Phases
cellular.content: Wound healing occurs in 4 phases: Hemostasis (platelet plug), Inflammation (WBCs migrate), Proliferation (granulation tissue, new vessels), and Remodeling (scar maturation over months). Healing by primary intention (surgical closure), secondary intention (open wound fills in), or tertiary intention (delayed closure).

left: Primary intention (approximated edges, minimal scarring)
Secondary intention (granulation tissue, larger scar)
Infection signs (redness, warmth, purulent drainage)
right: Wound measurement (length x width x depth)
Wound bed colors (red=granulation=good, yellow=slough, black=eschar=necrotic)
Drainage types (serous, sanguineous, serosanguineous, purulent)

medications.name: Normal Saline
medications.action: Gentle cleansing of wound bed

medications.name: Silver Sulfadiazine
medications.action: Prevents infection in burn wounds

medications.name: Hydrocolloid Dressing
medications.action: Promotes moist healing environment

pearls: Clean to dirty technique
Irrigate with normal saline at body temperature
Document size/color/drainage/odor
Adequate nutrition (protein/Vitamin C) essential for healing
diagnostics: Expect wound culture if infection suspected
Monitor wound measurements (length x width x depth) with each dressing change
Expect nutritional labs (albumin, prealbumin) to assess healing potential
Monitor WBC count for signs of infection
Expect wound photography per facility protocol
Monitor blood glucose in diabetic patients (affects healing)
management: Maintain sterile or clean technique per wound type and order
Follow prescribed dressing change schedule
Keep wound bed moist but surrounding skin dry
Ensure adequate nutrition (protein, Vitamin C, zinc)
Position patient to offload pressure from wound
Follow clean-to-dirty irrigation technique
nursingActions: Assess wound with each dressing change (size, bed color, drainage, odor)
Report signs of infection (redness, warmth, purulent drainage, odor)
Document wound assessment findings accurately
Maintain aseptic technique during dressing changes
Educate patient on wound care and signs of infection
Ensure adequate pain management before dressing changes
Report failure to progress in healing to provider

catheterization.title: Urinary Catheterization

cellular.title: Catheter-Associated Infection Risk
cellular.content: Catheterization provides drainage when normal voiding is impaired. Indwelling (Foley) catheters carry CAUTI risk - the #1 healthcare-associated infection. Biofilm formation on catheter surfaces begins within 24 hours. Strict sterile technique during insertion and closed system maintenance are essential to prevent infection.

left: Indications (urinary retention, accurate I&O, surgical, immobilization)
Insertion steps (sterile field, cleanse meatus, advance until urine flows, inflate balloon)
right: CAUTI signs (cloudy/foul urine, fever, suprapubic pain, new confusion in elderly)
Prevention (remove ASAP, hand hygiene, keep bag below bladder, maintain closed system)

medications.name: Lidocaine Gel
medications.action: Reduces discomfort during catheter insertion

medications.name: Phenazopyridine
medications.action: Relieves urinary tract discomfort

medications.name: Trimethoprim-Sulfamethoxazole
medications.action: Treats urinary tract infections including CAUTI

pearls: Always attempt least invasive method first (bladder scan)
Secure catheter to prevent traction injury
Monitor output (>30mL/hr adult)
Daily assessment for continued need
diagnostics: Expect urinalysis and culture if CAUTI suspected
Monitor urine output hourly (report < 30 mL/hr in adults)
Expect bladder scan before catheterization to confirm retention
Monitor urine color, clarity, and odor
Expect CBC if infection suspected
Monitor temperature for fever (CAUTI indicator)
management: Maintain strict sterile technique during insertion
Secure catheter to prevent traction injury
Keep drainage bag below bladder level at all times
Maintain closed drainage system (do not disconnect)
Remove catheter as soon as clinically indicated
Follow facility CAUTI prevention bundle
nursingActions: Assess for continued need for catheter daily
Monitor and document urine output every shift
Perform perineal hygiene and catheter care per protocol
Report signs of CAUTI (cloudy urine, fever, new confusion)
Ensure drainage bag is below bladder level and not touching floor
Empty drainage bag when 2/3 full using aseptic technique
Document catheter insertion date and size

ngtube-care.title: NG Tube Insertion and Care

cellular.title: Nasogastric Access
cellular.content: Nasogastric tubes serve two purposes: decompression (removing gas/fluid from stomach) and enteral feeding. Proper placement verification is critical to prevent aspiration. The tube passes through the nasopharynx, down the esophagus, and into the stomach. Misplacement into the lungs is the most dangerous complication.

left: Measurement (NEX - Nose to Ear to Xiphoid)
Insertion technique (head flexed forward, sip water to swallow)
Placement verification (pH <5.5 of aspirate, x-ray is gold standard)
right: Complications (aspiration, sinusitis, epistaxis, electrolyte imbalance from suctioning)
Decompression care (low intermittent suction, monitor output color/amount)
Feeding care (check residuals, elevate HOB 30-45 degrees)

medications.name: Metoclopramide
medications.action: Promotes gastric motility and emptying

medications.name: Omeprazole
medications.action: Reduces gastric acid for stress ulcer prophylaxis

medications.name: Ondansetron
medications.action: Blocks serotonin receptors to prevent nausea/vomiting

pearls: NEVER use auscultation alone to verify placement
Flush with 30mL water every 4 hours and before/after meds
Do not mix medications together through tube
Hold feedings if residual >250-500mL per facility policy
diagnostics: Expect chest/abdominal X-ray for placement verification (gold standard)
Monitor pH of gastric aspirate (should be < 5.5)
Monitor electrolytes (risk of imbalances from suctioning)
Expect I&O monitoring to be ordered
Monitor for metabolic alkalosis from loss of gastric acid
Expect abdominal assessment for distension and bowel sounds
management: Verify placement before each use (pH testing, X-ray)
Maintain HOB elevation 30-45 degrees during feedings
Flush tube with 30 mL water every 4 hours and before/after medications
Follow ordered suction settings (low intermittent for decompression)
Secure tube to nose to prevent migration
Do not mix medications together through the tube
nursingActions: Verify tube placement before each feeding or medication administration
Monitor and document output amount, color, and consistency
Assess nares for skin breakdown and reposition tape as needed
Report increased or bloody output immediately
Check gastric residual before intermittent feedings
Provide frequent oral and nasal care for comfort
