Report abdominal distension or vomiting around tube

iv-therapy.title: IV Therapy and Venipuncture

cellular.title: Vascular Access Principles
cellular.content: Intravenous therapy delivers fluids, medications, and blood products directly into the vascular system. Peripheral IV catheters access superficial veins (cephalic, basilic, median cubital). Osmolarity determines vein compatibility: isotonic (250-375 mOsm), hypertonic (>375 mOsm requires central access for prolonged use), hypotonic (<250 mOsm).

left: Site selection (distal to proximal, non-dominant hand, avoid areas of flexion/injury)
Gauge selection (18-20G for blood/surgery, 22-24G for meds/elderly)
Insertion technique (tourniquet, palpate, anchor, 15-30 degree angle)
right: Infiltration (cool/pale/swollen, stop infusion)
Phlebitis (red/warm/tender cord, remove IV)
Extravasation (vesicant leakage - antidote needed)
Air embolism (dyspnea/chest pain, left Trendelenburg)

medications.name: Heparin Flush
medications.action: Maintains patency of intermittent IV locks

medications.name: Normal Saline Flush
medications.action: Flushes IV line before and after medication administration

medications.name: Phytonadione (Vitamin K)
medications.action: Reverses warfarin anticoagulation

pearls: Change peripheral IV every 72-96 hours per policy
Always check the 5 Rights before administration
Calculate drip rates (gtt/min = volume x drop factor / time)
Never piggyback incompatible medications
diagnostics: Monitor IV site for signs of infiltration or phlebitis per facility protocol
Expect CBC and metabolic panel for patients on IV fluids
Monitor fluid balance with I&O documentation
Expect daily weight monitoring for patients on IV fluids
Monitor infusion rate and volume infused
Assess site patency by flushing before medication administration
management: Change peripheral IV every 72-96 hours per facility policy
Maintain aseptic technique during insertion and access
Select appropriate gauge and site for prescribed therapy
Calculate and verify drip rates before starting infusion
Use only compatible fluids and medications
Secure IV catheter to prevent dislodgement
nursingActions: Assess IV site at least every 1-2 hours for complications
Report signs of infiltration (swelling, coolness, pallor) immediately
Report signs of phlebitis (redness, warmth, tenderness along vein)
Document site assessment, patency, and any complications
Verify the 5 Rights before each IV medication administration
Flush IV line before and after medication with saline
Educate patient to report pain, swelling, or burning at IV site

blood-transfusion.title: Blood Transfusion Administration

cellular.title: Transfusion Immunology
cellular.content: Blood products replace oxygen-carrying capacity (pRBCs), clotting factors (FFP), or platelets. Transfusion reactions range from mild febrile to fatal hemolytic. ABO incompatibility triggers complement-mediated hemolysis destroying donor RBCs. Type and crossmatch ensures compatibility. Two-nurse verification at bedside is mandatory.

left: Pre-transfusion (consent, type & crossmatch, baseline vitals)
Two-nurse verification with patient ID and blood bank band
Large-bore IV access (18-20G preferred)
right: Acute Hemolytic (fever/chills/flank pain/dark urine, STOP immediately)
Febrile Non-Hemolytic (fever/chills, most common reaction)
Allergic (urticaria/itching, mild=antihistamine)
Anaphylactic (bronchospasm/hypotension)
TACO (fluid overload)
TRALI (acute respiratory distress within 6 hours)

medications.name: Diphenhydramine
medications.action: Pre-medication for clients with allergic transfusion history

medications.name: Epinephrine
medications.action: Emergency treatment for anaphylactic transfusion reaction

medications.name: Normal Saline (0.9%)
medications.action: Only compatible IV fluid with blood products

medications.name: Furosemide
medications.action: Treats fluid overload from TACO

pearls: Stay with client for first 15 minutes
Vitals at baseline/15min/30min/end
Use 0.9% NS only (never LR or D5W)
Infuse within 4 hours
Use blood tubing with 170-micron filter
diagnostics: Expect type and crossmatch to be ordered before transfusion
Monitor baseline vital signs before starting transfusion
Expect hemoglobin and hematocrit to be checked post-transfusion
Monitor for signs of transfusion reaction during infusion
Expect two-nurse verification of blood product at bedside
Monitor vital signs at baseline, 15 min, 30 min, and completion
management: Use only 0.9% Normal Saline with blood products (never LR or D5W)
Infuse blood products through appropriate blood tubing with 170-micron filter
Complete transfusion within 4 hours of leaving blood bank
Stay with patient for first 15 minutes of transfusion
STOP transfusion immediately if reaction occurs
Maintain large-bore IV access (18-20 gauge preferred)
nursingActions: Verify two forms of patient identification with two nurses at bedside
Obtain and document baseline vital signs before starting
Monitor vital signs at 15 min, 30 min, and at completion
Stay with patient for first 15 minutes and assess for reactions
Report any signs of reaction immediately (fever, chills, flank pain, hives)
If reaction occurs: STOP transfusion, maintain IV with NS, notify provider
Document blood product type, unit number, start/stop times, and patient response

chest-tube-mgmt.title: Chest Tube Management

cellular.title: Pleural Space Dynamics
cellular.content: Chest tubes drain air (pneumothorax) or fluid (hemothorax/pleural effusion) from the pleural space, restoring negative intrapleural pressure needed for lung expansion. The drainage system has three chambers: collection, water seal (prevents air re-entry), and suction control. Tidaling in the water seal chamber indicates proper function.

left: Indications (pneumothorax, hemothorax, pleural effusion, post-thoracic surgery)
Water seal chamber (tidaling normal, continuous bubbling = air leak)
Collection chamber (measure output hourly, >100mL/hr report)
right: Tension pneumothorax (tracheal deviation, absent breath sounds, JVD)
Accidental dislodgement (cover with sterile occlusive dressing taped on 3 sides)
Dependent loops (impairs drainage)
Clamping (generally contraindicated - risk of tension pneumo)

medications.name: Alteplase
medications.action: Breaks down fibrin in loculated pleural effusions

medications.name: Lidocaine
medications.action: Pain management at chest tube insertion site

medications.name: Ketorolac
medications.action: Systemic pain management for chest tube discomfort

pearls: Keep drainage system below chest level
Never clamp without an order
Encourage deep breathing and coughing
Milk tubing only if ordered (prevents clots)
Have petroleum gauze at bedside for emergencies
diagnostics: Expect chest X-ray after insertion to verify placement
Monitor drainage output hourly (report > 100 mL/hr)
Expect serial chest X-rays to assess lung re-expansion
Monitor water seal chamber for tidaling (normal) vs continuous bubbling (air leak)
Expect ABG or SpO2 monitoring for oxygenation status
Monitor respiratory rate and breath sounds bilaterally
management: Keep drainage system below chest level at all times
Maintain water seal with sterile water at 2 cm level
Encourage deep breathing and coughing exercises
Never clamp chest tube without a physician order
Keep petroleum gauze dressing at bedside for accidental dislodgement
Ensure all connections are securely taped
nursingActions: Assess respiratory status and breath sounds every 2-4 hours
Monitor and document drainage amount, color, and consistency hourly
Report sudden increase in drainage or change in character
Check for tidaling in water seal chamber (absence may indicate obstruction)
Report continuous bubbling in water seal (indicates air leak)
Maintain patient in semi-Fowler position to promote drainage
Have emergency supplies at bedside (petroleum gauze, sterile occlusive dressing)

trach-care.title: Tracheostomy Care and Suctioning

cellular.title: Bypassed Upper Airway
cellular.content: A tracheostomy bypasses the upper airway, eliminating the nose and mouth's natural warming, humidifying, and filtering functions. This increases infection risk and secretion production. The cuff, when inflated, seals the airway for mechanical ventilation but can cause tracheal necrosis if pressure exceeds capillary perfusion pressure (>25 cmH2O).

left: Routine care (clean inner cannula every 8h, change ties when soiled, clean stoma with NS and half-strength H2O2)
Suctioning technique (hyperoxygenate before, insert without suction, apply suction on withdrawal, max 10-15 seconds)
right: Accidental decannulation (stay calm, use obturator to reinsert, if <7 days old the stoma closes rapidly)
Mucus plug (inability to ventilate, suction or change inner cannula)
Tracheoinnominate fistula (massive bleed, apply digital pressure, hyperinflate cuff)

medications.name: Acetylcysteine (Mucomyst)
medications.action: Breaks down thick mucus secretions

medications.name: Normal Saline
medications.action: Humidification of inspired air

medications.name: Dexamethasone
medications.action: Reduces peristomal inflammation and airway edema

pearls: Keep spare trach and obturator at bedside ALWAYS
Suction only as needed (not on a schedule)
Cuff pressure <25 cmH2O
Communication alternatives (speaking valve, writing board)
Never cut gauze to place around trach (fraying risk)
diagnostics: Expect chest X-ray to verify tracheostomy placement
Monitor oxygen saturation continuously
Expect sputum culture if infection suspected
Monitor cuff pressure (maintain < 25 cmH2O)
Expect ABG if respiratory status changes
Monitor for signs of tracheal stenosis or granulation tissue
management: Clean inner cannula every 8 hours or per facility protocol
Maintain humidification of inspired air at all times
Suction only as needed (not on a schedule)
Keep spare tracheostomy tube and obturator at bedside
Maintain cuff pressure below 25 cmH2O to prevent tracheal necrosis
Change tracheostomy ties when soiled (two-person technique)
nursingActions: Assess respiratory status and oxygen saturation frequently
Hyperoxygenate before suctioning (10-15 seconds max per pass)
Report changes in secretion color, amount, or consistency
Maintain stoma site cleanliness with NS and half-strength H2O2
Provide alternative communication methods (writing board, speaking valve)
Report signs of accidental decannulation or mucus plugging immediately
Assess skin around stoma for breakdown or infection

osteoporosis-basics.title: Osteoporosis

cellular.title: Bone Density Loss
cellular.content: Imbalance between osteoblast bone formation and osteoclast bone resorption. Peak bone mass by age 30, then gradual loss. Estrogen decline accelerates loss in menopause.

left: Often silent until fracture
Loss of height
Kyphosis (dowager's hump)
right: DEXA scan T-score < -2.5
Fragility fractures (hip, wrist, vertebral)
Back pain from compression fractures

medications.name: Alendronate
medications.action: Inhibits osteoclast activity

medications.name: Calcium + Vitamin D
medications.action: Supports bone mineralization

pearls: Weight-bearing exercise strengthens bones
Fall prevention is critical
DEXA screening recommended for women >65
Avoid bending and twisting movements
diagnostics: Expect DEXA scan for bone density measurement
Monitor serum calcium and vitamin D levels
Expect alkaline phosphatase levels
Monitor height annually for loss (vertebral compression)
Expect X-ray if fracture suspected
Monitor renal function before bisphosphonate therapy
management: Administer bisphosphonate as prescribed (upright with full glass of water)
Ensure adequate calcium and vitamin D supplementation
Implement fall prevention measures
Encourage weight-bearing exercise
Avoid bending and twisting movements
Maintain safe home environment
nursingActions: Educate on proper bisphosphonate administration (upright 30 minutes, empty stomach)
Implement fall prevention measures (clear pathways, adequate lighting, non-slip footwear)
Report any new pain suggesting fracture (hip, wrist, back)
Encourage weight-bearing exercise as tolerated
Monitor calcium and vitamin D intake
Educate on avoidance of smoking and excessive alcohol

scoliosis-basics.title: Scoliosis

cellular.title: Spinal Curvature
cellular.content: Lateral curvature of the spine >10 degrees. Most common: adolescent idiopathic scoliosis (AIS), typically detected age 10-15 during growth spurts. More common in females. Adams forward bend test is screening method.

left: Asymmetric shoulders/hips
Rib hump on forward bending
right: Uneven waistline
One scapula more prominent

medications.name: NSAIDs
medications.action: Anti-inflammatory for associated pain

medications.name: Boston Brace
medications.action: Prevents curve progression for curves 25-45 degrees

pearls: School screening programs for early detection
Monitor closely during growth spurts
Brace compliance is key to preventing surgery
Post-surgical care: log-roll only, monitor neurological status
diagnostics: Expect Adams forward bend test for school screening
Monitor Cobb angle measurement on spinal X-ray
Expect full-length standing spinal X-ray
Monitor Risser sign for skeletal maturity assessment
Expect MRI if neurological symptoms or atypical curve
Monitor curve progression during growth spurts
management: Observe and monitor curves < 25 degrees every 6 months
Apply Boston brace for curves 25-45 degrees in growing adolescents
Ensure brace compliance 18-23 hours per day
Prepare for spinal fusion surgery if curve > 45-50 degrees
Provide emotional support for body image concerns
Encourage physical activity as tolerated
nursingActions: Assess brace fit and skin integrity under brace
Monitor compliance with brace wear schedule
Provide emotional support for adolescent body image concerns
Report pain, neurological changes, or respiratory compromise
Post-surgery: log-roll only, monitor neurovascular status
Educate on activity restrictions and follow-up schedule
Screen siblings for scoliosis (family history increases risk)

inhaled-spacers.title: Inhaled Spacers

cellular.title: Medication Delivery Optimization
cellular.content: Spacers are holding chambers that slow the velocity of aerosolized medication from MDIs, allowing more drug to reach the lungs instead of depositing in the oropharynx. Reduces systemic absorption and side effects (thrush from corticosteroids, tachycardia from bronchodilators).

riskFactors: History of oral thrush from inhaled corticosteroids

left: Shake MDI
Attach to spacer
Exhale fully before use
right: Press canister and inhale slowly 3-5 seconds
Hold breath 10 seconds
Wait 1 minute between puffs

medications.name: Albuterol
medications.action: Relaxes bronchial smooth muscle

medications.name: Fluticasone
medications.action: Reduces airway inflammation

pearls: Use spacer with all MDIs for optimal delivery
Clean spacer weekly with warm soapy water and air dry
Children and elderly benefit most from spacers
Spacer eliminates need for perfect hand-breath coordination
diagnostics: Observe patient inhaler technique
Monitor respiratory rate and effort
Assess breath sounds before and after treatment
Monitor oxygen saturation with pulse oximetry
Assess peak flow readings as ordered
Observe for medication side effects (tremor, tachycardia)
management: Ensure spacer is attached properly to MDI before use
Administer bronchodilator before corticosteroid as ordered
Provide mouth care after inhaled corticosteroid use
Report ineffective symptom relief to RN/provider
Clean spacer weekly per manufacturer instructions
Replace spacer as recommended
nursingActions: Teach and assess proper spacer technique at each visit
Remind patient to rinse mouth after corticosteroid use
Monitor heart rate after bronchodilator administration
Assess for oral thrush with each assessment
Document inhaler technique proficiency
Report signs of uncontrolled symptoms to RN/provider

meds-to-infants.title: Administering Medications to Infants

cellular.title: Pediatric Pharmacokinetics
cellular.content: Infants have immature liver/kidney function affecting drug metabolism and excretion. Body composition (higher water content, lower fat) affects drug distribution. Dosing is always weight-based (mg/kg). Oral route preferred when possible.

left: Use calibrated oral syringe (never household spoon)
Direct toward inner cheek in small amounts
right: Hold infant semi-upright
Allow time to swallow between pushes

medications.name: Acetaminophen
medications.action: Pain and fever relief

medications.name: Amoxicillin
medications.action: Most common pediatric antibiotic

pearls: Always verify weight-based dose calculation (double-check with another nurse)
Never mix medication in bottle - infant may not finish and won't get full dose
IM injections in vastus lateralis for infants
Always check for allergies with parents/caregivers
diagnostics: Verify current weight before dose calculation
Monitor vital signs before and after medication administration
Assess swallowing ability before oral administration
Monitor for adverse effects after administration
Assess pain level using age-appropriate scale
Monitor intake and output
management: Calculate dose based on current weight (mg/kg)
Use calibrated oral syringe for accurate measurement
Administer small amounts into inner cheek
Hold infant semi-upright during administration
Never add medication to infant formula bottle
Report adverse reactions to RN/provider immediately
nursingActions: Verify weight-based dose calculation with second nurse
Check allergies with parent/caregiver before administration
Use two patient identifiers before giving medication
Document medication administration accurately
Observe infant for 15-30 minutes after new medication
Educate caregivers on proper home medication administration
Report vomiting within 30 minutes of dose to RN/provider

cleansing-enemas.title: Cleansing Enemas

cellular.title: Bowel Evacuation Procedure
cellular.content: Enemas introduce fluid into the rectum/colon to stimulate peristalsis and evacuate fecal matter. Types: tap water (hypotonic - risk of fluid/electrolyte imbalance), normal saline (isotonic - safest), soap suds (irritant), fleet (hypertonic phosphate).

left: Left Sims position (left lateral, right knee flexed)
Insert tubing 3-4 inches in adults
right: Solution temperature: 105°F (40.5°C)
Hang bag 12-18 inches above anus

medications.name: Sodium Phosphate (Fleet)
medications.action: Pulls water into colon by osmotic action

medications.name: Mineral Oil
medications.action: Lubricates and softens hardened stool

pearls: Contraindicated in recent rectal surgery or low platelet count
Stop if client reports severe cramping
Never force the tube - risk of perforation
Document results: amount, color, consistency
diagnostics: Assess bowel sounds before administration
Monitor vital signs before and after procedure
Assess abdominal distension and tenderness
Document last bowel movement and characteristics
Monitor for cramping during administration
Assess results (amount, color, consistency)
management: Position patient in left Sims position
Warm solution to 105°F (40.5°C)
Insert tubing gently 3-4 inches in adults
Hang bag 12-18 inches above anus level
Administer solution slowly and stop if severe cramping occurs
Report absence of results to RN/provider
nursingActions: Verify order and type of enema prescribed
Explain procedure and provide privacy
Assist patient to left lateral (Sims) position
Monitor patient tolerance during administration
Document time, type, amount administered, and results
Report complications (bleeding, severe pain, no results) to RN/provider
Assist patient to bathroom or commode after procedure

wound-irrigation.title: Wound Irrigation

cellular.title: Mechanical Wound Cleansing
cellular.content: Wound irrigation mechanically removes debris, bacteria, and necrotic tissue from wound bed. The goal is to deliver enough pressure to cleanse without damaging healthy granulation tissue. Optimal pressure: 8-15 psi (achieved with 35mL syringe and 19-gauge needle/angiocatheter).

left: Contaminated wounds
Wounds with visible debris
right: Chronic wounds with biofilm
Irrigate from clean to dirty, top to bottom

medications.name: Normal Saline
medications.action: Isotonic solution for wound cleansing

medications.name: Wound Cleansers
medications.action: Surfactant-based wound cleansing

pearls: Use warm (body temperature) solution
Wear PPE including splash guard/gown
Document wound appearance before and after irrigation
Pulsatile lavage for deep wounds
diagnostics: Assess wound bed appearance before irrigation
Measure wound dimensions (length x width x depth)
Monitor wound drainage color, amount, and odor
Monitor vital signs if large wound irrigation
Assess periwound skin integrity
Document wound appearance before and after irrigation
management: Use normal saline at body temperature as standard irrigant
Use 35mL syringe with 19-gauge angiocatheter for optimal pressure (8-15 psi)
Irrigate from clean area to dirty area
Wear appropriate PPE including splash protection
Apply prescribed dressing after irrigation
Report signs of wound infection to RN/provider
nursingActions: Verify wound irrigation order and solution type
Pre-medicate for pain as ordered before procedure
Position patient to allow drainage away from wound
Use aseptic or sterile technique per wound type and order
Document wound assessment findings before and after procedure
Report changes in wound appearance or new drainage to RN/provider
Educate patient on wound care and infection signs

vac-dressing.title: VAC Dressing: Negative Pressure Wound Therapy

cellular.title: Wound Healing Physiology & Negative Pressure Mechanisms
cellular.content:
Negative Pressure Wound Therapy (NPWT), commonly known as wound VAC (Vacuum-Assisted Closure), applies controlled sub-atmospheric (negative) pressure to a wound bed through a sealed dressing system. Understanding how it works requires understanding wound healing at the cellular level.

Normal wound healing progresses through four overlapping phases: hemostasis (platelet plug and fibrin clot within minutes), inflammation (neutrophils arrive within 6 hours, macrophages by 24-48 hours to phagocytose debris and bacteria, releasing cytokines like TNF-α, IL-1, and IL-6 that recruit fibroblasts), proliferation (fibroblasts deposit collagen, endothelial cells form new capillaries via angiogenesis guided by VEGF, and keratinocytes migrate across the wound bed: this phase requires a moist, well-perfused wound bed), and remodeling (collagen cross-linking and maturation over months to years, reaching only ~80% of original tensile strength).

Chronic wounds stall in the inflammatory phase due to excessive matrix metalloproteinases (MMPs) that degrade new collagen faster than fibroblasts can deposit it, persistent bacterial biofilm that maintains inflammation, poor perfusion (diabetes, PVD), and excessive exudate that contains inflammatory cytokines and proteases that destroy growth factors.

NPWT addresses multiple barriers to healing simultaneously through several mechanisms:

1. Macrodeformation: The negative pressure physically draws wound edges together (reducing wound volume by 2-4% with each dressing change), decreasing the distance new tissue must cover.

2. Microdeformation: At the cellular level, the foam-tissue interface creates thousands of tiny mechanical forces on individual cells. This microstrain activates mechanotransduction pathways: integrins on cell membranes detect mechanical stretch and activate intracellular signaling cascades (FAK, ERK, MAPK pathways) that stimulate cell proliferation, migration, and extracellular matrix production. Fibroblasts under mechanical strain produce 2-3x more collagen.

3. Fluid removal: Continuous or intermittent suction removes excess wound exudate that contains inhibitory MMPs, inflammatory cytokines, and bacterial toxins. This shifts the wound microenvironment from inflammatory to proliferative.

4. Angiogenesis stimulation: The sub-atmospheric pressure creates a hypoxic gradient at the wound edges (relative to the wound center), and hypoxia is the most potent natural stimulus for VEGF release by macrophages, driving new blood vessel formation into the wound bed. Studies show NPWT increases local blood flow by 2-4x within the first few days.

5. Biofilm disruption: Mechanical removal of exudate disrupts bacterial biofilm and reduces bacterial load, though NPWT is NOT a substitute for surgical debridement of frankly infected wounds.

The system consists of: open-cell foam (black polyurethane: more aggressive granulation stimulation, or white polyvinyl alcohol: gentler, for painful wounds or those near tendons/organs), a non-adherent wound contact layer if needed, an adhesive transparent drape creating an airtight seal, a suction tube connecting to a portable pump, and a canister collecting wound fluid. Pressure is typically set at -75 to -125 mmHg, either continuous or intermittent (intermittent may stimulate more granulation but is less tolerated due to discomfort).

riskFactors: Skin grafts and flaps needing protection

left: Healthy granulation tissue forming (beefy red, bumpy appearance: indicates good angiogenesis and fibroblast activity)
Wound dimensions decreasing with serial measurements
Exudate decreasing in volume and transitioning from serosanguinous to serous
Intact seal with no air leaks (machine maintains consistent pressure)
Foam compressed evenly under dressing (confirms suction is distributed across wound bed)
right: Air leak (machine alarm, unable to maintain set pressure: inspect drape seal, especially around wound edges, bony prominences, skin folds, and tube connections)
Bleeding (frank red blood in tubing or canister: stop NPWT immediately, apply direct pressure, notify provider; risk factors include anticoagulant therapy, debridement injury to vessel)
Maceration of peri-wound skin (white, wrinkled skin around wound edges from moisture exposure: protect with skin barrier wipes or hydrocolloid strips)
Necrotic tissue in wound bed (NPWT is contraindicated over eschar or significant necrotic tissue: debride first)
Foul odor or purulent drainage (may indicate wound infection: obtain wound culture, may need to discontinue NPWT)

medications.name: Becaplermin (Regranex)
medications.action: Becaplermin is a recombinant form of human platelet-derived growth factor-BB (rhPDGF-BB). Normally, PDGF is released from platelet alpha-granules during hemostasis and from macrophages during inflammation. It binds to PDGF receptors (tyrosine kinase receptors) on fibroblasts, smooth muscle cells, and endothelial cells, activating the Ras-MAPK and PI3K-Akt signaling cascades. This promotes chemotaxis (migration of repair cells to the wound), mitogenesis (cell division of fibroblasts and endothelial cells), and extracellular matrix synthesis (collagen and glycosaminoglycan production). It is specifically FDA-approved for diabetic neuropathic foot ulcers that extend to subcutaneous tissue and have adequate blood supply

medications.name: Silver Sulfadiazine (Silvadene)
medications.action: Combines silver ions with sulfadiazine. Silver ions (Ag+) bind to bacterial cell wall proteins and DNA, disrupting membrane integrity and inhibiting DNA replication across gram-positive, gram-negative, and fungal organisms. Sulfadiazine provides additional bacteriostatic effect by competitively inhibiting para-aminobenzoic acid (PABA) in the folate synthesis pathway. The dual mechanism provides broad-spectrum antimicrobial coverage for wound colonization. Used on wounds at risk for infection before or between NPWT applications

medications.name: Collagenase (Santyl)
medications.action: Collagenase is a protease derived from Clostridium histolyticum that selectively breaks down denatured collagen in necrotic tissue (slough and eschar) while preserving viable collagen in healthy tissue. It cleaves collagen at specific peptide bonds, breaking necrotic tissue into small fragments that can be removed by the body's own phagocytic cells. This selective enzymatic debridement prepares the wound bed for NPWT by removing the necrotic tissue that NPWT cannot address

pearls: Foam MUST be cut to fit the wound precisely: it should fill the wound cavity without overlapping onto intact peri-wound skin (causes maceration and pressure injury on healthy tissue). Count the number of foam pieces placed in the wound and document: ALL pieces must be accounted for at each dressing change.

Maintain an airtight seal. Air leaks are the most common issue. Troubleshooting: ensure skin is clean and dry before applying drape, use skin prep/barrier wipes to improve adhesion, apply drape wrinkle-free, use additional drape strips to seal around tubes and irregular wound shapes. A small pinhole in the drape will prevent the machine from reaching target pressure.

Dressing changes are typically every 48-72 hours (Monday-Wednesday-Friday schedule is common). For infected wounds, change every 24 hours. Moisten foam with normal saline 15-30 minutes before removal to prevent it from adhering to granulation tissue (pulling off dry foam tears new granulation and is extremely painful).

Contraindications: malignancy in or near the wound, untreated osteomyelitis, non-enteric and unexplored fistulas, necrotic tissue with eschar (debride first), exposed blood vessels or organs, active uncontrolled bleeding. Anticoagulated patients require extra caution.

The VAC pump must remain on for a minimum of 22 hours per day for therapeutic benefit. Brief disconnections for bathing or ambulation are acceptable but should be minimized. Clamp the tubing before disconnecting to maintain wound bed stability.

Assess wound at each dressing change: measure dimensions (length x width x depth), describe tissue type (% granulation vs. slough vs. necrotic), note drainage color/amount/odor, inspect peri-wound skin. Document and photograph for comparison.
diagnostics: Wound measurement at each dressing change (length x width x depth)
Tissue assessment (granulation, slough, necrotic tissue percentage)
Drainage assessment (color, amount, odor)
Peri-wound skin assessment
Monitor for signs of wound infection
management: Maintain prescribed negative pressure setting (typically -125 mmHg)
Ensure airtight seal of drape over wound and foam
Change dressing every 48-72 hours (every 24 hours if infected)
Keep VAC pump on minimum 22 hours/day
Moisten foam with NS 15-30 minutes before removal to prevent pain
nursingActions: Assess wound at each dressing change and document findings
Count foam pieces placed in wound and verify all removed at dressing change
Monitor for airtight seal and troubleshoot air leaks
Report changes in drainage amount, color, or odor
Ensure VAC pump remains on and alarms are addressed promptly
Educate patient on importance of continuous therapy and reporting machine alarms

hemodialysis-care.title: Hemodialysis: Principles, Procedure, and Nursing Care

cellular.title: Renal Failure Physiology & Dialysis Principles
cellular.content:
Hemodialysis is a life-sustaining renal replacement therapy that performs the critical functions of failed kidneys. To understand hemodialysis, one must first understand what the kidneys do at the cellular level.

Healthy kidneys contain approximately 1 million nephrons each. Each nephron consists of a glomerulus (a capillary tuft that filters blood under hydrostatic pressure: producing ~180 liters of filtrate per day) and a tubular system (proximal tubule, loop of Henle, distal tubule, collecting duct) that selectively reabsorbs 99% of filtrate and secretes additional waste products. The nephron performs four critical functions: (1) filtration of metabolic waste products (urea from protein metabolism, creatinine from muscle metabolism, uric acid from nucleic acid metabolism), (2) regulation of fluid balance (adjusting water reabsorption via ADH-mediated aquaporin-2 channels in the collecting duct), (3) electrolyte homeostasis (Na+/K+ balance via aldosterone at the distal tubule, calcium/phosphorus balance via PTH and vitamin D), and (4) acid-base balance (bicarbonate reabsorption in the proximal tubule, hydrogen ion secretion, ammonia buffer generation).

When kidney function declines below ~10-15% (GFR <15 mL/min: Stage 5 CKD, also called end-stage renal disease/ESRD), these functions fail catastrophically. Uremic toxins accumulate, causing uremic syndrome: encephalopathy (confusion, asterixis: 'liver flap'), pericarditis (uremic pericarditis: friction rub, can progress to tamponade), peripheral neuropathy, platelet dysfunction (bleeding tendency), nausea/vomiting, pruritus (phosphate crystal deposition in skin), and uremic frost (rare: urea crystals on skin in extreme cases). Fluid overload causes pulmonary edema. Hyperkalemia causes fatal cardiac arrhythmias (peaked T waves → widened QRS → sine wave → asystole). Metabolic acidosis develops as the kidneys cannot regenerate bicarbonate.

Hemodialysis replaces these functions using three physical principles:

1. Diffusion: Movement of solutes across the semipermeable dialysis membrane from an area of higher concentration to lower concentration. Uremic toxins (urea, creatinine, potassium, phosphorus) are in high concentration in the patient's blood and low/absent in the dialysate, so they diffuse across the membrane into the dialysate. Conversely, bicarbonate is in higher concentration in the dialysate and diffuses INTO the patient's blood, correcting metabolic acidosis. The dialysate composition is carefully formulated: it contains sodium, chloride, calcium, magnesium, glucose, and bicarbonate buffer at physiologic concentrations but NO urea, creatinine, or potassium (or low potassium, typically 2 mEq/L).

2. Ultrafiltration: Removal of excess water by applying hydrostatic pressure across the dialysis membrane (transmembrane pressure). The machine creates a pressure gradient that pushes water from the blood compartment through the membrane into the dialysate compartment. The amount of fluid removed is programmed based on the patient's 'dry weight' (ideal weight without excess fluid). Typical fluid removal: 2-4 liters per session.

3. Convection: Water movement across the membrane drags dissolved solutes with it (solvent drag), removing 'middle molecules' (larger toxins like β2-microglobulin, MW ~11,800 Da) that diffusion alone cannot efficiently clear.

The dialysis membrane (dialyzer, also called the 'artificial kidney') contains thousands of hollow fiber capillaries made of synthetic biocompatible membranes (polysulfone, polyethersulfone). Blood flows inside the fibers, dialysate flows outside (countercurrent flow for maximum diffusion efficiency), and solute/water exchange occurs across the fiber walls. A standard hemodialysis session runs 3-4 hours, 3 times per week.

Vascular access is the 'lifeline' for hemodialysis patients. Three types exist in order of preference: (1) AV fistula (surgical anastomosis of artery to vein: lowest infection and thrombosis rates, longest lifespan, requires 2-3 months to mature), (2) AV graft (synthetic tube connecting artery to vein: higher infection and stenosis rates, can be used in 2-3 weeks), (3) Central venous catheter/CVC (tunneled catheter in internal jugular or femoral vein: highest infection rate, used when urgent dialysis is needed or fistula/graft is not available).

left: Adequate vascular access function: Thrill palpable (buzzing vibration felt over the AV fistula/graft: indicates turbulent arterial blood flow through the anastomosis) and bruit audible with stethoscope (whooshing sound)
Target fluid removal achieved (patient approaches 'dry weight': the weight at which the patient is normotensive without edema or pulmonary congestion)
Improvement in uremic symptoms during/after dialysis: improved mentation, decreased nausea, reduced pruritus
Labs trending toward normal: BUN and creatinine decreasing post-dialysis, potassium normalizing (3.5-5.0 mEq/L), bicarbonate correcting (22-26 mEq/L), phosphorus decreasing
right: Intradialytic hypotension (most common complication: occurs in 20-30% of sessions; caused by rapid fluid removal exceeding plasma refill rate from interstitial space; symptoms: dizziness, nausea, cramping, diaphoresis; treatment: Trendelenburg, NS bolus 100-250mL, decrease ultrafiltration rate)

Dialysis disequilibrium syndrome (rapid removal of urea from blood creates an osmotic gradient: urea in the brain remains high, water shifts into brain tissue → cerebral edema; symptoms: headache, nausea, seizures, altered LOC; prevented by shorter, gentler first sessions)
Muscle cramps (rapid fluid and electrolyte shifts: especially sodium and potassium changes during dialysis; treat with NS bolus, hypertonic saline 23.4%, adjust dialysate composition)

Air embolism (air enters the blood circuit through loose connections: emergency: clamp venous line, position patient LEFT side with HEAD DOWN (left lateral Trendelenburg: air rises to right ventricle apex away from pulmonary outflow tract), administer 100% O2)

Labs to monitor: Pre- and post-dialysis BUN (calculate Kt/V for dialysis adequacy: target >1.2), serum potassium (pre-dialysis K+ >6.0 is dangerous: peaked T waves on ECG), serum phosphorus (target <5.5 mg/dL), calcium (corrected for albumin), hemoglobin/hematocrit (CKD anemia: EPO deficiency), PTH (secondary hyperparathyroidism: target 150-600 pg/mL for dialysis patients), albumin (nutritional status: target >4.0 g/dL)

medications.name: Epoetin Alfa (Epogen/Procrit)
medications.action: Recombinant human erythropoietin (EPO). Healthy kidneys produce EPO in the peritubular interstitial cells of the renal cortex in response to tissue hypoxia (detected by hypoxia-inducible factor/HIF). EPO travels to the bone marrow and binds to EPO receptors on erythroid progenitor cells, activating the JAK2-STAT5 signaling pathway. This prevents apoptosis (programmed cell death) of these progenitor cells and promotes their differentiation and proliferation into mature red blood cells: a process taking ~7 days. In CKD/ESRD, damaged kidneys produce inadequate EPO, resulting in normocytic, normochromic anemia of chronic disease. Epoetin alfa replaces the missing hormone

medications.name: Sevelamer (Renagel/Renvela)
medications.action: A non-absorbable polymer that binds dietary phosphorus in the GI tract, forming an insoluble complex that is excreted in feces. This prevents phosphorus absorption from the gut. In ESRD, the kidneys cannot excrete phosphorus, leading to hyperphosphatemia. Elevated phosphorus binds serum calcium (forming calcium-phosphate complexes that deposit in soft tissues: vascular calcification, calciphylaxis), stimulates PTH secretion (secondary hyperparathyroidism → renal osteodystrophy), and contributes to cardiovascular mortality. Sevelamer is calcium-free (unlike calcium carbonate/acetate binders), reducing the risk of hypercalcemia and vascular calcification

medications.name: Heparin
medications.action: Binds to antithrombin III (AT-III), a natural anticoagulant. The heparin-AT-III complex undergoes a conformational change that increases AT-III's ability to inactivate thrombin (Factor IIa) and Factor Xa by 1000-fold. This prevents clotting within the extracorporeal dialysis circuit: without anticoagulation, blood clots rapidly when it contacts the foreign surfaces of the dialyzer membrane and tubing. Heparin is given as a bolus at the start of dialysis and may be continued as an infusion or intermittent boluses throughout the session

pearls: NEVER take blood pressure, draw blood, start IVs, or apply tourniquets on the access arm (AV fistula/graft). These can damage the access, promote thrombosis, or introduce infection. The access is the patient's lifeline: protect it.

Assess thrill and bruit EVERY shift. Absence of thrill/bruit = thrombosis until proven otherwise: notify the provider immediately. A change from a strong thrill to a weak one or from a bruit to silence over days suggests developing stenosis.

Post-dialysis nursing care: Monitor vital signs every 15-30 minutes for 1 hour (watch for delayed hypotension as fluid continues to redistribute). Weigh patient (compare to dry weight and pre-dialysis weight: fluid removal should match the difference). Assess access site for bleeding (apply firm pressure for 10-20 minutes after needle removal: patients are heparinized). Monitor for dialysis disequilibrium symptoms (headache, nausea, altered LOC).

Dietary restrictions in hemodialysis patients (essential teaching): Potassium restriction (<2000 mg/day: avoid bananas, oranges, tomatoes, potatoes, spinach, avocados); Phosphorus restriction (<1000 mg/day: avoid dairy, dark colas, processed foods, organ meats); Sodium restriction (<2000 mg/day: avoid canned soups, processed meats, fast food); Fluid restriction (typically 1000-1500 mL/day between dialysis sessions: based on urine output + 500-750 mL); Protein intake should be INCREASED (1.2 g/kg/day: dialysis removes amino acids, and malnutrition is a major cause of morbidity in dialysis patients).

Dialysis adequacy is measured by Kt/V (K=dialyzer clearance, t=time, V=volume of distribution of urea). Target Kt/V ≥1.2 per session, meaning at least 65% of urea is removed per treatment. If inadequate, increase dialysis time, increase blood flow rate, use a larger dialyzer, or increase frequency.

Missed or shortened dialysis sessions are dangerous. Hyperkalemia can develop within 48-72 hours without dialysis and cause fatal cardiac arrhythmias. Fluid overload leads to pulmonary edema. Educate patients on the life-threatening consequences of missing sessions.
diagnostics: Pre- and post-dialysis weight measurement
Vital signs monitoring (pre, during, post dialysis)
Access site assessment (thrill, bruit, signs of infection)
Monitor for signs of disequilibrium syndrome during dialysis
Assess electrolyte levels pre- and post-dialysis
management: NO BP, IV, or blood draws from fistula/graft arm
Administer medications as ordered (hold antihypertensives pre-dialysis if ordered)
Maintain dietary restrictions (low sodium, potassium, phosphorus)
Monitor fluid intake between sessions
Administer heparin during dialysis as ordered
nursingActions: Assess AV fistula/graft for thrill and bruit every shift
Weigh patient before and after dialysis
Monitor BP during dialysis (hypotension is most common complication)
Report signs of disequilibrium syndrome (headache, nausea, confusion, seizures)
Hold antihypertensives and medications dialyzed out per orders
Educate on fluid restriction between dialysis sessions

fracture-sprain-care.title: Fractures, Sprains, and Strains: Assessment and Management

cellular.title: Musculoskeletal Injury Pathophysiology: Bone, Ligament, and Muscle Tissue
cellular.content:
Understanding fractures, sprains, and strains requires knowledge of the three tissue types involved and how they respond to traumatic force.

Bone physiology and fracture healing: Bone is a dynamic, living tissue composed of an organic matrix (type I collagen: provides tensile strength and flexibility) and an inorganic mineral component (hydroxyapatite: calcium phosphate crystals that provide compressive strength and rigidity). At the cellular level, bone is maintained by three cell types working in a tightly regulated balance: osteoblasts (build new bone by secreting collagen matrix and initiating mineralization), osteoclasts (resorb/break down old bone through acid and enzymatic degradation), and osteocytes (mature bone cells embedded in lacunae that sense mechanical stress and coordinate bone remodeling through the RANK/RANKL/OPG signaling pathway).

When a bone fractures, the injury disrupts the periosteum, cortical bone, medullary canal, and surrounding blood vessels. Fracture healing progresses through four overlapping phases:

1. Hematoma formation (hours): Blood from torn periosteal and medullary vessels collects at the fracture site, forming a hematoma. This clot provides a fibrin scaffold and releases growth factors (PDGF, TGF-β, BMP) that recruit mesenchymal stem cells and inflammatory cells.

2. Soft callus formation (days to weeks): Granulation tissue replaces the hematoma. Chondroblasts differentiate from mesenchymal stem cells and produce a soft, cartilaginous callus (fibrocartilage) that bridges the fracture gap. This provides preliminary stabilization but NO weight-bearing capacity. Visible on X-ray as a 'cloud' around the fracture site.

3. Hard callus formation (weeks to months): Osteoblasts infiltrate the soft callus and begin depositing woven bone (immature, disorganized bone) through endochondral ossification: replacing the cartilage template with mineralized bone. The callus becomes progressively harder and more rigid. This process requires adequate calcium, phosphorus, vitamin D, and protein intake, as well as adequate blood supply.

4. Remodeling (months to years): Osteoclasts resorb the excess woven bone while osteoblasts deposit mature lamellar bone (organized, load-bearing bone) along the lines of mechanical stress (Wolff's law). The bone gradually returns to its original shape and strength, achieving ~80% of original strength by 3-6 months.

Fracture classification: Open (compound): bone protrudes through skin (high infection risk, surgical emergency) vs. Closed (simple): skin intact. Comminuted: bone shattered into multiple fragments. Greenstick: incomplete fracture through one cortex (pediatric: pliable bones bend rather than break completely). Spiral: twisting force (raise suspicion for child abuse in non-ambulatory children). Pathologic: fracture through diseased/weakened bone (osteoporosis, tumor, Paget disease) from minimal or no trauma. Stress/fatigue: microscopic fractures from repetitive loading (runners, military recruits).

Sprain physiology: A sprain is a stretch or tear of a ligament: the dense, fibrous connective tissue that connects bone to bone and stabilizes joints. Ligaments are composed primarily of type I collagen fibers arranged in parallel bundles with a sparse blood supply, which is why they heal slowly. Graded by severity: Grade I (mild stretch, microscopic fiber tears, no joint instability), Grade II (partial tear, moderate instability, significant pain and swelling), Grade III (complete rupture, gross joint instability, may paradoxically have LESS pain than Grade II because nerve fibers are completely torn).

Strain physiology: A strain is a stretch or tear of a muscle or tendon (muscle-to-bone connection). Muscles are composed of bundles of muscle fibers (multinucleated cells containing myofibrils) surrounded by connective tissue sheaths (endomysium, perimysium, epimysium). Strains typically occur at the musculotendinous junction where force transmission is highest. Muscle has excellent blood supply and regenerative capacity (satellite cells: muscle stem cells: can differentiate into new myocytes), so strains generally heal faster than sprains.

left: Fracture signs: Deformity (angulation, shortening, rotation of the limb), point tenderness directly over the fracture site, crepitus (grating sensation or sound when bone ends rub together: do NOT deliberately elicit this), loss of function (inability to bear weight or use the limb), ecchymosis (bruising from hematoma), swelling (edema from inflammatory response)

Sprain signs: Joint swelling and ecchymosis, pain with joint movement (especially the specific motion that stresses the torn ligament: e.g., anterior drawer test for ACL), joint instability with stress testing (laxity), decreased range of motion from pain and swelling

Strain signs: Muscle pain that worsens with contraction or stretching of the affected muscle, muscle spasm (involuntary contraction as a protective mechanism), weakness, possible palpable defect in the muscle belly (Grade III)
Neurovascular assessment (5 Ps of every musculoskeletal injury): Pain, Pulse (distal to injury), Pallor, Paresthesia, Paralysis: compare to uninjured side
right: Open fracture: Bone visible through wound: surgical emergency (cover with sterile saline-moistened dressing, do NOT attempt to reduce or push bone back in, splint in position found, administer IV antibiotics within 1 hour: typically cefazolin + gentamicin, tetanus prophylaxis)

Fat embolism syndrome (FES): Occurs 24-72 hours after long bone fractures (femur, pelvis): fat globules enter the bloodstream from the bone marrow, lodge in pulmonary capillaries, and trigger an inflammatory cascade. Classic triad: respiratory distress (dyspnea, tachypnea, hypoxemia: fat emboli obstruct pulmonary capillaries), neurological changes (confusion, agitation: fat crosses to cerebral circulation), petechial rash (non-blanching pinpoint red/purple spots on chest, axillae, conjunctivae: pathognomonic, appears 12-36 hours after respiratory symptoms). NO specific lab test: clinical diagnosis.

Compartment syndrome risk: Fractures (especially tibial) → bleeding and swelling within fascial compartments → pain out of proportion, pain with passive stretch, paresthesia → emergency fasciotomy needed within 6 hours

Labs: CBC (hemoglobin: blood loss from fracture hematoma, especially pelvic and femoral fractures can lose 1-3 liters internally), calcium and vitamin D (assess bone health), alkaline phosphatase (elevated during fracture healing: marker of osteoblast activity), CK (elevated in severe muscle strains/crush injuries), PT/INR (if on anticoagulants: bleeding risk), type and crossmatch for surgical cases

medications.name: Ketorolac (Toradol)
medications.action: Ketorolac non-selectively inhibits cyclooxygenase enzymes COX-1 and COX-2. COX-2 converts arachidonic acid (released from damaged cell membranes by phospholipase A2) into prostaglandins: specifically PGE2 and PGI2. These prostaglandins sensitize peripheral nociceptors (pain receptors) to bradykinin and histamine, amplify pain signaling at the dorsal horn, and promote vasodilation and increased vascular permeability at the injury site (causing the redness, warmth, and swelling of inflammation). By blocking COX-2, ketorolac reduces prostaglandin production, providing powerful analgesic and anti-inflammatory effects. It is as effective as low-dose opioids for acute musculoskeletal pain. However, COX-1 is constitutively expressed in the gastric mucosa (produces protective PGE2 that stimulates mucus and bicarbonate secretion) and in platelets (produces thromboxane A2 for platelet aggregation): blocking COX-1 causes GI mucosal injury and impaired hemostasis

medications.name: Enoxaparin (Lovenox)
medications.action: Enoxaparin binds to antithrombin III (AT-III), but unlike unfractionated heparin, it preferentially catalyzes AT-III's inactivation of Factor Xa (the convergence point of the intrinsic and extrinsic coagulation cascades) with relatively less effect on thrombin (Factor IIa). By inhibiting Factor Xa, it blocks the conversion of prothrombin to thrombin, preventing clot formation. Used for DVT prophylaxis in orthopedic fracture patients and post-surgical patients, who are at extremely high VTE risk due to Virchow's triad: venous stasis (immobilization), endothelial injury (fracture, surgery), and hypercoagulability (inflammatory response to injury activates the clotting cascade)

medications.name: Calcium Carbonate + Vitamin D3
medications.action: Calcium is the primary mineral component of hydroxyapatite crystals in bone. Vitamin D3 (cholecalciferol) is converted in the liver to 25-hydroxyvitamin D (calcidiol), then in the kidneys to 1,25-dihydroxyvitamin D (calcitriol: the active form). Calcitriol acts as a hormone that binds to vitamin D receptors (VDR) in the small intestine, upregulating expression of calcium-binding proteins (calbindin) and calcium transport channels (TRPV6), increasing intestinal calcium absorption from ~10% (without vitamin D) to ~30-40% (with adequate vitamin D). Without sufficient vitamin D, dietary calcium is poorly absorbed regardless of intake. Calcitriol also acts on osteoblasts to promote mineralization and on the parathyroid gland to suppress PTH secretion (preventing secondary hyperparathyroidism and bone resorption)

pearls: RICE for acute sprains and strains within first 48-72 hours: Rest (avoid weight-bearing on injured limb), Ice (20 minutes on, 20 minutes off: never apply ice directly to skin, use barrier), Compression (elastic bandage: snug but not tight, check distal circulation), Elevation (above heart level to reduce edema through gravity-assisted venous and lymphatic drainage). Some evidence now supports POLICE (Protection, Optimal Loading, Ice, Compression, Elevation): early controlled movement may promote healing better than complete rest.

Neurovascular checks are the HIGHEST PRIORITY nursing assessment for any fracture or cast/splint. Assess the 5 Ps (Pain, Pulse, Pallor, Paresthesia, Paralysis) and compare bilaterally. Perform every 1-2 hours for the first 24-48 hours, then every 4 hours. Any deterioration = potential compartment syndrome or vascular compromise and requires IMMEDIATE intervention.

Cast care teaching: Do NOT insert objects under the cast to scratch (risk of skin breakdown and infection that cannot be seen). Do NOT get the cast wet (plaster casts weaken; fiberglass casts trap moisture → maceration). Report any burning sensation under the cast, numbness/tingling, skin color changes, foul odor (infection), or inability to move fingers/toes. 'Window' the cast by cutting a section if compartment syndrome is suspected.

Open (compound) fractures require antibiotics within 1 hour to prevent osteomyelitis. First-generation cephalosporin (cefazolin) for Grade I-II, add aminoglycoside (gentamicin) for Grade III. Tetanus prophylaxis if not current. Surgical irrigation and debridement (I&D) within 6 hours. Infection risk increases exponentially with time.

Fat embolism syndrome (FES) prophylaxis: Early fixation of long bone fractures (within 24 hours) reduces FES incidence. Maintain adequate oxygenation and hydration. Monitor for the classic triad (respiratory distress → neuro changes → petechial rash) starting 24 hours post-fracture. There is NO definitive lab test: it is a clinical diagnosis. Treatment is supportive (oxygen, mechanical ventilation if needed, fluid resuscitation).

Pediatric fractures differ significantly: Children's bones are more porous and flexible (greenstick and buckle/torus fractures are common). The growth plate (physis) is the weakest point in the pediatric skeleton: Salter-Harris fractures through the growth plate can cause growth disturbance (Type I-V classification, Types III-V have highest risk of growth arrest). ALWAYS consider non-accidental trauma (child abuse) when the fracture pattern doesn't match the described mechanism, when there are multiple fractures at different healing stages, or when the child is pre-ambulatory with long bone fractures.
diagnostics: Neurovascular assessment of affected extremity (5 P's: Pain, Pallor, Pulselessness, Paresthesia, Paralysis)
Assess swelling and bruising
Monitor skin integrity under cast or splint
Assess pain level and location
Compare affected to unaffected extremity
management: Apply RICE (Rest, Ice, Compression, Elevation) for sprains
Immobilize fractures as ordered (cast, splint, traction)
Administer analgesics as ordered
Elevate affected extremity above heart level
Report signs of compartment syndrome immediately
nursingActions: Perform neurovascular checks every 1-2 hours post-injury or post-casting (5 P's)
Elevate affected extremity to reduce swelling
Apply ice as ordered (20 minutes on, 20 minutes off)
Monitor for compartment syndrome (pain out of proportion, pain with passive stretch)
Do not insert objects into cast for scratching
Educate patient on cast care (keep dry, report numbness/tingling/color changes)

herbals-safety.title: Herbal & Supplement Safety

cellular.title: Phytotherapy Safety
cellular.content: Herbals can have potent physiological effects and significant interactions with conventional medications, especially anticoagulants and CNS depressants.

left: St. John's Wort: Serotonin Syndrome risk
Ginkgo/Garlic: Bleeding risk
Ginseng: Hypoglycemia risk
right: Kava: Hepatotoxicity
Valerian: Excessive sedation
Licorice root: Hypokalemia/HTN

medications.name: Ginkgo Biloba
medications.action: Memory enhancement (unproven)

medications.name: St. John's Wort
medications.action: Antidepressant

pearls: Always ask about herbals in the pre-op assessment
G's (Ginkgo, Garlic, Ginger, Ginseng) = Bleeding risk
Herbals are not regulated like pharmaceuticals
diagnostics: Expect liver and renal function tests if hepatotoxicity suspected
Monitor coagulation studies (PT/INR) in patients on anticoagulants
Expect medication reconciliation to be completed
Monitor for signs of drug-herb interactions
Expect blood glucose monitoring if hypoglycemia risk
management: Document all herbal supplements in medication history
Advise to stop herbals 2 weeks before surgery as ordered
Report herbal use to healthcare team
Educate patients that herbals are not FDA-regulated
Avoid concurrent use of herbals with prescribed medications without approval
nursingActions: Ask about herbal and supplement use during every assessment
Report use of bleeding-risk herbals (Ginkgo, Garlic, Ginger, Ginseng) to physician
Monitor for signs of adverse interactions (bleeding, sedation, serotonin syndrome)
Document all herbals and supplements in the medication record
Educate patients about potential herb-drug interactions
Report unexplained symptoms that could indicate herbal interactions

electrolyte-safety.title: Electrolyte Imbalances

cellular.title: Ionic Homeostasis
cellular.content: Electrolytes (K, Na, Ca, Mg) are critical for nerve conduction, muscle contraction, and fluid balance. Imbalances lead to lethal cardiac and neurological complications.

left: Hyponatremia: Seizures/Confusion
Hypokalemia: U-waves/Cramps
Hypocalcemia: Trousseau/Chvostek
right: Hyperkalemia: Peaked T-waves
Hypernatremia: Thirst/Dry mucus
Hypercalcemia: Stones/Moans/Groans

medications.name: Calcium Gluconate
medications.action: Stabilizes cardiac membrane

medications.name: Magnesium Sulfate
medications.action: CNS Depressant

pearls: NEVER give Potassium IV Push (Lethal)
Monitor for cardiac changes with K+ shifts
Fluid follows Sodium
diagnostics: Monitor serum electrolyte levels (Na+, K+, Ca2+, Mg2+, PO4)
Monitor ECG for electrolyte-related changes
Expect metabolic panel to be drawn regularly
Monitor serum osmolality
Expect ABG if acid-base imbalance suspected
Monitor urine specific gravity
management: Administer electrolyte replacements as prescribed
Follow dietary modifications as ordered
Maintain IV access for replacement therapy
Implement cardiac monitoring for K+ and Ca2+ abnormalities
Follow fluid restriction or replacement orders
Maintain seizure precautions for hyponatremia/hypocalcemia
nursingActions: Monitor and report electrolyte results promptly
NEVER give potassium IV push (lethal)
Assess for signs of cardiac instability (palpitations, irregular pulse)
Monitor for neuromuscular changes (weakness, tremors, tetany)
Report ECG changes (peaked T waves, U waves) immediately
Educate patients on dietary sources of electrolytes
Document intake and output strictly

cardiac-meds.title: Vasoactive & Cardiac Meds

cellular.title: Hemodynamic Support
cellular.content: Inotropes (contractility), Vasopressors (SVR), and Antiarrhythmics are used to stabilize hemodynamics in critical care. Each class has narrow therapeutic windows and specific monitoring requirements.

left: Beta Blockers: Low HR/BP
ACE Inhibitors: Cough/Angioedema
Nitrates: Headache/Hypotension
right: Digoxin Toxicity: Yellow halos/Nausea
Amiodarone: Blue-gray skin
Epinephrine: Palpitations

medications.name: Digoxin
medications.action: Increases contractility

medications.name: Amlodipine
medications.action: Vasodilation

pearls: Hold Beta Blockers if HR < 50 or SBP < 100
ACE inhibitors can cause life-threatening angioedema
Potassium levels affect Digoxin toxicity
diagnostics: Monitor drug levels (digoxin, theophylline) as ordered
Monitor electrolytes (K+ affects digoxin toxicity)
Expect ECG monitoring for cardiac medications
Monitor renal and hepatic function tests
Monitor vital signs before and after administration
Expect CBC if bleeding risk from anticoagulants
management: Check apical pulse for 1 full minute before giving digoxin
Hold beta blockers if HR < 60 or SBP < 100 per protocol
Administer cardiac medications at scheduled times
Maintain continuous cardiac monitoring as ordered
Report signs of drug toxicity immediately
Follow medication administration safety checks
nursingActions: Assess vital signs before administering vasoactive medications
Report bradycardia (< 60) or hypotension (SBP < 90) before giving dose
Monitor for signs of digoxin toxicity (yellow halos, nausea, bradycardia)
Educate patients on medication adherence and side effects
Monitor for angioedema with ACE inhibitors
Report signs of drug toxicity to physician promptly
Document medication administration and patient response

anticoagulant-safety.title: Anticoagulation Mastery

cellular.title: Hemostasis Control
cellular.content: Anticoagulants prevent thrombus formation by interrupting the coagulation cascade. They do not dissolve existing clots but prevent them from growing larger.

left: Ecchymosis/Bruising
Hematuria
Epistaxis (Nosebleed)
Gingival bleeding
right: Hemorrhagic Stroke signs
Melena
Hypotension (Internal bleed)
HIT (Thrombocytopenia)

medications.name: Warfarin
medications.action: Inhibits clotting factors

medications.name: Enoxaparin
medications.action: Antithrombin activator

pearls: Monitor PTT for Heparin, PT/INR for Warfarin
Avoid Vitamin K-rich foods (kale/spinach) on Warfarin
Report black tarry stools immediately
diagnostics: Monitor PT/INR for warfarin therapy
Monitor aPTT for heparin therapy
Expect CBC with platelet count to be drawn
Monitor stool and urine for occult blood
Expect coagulation panel regularly
Monitor hemoglobin and hematocrit trends
management: Administer anticoagulants at consistent scheduled times
Maintain fall precautions for patients on anticoagulants
Apply pressure to venipuncture sites for 5+ minutes
Avoid IM injections when possible
Use electric razors instead of straight razors
Use soft-bristled toothbrush only
nursingActions: Monitor for signs of bleeding (bruising, petechiae, gum bleeding, melena)
Report INR > 3.0 or aPTT above therapeutic range immediately
Educate on consistent vitamin K intake with warfarin
Report black tarry stools or blood in urine immediately
Assess for signs of internal bleeding (abdominal distension, back pain)
Document all anticoagulant administration times
Educate on wearing medical alert identification

insulin-safety.title: Insulin & Diabetic Safety

cellular.title: Glucose Metabolism
cellular.content: Insulin facilitates glucose entry into cells. Over-administration or inadequate intake leads to hypoglycemia (neuroglycopenia), a medical emergency.

left: Hypoglycemia: Shakiness
Sweating/Diaphoresis
Confusion
Hunger
right: DKA: Kussmaul respirations
Fruity breath
Severe Dehydration
Altered LOC

medications.name: Lispro
medications.action: Bolus insulin

medications.name: Glargine
medications.action: Basal coverage

pearls: Check blood glucose before administration
Rule of 15 for hypoglycemia
Rotate injection sites
diagnostics: Monitor blood glucose before meals and at bedtime
Expect HbA1c to be ordered quarterly
Monitor serum potassium during DKA treatment
Expect metabolic panel for acid-base status in DKA
Monitor urine ketones as ordered
Expect point-of-care glucose checks per protocol
management: Check blood glucose before insulin administration
Ensure food is available before giving rapid-acting insulin
Follow sliding scale insulin protocol as ordered
Rotate injection sites systematically
Store insulin properly (refrigerate unopened, room temp opened)
Follow Rule of 15 for hypoglycemia (15g carbs, recheck in 15 min)
nursingActions: Monitor for signs of hypoglycemia (shakiness, sweating, confusion)
Report blood glucose < 70 mg/dL and treat immediately
Verify insulin type, dose, and expiration before administration
Monitor for signs of DKA (Kussmaul respirations, fruity breath)
Educate on sick day management and when to seek care
Document blood glucose levels and insulin administered
Report hyperglycemia > 300 mg/dL to physician

lithium-toxicity.title: Lithium & Mood Stabilizers

cellular.title: Ion Competition
cellular.content: Lithium is a salt that competes with Sodium in the kidneys. Dehydration or low sodium intake causes the kidneys to reabsorb lithium, leading to toxicity.

left: Therapeutic range: 0.6 - 1.2 mEq/L
Mild tremor
Nausea
Thirst
right: Toxicity (>1.5): Confusion
Ataxia
Coarse tremors
Blurred vision

medications.name: Lithium Carbonate
medications.action: Alters neurotransmitters

pearls: Avoid NSAIDs - they increase lithium levels
Ensure 2-3L fluid intake daily
Monitor renal function and thyroid
diagnostics: Expect serum lithium level to be drawn (therapeutic 0.6-1.2 mEq/L)
Monitor BUN and creatinine for renal function
Expect BMP to be ordered (electrolytes, especially sodium)
Expect thyroid function tests (lithium affects thyroid)
Monitor ECG for cardiac conduction changes
Expect urine specific gravity to be checked
management: Hold lithium dose and notify provider immediately if toxicity suspected
Maintain adequate hydration (2-3 L/day)
Maintain normal sodium intake (do not restrict)
Avoid NSAIDs, which increase lithium levels
Position safely if confusion or ataxia present
Prepare for possible hemodialysis if levels > 2.5 mEq/L
nursingActions: Monitor for signs of toxicity (coarse tremors, confusion, ataxia, blurred vision)
Report GI symptoms (nausea, vomiting, diarrhea) as early toxicity signs
Weigh patient daily to assess hydration status
Monitor intake and output
Educate patient on maintaining consistent fluid and sodium intake
Report changes in mental status or coordination immediately

factor-xa-inhibitors.title: Factor Xa Inhibitors (DOACs)

cellular.title: Direct Oral Anticoagulants
cellular.content: Direct oral anticoagulants (DOACs) selectively inhibit Factor Xa in the coagulation cascade, preventing conversion of prothrombin to thrombin and subsequent fibrin clot formation. Unlike warfarin, they have predictable pharmacokinetics requiring no routine INR monitoring. Examples: Rivaroxaban (Xarelto), Apixaban (Eliquis), Edoxaban (Savaysa).

left: Bleeding: Gingival, Epistaxis, Ecchymosis
Hematuria
Melena or GI Bleeding
No Routine Lab Monitoring Needed
right: Rivaroxaban: MUST Take With Food (absorption)
Shorter Half-Life Than Warfarin
Renal Clearance (dose adjust for kidney function)
No Dietary Restrictions (unlike Warfarin/Vit K)

medications.name: Andexanet Alfa (Andexxa)
medications.action: Recombinant modified Factor Xa that binds and sequesters Factor Xa inhibitors

medications.name: Prothrombin Complex Concentrate (PCC)
medications.action: Provides clotting factors to overcome anticoagulant effect

pearls: No dietary restrictions needed (unlike warfarin which requires consistent Vitamin K intake)
Renal dose adjustment required, especially with declining GFR - monitor kidney function
Shorter half-life than warfarin means missed doses lead to rapid loss of anticoagulant protection
Avoid concurrent use with strong CYP3A4 inhibitors (ketoconazole, ritonavir)
Assess for bleeding clinically - do NOT monitor with PT/INR (not reliable for DOACs)
diagnostics: Monitor for signs of bleeding (bruising, petechiae, blood in stool/urine)
Assess vital signs for unexplained tachycardia or hypotension
Monitor renal function trends as reported
Assess for neurological changes (intracranial bleed)
management: Administer medication at consistent times as prescribed
Do not crush or chew extended-release formulations
Report any signs of bleeding immediately
Hold medication and notify provider before procedures
Avoid concurrent NSAIDs and aspirin unless prescribed
nursingActions: Assess for signs of bleeding at every interaction
Monitor stool color (melena) and urine color (hematuria)
Educate patient on bleeding precautions (soft toothbrush, electric razor)
Remind patient of consistent dosing schedule
Report unexplained bruising or prolonged bleeding
Teach patient to inform all healthcare providers about anticoagulant use

methotrexate-safety-rpn.title: Methotrexate Safety: RPN Monitoring Essentials

cellular.title: Folate Antagonist Overview
cellular.content: Methotrexate is a folate antagonist used in autoimmune conditions, certain cancers, and ectopic pregnancy. It inhibits cell division by blocking folic acid metabolism. As an RPN, understanding safe handling, side effect monitoring, and timely reporting of abnormal findings is critical for patient safety.

left: Stomatitis (mouth sores, oral ulcers)
Nausea and vomiting
Fatigue and malaise
Hair thinning
right: Myelosuppression: fever, infection, bleeding
Hepatotoxicity: jaundice, RUQ pain
Pulmonary toxicity: dry cough, dyspnea
Skin rash or photosensitivity

medications.name: Methotrexate
medications.action: Blocks folic acid metabolism to reduce cell division

medications.name: Folic Acid
medications.action: Replaces folate depleted by methotrexate, reducing toxicity

pearls: Stomatitis is often the first sign of methotrexate toxicity - inspect the mouth at every assessment
Folic acid supplementation reduces side effects but must be taken as prescribed (usually not on methotrexate dosing day)
Report fever immediately - may indicate life-threatening neutropenia
Safe handling: methotrexate is a cytotoxic drug even at low doses
Patient education: avoid alcohol, report any signs of infection or unusual bruising
diagnostics: Monitor CBC results for signs of myelosuppression (low WBC, low platelets, low RBC)
Monitor liver function test results as reported
Assess for signs of infection (fever, sore throat, malaise)
Monitor oral cavity for stomatitis and mucositis
Assess for signs of bleeding (bruising, petechiae, blood in stool/urine)
Monitor intake and output
management: Administer folic acid supplement as prescribed (reduces side effects)
Follow safe handling procedures for cytotoxic medication
Ensure adequate hydration as ordered
Hold dose and report if patient shows signs of infection, bleeding, or severe mouth sores
Avoid administering if recent lab values show myelosuppression
Report any abnormal lab results to the RN or physician immediately
nursingActions: Inspect oral mucosa at each assessment for stomatitis (redness, ulceration, white patches)
Monitor temperature and report fever >38°C immediately (may indicate neutropenia)
Report any signs of bleeding (bruising, petechiae, gum bleeding, dark stools)
Use safe handling procedures: wear gloves when handling the medication
Educate patient to report sore throat, fever, mouth sores, or unusual bruising
Monitor for nausea and vomiting and report if persistent
Ensure patient is taking prescribed folic acid supplementation
Report signs of hepatotoxicity (jaundice, right upper quadrant pain, dark urine)

methotrexate-safety.title: Methotrexate: RN Pharmacology & Clinical Management

cellular.title: Dihydrofolate Reductase Inhibition & Folate Antagonism
cellular.content: Methotrexate (MTX) exerts its primary mechanism of action through competitive inhibition of dihydrofolate reductase (DHFR), the enzyme responsible for converting dihydrofolate to tetrahydrofolate. Tetrahydrofolate is essential for the synthesis of thymidylate, purines, and amino acids required for DNA and RNA synthesis. By blocking DHFR, methotrexate disrupts the folate cycle, halting DNA replication and cell division. This mechanism is particularly effective against rapidly dividing cells, which explains its therapeutic utility in both malignancies (cancer cells) and autoimmune conditions (activated lymphocytes and inflammatory cells). At low doses used for rheumatoid arthritis and other autoimmune conditions, methotrexate also acts through adenosine-mediated anti-inflammatory pathways. The drug accumulates intracellularly as polyglutamated forms, which have prolonged half-lives and sustained DHFR inhibition. In ectopic pregnancy management, methotrexate targets the rapidly dividing trophoblastic tissue, causing dissolution of the ectopic implantation without surgical intervention. Leucovorin (folinic acid) serves as the rescue agent by providing a reduced folate that bypasses the DHFR block, allowing normal cells to resume DNA synthesis while cancer cells (which take up leucovorin less efficiently) remain affected.

riskFactors: Renal impairment (methotrexate is primarily renally excreted - toxicity risk increases dramatically)
Hepatic disease or heavy alcohol use (additive hepatotoxicity)
Pre-existing myelosuppression or bone marrow dysfunction
Concurrent NSAID use (decreases renal clearance of methotrexate)
Third-space fluid collections (pleural effusion, ascites - acts as drug reservoir)

left: Stomatitis/mucositis (earliest clinical sign of toxicity)
Myelosuppression: pancytopenia (anemia, neutropenia, thrombocytopenia)
Hepatotoxicity: elevated transaminases progressing to fibrosis/cirrhosis with chronic use
Nausea, vomiting, anorexia
right: Methotrexate pneumonitis: dry cough, dyspnea, fever (can be fatal)
Nephrotoxicity: crystalluria with high-dose therapy (MTX precipitates in acidic urine)
Severe immunosuppression: opportunistic infections
Dermatologic: photosensitivity, radiation recall dermatitis

medications.name: Methotrexate
medications.action: Inhibits dihydrofolate reductase (DHFR), blocking DNA/RNA synthesis in rapidly dividing cells; adenosine-mediated anti-inflammatory effects at low doses

medications.name: Leucovorin (Folinic Acid)
medications.action: Provides reduced folate that bypasses the DHFR block, rescuing normal cells from methotrexate toxicity

medications.name: Folic Acid
medications.action: Replenishes folate stores depleted by chronic low-dose methotrexate therapy

pearls: Leucovorin rescue vs folic acid supplementation: leucovorin is for HIGH-dose MTX (oncology) emergencies; folic acid is for LOW-dose chronic therapy side effect reduction
Third-space fluid collections (pleural effusions, ascites) act as methotrexate reservoirs, causing prolonged toxicity - drain before treatment

Ectopic pregnancy protocol: monitor serial beta-hCG levels - expect initial rise before decline; separation pain (days 3-7) is expected, but severe pain or hemodynamic instability requires surgical evaluation
Drug interaction priority: NSAIDs + methotrexate = decreased renal clearance = increased toxicity risk
Pulmonary toxicity (MTX pneumonitis) can occur at ANY dose and is not dose-dependent - maintain high index of suspicion for new respiratory symptoms
diagnostics: Monitor CBC with differential weekly during initiation, then regularly (nadir for myelosuppression typically 7-14 days)
Monitor liver function tests (AST, ALT) at baseline and regularly throughout therapy
Monitor serum creatinine and BUN (renal function critical for drug clearance)
Expect serum methotrexate levels for high-dose oncology protocols
Monitor for signs of pulmonary toxicity (chest X-ray if new respiratory symptoms)
Assess oral mucosa systematically for early stomatitis
Monitor serum albumin (low albumin increases free drug concentration)
management: Administer leucovorin rescue as prescribed within 24 hours of high-dose methotrexate (timing is critical)
Ensure aggressive IV hydration and urine alkalinization (pH >7.0) for high-dose protocols to prevent crystalluria
Administer folic acid 1 mg daily (except on methotrexate day) for chronic low-dose therapy
Hold methotrexate and notify provider for WBC <3,000, platelets <100,000, or rising creatinine
Implement neutropenic precautions if ANC falls below threshold
Educate on strict pregnancy avoidance during treatment and for 3 months after discontinuation (teratogenic)
nursingActions: Verify lab results (CBC, LFTs, renal function) before each dose administration
Assess for drug interactions: NSAIDs, trimethoprim, proton pump inhibitors all increase toxicity
Monitor for signs of methotrexate pneumonitis (non-productive cough, dyspnea, fever - can occur at any dose)
Educate patient on teratogenicity and need for reliable contraception
Implement infection prevention measures during myelosuppressive nadir
For ectopic pregnancy protocol: educate patient on expected separation pain vs emergency symptoms
Monitor for signs of tumor lysis syndrome in oncology patients
Document methotrexate levels and leucovorin rescue timing precisely

methotrexate-safety-np.title: Methotrexate: NP Molecular Pharmacology & Prescriptive Management

cellular.title: Polyglutamation, Adenosine Pathway & AICAR Transformylase Inhibition
cellular.content: Methotrexate pharmacology extends far beyond simple DHFR inhibition when examined at the molecular level. After cellular uptake via the reduced folate carrier (RFC1/SLC19A1), methotrexate undergoes intracellular polyglutamation by folylpolyglutamate synthetase (FPGS), adding up to 5-6 glutamate residues. These polyglutamated forms (MTX-PG) are retained intracellularly with half-lives of weeks to months, creating a sustained pharmacological effect even with weekly dosing. MTX-PG directly inhibits multiple enzymes in the folate pathway: DHFR (blocking dihydrofolate to tetrahydrofolate conversion), thymidylate synthase (TS, blocking dTMP synthesis required for DNA replication), and critically, AICAR transformylase (aminoimidazole carboxamide ribonucleotide transformylase, also called ATIC). Inhibition of AICAR transformylase leads to intracellular accumulation of AICAR, which inhibits adenosine deaminase (ADA) and AMP deaminase, resulting in increased extracellular adenosine release. This adenosine pathway is now understood to be the PRIMARY anti-inflammatory mechanism of low-dose methotrexate in autoimmune disease. Adenosine binds to A2A and A3 receptors on inflammatory cells, suppressing NF-κB activation, reducing TNF-α, IL-6, and IL-8 production, inhibiting neutrophil adhesion, and promoting anti-inflammatory macrophage polarization (M2 phenotype). Pharmacogenomic variation significantly impacts methotrexate efficacy and toxicity. The MTHFR (methylenetetrahydrofolate reductase) C677T and A1298C polymorphisms reduce enzyme activity, leading to altered folate metabolism and potentially increased methotrexate toxicity. Patients homozygous for MTHFR C677T (TT genotype, prevalence ~10-15% in Caucasians) have 30-50% reduced enzyme activity, resulting in elevated homocysteine levels and increased susceptibility to methotrexate-induced myelosuppression, hepatotoxicity, and mucositis. ABCB1 (P-glycoprotein) and ABCC2 (MRP2) transporter polymorphisms affect methotrexate efflux from cells, while RFC1 (SLC19A1) G80A polymorphism affects cellular drug uptake, collectively influencing both efficacy and toxicity profiles.

riskFactors: MTHFR C677T homozygosity (TT genotype - increased toxicity risk)
Reduced folylpolyglutamate synthetase activity (decreased intracellular drug activation)
GGH (gamma-glutamyl hydrolase) overexpression (increased polyglutamate breakdown, reduced efficacy)
Renal impairment (GFR <30 mL/min - contraindicated; GFR 30-60 requires dose reduction)
Third-space fluid collections acting as drug reservoirs (pleural effusion, ascites)
Concurrent medications affecting renal tubular secretion (NSAIDs, probenecid, penicillins, PPIs)

left: AICAR accumulation → increased extracellular adenosine → anti-inflammatory effects (therapeutic mechanism)
Polyglutamated MTX sustained intracellular retention (weeks-months)
