Myelosuppression nadir at 7-14 days: pancytopenia (monitor ANC closely)
Hepatic fibrosis with chronic cumulative dosing (monitor after 3.5-4 g cumulative)
right: Methotrexate pneumonitis: hypersensitivity reaction (not dose-dependent, can occur at any time)
Nephrotoxicity: 7-OH-methotrexate crystallization in renal tubules (prevent with alkalinization)
Neurotoxicity with intrathecal administration: arachnoiditis, leukoencephalopathy
MTHFR polymorphism-related enhanced toxicity: increased mucositis, cytopenias at standard doses

medications.name: Methotrexate
medications.action: DHFR inhibition (antiproliferative), AICAR transformylase inhibition → adenosine-mediated anti-inflammatory pathway, thymidylate synthase inhibition; intracellular polyglutamation provides sustained effect

medications.name: Leucovorin (Calcium Folinate)
medications.action: 5-formyltetrahydrofolate that bypasses DHFR block, providing reduced folate directly for thymidylate and purine synthesis in normal cells

medications.name: Glucarpidase (Voraxaze)
medications.action: Enzymatically cleaves methotrexate to inactive metabolites (DAMPA and glutamate), rapidly reducing serum MTX levels by >97% within 15 minutes

pearls: MTHFR C677T TT genotype: consider pre-treatment genotyping - these patients may need 50% dose reduction and enhanced folic acid supplementation (5 mg/week vs standard 1 mg)
Adenosine pathway is the dominant anti-inflammatory mechanism at low doses - this is why methotrexate works for RA despite not being cytotoxic at 7.5-25 mg/week doses

Ectopic pregnancy management: single-dose vs multi-dose protocol selection is based on initial beta-hCG level, ectopic mass size, and presence of fetal cardiac activity - higher values favor multi-dose or surgical approach
Polyglutamation kinetics explain the 6-8 week delay in therapeutic onset for RA - counsel patients on expected timeline

Drug interaction mechanism: NSAIDs reduce renal prostaglandin synthesis → decreased GFR → decreased MTX clearance → toxicity. This interaction is the most clinically significant and frequently tested.
diagnostics: Order baseline and serial CBC with differential, comprehensive metabolic panel, hepatic panel
Monitor serum methotrexate levels at 24, 48, and 72 hours post high-dose infusion (target <0.05 μmol/L for leucovorin discontinuation)
Consider MTHFR genotyping for patients with unexplained toxicity at standard doses
Order baseline chest X-ray and pulmonary function tests for high-risk patients
Monitor liver fibrosis risk: consider FibroScan or liver biopsy after cumulative dose of 3.5-4 g in chronic therapy
Evaluate beta-hCG trends in ectopic pregnancy management (expect ≥15% decline between days 4-7)
Assess anti-MTX-PG antibody formation in patients with unexplained treatment failure
management: Prescribe methotrexate dosing based on indication: RA (7.5-25 mg/week PO/SC), ectopic pregnancy (50 mg/m² single-dose or multi-dose protocol), oncology (varies widely by protocol)
For ectopic pregnancy single-dose protocol: MTX 50 mg/m² IM on day 1, measure beta-hCG days 4 and 7, repeat dose if <15% decline between days 4-7
Risk stratify ectopic pregnancy patients: single-dose protocol for beta-hCG <5,000; multi-dose protocol for beta-hCG >5,000 or ectopic mass >3.5 cm or fetal cardiac activity present
Initiate leucovorin rescue protocol: 15 mg/m² IV/PO every 6 hours starting 24 hours after high-dose MTX, continue until serum MTX <0.05 μmol/L
Dose adjust for renal function: reduce by 50% for GFR 30-60 mL/min; contraindicated if GFR <30 mL/min
Consider pharmacogenomic-guided prescribing: patients with MTHFR TT genotype may benefit from higher folic acid supplementation (5 mg/week) and lower starting doses
nursingActions: Evaluate pharmacogenomic risk factors before initiating methotrexate therapy
Calculate BSA-based dosing accurately for oncology and ectopic pregnancy protocols
Monitor methotrexate clearance kinetics: delayed clearance (level >1 μmol/L at 48 hours) requires escalated leucovorin rescue
Assess for signs of methotrexate-induced lymphoproliferative disorder (rare but serious complication of chronic immunosuppression)
Manage ectopic pregnancy protocol: counsel patient on expected course, separation pain timeline, and emergency warning signs (severe abdominal pain, syncope, shoulder pain indicating hemoperitoneum)
Evaluate cumulative hepatotoxicity risk for long-term therapy and recommend appropriate monitoring intervals
Coordinate multidisciplinary care for complex oncology protocols including hydration, alkalinization, and rescue timing
Develop individualized monitoring plans based on patient-specific risk factors and pharmacogenomic profile

oxybutynin-rpn.title: Oxybutynin (Ditropan) - Anticholinergic for Overactive Bladder

cellular.title: Muscarinic Receptor Blockade
cellular.content: Oxybutynin is an anticholinergic and antispasmodic agent used to treat overactive bladder (OAB) and urinary urgency or incontinence. It works by blocking muscarinic M3 receptors on the detrusor muscle of the bladder, reducing involuntary detrusor contractions and increasing bladder capacity. This parasympathetic blockade decreases the urge to void and reduces episodes of urge incontinence. Because muscarinic receptors are found throughout the body (salivary glands, GI tract, eyes, sweat glands, CNS), systemic anticholinergic side effects are common and clinically significant.

riskFactors: Elderly patients (Beers Criteria - HIGH anticholinergic burden risk)
Concurrent use of other anticholinergic medications

left: Dry mouth (most common side effect)
Constipation
Blurred vision
Urinary retention
right: Cognitive impairment in elderly (confusion, agitation)
Dizziness and drowsiness
Heat intolerance (decreased sweating)
Tachycardia

medications.name: Oxybutynin (Ditropan)
medications.action: Blocks muscarinic M3 receptors on detrusor muscle, reducing involuntary bladder contractions

pearls: Beers Criteria flags oxybutynin as HIGH risk in elderly due to anticholinergic burden - causes falls, confusion, and cognitive decline
Dry mouth is the most common side effect - recommend sugar-free candy or gum and frequent sips of water
Educate patients to avoid hot environments because impaired sweating increases heat stroke risk
Monitor post-void residual volume - anticholinergics can worsen urinary retention, especially in men with BPH
Review the full medication list for anticholinergic stacking (diphenhydramine, tricyclics, promethazine combined with oxybutynin)
diagnostics: Monitor urinary output and voiding patterns
Assess post-void residual volume (bladder scan)
Monitor cognitive status in elderly patients at each assessment
Assess for signs of urinary retention (distended bladder, inability to void)
Monitor bowel function for constipation
Assess intraocular pressure in patients with glaucoma risk
management: Administer medication as prescribed (oral or transdermal patch)
Encourage adequate fluid intake to prevent urinary retention
Implement fall precautions especially in elderly patients
Ensure toileting schedule to support bladder retraining
Discontinue and notify provider if urinary retention develops
Avoid use in patients with uncontrolled narrow-angle glaucoma
nursingActions: Assess for dry mouth and provide comfort measures (sugar-free candy, frequent oral care)
Monitor for urinary retention (palpate bladder, assess voiding frequency and volume)
Assess cognitive function in elderly patients and report any new confusion or agitation
Educate patient to avoid hot environments due to impaired sweating and heat stroke risk
Monitor for constipation and encourage dietary fiber and fluids
Report falls or near-falls immediately (anticholinergic medications increase fall risk)
Educate patient to report blurred vision, dizziness, or difficulty urinating
Review medication list for other anticholinergic drugs (additive burden)

levetiracetam-rpn.title: Levetiracetam (Keppra) - Anticonvulsant for Seizure Management

cellular.title: Synaptic Vesicle Protein SV2A Modulation
cellular.content: Levetiracetam is a broad-spectrum anticonvulsant used for partial-onset seizures, myoclonic seizures, and primary generalized tonic-clonic seizures. Unlike older antiepileptics, levetiracetam has a unique mechanism of action - it binds to synaptic vesicle protein SV2A in the brain, which modulates neurotransmitter release from presynaptic vesicles. This binding reduces excessive neuronal firing without broadly suppressing normal brain activity. Levetiracetam is primarily eliminated by the kidneys (66% unchanged in urine), has minimal hepatic metabolism, and does not induce or inhibit cytochrome P450 enzymes, resulting in very few drug interactions compared to older anticonvulsants.

riskFactors: Renal impairment (requires dose adjustment - renally eliminated)
History of depression or psychiatric disorders
Suicidal ideation (FDA black box warning for all antiepileptics)
Abrupt discontinuation (risk of rebound seizures or status epilepticus)
Pregnancy (discuss risk-benefit - generally considered safer than many alternatives)
Children and adolescents (higher incidence of behavioral side effects)

left: Somnolence and fatigue
Dizziness
Behavioral changes (irritability, aggression)
Mood changes and emotional lability
right: Keppra rage (hostility, agitation, anger)
Headache
Coordination difficulties (ataxia)
Decreased appetite

medications.name: Levetiracetam (Keppra)
medications.action: Binds to synaptic vesicle protein SV2A, modulating neurotransmitter release and reducing excessive neuronal firing

pearls: NEVER stop levetiracetam abruptly - gradual taper over 2 to 4 weeks required to prevent rebound seizures or status epilepticus
Keppra rage refers to behavioral side effects (irritability, aggression, hostility, mood changes) - most common reason for discontinuation
Minimal drug interactions make levetiracetam a preferred choice for patients on multiple medications
Renal elimination means dose must be adjusted for kidney impairment - monitor renal function
FDA black box warning: all antiepileptics carry increased risk of suicidal thoughts and behavior - screen patients regularly
diagnostics: Monitor seizure activity (type, frequency, duration, postictal state)
Assess behavioral and mood changes at every visit (irritability, aggression, depression)
Monitor renal function (BUN, creatinine) for dose adjustment
Screen for suicidal ideation per FDA black box warning for antiepileptics
Assess for somnolence and dizziness affecting safety and daily function
Monitor for signs of infection (rare leukopenia)
management: Administer medication as prescribed - can be given with or without food
NEVER stop abruptly - taper gradually over 2 to 4 weeks to prevent status epilepticus
Adjust dose based on renal function as ordered
Implement seizure precautions (padded side rails, suction at bedside, oxygen available)
Ensure consistent dosing schedule to maintain therapeutic levels
Report behavioral changes or mood disturbances promptly for evaluation
nursingActions: Monitor and document seizure activity (onset, duration, type of movements, postictal state)
Assess for behavioral and mood changes at each interaction - report irritability, aggression, or mood swings (Keppra rage)
Screen for depression and suicidal ideation per facility protocol
Educate patient and family that medication must not be stopped abruptly
Monitor for excessive drowsiness or dizziness and implement fall precautions
Educate patient to avoid driving or operating heavy machinery until response to medication is known
Report any new psychiatric symptoms (hostility, anxiety, psychosis) immediately
Ensure medication is available and administered on time to prevent breakthrough seizures

short-acting-insulin-rpn.title: Short-Acting Insulin (Regular Insulin / Humulin R)

cellular.title: Exogenous Insulin and Glucose Regulation
cellular.content: Regular insulin (Humulin R, Novolin R) is a short-acting insulin that mimics the body's natural insulin response to meals. It facilitates glucose uptake into cells by binding to insulin receptors on cell membranes, activating glucose transporter proteins (GLUT4) that move glucose from the bloodstream into skeletal muscle, adipose tissue, and other cells. Regular insulin is the ONLY insulin that can be administered intravenously, making it critical for emergency management of diabetic ketoacidosis (DKA) and hyperkalemia. When given subcutaneously, onset is 30 to 60 minutes with a peak at 2 to 4 hours. When given IV, onset is approximately 15 minutes with a much shorter duration, requiring continuous infusion and close monitoring.

riskFactors: Renal impairment (delayed insulin clearance increases hypoglycemia risk)
Irregular meal patterns or skipped meals
NPO status without insulin dose adjustment
Concurrent use of beta-blockers (masks hypoglycemia symptoms)
Stacking insulin doses (giving correction before prior dose peaks)

left: Hypoglycemia: tremors, shakiness
Diaphoresis (sweating)
Tachycardia and palpitations
Confusion and irritability
right: Severe hypoglycemia: seizures, loss of consciousness
Injection site lipodystrophy
Hypokalemia (with IV insulin)
Weight gain

medications.name: Regular Insulin (Humulin R / Novolin R)
medications.action: Binds to insulin receptors, activates GLUT4 transporters to move glucose into cells; only insulin approved for IV administration

pearls: Regular insulin is the ONLY insulin that can be given intravenously - used for DKA and hyperkalemia emergencies
Clear before cloudy - when mixing Regular and NPH in one syringe, always draw up Regular (clear) first to prevent NPH from contaminating the Regular vial
Regular insulin is a CLEAR solution - if it appears cloudy, do NOT use it (NPH is the cloudy insulin)
IV insulin requires an infusion pump, continuous blood glucose monitoring every 1 to 2 hours, and potassium monitoring (insulin drives K+ into cells)
Administer subcutaneous regular insulin 30 minutes before meals - ensure the patient will actually eat to prevent hypoglycemia
diagnostics: Monitor blood glucose before meals and at bedtime per protocol
Monitor blood glucose every 1 to 2 hours during IV insulin infusion
Assess for signs and symptoms of hypoglycemia at each interaction
Monitor serum potassium during IV insulin administration (insulin shifts K+ intracellularly)
Assess injection sites for lipodystrophy
Monitor intake and output during DKA management
management: Administer subcutaneous regular insulin 30 minutes before meals
Follow sliding scale insulin protocol as ordered for glucose correction
For IV administration: use an infusion pump with continuous glucose monitoring
Draw up regular insulin BEFORE NPH when mixing in the same syringe (clear before cloudy)
Verify insulin type, dose, and expiration with a second nurse per facility policy
Follow Rule of 15 for hypoglycemia: give 15 g fast-acting carbohydrate, recheck glucose in 15 minutes
Ensure meal tray is available before administering mealtime insulin
nursingActions: Verify that regular insulin is a CLEAR solution - do NOT administer if cloudy (cloudy indicates NPH or contamination)
Check blood glucose before every insulin administration
Monitor for hypoglycemia signs: tremors, sweating, tachycardia, confusion, irritability
When mixing insulins: draw up Regular (clear) BEFORE NPH (cloudy) to prevent contamination
During IV insulin infusion: monitor glucose every 1 to 2 hours and potassium levels as ordered
Ensure patient eats within 30 minutes of subcutaneous regular insulin injection
Report blood glucose below 70 mg/dL and treat immediately per protocol
Educate patient on timing of meals relative to insulin injection and importance of not skipping meals

pressure-injury.title: Advanced Wound Care

cellular.title: Tissue Ischemia
cellular.content: Localized damage to the skin and underlying soft tissue usually over a bony prominence. Caused by intense or prolonged pressure in combination with shear.

left: Stage 1: Non-blanchable redness
Stage 2: Partial thickness/Blister
Stage 3: Full thickness (Fat visible)
right: Stage 4: Muscle/Bone visible
Unstageable: Eschar/Slough
Deep Tissue Injury: Purple/Maroon

medications.name: Hydrocolloid
medications.action: Moist wound healing

pearls: Turn every 2 hours
Nutrition: High protein and Vitamin C
Use pressure-redistribution surfaces
diagnostics: Expect serum albumin and prealbumin to be ordered
Monitor wound measurements and staging progression
Expect wound cultures if signs of infection present
Expect nutritional assessment referral
Monitor Braden Scale score regularly
Expect CBC if infection suspected
management: Reposition every 2 hours on a turning schedule
Use pressure-redistribution surfaces (mattresses, cushions)
Maintain nutritional support (high protein, vitamin C) as ordered
Keep skin clean and dry (moisture barrier cream for incontinence)
Follow wound care protocol as ordered for each stage
Avoid positioning directly on the wound
nursingActions: Perform skin assessments every shift and document findings
Reposition patient every 2 hours and document position changes
Assess and document wound characteristics (size, depth, drainage, odor)
Monitor nutritional intake and report inadequate intake
Keep linens wrinkle-free to reduce friction
Report any skin changes or wound deterioration immediately
Educate patient and caregivers on pressure injury prevention

tinea-corporis.title: Tinea Corporis (Ringworm)

cellular.title: Dermatophyte Infection
cellular.content: Superficial dermatophyte fungal infection. Dermatophytes digest keratin in skin. Spread by direct contact or fomites.

riskFactors: Direct contact with infected person or animal

left: Ring-shaped lesion
Raised erythematous border
Central clearing
right: Pruritus
Scaling
May spread to other body areas

medications.name: Clotrimazole
medications.action: Disrupts fungal cell membrane

medications.name: Terbinafine
medications.action: Inhibits squalene epoxidase in fungal cells

pearls: Keep area clean and dry
Do not share personal items (towels, combs, clothing)
Complete full course of treatment even if symptoms resolve
Wash clothing and linens in hot water
diagnostics: Expect KOH preparation of skin scraping for fungal hyphae
Monitor for secondary bacterial infection of lesions
Expect fungal culture if diagnosis uncertain
Monitor treatment response over 2-4 weeks
management: Keep affected area clean and dry
Apply topical antifungal as prescribed (extend 1 inch beyond border)
Avoid sharing personal items during treatment
Wash clothing and linens in hot water
Complete full course of treatment even if symptoms resolve
nursingActions: Assess lesion characteristics (size, borders, scaling)
Educate patient on proper application of topical medication
Report signs of secondary infection (increased redness, warmth, pus)
Teach infection prevention measures to family
Assess for lesions on other body areas (scalp, feet, groin)

oral-candidiasis.title: Oral Candidiasis (Thrush)

cellular.title: Opportunistic Fungal Overgrowth
cellular.content: Candida albicans overgrowth, opportunistic. Common in immunocompromised, infants, and inhaled corticosteroid users.

left: White patches on tongue/buccal mucosa
Patches can be scraped off
Red base underneath patches
right: Difficulty swallowing
Cottony feeling in mouth
Altered taste

medications.name: Nystatin
medications.action: Binds to fungal cell membrane sterols

medications.name: Fluconazole
medications.action: Inhibits fungal cytochrome P450

pearls: Rinse mouth after inhaled corticosteroids to prevent thrush
Sterilize pacifiers and bottle nipples for infants
Assess immune status if recurrent
Good oral hygiene is essential
diagnostics: Expect visual oral examination for white patches
Monitor for spread to esophagus (dysphagia indicates esophageal involvement)
Expect KOH preparation if diagnosis uncertain
Assess immune status if recurrent episodes
management: Administer nystatin suspension as prescribed (swish and swallow)
Rinse mouth after inhaled corticosteroid use to prevent occurrence
Sterilize pacifiers and bottle nipples for infants
Maintain good oral hygiene
Remove dentures for antifungal treatment
nursingActions: Assess oral cavity for white patches on tongue and buccal mucosa
Educate on proper nystatin administration technique (hold in mouth before swallowing)
Report difficulty swallowing to provider (may indicate esophageal spread)
Remind patients using inhaled corticosteroids to rinse mouth after each use
Monitor nutritional intake if oral discomfort affects eating

oral-candidiasis-rn.title: Oral Candidiasis - RN Clinical Decision-Making

cellular.title: Candida Albicans Pathogenesis & Mucosal Immune Breakdown
cellular.content:
Candida albicans is a dimorphic fungus that normally colonizes the oral cavity as a commensal yeast form. Under conditions of immune suppression or microbial dysbiosis, Candida undergoes a morphogenetic switch from yeast to hyphal form, which is critical for tissue invasion. The hyphal form produces invasive filaments that penetrate the epithelial barrier, triggering mucosal inflammation and the characteristic white pseudomembranous plaques.

Mucosal immune defense against Candida depends heavily on the T-helper 17 (Th17) cell axis. Th17 cells produce interleukin-17 (IL-17) and IL-22, which recruit neutrophils to the oral mucosa and stimulate epithelial cells to produce antimicrobial peptides such as β-defensins and histatins. When Th17 immunity is compromised  -  as in HIV/AIDS, chemotherapy-induced lymphopenia, or chronic corticosteroid use  -  the IL-17 signaling pathway is disrupted, allowing Candida to proliferate unchecked and transition to its invasive hyphal form.

Biofilm formation is another key virulence mechanism. Candida adheres to mucosal surfaces and denture materials, forming structured biofilms that are resistant to host immune clearance and antifungal penetration. Biofilm-associated Candida can be up to 1000 times more resistant to fluconazole than planktonic cells. This is clinically significant in denture wearers and patients with prolonged hospitalization.

In immunocompromised patients, oropharyngeal candidiasis can progress to esophageal candidiasis (an AIDS-defining illness when CD4 count falls below 200 cells/μL) and, in severe cases, candidemia with hematogenous dissemination to deep organs including the liver, spleen, kidneys, and endocardium. The transition from localized mucosal infection to systemic candidiasis carries significant mortality (30-40% for candidemia) and requires urgent systemic antifungal therapy.

riskFactors: Inhaled corticosteroid use without mouth rinsing
Broad-spectrum antibiotic therapy disrupting normal flora

left: Pseudomembranous: white plaques that scrape off revealing erythematous base
Erythematous: red atrophic patches without white plaques (denture stomatitis)
Angular cheilitis: fissuring at mouth corners
Hyperplastic: white plaques that do NOT scrape off (premalignant concern)
right: Dysphagia/odynophagia: esophageal extension warning sign
Recurrent episodes: investigate underlying immunodeficiency
Candidemia signs: fever, hypotension, multi-organ involvement
Biofilm formation on dentures: treatment failure risk

medications.name: Nystatin Oral Suspension
medications.action: Binds to ergosterol in fungal cell membrane causing membrane disruption and cell lysis

medications.name: Fluconazole
medications.action: Inhibits lanosterol 14α-demethylase (CYP51) blocking ergosterol synthesis in fungal cell membrane

medications.name: Clotrimazole Troches
medications.action: Inhibits ergosterol synthesis via local mucosal contact

pearls: Always investigate underlying cause of recurrent oral candidiasis  -  may be first presentation of HIV
Esophageal candidiasis is an AIDS-defining illness and requires systemic antifungal therapy
Biofilm on dentures requires physical removal plus chemical disinfection  -  antifungals alone are insufficient
Nystatin contains sucrose  -  use fluconazole in diabetic patients when possible
diagnostics: Perform thorough oral examination documenting distribution and morphology of lesions
Differentiate pseudomembranous (white plaques) from erythematous (red atrophic) and angular cheilitis forms
Expect KOH preparation showing budding yeast and pseudohyphae
Monitor for dysphagia or odynophagia indicating esophageal extension
Assess CD4 count in HIV patients (esophageal candidiasis is AIDS-defining below 200)
Expect fungal culture with speciation for recurrent or refractory cases
Monitor hepatic function if systemic antifungals initiated
management: Administer nystatin suspension as prescribed for mild cases (swish and swallow technique)
Administer fluconazole as prescribed for moderate-severe or recurrent cases
Ensure mouth rinsing after each inhaled corticosteroid dose to prevent recurrence
Remove and disinfect dentures nightly with chlorhexidine or dilute bleach solution
Address underlying immunosuppression where possible
Monitor for esophageal extension and report dysphagia immediately
Implement oral hygiene protocol including gentle tooth brushing and antimicrobial rinses
nursingActions: Perform comprehensive oral assessment each shift documenting location, size, and type of lesions
Assess for clinical signs differentiating immunocompromised vs immunocompetent presentation
Monitor nutritional intake and caloric adequacy when oral discomfort limits eating
Educate on proper nystatin technique (hold in mouth 2-5 minutes before swallowing)
Assess for signs of systemic dissemination in high-risk patients (fever, hemodynamic instability)
Report new-onset dysphagia or odynophagia immediately as potential esophageal candidiasis
Coordinate with pharmacy regarding drug interactions if fluconazole initiated (CYP3A4 inhibitor)
Educate immunocompromised patients on daily oral self-inspection

oral-candidiasis-np.title: Oral Candidiasis - NP Prescriptive & Molecular Management

cellular.title: Molecular Pathogenesis & Antifungal Resistance Mechanisms
cellular.content:
Candida albicans pathogenicity is driven by a coordinated arsenal of virulence factors at the molecular level. Secreted aspartyl proteases (SAPs 1-10) are a family of extracellular enzymes that degrade host mucosal proteins including E-cadherin, secretory IgA, complement components, and extracellular matrix proteins. SAPs 1-3 are predominantly expressed during mucosal infection and facilitate tissue invasion by disrupting epithelial integrity. SAP expression is regulated by environmental pH and nutrient availability, with acidic conditions favoring SAP2 production.

Agglutinin-like sequence (ALS) adhesins, particularly ALS3, mediate initial attachment to host epithelial cells and are essential for biofilm initiation. ALS3 functions as an invasin that triggers receptor-mediated endocytosis by binding to E-cadherin and N-cadherin on host cells, enabling Candida to actively penetrate epithelial barriers without requiring hyphal mechanical force alone.

The yeast-to-hypha morphogenetic switch is governed by multiple signaling pathways including the cAMP-PKA pathway, MAP kinase cascades, and transcription factors (Efg1, Cph1, Rim101). Environmental cues such as temperature (37°C), serum, CO₂ levels, neutral pH, and N-acetylglucosamine trigger this transition. The hyphal form expresses hypha-specific genes including HWP1 (hyphal wall protein 1), which serves as a substrate for host transglutaminases, covalently bonding the fungus to epithelial cells.

Azole resistance in Candida involves multiple mechanisms: (1) upregulation of ERG11 encoding the target enzyme lanosterol 14α-demethylase, (2) point mutations in ERG11 reducing drug binding affinity, (3) overexpression of efflux pumps (CDR1/CDR2 belonging to the ABC transporter family and MDR1 of the major facilitator superfamily), and (4) biofilm-mediated resistance through extracellular matrix sequestration of drug molecules. Echinocandin resistance develops through mutations in FKS1 and FKS2 genes encoding the catalytic subunit of β-1,3-glucan synthase, the drug target. These hot-spot mutations (particularly S645 in FKS1) reduce enzyme sensitivity to echinocandins by 50-3000 fold.

Non-albicans species (C. glabrata, C. krusei, C. auris) are increasingly prevalent in immunocompromised populations. C. krusei has intrinsic fluconazole resistance, C. glabrata frequently develops acquired azole resistance, and C. auris presents a global emerging threat with multi-drug resistance including resistance to all three major antifungal classes.

riskFactors: Solid organ transplant on calcineurin inhibitors
Head and neck irradiation with mucositis
ICU patients with central venous catheters
Patients on biologics targeting IL-17 pathway (secukinumab)

left: Oropharyngeal: pseudomembranous, erythematous, hyperplastic, or angular cheilitis variants
Esophageal: odynophagia, dysphagia, retrosternal pain; ulceration on EGD
Chronic mucocutaneous: recurrent oral/cutaneous/nail candidiasis from genetic immune defects
Candidemia: fever unresponsive to antibiotics, positive blood cultures
right: Azole-refractory disease: persistent infection after 14 days of adequate fluconazole dosing
Non-albicans species: C. glabrata (dose-dependent susceptibility), C. krusei (intrinsic resistance)
C. auris: emerging multi-drug resistant species with high transmission risk
Hepatosplenic candidiasis: persistent fever, elevated ALP, characteristic target lesions on imaging

medications.name: Fluconazole
medications.action: Inhibits lanosterol 14α-demethylase (CYP51) blocking ergosterol biosynthesis; excellent oral bioavailability (>90%) and CSF penetration

medications.name: Micafungin
medications.action: Inhibits β-1,3-glucan synthase disrupting fungal cell wall integrity; fungicidal against most Candida species

medications.name: Voriconazole
medications.action: Broader-spectrum CYP51 inhibition including activity against azole-resistant strains with specific ERG11 mutations

pearls: Azole resistance mechanisms are cumulative  -  patients with prolonged azole exposure develop progressively higher MICs through sequential acquisition of ERG11 mutations and efflux pump upregulation
C. auris is an emerging global threat with resistance to all three major antifungal classes  -  requires contact precautions and aggressive environmental decontamination
Chronic mucocutaneous candidiasis in children/young adults warrants genetic testing for STAT1 gain-of-function, STAT3 mutations, or IL-17 pathway defects
Echinocandin MIC creep in C. glabrata is an evolving concern  -  always request susceptibility testing for non-albicans isolates
diagnostics: Perform oral examination with speciation request on fungal culture (not just C. albicans)
Order antifungal susceptibility testing for recurrent or refractory cases
Evaluate CD4 count, HIV viral load, and complete immune panel
Order upper endoscopy (EGD) for suspected esophageal candidiasis with biopsy and brushings
Consider β-D-glucan assay and blood cultures if invasive candidiasis suspected
Assess hepatic and renal function before initiating systemic antifungals
Review medication list for CYP450 interactions with azoles
Order T-cell subset analysis if chronic mucocutaneous candidiasis suspected
management: Prescribe fluconazole 200 mg loading dose followed by 100-200 mg daily for 7-14 days for oropharyngeal disease
For esophageal candidiasis: fluconazole 400 mg loading then 200-400 mg daily for 14-21 days
For azole-refractory disease: prescribe itraconazole oral solution 200 mg daily or voriconazole 200 mg BID
For echinocandin-eligible refractory cases: micafungin 150 mg IV daily or caspofungin 70 mg load then 50 mg IV daily
Initiate secondary prophylaxis with fluconazole 100 mg daily in HIV patients until CD4 > 200 sustained
Address predisposing factors: optimize glycemic control, reduce immunosuppression when possible, manage xerostomia
For denture-associated candidiasis: prescribe miconazole denture lacquer or chlorhexidine soaks plus systemic therapy
Consider therapeutic drug monitoring for voriconazole (target trough 1-5.5 μg/mL)
nursingActions: Coordinate antifungal susceptibility testing and follow up on speciation results
Monitor for azole-associated hepatotoxicity (LFTs at baseline and 2 weeks)
Assess for QTc prolongation with azole therapy (baseline and follow-up ECG)
Monitor for echinocandin infusion reactions (histamine-mediated flushing)
Ensure appropriate dose adjustments for renal and hepatic impairment
Educate patients on long-term prophylaxis adherence and signs of breakthrough infection
Coordinate with infectious disease for refractory cases or multi-drug resistant species
Monitor for drug-drug interactions with azoles (warfarin, statins, calcineurin inhibitors, anticonvulsants)

cdiff-basics.title: C. Difficile Infection

cellular.title: Toxin-Mediated Colitis
cellular.content: Clostridioides difficile produces toxins A and B causing colitis. Overgrowth after antibiotic use disrupts normal flora. Spore-forming organism.

left: Watery diarrhea (>3 stools/day)
Foul-smelling stool
Abdominal cramping
right: Fever
Leukocytosis
Dehydration risk

medications.name: Oral Vancomycin
medications.action: First-line treatment for C. diff colitis

medications.name: Fidaxomicin
medications.action: Narrow-spectrum, targets C. diff

medications.name: Metronidazole
medications.action: Alternative for non-severe cases

pearls: Contact precautions with SOAP AND WATER - alcohol gel does NOT kill spores
Private room required
Bleach-based cleaning solutions required
Do not use antidiarrheals - they trap toxins in the colon
diagnostics: Expect stool sample for C. diff toxin assay
Monitor stool frequency, consistency, and volume
Expect CBC (leukocytosis is common)
Monitor for dehydration indicators (electrolytes, BUN/creatinine)
Expect abdominal X-ray if toxic megacolon suspected
management: Implement contact precautions immediately
Use soap and water for hand hygiene (alcohol does NOT kill spores)
Use bleach-based disinfectant for environmental cleaning
Discontinue offending antibiotics if possible
Avoid antidiarrheals (trap toxins in the colon)
Maintain hydration and electrolyte replacement
nursingActions: Monitor stool frequency, amount, and characteristics
Implement strict contact precautions with dedicated equipment
Perform hand hygiene with soap and water before and after care
Monitor for dehydration (skin turgor, mucous membranes, urine output)
Report signs of toxic megacolon (severe distension, fever, tachycardia)
Educate patient and visitors on contact precautions and hand hygiene

pertussis-basics.title: Pertussis (Whooping Cough)

cellular.title: Respiratory Cilia Damage
cellular.content: Bordetella pertussis toxin damages respiratory cilia. Three stages: catarrhal (most contagious, URI symptoms), paroxysmal (severe coughing fits with inspiratory whoop), convalescent (gradual recovery).

riskFactors: Infants too young for vaccination (< 2 months)
Waning immunity in adolescents and adults

left: Paroxysmal cough
Inspiratory whoop
Post-tussive vomiting
right: Cyanosis in infants
Apnea (infants may not whoop)
Exhaustion between paroxysms

medications.name: Azithromycin
medications.action: Treatment and post-exposure prophylaxis

medications.name: DTaP Vaccine
medications.action: Prevention - primary series in childhood

pearls: Droplet precautions required
Infants may not produce the classic whoop but present with apnea
Tdap booster in pregnancy (27-36 weeks) protects newborn
Most contagious during catarrhal stage
diagnostics: Expect nasopharyngeal swab for PCR testing
Monitor oxygen saturation especially in infants
Expect CBC (lymphocytosis is characteristic)
Monitor respiratory status for apnea episodes in infants
Expect chest X-ray if pneumonia suspected
management: Implement droplet precautions until 5 days of antibiotic therapy completed
Administer prescribed azithromycin as ordered
Maintain oxygen therapy as needed
Monitor respiratory status closely in infants
Isolate patient from vulnerable populations
Ensure post-exposure prophylaxis for close contacts
nursingActions: Implement droplet precautions and place patient in private room
Monitor respiratory status including oxygen saturation
Report apnea episodes in infants immediately
Suction secretions gently as needed
Provide calm environment to minimize coughing paroxysms
Educate on completing full course of antibiotics
Verify immunization status of patient and close contacts

atopic-dermatitis.title: Atopic Dermatitis (Eczema)

cellular.title: Impaired Skin Barrier
cellular.content: Chronic inflammatory skin condition. Impaired skin barrier (filaggrin deficiency) allows allergen penetration. IgE-mediated hypersensitivity. Part of atopic triad (asthma, allergic rhinitis, eczema).

riskFactors: Family history of atopy (asthma, allergic rhinitis, eczema)

left: Intense pruritus
Erythematous patches
Dry/scaly skin
right: Lichenification from chronic scratching
Flexural distribution (older children/adults)
Facial distribution in infants

medications.name: Emollients
medications.action: Restores skin barrier

medications.name: Hydrocortisone
medications.action: Reduces inflammation

medications.name: Diphenhydramine
medications.action: Reduces pruritus

pearls: Avoid triggers: harsh soaps, wool, extreme temperatures
Short lukewarm baths (not hot)
Cotton clothing preferred
Keep nails short to prevent excoriation
diagnostics: Expect clinical diagnosis based on presentation and history
Monitor for secondary bacterial infection (impetiginization)
Expect allergy testing if triggers unclear
Monitor serum IgE levels (often elevated)
Assess skin integrity with each visit
management: Apply emollients immediately after bathing (first-line treatment)
Use topical corticosteroids as prescribed for flares
Avoid known triggers (harsh soaps, wool, extreme temperatures)
Use lukewarm baths (not hot water)
Dress in cotton clothing
Keep fingernails short to prevent excoriation
nursingActions: Assess skin for erythema, scaling, lichenification, and excoriation
Educate on emollient application technique (apply within 3 minutes of bathing)
Report signs of secondary infection (increased redness, weeping, crusting)
Teach trigger avoidance strategies to patient and family
Monitor for side effects of topical corticosteroids (skin thinning)
Provide emotional support for chronic condition management

impetigo.title: Impetigo

cellular.title: Bacterial Skin Infection
cellular.content: Impetigo is a highly contagious superficial bacterial skin infection caused primarily by Staphylococcus aureus (most common) or Group A Streptococcus (Streptococcus pyogenes). The bacteria invade the epidermis through breaks in the skin (insect bites, abrasions, eczema). Non-bullous impetigo (70% of cases) begins as vesicles that rupture and form characteristic honey-colored crusted lesions. Bullous impetigo is caused by toxin-producing S. aureus that causes large fluid-filled blisters. The infection spreads rapidly through direct contact and autoinoculation (touching lesions then touching other body areas).

left: Honey-colored (golden-yellow) crusted lesions: hallmark finding
Erythematous base beneath crusts
Lesions commonly on face (perioral, perinasal) and extremities
Pruritus (itching) with surrounding erythema
Regional lymphadenopathy may be present
right: Highly contagious: spreads by direct contact and autoinoculation
Contact Precautions required until 24 hours after antibiotic treatment started
Bullous form: Large fluid-filled blisters (S. aureus toxin-mediated)
Risk factors: Poor hygiene, crowded living, warm humid climate, skin breaks
Complication: Post-streptococcal glomerulonephritis (with GAS strains)

medications.name: Mupirocin (Bactroban) 2% Ointment
medications.action: Inhibits bacterial protein synthesis by binding to isoleucyl-tRNA synthetase, effective against S. aureus and S. pyogenes

medications.name: Cephalexin (Keflex)
medications.action: Inhibits bacterial cell wall synthesis; effective against gram-positive organisms including S. aureus and S. pyogenes

pearls: Strict hand hygiene is the most important measure to prevent spread. Wash hands thoroughly after touching lesions.
Keep child's fingernails trimmed short to prevent scratching and autoinoculation to other body areas.
Do NOT share towels, washcloths, clothing, or personal items. Launder linens and clothing in hot water separately.
Child may return to school/daycare 24 hours after starting antibiotic treatment and lesions are no longer weeping.
diagnostics: Visual assessment of lesion characteristics (honey-crusted)
Monitor for spread to other body areas
Assess for signs of systemic infection
Monitor for post-streptococcal complications (glomerulonephritis)
management: Apply topical antibiotics as prescribed (mupirocin)
Administer oral antibiotics as ordered for widespread cases
Gently remove crusts with warm soaks before applying medication
Keep affected areas clean and dry
Prevent spread through good hygiene
nursingActions: Assess lesion characteristics and document progression
Teach proper wound cleansing technique (warm soaks to remove crusts)
Educate on preventing spread (separate towels, hand hygiene)
Monitor for signs of post-streptococcal glomerulonephritis (cola-colored urine, facial edema)
Educate on completing full course of antibiotics
Report worsening or spreading lesions

head-lice.title: Head Lice (Pediculosis Capitis)

cellular.title: Parasitic Infestation
cellular.content: Pediculosis capitis is an infestation of the scalp by the human head louse (Pediculus humanus capitis). The louse is an obligate ectoparasite that feeds on human blood from the scalp, causing intense pruritus from an allergic reaction to louse saliva. Female lice lay eggs (nits) that cement firmly to the hair shaft near the scalp. Nits hatch in 7-10 days, and the louse life cycle is approximately 30 days. Transmission occurs primarily through direct head-to-head contact and less commonly through shared personal items (combs, hats, pillows). Head lice do NOT transmit disease and are not a sign of poor hygiene.

left: Intense scalp pruritus (itching): primary symptom
Nits (eggs) firmly attached to hair shafts near scalp, especially behind ears and nape
Live lice visible on scalp and hair (small, grayish-white, wingless insects)
Excoriations (scratch marks) on scalp from intense itching
Secondary bacterial infection of excoriated areas possible
right: Nits are oval, yellowish-white, firmly cemented to hair shaft (do not flake off like dandruff)
Transmission: Direct head-to-head contact (most common), shared combs/hats/pillows
NOT a sign of poor hygiene: affects all socioeconomic groups
School-age children (3-11 years) most commonly affected
No disease transmission: lice are a nuisance, not a health hazard

medications.name: Permethrin 1% (Nix) Shampoo
medications.action: Disrupts sodium channel function in louse nerve cells, causing paralysis and death of lice and nits

medications.name: Nit Comb (Fine-Toothed)
medications.action: Physically removes nits and dead lice from hair shafts through systematic combing

pearls: Treat ALL household contacts and close contacts simultaneously to prevent re-infestation cycle.
Wash all bedding, clothing, and towels in HOT water (130°F/54°C) and dry on high heat. Items that cannot be washed should be sealed in plastic bags for 2 weeks.
Do NOT share combs, brushes, hats, hair accessories, helmets, or pillows. Educate children about not sharing these items at school.
Nits found >1/4 inch from scalp are likely non-viable (already hatched or dead): focus treatment on nits close to the scalp.
diagnostics: Visual inspection of scalp for nits (eggs) and live lice
Assess behind ears and at nape of neck (preferred sites)
Monitor for secondary excoriation from scratching
Assess family members and close contacts
management: Apply pediculicide shampoo as prescribed (permethrin)
Remove nits with fine-toothed comb after treatment
Wash all bedding and clothing in hot water (130°F)
Vacuum upholstered furniture and carpets
Repeat treatment in 7-10 days as directed
nursingActions: Educate family on proper application of pediculicide
Teach nit removal technique with fine-toothed comb
Instruct on environmental decontamination (bedding, brushes)
Educate that head lice do not jump or fly (direct contact transmission)
Screen household contacts for infestation
Reassure family that head lice are not a sign of poor hygiene

pinworms.title: Pinworms (Enterobiasis)

cellular.title: Parasitic Intestinal Infection
cellular.content: Enterobiasis is caused by Enterobius vermicularis, a small white parasitic nematode (roundworm) that infects the human intestinal tract. The infection cycle begins with ingestion of microscopic pinworm eggs via the fecal-oral route (contaminated hands, food, or fomites). Eggs hatch in the small intestine, and larvae migrate to the cecum and appendix where they mature into adult worms. At night, gravid female worms migrate to the perianal area to deposit thousands of eggs, causing intense perianal pruritus. Scratching deposits eggs under fingernails, perpetuating the cycle through autoinoculation and person-to-person transmission. Pinworm is the most common helminth infection in the United States, predominantly affecting school-age children.

left: Intense perianal pruritus (itching), especially at night when female worms migrate to lay eggs
Restlessness, irritability, and disturbed sleep due to nocturnal itching
Perianal excoriation and erythema from persistent scratching
Visible small white thread-like worms (~1cm) in stool or perianal area
Occasional abdominal pain, nausea, or decreased appetite
right: Tape Test (Scotch Tape Test): Gold standard for diagnosis: apply transparent tape to perianal area in early morning BEFORE bathing
Fecal-oral transmission: eggs survive on surfaces, clothing, bedding for 2-3 weeks
Autoinfection cycle: scratching → eggs under nails → hand-to-mouth → reinfection
Entire family/household typically infected: treat ALL members simultaneously
Most common in school-age children (5-10 years); often asymptomatic carriers

medications.name: Mebendazole (Vermox)
medications.action: Inhibits microtubule synthesis in the worm, blocking glucose uptake and depleting glycogen stores, leading to parasite death

medications.name: Albendazole (Albenza)
medications.action: Inhibits tubulin polymerization, disrupting glucose absorption and energy metabolism in the parasite

pearls: Tape Test (Scotch Tape Test) is the diagnostic method: apply clear adhesive tape to perianal area FIRST thing in morning before bathing or bowel movement, then examine under microscope for eggs.
Treat ALL family members/household contacts simultaneously, even if asymptomatic, to break the cycle of reinfection.
Strict hand hygiene: wash hands thoroughly with soap and water after toileting and before eating. Keep fingernails trimmed very short.
Wash all bedding, towels, and undergarments in HOT water daily during treatment period. Avoid shaking linens (disperses eggs into air).
diagnostics: Tape test (transparent tape applied to perianal area first thing in morning)
Assess for perianal itching especially at night
Monitor for signs of secondary skin infection from scratching
management: Administer mebendazole or albendazole as prescribed
Treat all household members simultaneously
Wash all bedding and clothing in hot water
Maintain strict hand hygiene before meals and after toileting
Repeat treatment in 2 weeks as ordered
nursingActions: Educate family on tape test collection technique (early morning before bathing)
Teach thorough hand hygiene before eating and after toileting
Instruct on keeping fingernails short to prevent reinfection
Educate on washing bedding in hot water daily during treatment
Teach that all household members should be treated simultaneously
Monitor for perianal excoriation and provide skin care

diaper-candidiasis.title: Diaper Candidiasis (Diaper Yeast Infection)

cellular.title: Candida Albicans Fungal Infection
cellular.content: Diaper candidiasis is a superficial fungal infection of the perineal skin caused by Candida albicans, an opportunistic yeast that thrives in warm, moist, occluded environments such as the diaper area. Candida is part of normal GI flora and colonizes the perineum; overgrowth occurs when the skin barrier is compromised by prolonged moisture exposure, friction, and alkaline pH from urine and stool. The hallmark feature distinguishing candidal diaper dermatitis from irritant contact diaper rash is the presence of satellite lesions (small papules and pustules surrounding the main erythematous area) and involvement of skin folds (intertriginous areas). Candidal diaper rash typically develops after 3+ days of existing diaper rash that has not responded to standard barrier creams.

riskFactors: Oral thrush (Candida spreads through GI tract)

left: Beefy red, sharply demarcated erythematous rash in perineal area
Satellite lesions (small papulopustules extending beyond the border of main rash)
Involvement of skin folds and creases (intertriginous areas)
Rash does NOT respond to standard diaper barrier creams (zinc oxide)
May have white patches or scaling at margins of lesions
right: Distinguishing feature: Satellite lesions + skin fold involvement (vs. irritant rash which spares folds)
Often develops after antibiotic use (disrupts normal flora, allows Candida overgrowth)
May coexist with oral thrush (check mouth for white patches)
Warm, moist, occluded diaper environment promotes Candida growth
Risk factors: Prolonged diaper wetness, diarrhea, recent antibiotic use, immunosuppression

medications.name: Nystatin Cream/Ointment
medications.action: Binds to ergosterol in the fungal cell membrane, creating pores that increase membrane permeability, leading to cell death

medications.name: Clotrimazole 1% Cream
medications.action: Inhibits ergosterol synthesis by blocking the enzyme lanosterol 14-alpha-demethylase, disrupting fungal cell membrane integrity

pearls: Key differentiator from regular irritant diaper rash: Satellite lesions (papulopustules beyond the rash border) and involvement of skin FOLDS (irritant rash typically spares skin folds).
Keep diaper area clean and DRY: change diapers frequently (at least every 2 hours and immediately after soiling). Allow diaper-free air exposure time when possible.
Check for concurrent oral thrush (white patches on buccal mucosa/tongue). If present, treat both simultaneously to prevent reinfection cycle via GI tract.
Teach parents: Apply antifungal cream FIRST, then barrier cream (zinc oxide) on top if needed. Do NOT use cornstarch (promotes fungal growth). Avoid baby powder (aspiration risk).
diagnostics: Visual assessment of rash (beefy red with satellite lesions)
Differentiate from irritant diaper dermatitis (sharp borders vs. diffuse)
Assess oral cavity for concurrent thrush
Monitor for secondary bacterial infection
management: Apply antifungal cream as prescribed (nystatin or clotrimazole)
Change diapers frequently to keep area dry
Allow diaper-free time for air exposure
Gently cleanse with mild soap and warm water
Avoid occlusive barrier creams over antifungal
nursingActions: Assess rash characteristics (beefy red, satellite lesions indicate Candida)
Apply antifungal medication before barrier cream
Educate parents on frequent diaper changes
Teach gentle cleansing technique (no wipes with alcohol/fragrance)
Monitor for improvement with treatment
Assess mouth for concurrent oral thrush (treat simultaneously)

head-to-toe-rpn.title: Head-to-Toe Assessment (RPN/LVN)

cellular.title: Systematic Assessment Framework
cellular.content:
A head-to-toe assessment is a systematic, cephalocaudal (head-to-foot) physical examination performed at the beginning of each shift, upon patient admission, and whenever clinical status changes. The four techniques used in sequence are: Inspection (visual observation), Palpation (touch), Percussion (tapping to assess density), and Auscultation (listening with stethoscope). For abdominal assessment, the order changes: Inspect → Auscultate → Percuss → Palpate (auscultate BEFORE palpation/percussion to avoid altering bowel sounds).

The assessment follows a systematic order:
1. General Survey: Appearance, posture, hygiene, LOC, distress level
2. Head: Skull symmetry, hair distribution, scalp lesions, facial symmetry
3. Eyes: PERRLA (Pupils Equal Round Reactive to Light and Accommodation), visual acuity, conjunctiva color, sclera
4. Ears: External ear inspection, hearing response, tympanic membrane if trained
5. Nose and Sinuses: Patency, discharge, tenderness over sinuses
6. Mouth and Throat: Mucous membranes, dentition, tongue, tonsils, gag reflex
7. Neck: ROM, JVD, thyroid, lymph nodes, tracheal position (midline)
8. Chest/Respiratory: Respiratory rate/pattern/effort, lung sounds all fields bilaterally
9. Cardiovascular: Heart sounds (S1, S2), rate/rhythm, peripheral pulses, capillary refill, edema
10. Abdomen: Inspect → Auscultate → Percuss → Palpate, bowel sounds all 4 quadrants
11. Musculoskeletal: ROM, strength, symmetry, gait
12. Neurological: LOC, orientation (person/place/time/situation), cranial nerves, sensation, reflexes
13. Integumentary: Skin color, turgor, moisture, lesions, pressure points
14. Peripheral Vascular: Pulses, temperature, color, edema, Homans sign (historical)

riskFactors: Incomplete assessment leading to missed findings
Altered level of consciousness limiting subjective data

left: General: Alert and oriented x4, appropriate affect, no acute distress
HEENT: Normocephalic, PERRLA 3mm bilaterally, moist mucous membranes, trachea midline
Respiratory: Lungs clear to auscultation bilaterally, RR 12-20, SpO2 >95%, no accessory muscle use
Cardiovascular: S1/S2 present, regular rate and rhythm, peripheral pulses 2+ bilaterally, cap refill <3 sec
Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, non-distended, bowel sounds active all 4 quadrants
Neuro: Alert, oriented x4, pupils equal and reactive, moves all extremities equally
right: Abnormal LOC: Confusion, lethargy, not oriented: escalate immediately
Unequal pupils: Anisocoria may indicate increased ICP or CN III compression: URGENT
Crackles/wheezing/diminished breath sounds: Assess SpO2, position upright, notify provider
Irregular rhythm, new murmur, absent pulses: Obtain 12-lead ECG, assess perfusion
Rigid/distended abdomen, absent bowel sounds: May indicate obstruction or peritonitis: NPO, notify provider
New weakness, facial droop, speech changes: Activate stroke protocol (FAST) immediately

medications.name: Stethoscope
medications.action: Acoustic device used for auscultation of heart sounds (S1, S2, murmurs), lung sounds (crackles, wheezes, rhonchi), bowel sounds, and blood pressure. Bell for low-pitched sounds (S3, S4, murmurs), diaphragm for high-pitched sounds (S1, S2, breath sounds, bowel sounds)

medications.name: Penlight
medications.action: Used to assess pupillary response (direct and consensual), oral cavity inspection, and skin assessment in poorly lit areas. Pupil assessment: Shine light from lateral side into each eye, assess for brisk constriction (direct response) and constriction in opposite eye (consensual response)

pearls: Always perform the assessment in the same systematic order to avoid missing findings: consistency prevents errors.
Compare bilaterally: Always compare one side to the other (lung sounds, pulses, pupils, strength, sensation). Asymmetry is often more significant than individual findings.
For abdominal assessment, remember: Inspect → Auscultate → Percuss → Palpate. Auscultate BEFORE palpation because touching the abdomen can alter bowel sounds.
Document using approved terminology: 'Lungs CTA bilaterally' (clear to auscultation), 'Abdomen NTND' (non-tender, non-distended), 'A&Ox4' (alert and oriented to person, place, time, situation).
Cluster assessments: Perform all interventions during one visit to minimize patient disruption and maximize rest periods. However, never rush an assessment: thoroughness prevents missed findings.

lifespan.title: Across the Lifespan
lifespan.content:
Pediatric: Assess fontanelles in infants (bulging = increased ICP, sunken = dehydration). Use pediatric-specific vital sign ranges. Perform assessment in parent's lap for toddlers. Leave invasive assessments (ears, throat) for last.

Elderly: Allow extra time. Assess for fall risk, skin breakdown (check bony prominences), cognitive changes. Distinguish acute confusion (delirium) from chronic (dementia). Medications may mask normal assessment findings.

diagnostics: Systematic cephalocaudal assessment using inspection, palpation, auscultation
Vital signs measurement (T, P, R, BP, SpO2, pain)
Skin assessment (color, temperature, turgor, integrity)
Neurological assessment (LOC, orientation, pupil response)
management: Report abnormal findings to RN/charge nurse promptly
Document all findings accurately and completely
Compare current findings to patient baseline
Prioritize assessment based on patient condition
Escalate concerns using SBAR communication
nursingActions: Perform systematic head-to-toe assessment at beginning of each shift
Assess neurological status (LOC, orientation, pupils)
Assess cardiovascular (heart sounds, peripheral pulses, edema)
Assess respiratory (breath sounds, rate, effort, SpO2)
Assess GI (bowel sounds, abdominal tenderness, last BM)
Assess skin integrity, IV sites, drains, and wounds
Document findings and report changes from baseline

vital-signs-assessment.title: Vital Signs: Complete Assessment Guide

cellular.title: Physiological Basis of Vital Signs
cellular.content:
Vital signs reflect the body's core physiological functions and homeostatic mechanisms. They are the most fundamental assessment data collected by nurses.

Temperature: Regulated by the hypothalamus. Core body temperature reflects the balance between heat production (metabolism, muscle activity, hormones like thyroxine) and heat loss (radiation, conduction, convection, evaporation). Normal: 36.5-37.5°C (97.7-99.5°F).

Pulse: Each heartbeat sends a pressure wave through the arterial system. Rate, rhythm, and amplitude reflect cardiac function, volume status, and autonomic tone. Normal adult: 60-100 bpm.

Respiration: Controlled by the medullary respiratory center, with input from central and peripheral chemoreceptors sensing CO2, O2, and pH. Rate, depth, rhythm, and effort reflect respiratory and metabolic status. Normal adult: 12-20 breaths/min.

Blood Pressure: The force exerted by circulating blood on vessel walls. Systolic = ventricular contraction; Diastolic = ventricular relaxation. Determined by cardiac output (HR × stroke volume) and systemic vascular resistance. Normal adult: <120/80 mmHg.

Pain (5th vital sign): Subjective experience influenced by nociceptor activation, inflammatory mediators, and CNS processing. Always assessed alongside traditional vital signs.

Pulse Oximetry: SpO2 measures the percentage of hemoglobin saturated with oxygen using light absorption. Normal: 95-100%.

left: Temperature: Oral 36.5-37.5°C, Axillary subtract 0.3-0.6°C, Rectal add 0.3-0.6°C, Tympanic comparable to core
Pulse: Rate 60-100 bpm, Regular rhythm, 2+ amplitude, Radial most common site
Respirations: Rate 12-20/min, Regular rhythm, Unlabored, No accessory muscle use
BP: <120/80 normal, Proper cuff size (bladder 80% of arm circumference), Support arm at heart level
SpO2: 95-100% normal, >92% acceptable for COPD patients, Apply to warm, well-perfused digit
right: Fever >38.3°C: Tachycardia, diaphoresis, may indicate infection: obtain cultures before antibiotics
Tachycardia >100: Pain, fever, dehydration, anxiety, hemorrhage, hypoxia: investigate the cause
Bradycardia <60: May be normal in athletes, or indicate medication effect (beta-blockers), increased ICP, hypothyroidism
Tachypnea >20: Early sign of respiratory distress, metabolic acidosis, pain, anxiety: assess SpO2
Hypotension <90/60: Assess for hemorrhage, sepsis, dehydration, medication effect: position supine, IV fluids
SpO2 <90%: Respiratory failure: apply O2, elevate HOB, prepare for escalation

medications.name: Sphygmomanometer
medications.action: Measures arterial blood pressure using an inflatable cuff to occlude the brachial artery and a manometer to measure the pressure. Korotkoff sounds are auscultated: Phase 1 (first sound = systolic), Phase 5 (disappearance = diastolic). Automated devices use oscillometry

pearls: Count respirations without the patient knowing: if they are aware, they may consciously alter their breathing pattern. Count while appearing to take the pulse (keep fingers on wrist).

Orthostatic (postural) vital signs: Measure BP and HR lying → sitting → standing (wait 1-3 min between positions). A drop of ≥20 mmHg systolic, ≥10 mmHg diastolic, or HR increase ≥20 bpm is positive for orthostatic hypotension.
Widening pulse pressure (systolic rises, diastolic falls) with bradycardia = Cushing's triad: late sign of increased ICP. This is a medical emergency.
Never delegate the first assessment of vital signs to unlicensed personnel: the RPN/RN must establish the baseline.
Pain is subjective: the patient's self-report is the most reliable indicator. Use age-appropriate scales: NRS (0-10) for adults, Wong-Baker FACES for children, FLACC for nonverbal patients.
diagnostics: Temperature measurement (oral, tympanic, temporal, rectal, axillary)
Pulse assessment (rate, rhythm, quality)
Respiratory assessment (rate, depth, pattern)
Blood pressure measurement (correct cuff size, positioned at heart level)
Oxygen saturation via pulse oximetry
management: Report abnormal vital signs to RN/charge nurse immediately
Retake vital signs if values seem inconsistent with patient status
Monitor trending of vital signs over time
Implement standing orders for out-of-range values if applicable
nursingActions: Measure vital signs at prescribed intervals and PRN
Use correct equipment and technique for each measurement
Report abnormal values immediately using SBAR
Trend vital signs to identify patterns of deterioration
Document all values accurately with time of measurement
Assess pain as the 5th vital sign using appropriate scale

pain-assessment-rpn.title: Pain Assessment Scales (NRS, FACES, Wong-Baker)

cellular.title: Neurophysiology of Pain
cellular.content:
Pain is a complex sensory and emotional experience involving multiple neural pathways.

Nociception involves four steps:
1. Transduction: Noxious stimuli (thermal, mechanical, chemical) activate nociceptors (free nerve endings) in peripheral tissues, converting the stimulus into electrical signals. Inflammatory mediators (prostaglandins, bradykinin, substance P, histamine) lower the pain threshold (sensitization).
2. Transmission: Signals travel via A-delta fibers (fast, sharp, well-localized pain) and C fibers (slow, dull, diffuse, aching pain) to the dorsal horn of the spinal cord, then ascend via the spinothalamic tract to the thalamus and somatosensory cortex.
3. Perception: The brain interprets the signal as pain. This is where pain becomes a CONSCIOUS experience. Influenced by attention, emotion, past experience, and cultural factors.
4. Modulation: Descending pathways from the brainstem release endorphins, enkephalins, serotonin, and norepinephrine that can inhibit or amplify pain signals (gate control theory).

Pain is always subjective: the patient's self-report is the single most reliable indicator of pain. Pain is whatever the patient says it is, occurring whenever they say it does (Margo McCaffery).

riskFactors: History of substance abuse (may underreport or overreport)

left: Numeric Rating Scale (NRS): Patient rates pain 0-10 (0=no pain, 10=worst imaginable). Most widely used for adults and children >8 years who can self-report
Wong-Baker FACES Scale: 6 facial expressions from smiling (no hurt) to crying (hurts worst). For children ages 3-8 years and adults with communication barriers
FLACC Scale: Face, Legs, Activity, Cry, Consolability: scored 0-2 each (total 0-10). For infants, preverbal children, and cognitively impaired adults who cannot self-report
CPOT (Critical Care Pain Observation Tool): For intubated/sedated ICU patients. Assesses facial expression, body movements, muscle tension, ventilator compliance
Behavioral indicators: Grimacing, guarding, splinting, moaning, restlessness, diaphoresis, tachycardia, hypertension, decreased mobility
right: Acute Pain: Sudden onset, identifiable cause, time-limited. Associated with autonomic responses (tachycardia, hypertension, diaphoresis, pupil dilation)
Chronic Pain: Persists >3-6 months beyond expected healing time. Autonomic adaptation occurs (vital signs may be NORMAL despite severe pain). Depression and sleep disturbance common
Neuropathic Pain: Burning, tingling, shooting, electric shock quality. Results from nerve damage (diabetic neuropathy, phantom limb, postherpetic neuralgia)
Referred Pain: Felt at a location distant from the source. Examples: Cardiac pain → left arm/jaw, Gallbladder → right shoulder (Kehr sign), Appendicitis → periumbilical → RLQ (McBurney)
Breakthrough Pain: Transient flare of pain that exceeds baseline despite around-the-clock analgesic therapy. Requires PRN rescue dose

medications.name: Acetaminophen (Tylenol)
medications.action: Inhibits prostaglandin synthesis in the CNS (not peripherally: therefore NO anti-inflammatory effect). Provides mild-moderate pain relief and fever reduction

medications.name: Morphine
medications.action: Binds mu-opioid receptors in the CNS and spinal cord, inhibiting ascending pain pathways and altering the emotional response to pain. Provides moderate-severe pain relief

pearls: The patient's self-report is always the GOLD STANDARD for pain assessment: believe the patient. Pain is subjective and cannot be objectively measured.
Reassess pain after every intervention: 30 min after IV medication, 60 min after oral medication, 15-20 min after repositioning or non-pharmacological intervention.

Multimodal analgesia is best practice: Combine non-opioid (acetaminophen, NSAIDs), opioid (if needed), and non-pharmacological methods (positioning, ice/heat, distraction, relaxation) for synergistic effect and reduced opioid requirements.
For nonverbal patients (intubated, cognitively impaired, neonates), use behavioral pain tools (FLACC, CPOT) rather than assuming no pain. Absence of self-report does NOT equal absence of pain.
Document: Pain intensity (using standardized scale), location, quality (sharp/dull/burning/aching), onset, duration, aggravating/alleviating factors, impact on function, and patient's pain goal.
diagnostics: Numeric Pain Scale (0-10) for adults who can self-report
Wong-Baker FACES scale for children and non-verbal patients
FLACC scale for infants and young children
Behavioral assessment for non-verbal/cognitively impaired patients
Vital signs (pain may cause tachycardia, hypertension)
management: Administer prescribed analgesics as ordered
Implement non-pharmacological pain interventions (positioning, ice/heat, distraction)
Reassess pain 30 minutes after IV medication, 60 minutes after oral medication
Report uncontrolled pain to RN/physician
Document pain level before and after interventions
nursingActions: Assess pain using appropriate validated scale
Believe patient's self-report of pain (gold standard)
Administer prescribed analgesics and reassess effectiveness
Use non-pharmacological interventions as adjunct to medication
Document pain assessment, interventions, and reassessment
Report inadequate pain relief to provider for order adjustment

gcs-assessment-rpn.title: Glasgow Coma Scale (GCS)

cellular.title: Neurological Basis of the GCS
cellular.content:
The Glasgow Coma Scale is the most widely used standardized tool for assessing level of consciousness and neurological function. It evaluates three independent responses: Eye Opening, Verbal Response, and Motor Response. Total score ranges from 3 (deep coma/brain death) to 15 (fully alert and oriented).

Eye Opening (E): Tests the arousal component of consciousness, primarily mediated by the reticular activating system (RAS) in the brainstem.
• 4 = Spontaneous (eyes open without stimulation: RAS functioning)
• 3 = To voice/command (opens eyes when spoken to)
• 2 = To pressure/pain (opens eyes only to painful stimulus)
• 1 = None (no eye opening even with pain: severe brainstem dysfunction)

Verbal Response (V): Tests the cognitive/awareness component, primarily involving the cerebral cortex and language centers (Broca and Wernicke areas).
• 5 = Oriented (knows who, where, when, and what happened)
• 4 = Confused (speaks in sentences but is disoriented)
• 3 = Inappropriate words (random words, no sustained conversation)
• 2 = Incomprehensible sounds (moaning, groaning, no words)
• 1 = None (no verbal response)

Motor Response (M): Tests both cortical and brainstem motor pathways. This is the most clinically significant component.
• 6 = Obeys commands (follows instructions: 'lift your arms')
• 5 = Localizes pain (reaches toward and tries to remove the pain source)
• 4 = Withdrawal/flexion (pulls away from pain: spinal reflex)
• 3 = Abnormal flexion (decorticate posturing: arms flexed, legs extended: cortical damage)
• 2 = Extension (decerebrate posturing: arms and legs extended and internally rotated: brainstem damage)
• 1 = None (no motor response: severe brainstem injury)

Critical thresholds: GCS ≤8 = Severe (coma: cannot protect airway, intubation indicated), GCS 9-12 = Moderate, GCS 13-15 = Mild.

left: GCS 15: Fully alert, oriented, follows commands: normal neurological baseline
GCS 13-14: Mild impairment: confused but responsive, monitor closely q1-2h
GCS 9-12: Moderate impairment: may open eyes to pain, confused speech, localizes pain
Trend is critical: A GCS drop of ≥2 points is significant and requires immediate intervention
Motor component is the strongest predictor of outcome: document and trend separately
right: GCS ≤8: SEVERE: coma, cannot protect airway. Intubation indicated. Neurosurgical consult.
Decorticate posturing (GCS Motor 3): Arms flexed to chest, legs extended: damage ABOVE brainstem (cortical). Memory: 'cor' = core = flexion toward body core
Decerebrate posturing (GCS Motor 2): Arms and legs extended, internally rotated: damage AT brainstem level. WORSE prognosis. Memory: 'de-cerebrate' = brain is being destroyed
Unequal pupils + declining GCS: Herniation syndrome: EMERGENCY. Elevate HOB 30°, hyperventilate, mannitol
GCS cannot be accurately assessed if patient is sedated, paralyzed, or intubated (verbal component untestable: document as 'T' for tube)

medications.name: Mannitol
medications.action: Creates an osmotic gradient that draws water from cerebral tissue into the intravascular space, reducing cerebral edema and intracranial pressure (ICP)

pearls: Always report the TOTAL score AND individual components: E4V5M6 = GCS 15. The breakdown matters more than the total: a patient with E4V1M6 (GCS 11) has a very different clinical picture than E3V4M4 (also GCS 11).
A GCS drop of ≥2 points from baseline is clinically significant and requires IMMEDIATE reassessment and provider notification.
Decorticate (flexion) is BETTER than decerebrate (extension): both are ominous, but decerebrate indicates damage at a deeper (brainstem) level with worse prognosis.
For intubated patients, document verbal component as '1T' or 'VT' and calculate the score without the verbal component (best possible = E4VTM6).

Apply painful stimuli correctly: Trapezius squeeze (pinch the trapezius muscle) or central pressure (sternal rub) for central stimulation; nail bed pressure for peripheral stimulation. Peripheral stimulus tests spinal reflexes, not brain function.
diagnostics: Eye opening response (1-4 points)
Verbal response (1-5 points)
Motor response (1-6 points)
Total GCS score (3-15)
Pupil size and reactivity assessment
management: Report GCS ≤ 8 immediately (indicates need for airway protection)
Report any decrease of 2+ points from baseline
Monitor trends over time rather than single values
Implement safety precautions for decreased LOC
nursingActions: Assess and calculate GCS score at prescribed intervals
Document each component separately (E, V, M) plus total score
Report declining GCS immediately to RN/physician
Assess pupil size, equality, and reactivity
Report GCS ≤ 8 immediately (patient cannot protect airway)
Compare current GCS to baseline and trend over time

braden-scale-rpn.title: Braden Scale for Pressure Injury Risk

cellular.title: Pathophysiology of Pressure Injuries
cellular.content:
Pressure injuries (formerly 'pressure ulcers' or 'bedsores') develop when sustained pressure on soft tissue exceeds capillary closing pressure (~32 mmHg), causing localized ischemia, tissue hypoxia, and cell death. The damage occurs from the inside out: deep tissue injury often precedes visible skin changes.

The Braden Scale is the most widely used evidence-based tool for predicting pressure injury risk. It assesses SIX subscales, each scored 1-4 (except friction/shear which is 1-3):

1. Sensory Perception (1-4): Ability to respond to pressure-related discomfort. Score 1 = completely limited (unconscious/paralyzed), Score 4 = no impairment.
2. Moisture (1-4): Degree to which skin is exposed to moisture. Score 1 = constantly moist, Score 4 = rarely moist.
3. Activity (1-4): Degree of physical activity. Score 1 = bedfast, Score 4 = walks frequently.
4. Mobility (1-4): Ability to change and control body position. Score 1 = completely immobile, Score 4 = no limitations.
5. Nutrition (1-4): Usual food intake pattern. Score 1 = very poor, Score 4 = excellent.
6. Friction and Shear (1-3): Score 1 = problem (requires assistance, slides in bed), Score 3 = no apparent problem.

Total Score: Range 6-23. LOWER scores = HIGHER risk.
• 15-18 = Mild risk
• 13-14 = Moderate risk
• 10-12 = High risk
• ≤9 = Very high risk

left: Stage 1: Non-blanchable erythema of intact skin: redness that does NOT turn white when pressed. First visible sign. Skin may be warm, firm, or painful
Stage 2: Partial-thickness skin loss: shallow open ulcer with red-pink wound bed. May present as intact or ruptured blister. NO slough or eschar
Stage 3: Full-thickness skin loss: subcutaneous fat may be visible, but bone/tendon/muscle NOT exposed. Slough may be present. Undermining and tunneling may occur
Stage 4: Full-thickness tissue loss with EXPOSED bone, tendon, or muscle. Slough or eschar may be present. Often includes undermining and tunneling
right: Unstageable: Full-thickness loss: base of ulcer covered by slough (yellow/tan/gray) or eschar (black/brown). TRUE depth cannot be determined until slough/eschar is removed
Deep Tissue Injury (DTI): Intact skin that is purple/maroon or blood-filled blister. Indicates damage to underlying tissue from pressure/shear. May rapidly evolve to deeper stages
Risk factors: Immobility (#1 risk), malnutrition, incontinence, decreased sensation, advanced age, diabetes, vascular disease, obesity, friction/shear
Prevention is KEY: Reposition q2h, float heels off bed, nutrition optimization (protein, calories, vitamin C, zinc), moisture management, pressure-redistribution mattress

medications.name: Pressure-Redistribution Mattress
medications.action: Distributes body weight over a larger surface area, reducing peak interface pressure below capillary closing pressure (32 mmHg). Types include alternating pressure, low-air-loss, and air-fluidized surfaces

pearls: Lower Braden Score = Higher risk. Remember: 'Low is Bad': a score of 9 is very high risk, while 23 is no risk.

Reposition every 2 hours: this is the single most important nursing intervention for pressure injury prevention. Use the 30-degree lateral position (not 90-degree) to avoid pressure on the trochanter.
Float heels off the bed completely: heels are the second most common site for pressure injuries (after sacrum). Use a pillow under the calves, NOT under the heels.
Assess nutritional status: Protein (albumin, prealbumin), calories, vitamin C, and zinc are essential for tissue integrity and wound healing. Malnutrition is a major modifiable risk factor.
Document Braden Scale score on admission and every shift (or per policy). Implement a care plan based on the score: interventions should escalate as the score decreases.
diagnostics: Sensory perception assessment (1-4 points)
Moisture exposure assessment (1-4 points)
Activity level assessment (1-4 points)
Mobility assessment (1-4 points)
Nutrition assessment (1-4 points)
Friction and shear assessment (1-3 points)
management: Reposition every 2 hours (use 30-degree lateral position)
Use pressure-redistribution mattress for high-risk patients
Optimize nutrition (high protein, vitamin C, zinc)
Manage moisture and incontinence promptly
Float heels off bed using pillow under calves
nursingActions: Perform Braden Scale assessment on admission and every shift
Implement prevention interventions based on score (lower score = higher risk)
Reposition patient every 2 hours and document
Assess skin over bony prominences each shift (sacrum, heels, occiput)
Report Braden score ≤ 12 (high risk) to RN/charge nurse
Document skin assessment findings and prevention measures implemented

comprehensive-health-assessment.title: Comprehensive Health Assessment (RN Level)

cellular.title: Framework for Comprehensive Assessment
cellular.content:
A comprehensive health assessment is the systematic collection of subjective and objective data that forms the foundation for the nursing process. It goes beyond the focused or shift assessment to create a complete picture of the patient's health status, including physical, psychological, social, cultural, and spiritual dimensions.

Components:

A. Health History (Subjective Data):
1. Biographical data: Age, sex, occupation, education, marital status
2. Chief complaint (CC): The reason for seeking care, in patient's own words
3. History of Present Illness (HPI): OLDCARTS: Onset, Location, Duration, Character, Aggravating factors, Relieving factors, Timing, Severity
4. Past Medical History (PMH): Chronic illnesses, surgeries, hospitalizations, injuries, transfusions
5. Medications: Prescription, OTC, herbals, supplements: with doses, frequency, adherence
6. Allergies: Drug, food, environmental: document reaction type (anaphylaxis vs intolerance)
7. Family History (FH): Hereditary conditions, causes of death in first-degree relatives
8. Social History (SH): Tobacco, alcohol, drugs, exercise, diet, occupation, living situation, support system
9. Review of Systems (ROS): Systematic head-to-toe symptom inquiry across all body systems

B. Physical Examination (Objective Data):
Systematic cephalocaudal assessment using inspection, palpation, percussion, and auscultation (IPPA). Includes general survey, vital signs, and all body systems.

C. Functional Assessment:
ADLs (Activities of Daily Living): Bathing, dressing, toileting, transferring, continence, feeding
IADLs (Instrumental ADLs): Cooking, cleaning, managing finances, transportation, medication management

D. Psychosocial Assessment:
Mood, affect, coping mechanisms, support systems, spiritual needs, cultural considerations, health literacy

riskFactors: Cognitive impairment affecting health history accuracy
Multiple comorbidities with complex medication regimens

left: Subjective Data (what the patient tells you): Chief complaint, symptom description (OLDCARTS), medical history, medication list, allergies, family history, social history, review of systems, pain level, functional status
