Health History Mnemonics: HPI = OLDCARTS (Onset, Location, Duration, Character, Aggravating, Relieving, Timing, Severity). Allergies = document REACTION (rash vs anaphylaxis). Social = ETOH, tobacco, drugs quantified

Review of Systems: Systematically ask about EACH body system: constitutional (weight changes, fever, fatigue), HEENT, respiratory, cardiovascular, GI, GU, musculoskeletal, neurological, psychiatric, integumentary
Functional Assessment: Katz Index (ADLs), Lawton Scale (IADLs), fall risk, mobility level, cognitive status, sensory deficits (vision, hearing)
right: Objective Data (what you observe/measure): Vital signs, physical exam findings, diagnostic results, behavior, appearance, gait, affect

Physical Exam: General survey → VS → HEENT → Neck → Chest/Lungs → Heart/Vascular → Abdomen → MSK → Neuro → Integumentary → GU (as indicated). Compare bilaterally. Document normal AND abnormal findings
Documentation: Use approved frameworks: SOAPIE (Subjective, Objective, Assessment, Plan, Intervention, Evaluation), or DAR (Data, Action, Response), or Narrative
Critical Thinking: Don't just collect data: INTERPRET it. Cluster findings, identify patterns, recognize deviations from normal, prioritize problems, and develop a plan of care

medications.name: Assessment Documentation Tools
medications.action: Standardized methods for organizing and communicating assessment data: SBAR (Situation, Background, Assessment, Recommendation) for handoff; SOAPIE for charting; Gordon's Functional Health Patterns for holistic assessment organization

pearls: ALWAYS start with the patient's chief complaint: what brought them here? This guides the depth and focus of your assessment even within a comprehensive framework.

Use therapeutic communication during the health history: Open-ended questions first ('Tell me about your pain'), then focused/closed-ended questions to clarify ('Is it sharp or dull?'). Avoid leading questions.

Document pertinent negatives: what you specifically looked for and did NOT find. 'Denies chest pain, SOB, palpitations' is as important as documenting positive findings for building the clinical picture.

The assessment is not a one-time event: it is continuous. Initial comprehensive assessment → ongoing focused assessments → reassessment after every intervention. Clinical status changes require reassessment.

Cultural competence: Ask about health beliefs, traditional remedies, dietary practices, religious considerations, decision-making patterns, and communication preferences. Use professional interpreters for language barriers: NEVER family members for medical interpretation.
diagnostics: Comprehensive health history using OLDCARTS framework
Complete head-to-toe physical examination (IPPA)
Functional assessment (ADLs and IADLs)
Psychosocial and spiritual assessment
Review of systems across all body systems
Diagnostic test review and correlation with clinical findings
management: Develop nursing care plan based on assessment findings
Prioritize problems using clinical judgment (Maslow's hierarchy)
Initiate appropriate nursing interventions for identified problems
Collaborate with interdisciplinary team for complex findings
Plan for reassessment intervals based on acuity
nursingActions: Collect comprehensive subjective data (health history, ROS, social history)
Perform systematic physical examination using inspection, palpation, percussion, auscultation
Assess functional status using validated tools (Katz ADL, Lawton IADL)
Evaluate psychosocial needs (coping, support system, spiritual concerns)
Document findings completely and accurately
Identify and prioritize nursing diagnoses based on assessment data

primary-survey-rn.title: Primary Survey (ABCDE) Assessment

cellular.title: Trauma and Emergency Assessment Framework
cellular.content:
The Primary Survey is a rapid, systematic assessment approach used in trauma and emergency situations to identify and immediately treat life-threatening conditions. It follows the ABCDE framework, addressing threats to life in order of priority. Each step must be assessed and managed BEFORE moving to the next: you cannot assess breathing if the airway is compromised.

A: Airway (with C-spine protection):
• Is the airway patent? Look for obstruction (tongue, blood, vomit, foreign body, edema)
• Listen for stridor, gurgling, snoring (signs of partial obstruction)
• If trauma suspected: Maintain c-spine immobilization with jaw thrust (NOT head-tilt chin-lift)
• Interventions: Suction, oral/nasal airway, intubation if needed

B: Breathing:
• Is the patient breathing? Rate, depth, rhythm, symmetry, effort
• Expose the chest: look for tracheal deviation, JVD, flail chest, open wounds
• Auscultate bilaterally: absent/diminished breath sounds?
• SpO2 monitoring
• Life threats: Tension pneumothorax, open pneumothorax, massive hemothorax, flail chest
• Interventions: O2, needle decompression, chest seal, intubation

C: Circulation:
• Is there a pulse? Rate, quality, regularity
• Signs of shock: Tachycardia, hypotension, cool/pale/diaphoretic skin, delayed cap refill
• Identify and control hemorrhage: Direct pressure, tourniquet, pelvic binder
• Two large-bore IVs (14-16 gauge), warm crystalloid or blood products
• Life threats: Hemorrhagic shock, cardiac tamponade, massive hemorrhage

D: Disability (Neurological):
• AVPU: Alert, Verbal response, Pain response, Unresponsive
• GCS (Glasgow Coma Scale): Eye + Verbal + Motor = 3-15
• Pupils: Size, equality, reactivity (PERRLA)
• Blood glucose (hypoglycemia can mimic neurological emergency)
• Lateralizing signs: Unilateral weakness, unequal pupils

E: Exposure/Environment:
• Completely undress the patient (cut clothing if needed) to identify ALL injuries
• Log roll to assess posterior surfaces
• Prevent hypothermia: Warm blankets, warm IV fluids, warm environment
• The 'lethal triad' of trauma: Hypothermia + Acidosis + Coagulopathy

riskFactors: Distracting injuries masking more critical problems
Unresponsive patient with no available history
Mass casualty situations with limited resources

left: A: Patent airway: no stridor, gurgling, or snoring. Speaking in full sentences. Trachea midline
B: Breathing: bilateral chest rise, clear lung sounds, RR 12-20, SpO2 >95%, no accessory muscle use
C: Circulation: pulse present, strong and regular, BP within normal limits, cap refill <3 sec, warm and dry skin, no active hemorrhage
D: Disability: Alert (AVPU), GCS 15, PERRLA, blood glucose normal, no lateralizing signs
E: Fully exposed and inspected: no additional injuries identified, patient kept warm
right: A: Compromised: Stridor, gurgling, inability to speak, cyanosis: IMMEDIATELY intervene: suction, position, airway adjunct, prepare for intubation
B: Compromised: Absent breath sounds, tracheal deviation, JVD, respiratory distress: consider tension pneumothorax (needle decompression), massive hemothorax (chest tube)
C: Compromised: Tachycardia, hypotension, cool/pale skin, active bleeding: control hemorrhage, 2 large-bore IVs, fluid resuscitation, prepare for transfusion
D: Compromised: GCS ≤8 (protect airway), unequal pupils (herniation), focal deficits (stroke vs mass lesion): CT scan, neurosurgical consult
E: Hypothermia: Core temp <35°C in trauma = lethal triad risk. Active warming mandatory. Log roll to check posterior: missed injuries kill

medications.name: Tranexamic Acid (TXA)
medications.action: Inhibits plasminogen activation and fibrinolysis, stabilizing blood clots and reducing hemorrhage. Used in trauma with significant hemorrhage or risk of hemorrhage

pearls: ABCDE is SEQUENTIAL: you must fix 'A' before assessing 'B.' A patient with an obstructed airway will die before any other problem matters. Airway is ALWAYS first.
In trauma, assume c-spine injury until cleared: use jaw thrust (NOT head-tilt chin-lift) to open the airway. Maintain manual in-line stabilization during all airway maneuvers.

The most common preventable cause of death in trauma is uncontrolled hemorrhage. Control bleeding with direct pressure first, then tourniquet for extremity hemorrhage that is not controlled by direct pressure.
Reassess ABCDE after EVERY intervention: the primary survey is not a one-time assessment. The patient's condition can change rapidly, and previously stable elements can deteriorate.

The secondary survey (complete head-to-toe) is only performed AFTER the primary survey is complete and all life threats are addressed. Never get distracted by dramatic-looking but non-life-threatening injuries.
diagnostics: A - Airway assessment with cervical spine protection
B - Breathing assessment (rate, depth, bilateral chest rise, SpO2)
C - Circulation assessment (pulse quality, skin color/temp, capillary refill, hemorrhage control)
D - Disability assessment (GCS, pupil response, glucose level)
E - Exposure/Environment (full body examination, temperature management)
management: Address life threats in ABCDE sequence
Activate rapid response or code team as needed
Initiate BLS/ACLS protocols as indicated
Establish IV access and cardiac monitoring
Prepare for definitive interventions (intubation, chest decompression, blood products)
nursingActions: Perform rapid primary survey using ABCDE framework
Address airway obstruction before moving to breathing assessment
Control hemorrhage with direct pressure
Assess neurological status rapidly (GCS, pupils)
Expose patient fully while preventing hypothermia
Reassess primary survey after each intervention

nih-stroke-scale.title: NIH Stroke Scale (NIHSS) Assessment

cellular.title: Standardized Stroke Severity Assessment
cellular.content:
The National Institutes of Health Stroke Scale (NIHSS) is a systematic, validated assessment tool used to quantify stroke severity, guide treatment decisions (particularly thrombolytic eligibility), and predict patient outcomes. It is performed at initial presentation, before and after thrombolytic administration, every 6 hours for the first 24 hours, and at discharge.

The NIHSS evaluates 11 categories of neurological function, each scored on a scale (typically 0-2, 0-3, or 0-4), with 0 = normal. Total score ranges from 0 to 42.

1a. Level of Consciousness (LOC): 0-3 (alert → coma)
1b. LOC Questions: 0-2 (month and age: both correct → neither correct)
1c. LOC Commands: 0-2 (open/close eyes, grip/release: both correct → neither)
2. Best Gaze: 0-2 (normal → forced deviation)
3. Visual Fields: 0-3 (no loss → bilateral hemianopia/blind)
4. Facial Palsy: 0-3 (normal → complete paralysis bilateral)
5a/5b. Motor Arm (L/R): 0-4 (no drift → no movement)
6a/6b. Motor Leg (L/R): 0-4 (no drift → no movement)
7. Limb Ataxia: 0-2 (absent → present in 2 limbs)
8. Sensory: 0-2 (normal → severe/total loss)
9. Best Language: 0-3 (no aphasia → mute/global aphasia)
10. Dysarthria: 0-2 (normal → unintelligible/anarthric)
11. Extinction/Inattention: 0-2 (no abnormality → profound neglect)

Score interpretation:
• 0 = No stroke symptoms
• 1-4 = Minor stroke
• 5-15 = Moderate stroke
• 16-20 = Moderate-severe stroke
• 21-42 = Severe stroke

tPA eligibility: Generally considered for NIHSS ≥4 within the treatment window (up to 4.5 hours from onset for IV tPA). NIHSS >25 may have increased hemorrhagic transformation risk.

riskFactors: Posterior circulation stroke (may be missed by NIHSS)
Young patients with stroke risk factors

left: Score 0-4 (Minor): Minimal deficits, likely lacunar stroke. May not qualify for tPA but still requires workup, monitoring, and secondary prevention
Score 5-15 (Moderate): Significant deficits: weakness, speech changes, visual field loss. Good candidate for thrombolytic therapy within window
Score 16-20 (Moderate-Severe): Major deficits with significant disability likely. Consider thrombectomy for large vessel occlusion
Score 21-42 (Severe): Devastating stroke with dense hemiplegia, global aphasia, gaze deviation. High risk of hemorrhagic transformation with tPA
right: Time is Brain: Every minute of ischemic stroke destroys approximately 1.9 million neurons. Door-to-needle time goal for tPA: ≤60 minutes
NIHSS must be performed by CERTIFIED assessors: consistency and accuracy are critical for treatment decisions and trending
Score EACH item independently: do not let findings from one category influence scoring of another. Score what you SEE, not what you think is happening
Document the EXACT time of onset (or last known normal): this determines the treatment window. If onset is unknown, use the last time the patient was seen at baseline
Reassess NIHSS: Before tPA, 2 hours after tPA, 24 hours post-treatment, at discharge, and any time there is a clinical change

medications.name: Alteplase (tPA / Activase)
medications.action: Converts plasminogen to plasmin, which enzymatically degrades fibrin clots, restoring blood flow to ischemic brain tissue. Must be administered within 4.5 hours of symptom onset (3 hours for >80 years old or certain contraindications)

pearls: NIHSS scoring must be precise and consistent: even small score changes guide major treatment decisions (tPA, thrombectomy). Certification training is required.
Score what the patient DOES, not what you think they CAN do. If the patient doesn't attempt the task, score it as unable. Don't coach or encourage beyond standard prompts.
Motor drift assessment: Ask patient to hold arms extended (palms up) at 90° sitting or 45° supine for 10 seconds. ANY downward drift before 10 seconds is abnormal. Test each arm separately.

The NIHSS underestimates posterior circulation strokes (vertebrobasilar): a patient can have a devastating basilar artery occlusion with a relatively low NIHSS score because ataxia, vertigo, and cranial nerve deficits are not heavily weighted.

Always correlate NIHSS with imaging. A high NIHSS with a large vessel occlusion on CTA = candidate for mechanical thrombectomy (up to 24 hours in select patients with salvageable tissue on perfusion imaging).
diagnostics: Level of consciousness assessment (LOC questions and commands)
Best gaze (horizontal eye movements)
Visual fields testing
Facial palsy assessment
Motor arm and leg drift testing (5 and 10 seconds)
Limb ataxia testing
Sensory assessment
Best language (naming, reading, describing)
Dysarthria assessment
Extinction and inattention (double simultaneous stimulation)
management: Calculate and document total NIHSS score (0-42)
Report NIHSS ≥ 6 as moderate-severe stroke requiring intervention
Activate stroke team for acute presentations within treatment window
Serial NIHSS assessments to detect improvement or deterioration
Coordinate with stroke team for thrombolytic eligibility assessment
nursingActions: Perform NIHSS assessment accurately using standardized script
Document each component score and total score
Perform serial assessments at prescribed intervals (q1h initially)
Report any increase of ≥ 2 points immediately (worsening stroke)
Assess dysphagia before allowing oral intake
Monitor for complications of stroke (aspiration, hemorrhagic conversion, cerebral edema)

sepsis-screening-rn.title: Sepsis Screening (qSOFA, SIRS Criteria)

cellular.title: Pathophysiology of Sepsis and Screening Tools
cellular.content:
Sepsis is a life-threatening organ dysfunction caused by a dysregulated host response to infection. The immune system's overactivation triggers a cascade of inflammatory mediators (TNF-α, IL-1, IL-6), endothelial damage, microvascular thrombosis, and tissue hypoperfusion leading to progressive organ failure.

Screening Tools:

1. SIRS Criteria (Systemic Inflammatory Response Syndrome): ≥2 of the following:
• Temperature >38°C (100.4°F) OR <36°C (96.8°F)
• Heart rate >90 bpm
• Respiratory rate >20/min OR PaCO2 <32 mmHg
• WBC >12,000 OR <4,000 OR >10% bands (immature neutrophils)
SIRS is SENSITIVE but not SPECIFIC: many conditions trigger SIRS without infection.

2. qSOFA (Quick Sequential Organ Failure Assessment): ≥2 of:
• Respiratory rate ≥22/min
• Altered mentation (any change from baseline)
• Systolic blood pressure ≤100 mmHg
qSOFA is a bedside screening tool: it does NOT require lab work. Score ≥2 should trigger a full sepsis workup and SOFA calculation.

3. SOFA Score (Sequential Organ Failure Assessment): Assesses 6 organ systems (respiratory, coagulation, liver, cardiovascular, CNS, renal) with scores 0-4 each. An acute increase of ≥2 points from baseline in the setting of known/suspected infection = Sepsis.

4. Septic Shock: Sepsis PLUS vasopressor requirement to maintain MAP ≥65 mmHg AND serum lactate >2 mmol/L despite adequate volume resuscitation.

The SEP-1 bundle (CMS Core Measure) requires within 3 hours of sepsis recognition:
• Measure lactate
• Obtain blood cultures BEFORE antibiotics
• Administer broad-spectrum antibiotics
• Begin 30 mL/kg crystalloid for hypotension or lactate ≥4

Within 6 hours: Reassess volume status, repeat lactate if initial was elevated, vasopressors if hypotension persists after fluids.

left: SIRS Criteria (≥2): Temp >38°C or <36°C, HR >90, RR >20 or PaCO2 <32, WBC >12K or <4K or >10% bands
qSOFA (≥2): RR ≥22, altered mentation, SBP ≤100: bedside tool, no labs needed
Early sepsis signs: Tachycardia, tachypnea, fever or hypothermia, confusion/agitation, warm flushed skin (warm shock: vasodilation phase), decreased urine output
Lactate: Early marker of tissue hypoperfusion. Normal <2 mmol/L. Elevated lactate in suspected infection = RED FLAG for sepsis
right: Late/decompensated signs: Hypotension, cool/mottled/cyanotic skin (cold shock: vasoconstriction phase), oliguria, obtundation, DIC (petechiae, oozing)
Organ dysfunction indicators: PaO2/FiO2 <300 (lungs), platelets <100K (coag), bilirubin >2 (liver), creatinine >2 (kidneys), GCS <15 (CNS), MAP <70 (cardiovascular)
Septic Shock: MAP <65 despite fluids + vasopressors + lactate >2 mmol/L. Mortality 40-60%
Nursing priority: Recognize early, obtain cultures BEFORE antibiotics, administer antibiotics within 1 HOUR of recognition, aggressive fluid resuscitation (30 mL/kg crystalloid)

medications.name: Norepinephrine (Levophed)
medications.action: Potent alpha-1 agonist with moderate beta-1 activity. Causes peripheral vasoconstriction (raises SVR) and mild increase in cardiac output, raising MAP to target ≥65 mmHg

medications.name: Broad-Spectrum Antibiotics
medications.action: Empiric coverage targeting the most likely pathogens based on suspected source of infection. Administered WITHIN 1 HOUR of sepsis recognition: every hour of delay in antibiotics increases mortality by approximately 7.6%

pearls: Hour-1 Bundle is critical: Measure lactate, blood cultures, broad-spectrum antibiotics, begin crystalloid 30 mL/kg if hypotensive or lactate ≥4. Every hour of delay increases mortality.
Blood cultures BEFORE antibiotics: but NEVER delay antibiotics waiting for cultures. If cultures cannot be obtained within minutes, give antibiotics and draw cultures when possible.

Lactate is the most important biomarker for sepsis severity and treatment response. Repeat lactate within 2-4 hours if initial value is elevated. Lactate clearance >10% in 6 hours is associated with improved survival.

Fluid responsiveness: Not all septic patients respond to fluids. Assess for fluid responsiveness using passive leg raise test, pulse pressure variation, or stroke volume variation BEFORE giving additional fluid boluses.

Sepsis can present atypically in elderly (hypothermia instead of fever, confusion without fever), neonates (temperature instability, poor feeding, lethargy), and immunocompromised patients (blunted inflammatory response). Maintain a HIGH index of suspicion in vulnerable populations.
diagnostics: qSOFA screening (RR ≥ 22, altered mentation, SBP ≤ 100)
SIRS criteria (temp >38 or <36, HR >90, RR >20, WBC >12K or <4K)
Serum lactate level (≥ 2 mmol/L indicates tissue hypoperfusion)
Blood cultures (2 sets from different sites before antibiotics)
CBC with differential, BMP, coagulation studies
Procalcitonin level for bacterial infection differentiation
management: Initiate Hour-1 Sepsis Bundle: measure lactate, blood cultures, broad-spectrum antibiotics, 30 mL/kg crystalloid if hypotensive or lactate ≥ 4
Reassess volume status and tissue perfusion after initial fluid bolus
Initiate vasopressors if hypotension persists after fluid resuscitation (target MAP ≥ 65)
De-escalate antibiotics within 48-72 hours based on culture results
nursingActions: Screen all patients with suspected infection using qSOFA/SIRS criteria
Obtain blood cultures BEFORE antibiotic administration (but do not delay antibiotics)
Administer antibiotics within 1 hour of sepsis recognition
Initiate fluid resuscitation (30 mL/kg crystalloid) and monitor response
Monitor lactate levels and lactate clearance
Assess for end-organ dysfunction (urine output, mental status, perfusion)

comprehensive-hpi-np.title: Comprehensive History and Physical (NP Level)

cellular.title: Advanced Practice H&P Framework
cellular.content:
The comprehensive history and physical examination (H&P) is the cornerstone of advanced practice nursing. Unlike the focused assessment performed by RNs, the NP-level H&P involves autonomous clinical decision-making, differential diagnosis formulation, and the integration of subjective and objective data to determine a medical diagnosis and treatment plan.

The H&P follows the traditional medical model with nursing integration:

1. Chief Complaint (CC): In the patient's own words. Example: 'I've had chest pain for 3 hours.'

2. History of Present Illness (HPI): The most critical component. Uses OLDCARTS expanded with:
• Onset: Sudden vs gradual, what was patient doing?
• Location: Precise anatomical location, radiation pattern
• Duration: Constant vs intermittent, total duration, episode length
• Character: Patient's description of quality (sharp, dull, pressure, burning, tearing)
• Associated symptoms: What else occurred? (nausea, diaphoresis, SOB, fever)
• Aggravating/Relieving factors: Position, activity, food, medications tried
• Timing: Time of day, relationship to meals/activity/sleep
• Severity: 0-10 scale, impact on function, comparison to prior episodes
• Context: What does the patient think is wrong? What are they most concerned about?

3. Past Medical History: All chronic conditions, surgical history (dates, complications), hospitalizations, trauma, blood transfusions, psychiatric history, obstetric history (GTPAL)

4. Medications: Complete list with doses, routes, frequency, adherence, recent changes, OTC, herbals, supplements, recreational substances

5. Allergies: Drug, food, environmental, latex. Document the SPECIFIC REACTION (anaphylaxis vs GI upset vs rash: this determines clinical decision-making)

6. Family History: Three-generation genogram ideally. First-degree relatives: conditions and age of onset/death. Screen for: cardiovascular disease, diabetes, cancer, mental illness, autoimmune, genetic disorders

7. Social History: Expanded to include occupation (exposures), living situation, relationships, education level, health literacy, exercise, diet, tobacco (pack-years), alcohol (CAGE/AUDIT), illicit drugs, sexual history (partners, practices, protection, STI history), domestic violence screening, firearms access

8. Review of Systems (ROS): 14-system comprehensive review. Document both positive AND pertinent negative responses.

9. Physical Examination: Complete systematic exam with advanced techniques (ophthalmoscopy, otoscopy, speculum/bimanual exam as indicated)

10. Assessment/Differential Diagnosis: List of potential diagnoses ranked by probability. Include 'cannot miss' diagnoses (worst-case scenarios that must be ruled out)

11. Plan: Diagnostics, treatment, education, follow-up, referrals

riskFactors: Complex presentations with multiple overlapping symptoms
Patient unable to provide reliable history
Language or cultural barriers limiting history quality
Atypical presentations in elderly or immunocompromised

left: HPI Mastery: The HPI should read as a clinical narrative that paints a vivid picture. A well-written HPI can convey 80% of the diagnosis before the physical exam
Differential Diagnosis Process: Generate list → Rank by probability AND severity → Identify 'cannot miss' diagnoses → Order diagnostics to rule in/rule out systematically

Red Flags to Never Miss: Chest pain (ACS, PE, aortic dissection, tamponade), worst headache of life (SAH), new focal neuro deficits (stroke), fever with neutropenia (sepsis), acute abdomen (surgical emergency)
Clinical Reasoning: Bayesian thinking: prior probability × likelihood ratio = posterior probability. Pre-test probability determines whether a test is useful
right: E&M Coding: Documentation level determines billing. Comprehensive H&P = highest complexity (5+ HPI elements, 10+ ROS systems, complete PFSH, comprehensive exam, high complexity MDM)
Documentation Standards: Must support medical necessity for all diagnostics and treatments ordered. Pertinent negatives are essential for differential exclusion
Legal Considerations: The H&P is a medical-legal document. It must be accurate, timely, legible, and complete. Omissions can be interpreted as 'not performed'
Collaborative Practice: Know your state/provincial scope of practice. Some jurisdictions require collaborative agreements with physicians. Document all consultations and referrals
Evidence-Based Practice: Integrate clinical expertise + patient preferences + best available research evidence into every clinical decision

medications.name: Clinical Decision Support Tools
medications.action: Evidence-based tools that assist NPs in clinical reasoning: Wells Criteria (PE/DVT probability), HEART Score (chest pain risk stratification), CHA₂DS₂-VASc (stroke risk in AFib), CURB-65 (pneumonia severity), Ottawa Ankle/Knee Rules (imaging necessity), PHQ-9 (depression screening), GAD-7 (anxiety screening)

pearls: The HPI is the most diagnostically valuable component: a thorough HPI correctly suggests the diagnosis 75-80% of the time before you even examine the patient.

Always ask: 'What do you think is going on?' and 'What are you most worried about?': the patient's illness experience and concerns are essential for patient-centered care and may reveal critical information.

Document your clinical reasoning: Why did you order this test? Why did you choose this treatment? What diagnosis are you ruling out? This protects you legally and demonstrates medical decision-making complexity.

Red flag rule: For every chief complaint, have a mental list of 'cannot miss' diagnoses. Chest pain? Rule out MI, PE, aortic dissection, tamponade. Headache? Rule out SAH, meningitis, mass lesion, temporal arteritis.

The assessment is where you demonstrate NP-level thinking: Don't just list problems: synthesize the data into a coherent clinical picture, generate a ranked differential, and articulate your reasoning for the working diagnosis and plan.
diagnostics: Order targeted diagnostics based on HPI findings and differential
Order imaging guided by symptom localization and red flags
Order laboratory studies to rule in/out differential diagnoses
Order point-of-care testing for acute presentations
Order specialty consultations based on assessment findings
management: Develop differential diagnosis list ranked by probability and severity
Prescribe empiric treatment while awaiting diagnostic results if indicated
Order urgent workup for red-flag symptoms
Develop evidence-based treatment plan addressing root cause
Schedule follow-up based on clinical severity and diagnostic timeline
nursingActions: Obtain comprehensive HPI using OLDCARTS framework with detailed characterization
Elicit pertinent positives AND pertinent negatives for each differential
Perform complete review of systems to identify associated symptoms
Correlate physical examination findings with HPI data
Document clinical reasoning linking HPI to differential diagnoses
Identify red-flag symptoms requiring emergent evaluation

differential-diagnosis-np.title: Differential Diagnosis Formulation (NP Level)

cellular.title: Clinical Reasoning and Diagnostic Framework
cellular.content:
Differential diagnosis is the systematic process of distinguishing between diseases or conditions that share similar clinical features. It is the hallmark of advanced practice nursing and requires integrating knowledge of pathophysiology, epidemiology, clinical presentation patterns, and diagnostic testing.

The Diagnostic Reasoning Process:

1. Pattern Recognition (System 1 Thinking): Rapid, intuitive recognition based on experience. 'This looks like pneumonia.' Fast but prone to cognitive bias.

2. Analytical Reasoning (System 2 Thinking): Deliberate, systematic hypothesis testing. Generate → Test → Refine. Slower but more accurate for complex or atypical presentations.

3. Dual Process Model: Expert clinicians use BOTH: pattern recognition triggers initial hypotheses, then analytical reasoning tests and refines them.

Steps in Differential Diagnosis:
1. Gather complete clinical data (H&P)
2. Identify the problem representation: Summarize the case in one sentence. Example: '65-year-old male smoker with acute-onset pleuritic chest pain, dyspnea, and unilateral leg swelling.'
3. Generate an initial differential list (broad)
4. Apply illness scripts: Compare the patient's presentation against known disease patterns
5. Prioritize by probability AND severity (cannot-miss diagnoses)
6. Select and interpret diagnostic tests (understand pre-test probability, sensitivity, specificity)
7. Narrow the differential based on results
8. Reach a working diagnosis and initiate treatment
9. Monitor response: if treatment fails, reconsider the differential

Common Cognitive Biases:
• Anchoring: Locking onto an initial diagnosis despite contradictory evidence
• Availability: Overweighting diagnoses you've seen recently
• Premature Closure: Accepting a diagnosis before fully verifying it
• Confirmation Bias: Seeking information that supports your hypothesis while ignoring contradicting data
• Diagnosis Momentum: Once a diagnosis is assigned (by another provider), it becomes difficult to consider alternatives

riskFactors: Premature closure (accepting diagnosis before considering alternatives)
Atypical presentations in elderly, pediatric, or immunocompromised patients

left: Problem Representation: One-sentence summary capturing age, sex, risk factors, acuity, and key features. This frames your differential generation

Illness Scripts: Mental templates for each disease: epidemiology (who gets it?), pathophysiology (why?), clinical features (what does it look like?), time course (how does it progress?), diagnostics (how do you confirm?)

Prioritization Matrix: Rank differentials by BOTH probability (likely) and severity (dangerous). A low-probability but life-threatening diagnosis must be ruled out before a high-probability benign one
Diagnostic Testing Strategy: Choose tests based on pre-test probability. High pre-test probability → confirmatory test (high specificity). Low pre-test probability → screening test (high sensitivity)
right: Cannot-Miss Diagnoses by Chief Complaint:
Chest Pain: MI, PE, aortic dissection, tension pneumothorax, cardiac tamponade, esophageal rupture
Headache: SAH, meningitis, temporal arteritis, intracranial mass, cavernous sinus thrombosis
Abdominal Pain: Ruptured AAA, mesenteric ischemia, ectopic pregnancy (reproductive age), appendicitis, bowel obstruction
Dyspnea: PE, tension pneumothorax, anaphylaxis, cardiac tamponade, acute HF exacerbation
Syncope: Cardiac arrhythmia, PE, aortic stenosis, ruptured AAA, ectopic pregnancy

medications.name: Bayesian Reasoning Framework
medications.action: Pre-test probability × Likelihood ratio = Post-test probability. Pre-test probability is determined by prevalence, clinical features, and risk factors. Likelihood ratio (+LR and -LR) is determined by test characteristics (sensitivity and specificity). A positive test with high +LR significantly increases disease probability; a negative test with low -LR significantly decreases it

pearls: Always ask: 'What is the worst thing this could be?' before asking 'What is the most likely thing this is?' Missing a common diagnosis causes morbidity; missing a dangerous diagnosis causes mortality.
The problem representation is the single most important step: if you frame the case correctly, the differential generates itself. Practice summarizing cases in one sentence.

Cognitive debiasing strategies: Use diagnostic checklists, consider the opposite hypothesis, ask 'What else could this be?', perform a diagnostic timeout, and always revisit the differential when treatment isn't working.

Test ordering should be purposeful: Ask 'How will this result change my management?' before ordering any test. If the result won't change what you do, the test is not indicated (and wastes resources and may lead to false positives).
When the diagnosis is unclear, treat the most dangerous possibility while continuing the workup. You can always de-escalate treatment; you cannot undo a missed life-threatening diagnosis.
diagnostics: Order targeted laboratory studies to differentiate between competing diagnoses
Order imaging studies based on clinical probability
Order tissue sampling or biopsy when indicated for definitive diagnosis
Apply clinical decision rules (Wells, Ottawa, PERC) to guide workup
Order genetic or molecular testing when hereditary conditions suspected
management: Rank differential diagnoses by probability AND worst-case scenario (don't miss diagnoses)
Prescribe empiric treatment for most likely diagnosis while ruling out dangerous alternatives
Apply evidence-based clinical pathways for common presentations
Initiate time-sensitive treatments before all results return (sepsis, MI, stroke)
Reassess and narrow differential as diagnostic data returns
nursingActions: Generate comprehensive differential diagnosis for each chief complaint
Apply systematic approach: most common, most dangerous, most treatable
Use clinical decision rules to risk-stratify patients
Correlate history, exam, and diagnostic findings to refine differential
Document clinical reasoning and decision-making process
Identify cognitive biases and apply debiasing strategies

ecg-advanced-np.title: 12-Lead ECG: Advanced Interpretation (NP Level)

cellular.title: Cardiac Electrophysiology and ECG Waveform Analysis
cellular.content:
Advanced 12-lead ECG interpretation requires understanding the cardiac conduction system at the cellular level and correlating electrical patterns with anatomical regions of the heart.

Cardiac Conduction Pathway:
SA Node (intrinsic rate 60-100) → Atrial depolarization (P wave) → AV Node (0.12-0.20 sec delay = PR interval) → Bundle of His → Right and Left Bundle Branches → Purkinje Fibers → Ventricular depolarization (QRS complex, <0.12 sec) → Ventricular repolarization (T wave)

12-Lead Anatomical Correlation:
• Inferior: Leads II, III, aVF → Right Coronary Artery (RCA) territory
• Anterior: Leads V1-V4 → Left Anterior Descending (LAD) territory (the 'widow maker')
• Lateral: Leads I, aVL, V5-V6 → Left Circumflex (LCx) territory
• Septal: Leads V1-V2 → LAD septal perforators
• Right Ventricular: V4R → RCA proximal
• Posterior: Reciprocal changes in V1-V3 (ST depression, tall R waves)

Systematic ECG Interpretation (12-Step Method):
1. Rate: Calculate (300 ÷ number of large boxes between R-R)
2. Rhythm: Regular or irregular? P waves present and consistent?
3. P Waves: Shape, direction, one P per QRS?
4. PR Interval: 0.12-0.20 sec (3-5 small boxes). Prolonged = AV block
5. QRS Duration: <0.12 sec normal. Wide QRS = bundle branch block or ventricular origin
6. QRS Axis: Normal -30° to +90°. LAD or RAD suggests pathology
7. QRS Morphology: Q waves (necrosis), R wave progression V1-V6, bundle branch block patterns
8. ST Segment: Elevation (STEMI, pericarditis) or Depression (ischemia, reciprocal changes)
9. T Waves: Inversion (ischemia, strain), peaked (hyperkalemia), flattened (hypokalemia)
10. QT Interval: Corrected QTc <0.44 sec (male), <0.46 sec (female). Prolonged QTc = Torsades risk
11. U Waves: May indicate hypokalemia
12. Compare with prior ECGs: New changes vs chronic findings

STEMI Criteria: ST elevation ≥1mm in 2 or more contiguous leads (≥2mm in V1-V3 for men >40, ≥2.5mm in V1-V3 for men <40, ≥1.5mm in V1-V3 for women). Immediate cardiac catheterization required (door-to-balloon <90 min).

left: Normal Sinus Rhythm: Rate 60-100, regular, P before every QRS, PR 0.12-0.20, QRS <0.12, normal axis
STEMI: ST elevation in contiguous leads with reciprocal ST depression. Identify territory → culprit artery → activate cath lab
NSTEMI: ST depression and/or T-wave inversion WITHOUT ST elevation. Troponin positive. Medical management initially, possible cath
Bundle Branch Blocks: RBBB = 'M' pattern in V1 (rSR'), wide S in I/V6. LBBB = broad notched R in I/V6, deep S in V1. NEW LBBB with chest pain = treat as STEMI
right: Hyperkalemia: Peaked T waves → widened QRS → sine wave → asystole. K+ >6.5 is an EMERGENCY
Atrial Fibrillation: Irregularly irregular, no P waves, fibrillatory baseline. Assess rate control and stroke risk (CHA₂DS₂-VASc)

Heart Blocks: 1st degree (prolonged PR >0.20), 2nd degree Type I Wenckebach (progressive PR prolongation then dropped QRS), 2nd degree Type II (constant PR then suddenly dropped QRS: dangerous), 3rd degree complete (P and QRS dissociated: pacemaker needed)
Prolonged QT: QTc >500 ms = high risk for Torsades de Pointes. Review medications (many drugs prolong QT). Avoid hypokalemia/hypomagnesemia

medications.name: Amiodarone
medications.action: Blocks potassium channels (primary), sodium channels, calcium channels, and beta-adrenergic receptors. Prolongs action potential duration and refractory period in all cardiac tissue. Used for ventricular and atrial arrhythmias

pearls: Systematic interpretation prevents missed findings: use the same 12-step method EVERY time. Speed comes with practice, not by skipping steps.
Always compare with prior ECGs when available: a new finding is more clinically significant than a chronic one. 'New LBBB + chest pain = STEMI until proven otherwise.'

Reciprocal changes CONFIRM a STEMI: if you see ST elevation in inferior leads (II, III, aVF), look for reciprocal ST depression in lateral leads (I, aVL). Reciprocal changes increase specificity for acute MI.

In posterior MI, the standard 12-lead may only show ST depression in V1-V3 (mirror image of posterior ST elevation). If suspected, obtain posterior leads (V7-V9) for direct confirmation. Always obtain right-sided leads (V4R) for inferior STEMI to assess RV involvement.

Drug-induced QT prolongation is one of the most common medication safety issues. Common offenders: Haloperidol, ondansetron, fluoroquinolones, macrolides, methadone, antipsychotics, Class IA/III antiarrhythmics. Check QTc before starting any of these and correct K+/Mg2+ first.
diagnostics: Order and interpret 12-lead ECG with systematic analysis
Order serial ECGs for evolving presentations (q15-30min for ACS)
Order continuous telemetry with ST-segment monitoring
Order electrolyte panel correlating with ECG findings (K+, Mg2+, Ca2+)
Order echocardiography for structural correlation
Order drug levels when toxicity suspected
management: Prescribe antiarrhythmic therapy based on rhythm diagnosis
Prescribe anticoagulation for atrial fibrillation using CHA2DS2-VASc scoring
Order emergent reperfusion for STEMI (activate cath lab)
Prescribe electrolyte replacement for ECG-altering imbalances
Order pacing for symptomatic bradycardia or high-degree blocks
Discontinue QT-prolonging medications when QTc > 500 ms
nursingActions: Perform systematic ECG interpretation: rate, rhythm, axis, intervals, ST/T changes
Identify STEMI patterns by coronary artery territory
Recognize lethal arrhythmias requiring immediate intervention
Correlate ECG changes with electrolyte values and medication effects
Document ECG interpretation with clinical correlation
Calculate QTc and identify risk for Torsades de Pointes

sofa-apache-np.title: SOFA and APACHE II Scoring (NP Level)

cellular.title: Critical Care Severity Scoring Systems
cellular.content:
Severity scoring systems are standardized tools used in critical care to quantify illness severity, predict mortality, guide treatment intensity, and facilitate quality benchmarking. Two of the most widely used are SOFA and APACHE II.

SOFA Score (Sequential Organ Failure Assessment):
Assesses dysfunction in 6 organ systems, each scored 0-4 (total 0-24):

1. Respiratory: PaO2/FiO2 ratio
• 0 = ≥400, 1 = <400, 2 = <300, 3 = <200 (with ventilatory support), 4 = <100 (with ventilatory support)

2. Coagulation: Platelet count (×10³/µL)
• 0 = ≥150, 1 = <150, 2 = <100, 3 = <50, 4 = <20

3. Liver: Bilirubin (mg/dL)
• 0 = <1.2, 1 = 1.2-1.9, 2 = 2.0-5.9, 3 = 6.0-11.9, 4 = >12.0

4. Cardiovascular: Mean Arterial Pressure / Vasopressor dose
• 0 = MAP ≥70, 1 = MAP <70, 2 = Dopamine ≤5 or Dobutamine any, 3 = Dopamine >5 or Epi ≤0.1 or Norepi ≤0.1, 4 = Dopamine >15 or Epi >0.1 or Norepi >0.1

5. CNS: Glasgow Coma Scale
• 0 = 15, 1 = 13-14, 2 = 10-12, 3 = 6-9, 4 = <6

6. Renal: Creatinine (mg/dL) or urine output
• 0 = <1.2, 1 = 1.2-1.9, 2 = 2.0-3.4, 3 = 3.5-4.9 or UO <500mL/day, 4 = >5.0 or UO <200mL/day

Sepsis-3 Definition: An acute increase of ≥2 SOFA points from baseline + known/suspected infection = SEPSIS.
SOFA score ≥2 has an overall mortality of approximately 10%.

APACHE II (Acute Physiology and Chronic Health Evaluation):
Scored 0-71 based on 12 physiologic variables (worst values in first 24 hours of ICU admission), age points, and chronic health points. Higher scores = higher predicted mortality. Used for ICU admission risk stratification and outcomes research.

riskFactors: ICU patients with multisystem organ dysfunction

left: SOFA Applications: Diagnose sepsis (≥2 point increase), track organ dysfunction trajectory, assess treatment response, predict ICU mortality, guide escalation/de-escalation decisions
SOFA Trending: Calculate daily. Improving SOFA = organ recovery. Worsening SOFA = deterioration requiring intervention escalation. A change of ≥1 point is clinically meaningful
APACHE II: Calculated once at ICU admission (uses worst values in first 24 hours). Predicts hospital mortality percentage. Used for ICU benchmarking and research
Score thresholds: SOFA ≥11 associated with >80% mortality. APACHE II ≥25 associated with >50% mortality
right: NP Role in Severity Scoring: Order and interpret labs needed for scoring (PaO2, bilirubin, creatinine, platelets, ABG), assess GCS accurately, document vasopressor doses, calculate MAP
Clinical Decision-Making: Use scores to justify ICU admission, guide goals-of-care discussions, determine appropriateness of aggressive intervention vs palliative approach

Limitations: Scores predict population outcomes, NOT individual patient outcomes. A patient with a high predicted mortality may still survive with excellent care: never use scores alone to make decisions
Communication: Use scores when communicating with consulting teams: 'SOFA increased from 6 to 10 overnight' provides objective data about clinical trajectory

medications.name: Vasopressor Titration
medications.action: Vasopressor requirements directly impact SOFA cardiovascular score. Norepinephrine, epinephrine, vasopressin, and dopamine doses are used to calculate cardiovascular component. Higher vasopressor doses = higher SOFA score = greater cardiovascular dysfunction

pearls: SOFA is for TRENDING: calculate it daily to track organ dysfunction trajectory. A single value is less useful than the trend. Worsening SOFA despite treatment = re-evaluate the plan.
For sepsis diagnosis, it's the CHANGE in SOFA that matters, not the absolute value. Baseline SOFA is assumed to be 0 for patients without known pre-existing organ dysfunction.
APACHE II uses the WORST values in the first 24 hours of ICU admission: ensure all required labs and assessments are completed within this window for accurate scoring.

Use severity scores to facilitate goals-of-care discussions with families: 'Based on objective measures of organ function, your loved one's organs are showing signs of worsening despite maximum treatment...'

Neither SOFA nor APACHE II should be used in isolation to make withdrawal-of-care decisions. They are prognostic tools that inform discussion, not decision tools that replace clinical judgment and family values.
diagnostics: Calculate SOFA score using 6 organ systems (respiratory, coagulation, liver, cardiovascular, CNS, renal)
Calculate APACHE II score on ICU admission (12 physiologic variables + age + chronic health)
Order comprehensive metabolic panel, CBC, ABG, lactate for scoring
Order GCS assessment for neurological component
Serial SOFA scores to trend organ dysfunction (every 24-48 hours)
management: Use SOFA score to guide treatment intensity and resource allocation
Initiate organ-specific support based on failing systems
Use APACHE II for ICU mortality prediction and benchmarking
Intensify treatment for rising SOFA scores (worsening organ dysfunction)
Consider goals-of-care discussions for persistently high scores
Use serial SOFA trends to assess treatment response
nursingActions: Calculate and document SOFA score accurately using current clinical data
Assess each organ system component systematically
Trend SOFA scores to identify improvement or deterioration
Communicate scoring trends and clinical implications to team
Correlate scoring with clinical assessment for comprehensive evaluation
Use scoring data to support prognostication and care planning discussions

point-of-care-us-np.title: Point-of-Care Ultrasound (POCUS) Assessment

cellular.title: Bedside Ultrasound in Advanced Practice
cellular.content:
Point-of-Care Ultrasound (POCUS) is a focused, goal-directed ultrasound examination performed by the clinician at the bedside to answer specific clinical questions in real time. Unlike comprehensive radiology-performed ultrasound, POCUS is designed to rapidly guide clinical decision-making.

Common POCUS Applications for NPs:

1. FAST Exam (Focused Assessment with Sonography in Trauma):
• 4 views: Right Upper Quadrant (Morrison's pouch/hepatorenal space), Left Upper Quadrant (splenorenal space), Suprapubic (pelvic/Douglas pouch), Subxiphoid (pericardial)
• Purpose: Detect FREE FLUID (blood) in the abdomen/pelvis and pericardial effusion
• Extended FAST (eFAST): Adds bilateral anterior chest views to detect pneumothorax

2. Cardiac (Focused Echocardiography):
• Parasternal long axis, parasternal short axis, apical 4-chamber, subxiphoid views
• Assess: LV function (gross EF estimation), RV dilation, pericardial effusion/tamponade, volume status (IVC assessment)

3. Lung Ultrasound:
• A-lines (horizontal artifacts = normal aerated lung or pneumothorax)
• B-lines (vertical laser-like artifacts = pulmonary edema: count correlates with severity)
• Lung sliding (present = no pneumothorax; absent = possible pneumothorax)
• Pleural effusion identification and marking for thoracentesis

4. IVC Assessment (Volume Status):
• Measured in subxiphoid or right midaxillary view
• IVC diameter >2.1 cm with <50% respiratory variation = elevated CVP (fluid overloaded)
• IVC diameter <2.1 cm with >50% collapse = low CVP (likely volume responsive)

5. Procedure Guidance:
• Ultrasound-guided peripheral IV placement
• Central line insertion (internal jugular)
• Thoracentesis/paracentesis marking
• Lumbar puncture assistance
• Abscess identification for I&D

6. DVT Assessment:
• 2-point compression ultrasound: Common femoral vein and popliteal vein
• Non-compressible vein = positive for DVT

riskFactors: Procedure guidance needed (central line, paracentesis, thoracentesis)

left: FAST Positive: Free fluid in Morrison's pouch, splenorenal space, or pelvis in trauma = intraperitoneal hemorrhage. Positive FAST + hemodynamically unstable = emergent surgery
Cardiac Tamponade on POCUS: Pericardial effusion + RV diastolic collapse + IVC plethora (distended, no respiratory variation). Emergency pericardiocentesis indicated
B-Lines on Lung US: ≥3 B-lines per intercostal space = interstitial syndrome (pulmonary edema, ARDS, pneumonia). Bilateral B-lines + dyspnea = diuresis or positive pressure ventilation
IVC Assessment: Plethoric IVC (>2.1 cm, no collapse) = volume overloaded, elevated right heart pressures. Small IVC with respiratory collapse = volume depletion, likely fluid responsive
right: Absent Lung Sliding: No respiratory movement of pleural interface + absent B-lines + 'stratosphere sign' on M-mode = pneumothorax. If 'lung point' identified, confirms diagnosis and marks the border
DVT Compression: Non-compressible vein at femoral or popliteal area = DVT positive. Must compress in transverse view. Partial compression or echogenic thrombus visible

Limitations: POCUS is a SCREENING tool, not a comprehensive exam. Positive or indeterminate findings may require formal imaging. Skill-dependent: accuracy correlates directly with training and experience
Documentation: Record images and clips. Document: Indication, findings (positive/negative for specific question), clinical correlation, and any need for formal imaging follow-up

medications.name: Ultrasound Machine Settings
medications.action: Transducer selection determines image quality: Curvilinear (abdominal/FAST, 2-5 MHz, deep penetration), Phased Array (cardiac, 1-5 MHz, small footprint for intercostal), Linear (vascular/superficial, 5-15 MHz, high resolution shallow structures). Depth, gain, and preset adjustments optimize image quality for each application

pearls: POCUS answers YES/NO clinical questions at the bedside in seconds to minutes: it does not replace formal imaging but accelerates clinical decision-making in acute settings.

The eFAST exam can be completed in <3 minutes and detects free fluid, pericardial effusion, and pneumothorax: three potentially lethal conditions in trauma. Every NP in acute care should be proficient.

IVC assessment is a rapid, non-invasive surrogate for CVP/volume status. A flat IVC that collapses with inspiration = 'give fluid.' A plethoric IVC that doesn't collapse = 'the patient doesn't need more fluid (and may need diuresis).'
Lung ultrasound is MORE SENSITIVE than chest X-ray for detecting pneumothorax, pleural effusion, and pulmonary edema. A-lines = normal or pneumothorax (check for lung sliding). B-lines = wet lungs.

Credentialing: POCUS competency requires structured training, supervised scans, and ongoing quality assurance. Document your training, maintain a scan log, and follow your institution's POCUS credentialing requirements.
diagnostics: Perform focused cardiac ultrasound (parasternal long/short axis, apical 4-chamber, subcostal)
Perform lung ultrasound (A-lines, B-lines, pleural effusion, pneumothorax)
Perform IVC assessment for volume status (diameter and collapsibility)
Perform FAST exam for trauma (free fluid in abdomen/pelvis)
Perform procedure guidance imaging (vascular access, drainage procedures)
Perform focused abdominal ultrasound (AAA screening, hydronephrosis, gallbladder)
management: Integrate POCUS findings with clinical assessment for diagnosis
Use POCUS to guide fluid resuscitation (IVC assessment)
Use POCUS to identify reversible causes of cardiac arrest (tamponade, PE, hypovolemia)
Guide procedural interventions with real-time imaging
Order formal imaging studies when POCUS findings are inconclusive
Document POCUS findings with images saved to medical record
nursingActions: Perform focused POCUS examinations for specific clinical questions
Integrate POCUS findings with bedside assessment
Use RUSH protocol (Rapid Ultrasound in Shock) for undifferentiated shock
Identify critical findings requiring immediate intervention (tamponade, tension pneumothorax)
Guide procedures with real-time ultrasound (central line, thoracentesis)
Document and archive POCUS studies per institutional protocol

hand-hygiene.title: Hand Hygiene and Handwashing

cellular.title: Infection Prevention Foundation
cellular.content: Hand hygiene is the single most important measure to prevent healthcare-associated infections (HAIs). The transient flora on hands: bacteria, viruses, and fungi acquired through patient contact or contaminated surfaces: are responsible for the majority of cross-transmission events in healthcare settings. Proper hand hygiene mechanically removes or chemically inactivates these transient organisms. The WHO identifies 5 Moments of Hand Hygiene: (1) Before patient contact, (2) Before an aseptic procedure, (3) After body fluid exposure risk, (4) After patient contact, and (5) After contact with patient surroundings. Failure to perform hand hygiene at these critical moments is the leading modifiable cause of HAIs, which affect millions of patients annually and contribute to increased morbidity, mortality, and healthcare costs. Alcohol-based hand rubs (ABHR) containing 60-95% alcohol are the preferred method for routine hand hygiene when hands are not visibly soiled, as they are faster, more effective against most organisms, and less irritating to skin than soap and water. However, soap and water is REQUIRED when hands are visibly soiled, after caring for patients with Clostridioides difficile (C.diff) or norovirus (alcohol does not kill spores or non-enveloped viruses effectively), and after using the restroom.

riskFactors: Lack of accessible hand hygiene supplies
Caring for patients with multidrug-resistant organisms

left: WHO 5 Moments: Before patient contact: reduce transmission of organisms from HCW hands to patient
WHO 5 Moments: Before aseptic procedure: prevent introduction of organisms into sterile sites (IV insertion, wound care, catheterization)
WHO 5 Moments: After body fluid exposure risk: protect HCW and prevent cross-contamination after contact with blood, secretions, excretions, mucous membranes, non-intact skin
WHO 5 Moments: After patient contact: prevent colonization of HCW hands with patient flora and reduce environmental contamination
WHO 5 Moments: After contact with patient surroundings: surfaces near patient harbor pathogens (bed rails, IV poles, call bell, bedside table)
right: Proper handwashing technique: Wet hands with warm water → Apply soap → Lather all surfaces (palms, backs, between fingers, thumbs, under nails, wrists) for minimum 20 seconds → Rinse thoroughly under running water → Dry with clean paper towel → Use paper towel to turn off faucet

ABHR technique: Apply product to palm of one hand (enough to cover all surfaces) → Rub hands together covering all surfaces (palms, backs, between fingers, thumbs, fingertips) until dry (approximately 20 seconds) → Do NOT wipe or rinse off
ABHR is preferred for routine decontamination when hands are NOT visibly soiled: faster, more effective, less skin irritation
Soap and water REQUIRED: Visibly soiled hands, after caring for C.diff patients (spore-forming), after caring for norovirus patients (non-enveloped virus), before eating, after using restroom
Artificial nails, chipped nail polish, and rings with stones harbor microorganisms: facility policies typically prohibit these for direct care providers

medications.name: Alcohol-Based Hand Rub (ABHR)
medications.action: Contains 60-95% isopropanol or ethanol that denatures proteins and dissolves lipid membranes of most bacteria, fungi, and enveloped viruses on contact, providing rapid broad-spectrum antimicrobial activity within 15-30 seconds

medications.name: Antimicrobial Soap (Chlorhexidine Gluconate / CHG)
medications.action: Provides mechanical removal of transient flora through friction and surfactant action combined with residual antimicrobial activity from chlorhexidine that binds to skin and continues killing organisms after rinsing

pearls: Hand hygiene is the #1 most effective intervention to prevent healthcare-associated infections. Compliance rates in healthcare are often below 50%: every nurse must be a champion for hand hygiene.
Remember: ABHR is preferred for routine hand hygiene, but soap and water is REQUIRED for C.diff, norovirus, and visibly soiled hands. If you can see it, you must wash it.

The WHO 5 Moments framework provides a systematic approach: Before patient contact, Before aseptic procedure, After body fluid exposure risk, After patient contact, After contact with patient surroundings.

Proper technique matters as much as frequency: ensure all surfaces are covered (palms, backs, between fingers, thumbs, fingertips, wrists) for a minimum of 20 seconds. Singing 'Happy Birthday' twice approximates 20 seconds.
Skin integrity is essential: damaged skin harbors more organisms. Use facility-provided moisturizers, avoid hot water, and pat hands dry rather than rubbing to maintain skin health.
diagnostics: Hand hygiene compliance audits
HAI rate monitoring
Observation of WHO 5 Moments compliance
Skin integrity assessment of hands
management: Follow WHO 5 Moments of Hand Hygiene
Use ABHR when hands not visibly soiled
Use soap and water for C. diff, norovirus, and visibly soiled hands
Apply hand moisturizer to prevent skin breakdown
Maintain short, clean natural nails
nursingActions: Perform hand hygiene before and after every patient contact
Use ABHR for routine decontamination (minimum 20 seconds)
Use soap and water for C. diff and norovirus patients
Report low hand hygiene compliance observations
Educate patients and visitors on hand hygiene importance
Assess hand skin integrity and use moisturizer to prevent cracking

ppe-basics.title: PPE: Donning and Doffing

cellular.title: Personal Protective Equipment Principles
cellular.content: Personal Protective Equipment (PPE) creates a physical barrier between the healthcare worker (HCW) and infectious agents, preventing transmission through contact, droplet, and airborne routes. PPE selection is based on the anticipated exposure and the type of transmission-based precautions required. The fundamental components include gloves (contact barrier), gowns (body/clothing protection), masks/respirators (respiratory protection), and eye protection (mucous membrane protection). The ORDER of donning (putting on) and doffing (removing) PPE is critical to prevent self-contamination. Donning order: Gown → Mask/Respirator → Eye Protection (goggles or face shield) → Gloves. Doffing order: Gloves → Eye Protection → Gown → Mask/Respirator. The doffing sequence is designed so that the most contaminated items (gloves) are removed first, and the mask/respirator (protecting the most vulnerable entry point: the respiratory tract) is removed last, after hands have been cleaned. Common errors during doffing: touching the outside of contaminated PPE with bare hands, removing the mask before other items, or failing to perform hand hygiene between steps: are the primary causes of HCW self-contamination and subsequent infection transmission.

riskFactors: Incorrect PPE selection for precaution type
Failure to perform hand hygiene between steps

left: DONNING Order (remember: 'Going May Eat Grapes': Gown, Mask, Eye protection, Gloves)
Step 1: GOWN: Secure at neck and waist ties. Gown should fully cover torso, fit comfortably, and have long sleeves with cuffs
Step 2: MASK/RESPIRATOR: Secure ties or elastic bands. Surgical mask for droplet precautions. N95 respirator for airborne precautions (must be fit-tested). Mold nose piece to face, ensure no gaps
Step 3: EYE PROTECTION: Position goggles over eyes or face shield over face, adjust to fit securely. Goggles must have anti-fog features and seal around eyes
Step 4: GLOVES: Select proper size. Pull glove cuffs OVER the cuffs of the gown to create a continuous barrier with no exposed skin
right: DOFFING Order (remember: 'Gloves are the Grossest': remove first): Gloves → Eye Protection → Gown → Mask

Step 1: GLOVES: Grasp outside of one glove at wrist, peel off turning inside out. Hold removed glove in remaining gloved hand. Slide ungloved finger under wrist of remaining glove, peel off inside out over first glove. Discard. Perform hand hygiene IMMEDIATELY

Step 2: EYE PROTECTION: Remove by handling the headband or earpieces (these are considered 'clean'). Do NOT touch the front of goggles/face shield (contaminated). Discard or place in designated receptacle for reprocessing
Step 3: GOWN: Unfasten ties. Pull gown away from body, rolling it inside out (contaminated surface inward). Fold or roll into a bundle. Discard. Perform hand hygiene

Step 4: MASK/RESPIRATOR: Remove LAST (respiratory protection maintained as long as possible). Grasp ties/elastics only: do NOT touch the front of the mask. Lift away from face. Discard. Perform hand hygiene IMMEDIATELY

medications.name: N95 Respirator
medications.action: Filters at least 95% of airborne particles ≥0.3 microns in diameter, including aerosolized droplet nuclei containing pathogens such as M. tuberculosis, measles virus, and varicella-zoster virus, providing respiratory protection against airborne transmission

medications.name: Surgical Mask
medications.action: Provides barrier protection against large respiratory droplets (>5 microns) generated by coughing, sneezing, or talking, and protects the wearer's nose and mouth from splashes and sprays of blood and body fluids

medications.name: Face Shield / Goggles
medications.action: Protects the mucous membranes of the eyes (and face, in the case of face shields) from splashes, sprays, and spatters of blood, body fluids, secretions, and excretions that may contain infectious agents

pearls: Donning order: Gown → Mask/Respirator → Eye Protection → Gloves. Memory aid: 'Going May Eat Grapes.' Gloves go on LAST so they can overlap the gown cuffs.
Doffing order: Gloves → Eye Protection → Gown → Mask/Respirator. The most contaminated item (gloves) comes off FIRST. The mask comes off LAST to maintain respiratory protection as long as possible.
Perform hand hygiene BETWEEN doffing steps: especially immediately after removing gloves and after removing gown. This is the most commonly missed step and a major source of self-contamination.
The #1 doffing error is touching the outside (contaminated surface) of PPE with bare or clean hands. Always grasp ties, bands, or edges: never the front surface of masks, goggles, or gowns.
N95 respirators require annual fit testing AND a user seal check every time they are donned. If you have facial hair in the seal area, you cannot achieve a proper seal: use a PAPR instead.
diagnostics: Observe for proper donning sequence (Gown → Mask → Eye Protection → Gloves)
Verify proper doffing sequence (Gloves → Eye Protection → Gown → Mask)
Fit test verification for N95 respirators
Assess for PPE integrity (tears, gaps)
management: Follow facility PPE protocols for each precaution type
Don PPE before entering patient room
Doff PPE before leaving patient room (except N95 for airborne)
Perform hand hygiene between each step of doffing
Report PPE supply shortages to charge nurse
nursingActions: Don PPE in correct order: Gown, Mask, Eye Protection, Gloves
Doff PPE in correct order: Gloves, Eye Protection, Gown, Mask
Perform hand hygiene immediately after removing gloves and after all PPE removed
Ensure N95 is fit-tested annually and seal-checked each use
Report any PPE breaches or self-contamination immediately
Educate patients and visitors on PPE requirements for their precaution type

isolation-precautions-rpn.title: Isolation Precautions Overview

cellular.title: Chain of Infection and Transmission Prevention
cellular.content: Isolation precautions are infection prevention strategies designed to interrupt the chain of infection by targeting the mode of transmission. The chain of infection requires six links: (1) Infectious agent (pathogen), (2) Reservoir (where the organism lives and multiplies), (3) Portal of exit (how it leaves the reservoir), (4) Mode of transmission (how it travels to a new host), (5) Portal of entry (how it enters the new host), and (6) Susceptible host. Isolation precautions target the MODE OF TRANSMISSION link. There are two tiers of precautions: STANDARD PRECAUTIONS apply to ALL patients regardless of known or suspected infection status and include hand hygiene, PPE based on anticipated exposure, respiratory hygiene/cough etiquette, safe injection practices, and proper handling of contaminated equipment and surfaces. TRANSMISSION-BASED PRECAUTIONS are used IN ADDITION to standard precautions when the route of transmission is not completely interrupted by standard precautions alone. There are three categories: AIRBORNE (particles <5 microns that remain suspended: TB, measles, varicella), DROPLET (particles >5 microns that fall within 3-6 feet: influenza, pertussis, meningococcal meningitis), and CONTACT (direct or indirect physical transfer: MRSA, C.diff, scabies, VRE). A patient may require more than one type of transmission-based precaution simultaneously.

left: STANDARD PRECAUTIONS (Tier 1): Apply to ALL patients, ALL encounters, regardless of diagnosis: treat all blood, body fluids, secretions, excretions (except sweat), non-intact skin, and mucous membranes as potentially infectious

AIRBORNE Precautions: Required for pathogens transmitted via droplet nuclei <5 microns that remain suspended in air and travel long distances: TB (Mycobacterium tuberculosis), Measles (Rubeola), Varicella (Chickenpox), Disseminated Herpes Zoster

DROPLET Precautions: Required for pathogens transmitted via large respiratory droplets >5 microns generated during coughing, sneezing, talking: travel ≤3-6 feet then fall: Influenza, Pertussis (Whooping Cough), Meningococcal Meningitis, Mumps, Rubella

CONTACT Precautions: Required for pathogens transmitted by direct physical contact or indirect contact through contaminated objects/surfaces: MRSA, VRE, C.difficile, Scabies, Lice, RSV (in pediatrics), Wound infections with drug-resistant organisms
Multiple precaution types can apply simultaneously: e.g., Varicella requires BOTH Airborne AND Contact precautions (vesicular lesions are infectious on contact)
right: Airborne room requirements: Airborne Infection Isolation Room (AIIR): negative pressure, door closed at all times, 6-12 air changes per hour, air exhausted to outside or HEPA-filtered. N95 or PAPR required for all who enter

Droplet room requirements: Private room preferred. If unavailable, cohort patients with SAME organism. Maintain >3 feet between patients. Curtain between beds. Surgical mask within 6 feet of patient. No negative pressure required

Contact room requirements: Private room preferred. Dedicated equipment (stethoscope, BP cuff, thermometer) that stays in the room. Gown and gloves for ALL interactions with patient or patient environment. Remove PPE before leaving room

Signage: Appropriate precaution signage MUST be posted at the door BEFORE anyone enters. Include type of precaution, required PPE, and any special instructions. Communicate precaution status during handoff/report

Patient transport: Limit transport to medically necessary only. Airborne patients wear surgical mask during transport. Droplet patients wear surgical mask during transport. Contact patients: ensure wounds are covered, clean linens for transport

medications.name: Standard Precautions Protocol
medications.action: Establishes baseline infection prevention practices applied to ALL patient encounters regardless of suspected or confirmed infection status, including hand hygiene, use of PPE based on anticipated exposure, respiratory hygiene/cough etiquette, safe injection practices, sterile instruments and devices, and clean and disinfected environmental surfaces

medications.name: Transmission-Based Precaution Signage
medications.action: Provides visual identification of required precautions at the point of entry to patient rooms, ensuring all healthcare workers, visitors, and ancillary staff are aware of required PPE and procedures before entering the patient environment

pearls: Standard precautions apply to ALL patients: not just those with known infections. This is the single most important concept in infection prevention.
Memory aid for airborne organisms: 'MTV': Measles, TB, Varicella (Chickenpox). These require negative pressure rooms and N95 respirators.
Contact precautions require dedicated equipment in the room (stethoscope, BP cuff, thermometer). This prevents indirect contact transmission through shared equipment.
C.diff requires BOTH contact precautions AND soap and water hand hygiene (not ABHR). Alcohol does not kill C.diff spores: mechanical removal through handwashing is essential.

Always communicate isolation precaution status during patient handoff/report and when calling for transport, diagnostic testing, or consultations. Every person entering the room must know the required precautions.
diagnostics: Verify isolation type ordered (contact, droplet, airborne)
Monitor for signs of infection transmission
Assess patient's understanding of isolation requirements
Monitor compliance with precaution protocols
management: Implement correct precaution type as ordered
Use designated equipment for isolated patients
Maintain proper signage on patient door
Coordinate care to minimize entries into room
Ensure adequate PPE supply outside room
nursingActions: Post appropriate isolation signage on patient door
Don and doff PPE correctly for each precaution type
Educate patient and visitors on isolation requirements
Use dedicated equipment (stethoscope, BP cuff) in patient room
Coordinate care activities to minimize room entries
Report any breaks in isolation technique to charge nurse

sterile-technique.title: Sterile Technique and Asepsis

cellular.title: Principles of Surgical Asepsis
cellular.content: Sterile technique (surgical asepsis) is the practice of creating and maintaining a completely pathogen-free environment to prevent the introduction of ANY microorganisms into a sterile body area. This differs from medical asepsis (clean technique), which aims to REDUCE the number and transfer of pathogens but does not eliminate them entirely. Sterile technique is required whenever the skin's integrity is intentionally broken or when accessing normally sterile body cavities and tissues. This includes surgical procedures, urinary catheterization, central line insertion and dressing changes, wound irrigation, lumbar puncture, chest tube insertion, and any invasive procedure that enters sterile body cavities. The fundamental principle of sterile technique is that ANY break in sterility: no matter how small: requires the entire sterile field and any contaminated supplies to be discarded and the setup to be restarted. There is no such thing as 'a little contaminated': sterile is an absolute state. Key principles include: only sterile items are placed on a sterile field, a sterile barrier that has been permeated by moisture (strike-through contamination) is no longer sterile, once a sterile package is opened the edges (1-inch border) are considered unsterile, sterile fields must be prepared as close to the time of use as possible and never left unattended, and anything below the waist or above the shoulders is considered unsterile.

left: STERILE (Surgical) Asepsis: Goal is to ELIMINATE all microorganisms from an area. Used for invasive procedures. Requires sterile equipment, sterile gloves, sterile field. ANY contamination = restart entire setup

MEDICAL (Clean) Asepsis: Goal is to REDUCE number and transfer of pathogens. Used for routine care. Includes hand hygiene, clean gloves, clean equipment. Examples: oral medication administration, bathing, linen changes

Sterile Field Setup Rules: Open sterile package flap AWAY from body first (prevents reaching over sterile field), then sides, then nearest flap last. 1-inch border around the drape is UNSTERILE. Items placed by dropping onto field: never reach across

Sterile Gloving: Open inner glove package on clean surface. Pick up first glove by the folded cuff (inside surface) with non-dominant hand. Slide dominant hand in. Pick up second glove by sliding sterile gloved fingers UNDER the cuff. No bare skin touches outer sterile surface

When Sterile Technique Required: Surgical procedures, Urinary catheterization (insertion), Central line insertion and dressing changes, Wound irrigation, Chest tube insertion, Lumbar puncture, Tracheostomy care (initial), Parenteral (IV) medication preparation
right: BREAKS in Sterile Technique (contamination: MUST restart): Reaching across the sterile field, Turning your back to the sterile field, Dropping a non-sterile item onto the field, Allowing the field to become wet (strike-through contamination), Holding sterile items below waist level

Additional breaks: Leaving the sterile field unattended or out of continuous view, Coughing or sneezing over the sterile field, Allowing a non-scrubbed person to reach across the field, Using expired sterile supplies, Opening a package and finding a hole or tear in the wrapper

Strike-through contamination: Moisture creates a pathway (wick effect) for microorganisms to travel from an unsterile surface through the barrier to the sterile surface. Even a small area of moisture renders the entire field unsterile

Time considerations: Sterile fields should be set up as close to time of use as possible. A sterile field left unattended is considered contaminated. Prolonged exposure to air increases risk of airborne contamination
If in doubt, consider it CONTAMINATED: err on the side of caution. Starting over is always safer than risking surgical site infection or other complications from a potentially contaminated field

medications.name: Sterile Normal Saline (0.9% NaCl) for Irrigation
medications.action: Provides isotonic sterile solution for wound irrigation that mechanically removes debris, bacteria, and necrotic tissue from wounds while maintaining physiologic osmolality that does not damage healthy tissue cells

medications.name: Chlorhexidine Gluconate (CHG) Skin Prep
medications.action: Provides broad-spectrum antimicrobial activity against gram-positive and gram-negative bacteria, fungi, and some viruses through disruption of cell membranes. Has rapid onset (within 15-30 seconds) and sustained residual activity that continues killing organisms for up to 48 hours after application

pearls: The cardinal rule: If sterility is questioned or uncertain, consider the item or field CONTAMINATED. Starting over is always the correct choice: never assume sterility.
Never turn your back on a sterile field or leave it unattended. If you must leave, the field is considered contaminated and must be re-established.
The 1-inch border rule: The outer 1 inch of any sterile drape is considered unsterile. Place all sterile items well within the center of the field, away from the edges.
Strike-through contamination occurs when moisture penetrates a sterile barrier, creating a pathway for microorganisms. If any part of the sterile field becomes wet, the ENTIRE field is contaminated.
Sterile items held below waist level are considered contaminated: always keep sterile items within the sterile field and above waist level within your line of sight.
diagnostics: Observe for breaks in sterile technique
Monitor surgical site infection rates
Assess sterile field integrity throughout procedure
Verify package integrity and expiration dates before opening
management: Maintain 1-inch border as non-sterile on drape
Keep all sterile items above waist level
Open sterile packages away from body
Replace any item that falls below waist or touches non-sterile surface
Keep sterile field in continuous view
nursingActions: Verify package integrity and expiration date before opening
Open sterile packages using aseptic technique (flap away first, sides next, nearest last)
Maintain sterile field in continuous line of sight
Report any breaks in sterile technique immediately
Keep hands above waist and within sterile field at all times
Consider any item below the waist or out of sight as contaminated

airborne-precautions.title: Airborne Precautions (RN Level)

cellular.title: Pathophysiology of Airborne Transmission
cellular.content: Airborne transmission occurs when infectious agents are carried by droplet nuclei: tiny particles less than 5 microns in diameter that result from the evaporation of larger respiratory droplets or are generated during aerosol-generating procedures. Due to their extremely small size, these particles remain suspended in the air for extended periods (hours) and can travel long distances on air currents, far beyond the 3-6 foot range of standard droplet transmission. They can be inhaled by susceptible individuals who have never been in face-to-face contact with the infectious person, making airborne transmission particularly dangerous in healthcare settings. The organisms capable of airborne transmission include Mycobacterium tuberculosis (TB), Measles virus (Rubeola), Varicella-Zoster Virus (VZV: Chickenpox and Disseminated Herpes Zoster), and in some circumstances, Smallpox. Airborne precautions require the most rigorous environmental controls of any transmission-based precaution category, including Airborne Infection Isolation Rooms (AIIR) with negative pressure ventilation, HEPA filtration, and the use of N95 respirators or Powered Air-Purifying Respirators (PAPRs) by all persons entering the room. The negative pressure system ensures that air flows INTO the room from the hallway (rather than out), preventing contaminated air from escaping into corridors and adjacent spaces. AIIR rooms must maintain 6-12 air changes per hour, with air exhausted directly to the outside or recirculated through HEPA filtration.

left: Airborne particles <5 microns remain suspended in air indefinitely and travel long distances on air currents: can infect individuals who never had direct contact with the source patient

Conditions requiring airborne precautions: Tuberculosis (pulmonary/laryngeal), Measles (Rubeola), Varicella (Chickenpox), Disseminated Herpes Zoster (Shingles crossing dermatomes or in immunocompromised)

TB-Specific Signs: Persistent cough >2-3 weeks, hemoptysis, night sweats, unexplained weight loss, low-grade fever, fatigue. High-risk populations: immunocompromised (HIV), homeless, incarcerated, close contacts, immigrants from endemic areas

Measles Signs: High fever (often >104°F/40°C), cough, coryza (runny nose), conjunctivitis ('3 C's'), Koplik spots (blue-white spots on buccal mucosa: pathognomonic), maculopapular rash starting on face and spreading downward

Varicella Signs: Pruritic vesicular rash in successive crops (lesions in different stages: macule, papule, vesicle, crust), low-grade fever, malaise. Infectious from 1-2 days before rash until ALL lesions have crusted over
right: AIIR (Airborne Infection Isolation Room) Requirements: Negative pressure (air flows IN from hallway), 6-12 air changes per hour, air exhausted directly outside or through HEPA filter, door MUST remain closed at all times, monitor negative pressure daily

N95 Respirator: MUST be fit-tested annually and with each new model/size. User seal check performed EVERY time it is donned. Filters ≥95% of particles ≥0.3 microns. If fit test fails → use PAPR (Powered Air-Purifying Respirator)

PAPR (Powered Air-Purifying Respirator): Battery-powered unit with blower, HEPA filters, and loose-fitting hood/helmet. Alternative for those who cannot wear N95 (facial hair, failed fit test, respiratory conditions). Does NOT require fit testing

Patient Transport: Limit to medically essential only. Place a surgical mask on the PATIENT before transport (source control). Notify receiving department in advance so they can prepare. Patient does NOT need N95: surgical mask contains their respiratory secretions

TB Management: Diagnosis confirmed by AFB (Acid-Fast Bacilli) sputum cultures (3 specimens, early morning, 8-24 hours apart).
