cellular.content: Many critical medications (vasopressors, insulin, heparin, sedatives) are delivered as continuous IV infusions at precisely controlled rates. These are typically ordered in mcg/kg/min, mcg/min, units/hr, or mg/hr and must be converted to mL/hr to program the infusion pump. Titration means adjusting the infusion rate up or down based on patient response (e.g., increasing dopamine for blood pressure, adjusting insulin for glucose levels). Errors in infusion rate calculations can cause immediate life-threatening effects: a vasopressor running too fast can cause severe hypertension and stroke, while running too slowly can result in refractory hypotension and organ failure. These calculations require meticulous attention to drug concentration, patient weight, and ordered rate.

riskFactors: Incorrect drug concentration used in calculation
Failure to account for patient weight in mcg/kg/min orders
Failure to titrate according to protocol parameters
Using the wrong units (mg vs mcg: 1000-fold difference)
Multiple simultaneous drips with drug interactions

left: Drug concentration (mg or mcg per mL)
Ordered rate (mcg/min, mcg/kg/min, units/hr)
Patient weight (for weight-based orders)
Target parameter (MAP, glucose, PTT)
right: Calculated mL/hr for pump
Titration range (min to max rate)
Step increment for titration
Monitoring frequency

medications.name: Example: Dopamine
medications.action: Order: Dopamine 5 mcg/kg/min. Patient: 70 kg. Concentration: 400 mg in 250 mL (1,600 mcg/mL). Formula: (5 × 70 × 60) ÷ 1600 = 21,000 ÷ 1600 = 13.1 mL/hr

medications.name: Example: Heparin Drip
medications.action: Order: Heparin 1000 units/hr. Concentration: 25,000 units in 500 mL (50 units/mL). Formula: 1000 units/hr ÷ 50 units/mL = 20 mL/hr

medications.name: Example: Insulin Drip
medications.action: Order: Insulin 4 units/hr. Concentration: 100 units in 100 mL NS (1 unit/mL). Formula: 4 units/hr ÷ 1 unit/mL = 4 mL/hr

pearls: Unit conversion is critical: 1 mg = 1,000 mcg. Always convert the drug concentration to match the ordered units before calculating.
For mcg/kg/min orders, you need THREE pieces of information: the ordered rate, the patient's weight in kg, and the drug concentration.
Standard concentrations are used for safety (e.g., dopamine 400 mg/250 mL, heparin 25,000 units/500 mL). Know your facility's standard concentrations.
Titration documentation: record the time, new rate, parameter value that prompted the change, and patient response.
If a patient on a vasopressor drip needs to use the bathroom, DO NOT disconnect the drip  -  use a bedpan or bedside commode.
Always calculate what dose the patient is receiving when you take over care  -  do not assume the previous nurse's rate is correct.
Smart pumps with drug libraries reduce programming errors but do NOT eliminate them  -  always verify the calculation independently.
diagnostics: Verify the drug concentration on the infusion bag label
Confirm patient weight in kg for weight-based infusions
Check the titration protocol parameters (target, max rate, step increments)
Monitor the target parameter at ordered intervals (BP, HR, glucose, PTT)
Verify pump programming with a second nurse for all high-alert infusions
management: For mcg/min orders: (Ordered mcg/min × 60 min/hr) ÷ Concentration (mcg/mL) = mL/hr
For mcg/kg/min: (Ordered mcg/kg/min × weight in kg × 60 min/hr) ÷ Concentration (mcg/mL) = mL/hr
For units/hr: Ordered units/hr ÷ Concentration (units/mL) = mL/hr
For mg/hr: Ordered mg/hr ÷ Concentration (mg/mL) = mL/hr
Titrate by adjusting the rate according to the protocol step increments
nursingActions: Label all infusion lines at the pump and at the patient connection
Use a dedicated IV line for high-alert medications when possible
Monitor the target parameter at the ordered frequency
Document rate changes, the parameter that triggered the change, and patient response
Report inability to achieve target within the maximum ordered rate
Perform independent double-check of pump programming
Never run infusion pumps in 'bolus' mode unless specifically ordered

med-math-pediatric-dosing.title: Pediatric Dosing & Safety

cellular.title: Pediatric Pharmacokinetics
cellular.content: Children are NOT small adults. Their drug metabolism, distribution, and elimination differ significantly from adults due to immature organ systems. Neonates have immature hepatic enzymes (cytochrome P450 system not fully developed until age 1-2), higher body water content (70-80% vs 60% in adults, affecting water-soluble drug distribution), lower protein binding (more free drug circulating), and immature renal function (GFR reaches adult levels around age 1-2). These factors mean that pediatric doses MUST be individually calculated based on weight (mg/kg) or body surface area (BSA in m²), never simply reduced from adult doses. The margin of safety is narrower, and errors are more likely to be fatal.

riskFactors: Weight-based calculation errors (most common pediatric med error)
Using adult doses reduced by fraction (incorrect approach)
Volume errors in small syringes (0.01 mL matters)
Concentration errors when multiple formulations exist
Caregiver measuring errors at home (using kitchen spoons)

left: Weight in kg (measured, not estimated)
mg/kg dose from drug reference
Available concentration
Age-appropriate formulation
right: Calculated safe dose range
Calculated volume to administer
Volume measurable with available syringe
Caregiver teaching completed

medications.name: Acetaminophen (Tylenol)
medications.action: Pediatric dose: 10-15 mg/kg/dose every 4-6 hours. Max: 75 mg/kg/day or 4 g/day. Example: 10 kg child = 100-150 mg per dose

medications.name: Ibuprofen (Advil/Motrin)
medications.action: Pediatric dose: 5-10 mg/kg/dose every 6-8 hours. Max: 40 mg/kg/day. Only for children ≥ 6 months. Example: 15 kg child = 75-150 mg per dose

medications.name: Amoxicillin
medications.action: Standard: 25-50 mg/kg/day divided every 8 hours. High dose (otitis media): 80-90 mg/kg/day divided every 12 hours. Example: 20 kg child standard = 500-1000 mg/day

pearls: Golden rule: Always use mg/kg dosing from a pediatric drug reference. NEVER guess, estimate, or use a fraction of the adult dose.
The two most dangerous pediatric medication errors: (1) using pounds instead of kilograms, (2) using the wrong concentration formulation.
Infant acetaminophen drops and children's liquid have DIFFERENT concentrations  -  using the wrong one causes overdose or underdose.
Oral syringes are preferred over dosing cups for accuracy. A kitchen teaspoon is NOT 5 mL  -  it varies from 2-7 mL.
For neonates, doses may be in mcg/kg. Remember: 1 mg = 1,000 mcg. A decimal error here is catastrophic.
Body Surface Area (BSA) method: used for chemotherapy and some specialized drugs. BSA (m²) = √(height cm × weight kg ÷ 3600).
Teach parents: 'The dose that is right for one child may be wrong for another child of a different weight. Never share medications between children.'
diagnostics: Obtain accurate weight in kilograms at every visit
Verify drug reference for pediatric-specific dosing
Calculate the safe dose range for the child's weight
Verify the calculation with a second nurse
Check for age-appropriate formulation (liquid, chewable, suppository)
management: Always weigh in kilograms  -  never estimate
Calculate using the pediatric-specific mg/kg dose from drug reference
Calculate both the single-dose AND daily maximum
Verify the ordered dose falls within the safe range
Select the correct concentration (pediatric formulations may differ from adult)
Use oral syringes (not IV syringes) for oral medications
Round to a measurable volume (nearest 0.1 mL for oral, 0.01 mL for parenteral in neonates)
nursingActions: Double-check all calculations with a second nurse
Use the smallest appropriate syringe for accuracy
Administer oral medications slowly to prevent aspiration
Position infant upright or semi-upright for oral medications
Educate caregivers on proper measuring techniques at home
Teach caregivers to use the oral syringe provided, not kitchen spoons
Document the weight, dose calculated, and amount administered

med-math-reconstitution.title: Reconstitution & Dilution Calculations

cellular.title: Preparing Powdered Medications
cellular.content: Many injectable and some oral medications are supplied as dry powder (lyophilized) because they are unstable in liquid form. The nurse must add a specific volume of diluent (sterile water, normal saline, or bacteriostatic water) to create a solution with a known concentration before administering. The reconstitution process requires reading the manufacturer's label carefully for the type and volume of diluent, the resulting concentration after mixing, and the expiration time once reconstituted. Multi-dose vials require labeling with the date/time of reconstitution, the resulting concentration, the nurse's initials, and the expiration. Errors in reconstitution  -  wrong diluent, wrong volume, wrong concentration  -  can result in incorrect dosing, precipitation, or patient harm.

riskFactors: Using the wrong diluent (can cause precipitation or hemolysis)
Adding incorrect volume of diluent (changes concentration)
Not reading the vial label for specific reconstitution instructions
Failure to label reconstituted multi-dose vials
Using reconstituted medication past its expiration
Not accounting for displacement volume in high-concentration reconstitution

left: Powder vial concentration after reconstitution
Diluent type (SWFI, NS, BWFI)
Diluent volume to add
Beyond-use date after reconstitution
right: Calculated dose volume to withdraw
Labeled vial (date, time, concentration)
Clear solution (no particles, no cloudiness)
Correct route of administration

medications.name: Example: Penicillin G
medications.action: Vial: 5,000,000 units powder. Directions: Add 8 mL SWFI for concentration of 500,000 units/mL. Order: 750,000 units IM. Volume: 750,000 ÷ 500,000 × 1 mL = 1.5 mL

medications.name: Example: Cefazolin
medications.action: Vial: 1 g powder. Directions: Add 2.5 mL SWFI for concentration of 330 mg/mL. Order: 500 mg IV. Volume: 500 ÷ 330 × 1 mL = 1.52 mL. Further dilute in 50-100 mL NS for IV infusion.

medications.name: Diluent Types
medications.action: SWFI (Sterile Water for Injection): most common, single-use. NS (0.9% Normal Saline): used when SWFI is contraindicated. BWFI (Bacteriostatic Water): contains preservative (benzyl alcohol), for multi-dose vials. D5W: rarely used for reconstitution, more common for further dilution.

pearls: Displacement volume: the powder itself occupies space. If you add 3 mL diluent to a 1 g vial and get 3.5 mL total volume, the displacement volume is 0.5 mL. This matters for dose accuracy.
BWFI (bacteriostatic water) is NEVER used for neonates  -  the benzyl alcohol preservative causes gasping syndrome and can be fatal.
After reconstitution, the clock starts ticking on stability. Most reconstituted medications are stable for 24 hours at room temperature or 7 days refrigerated  -  but ALWAYS check the specific drug.
If the reconstituted solution appears cloudy, has particles, or has changed color, DO NOT administer  -  discard and prepare a new dose.
For IV piggyback medications, reconstitution is Step 1, followed by further dilution in a compatible IV solution (usually NS or D5W).
Always label reconstituted multi-dose vials: Date, Time, Concentration (mg/mL), Initials, Expiration date/time.
diagnostics: Read the manufacturer's reconstitution instructions on the vial label
Verify the correct diluent type and volume
Note the resulting concentration after reconstitution
Check the stability/expiration time of the reconstituted solution
Verify the ordered dose and calculate the volume to withdraw
management: Add the specified diluent volume to the powder vial
Gently swirl or roll the vial  -  do NOT shake vigorously (causes foaming and protein denaturation)
Note the resulting concentration (mg/mL or units/mL)
Calculate the volume needed: Desired dose ÷ Reconstituted concentration = Volume to withdraw
Label multi-dose vials with date, time, concentration, expiration, and initials
Discard single-use vials after withdrawing the dose
nursingActions: Read the package insert for reconstitution instructions
Select the correct diluent as specified
Add diluent slowly to prevent excessive foaming
Ensure powder is completely dissolved before withdrawing
Label the vial immediately after reconstitution
Calculate and withdraw the correct volume for the ordered dose
Discard any remaining medication per facility policy and beyond-use dating

med-math-unit-conversions.title: Unit Conversions in Medication Math

cellular.title: Systems of Measurement
cellular.content: Medication calculations require fluency in converting between metric units (mg, mcg, g, L, mL), household measurements (teaspoons, tablespoons, ounces, cups), and occasionally apothecary measures (grains). The metric system is the standard in healthcare, and all medication orders should use metric units. However, nurses must convert household measurements when educating patients about home medication administration and when interpreting older orders. Critical conversions include mg to mcg (×1000), g to mg (×1000), L to mL (×1000), and pounds to kilograms (÷2.2). Errors in unit conversion are among the most dangerous medication errors  -  a 1000-fold error from confusing mg and mcg can be fatal.

riskFactors: Failure to include units in calculations
Relying on memory instead of verified conversion factors

left: Metric: kg, g, mg, mcg, L, mL
Household: tsp, tbsp, oz, cup
Weight: kg, lb
Temperature: °C, °F
right: 1 mg = 1,000 mcg
1 kg = 2.2 lb

medications.name: Example: mg to mcg
medications.action: Order: Levothyroxine 0.15 mg. Available: 150 mcg tablets. Are these the same? 0.15 mg × 1000 = 150 mcg. Yes, the order matches what is available  -  administer 1 tablet.

medications.name: Example: Household to Metric
medications.action: The patient's discharge medication is 10 mL twice daily. They ask: 'How many teaspoons is that?' Conversion: 10 mL ÷ 5 mL/tsp = 2 teaspoons. But teach them to use the measuring syringe provided!

pearls: The 'staircase' method for metric conversions: King Henry Died By Drinking Chocolate Milk = Kilo, Hecto, Deka, Base, Deci, Centi, Milli. Each step = multiply or divide by 10.
Critical conversions to memorize: 1 g = 1000 mg, 1 mg = 1000 mcg, 1 L = 1000 mL, 1 kg = 2.2 lb, 1 tsp = 5 mL, 1 tbsp = 15 mL, 1 oz = 30 mL.
ISMP 'Do Not Use' abbreviations: Write 'units' (not 'U'), write 'mcg' (not 'μg'), write 'mL' (not 'cc').
Dimensional analysis prevents errors because if the units don't cancel correctly, the setup is wrong  -  it's a built-in error check.
When in doubt, ask: 'Does this answer make sense?' A single dose of 500,000 mg of anything should raise a red flag.
For pediatric patients, the difference between mg and mcg is the difference between a therapeutic dose and a lethal dose.
diagnostics: Identify the starting unit and the target unit
Select the correct conversion factor
Set up the conversion so units cancel (dimensional analysis)
Verify the answer makes sense (is it reasonable?)
management: Metric conversions: multiply or divide by 1000 (move decimal 3 places)
1 kg = 2.2 lb
1 tsp = 5 mL, 1 tbsp = 15 mL, 1 oz = 30 mL
1 cup = 240 mL (8 oz)
1 grain (gr) = 60 mg (apothecary, rarely used)
nursingActions: Always write units with every number in a calculation
Use dimensional analysis to verify unit cancellation
If converting between systems, double-check with a conversion table
Teach patients household equivalents for home medication administration
Report orders written in non-standard units to pharmacy
Never abbreviate 'units' as 'U' (can be misread as '0'  -  write 'units')

med-math-drip-rate-formulas.title: Drip Rate Formulas & Shortcuts

cellular.title: Mastering Drip Rate Calculations
cellular.content: Drip rate calculations are essential for nursing exams and clinical practice when managing gravity IV infusions. The universal drip rate formula (Volume × Drop Factor ÷ Time in minutes = gtt/min) can be simplified using shortcuts when specific drop factors are used. Understanding these shortcuts saves time on exams and reduces calculation errors in practice. The drop factor is determined by the IV tubing  -  it is printed on the tubing package and indicates how many drops equal 1 mL. Macrodrip tubing (10, 15, or 20 gtt/mL) is used for standard adult infusions and fluid resuscitation. Microdrip tubing (60 gtt/mL) is used when precise flow control is needed (pediatrics, keep-open rates, medication infusions).

riskFactors: Using wrong drop factor for the tubing being used
Forgetting to convert hours to minutes
Not verifying the rate by counting drops after setting up
Gravity infusion rate drift due to patient position changes

left: 10 gtt/mL (macrodrip)
15 gtt/mL (macrodrip)
20 gtt/mL (macrodrip)
60 gtt/mL (microdrip)
right: ÷ 6 shortcut
÷ 4 shortcut
÷ 3 shortcut
= mL/hr shortcut

medications.name: Macrodrip 10 gtt/mL Example
medications.action: Order: 1000 mL over 8 hours. mL/hr = 1000 ÷ 8 = 125 mL/hr. Shortcut: 125 ÷ 6 = 20.8 → 21 gtt/min. Full formula confirms: (1000 × 10) ÷ 480 = 20.8 → 21 gtt/min

medications.name: Macrodrip 15 gtt/mL Example
medications.action: Order: 1000 mL over 8 hours. mL/hr = 125 mL/hr. Shortcut: 125 ÷ 4 = 31.25 → 31 gtt/min. Full formula: (1000 × 15) ÷ 480 = 31.25 → 31 gtt/min

medications.name: Microdrip 60 gtt/mL Example
medications.action: Order: 500 mL over 12 hours. mL/hr = 500 ÷ 12 = 41.7 → 42 mL/hr. Shortcut: gtt/min = mL/hr = 42 gtt/min! Full formula: (500 × 60) ÷ 720 = 41.7 → 42 gtt/min

pearls: Drop factor shortcuts save time on exams: 60 gtt/mL → gtt/min = mL/hr. 20 gtt/mL → ÷3. 15 gtt/mL → ÷4. 10 gtt/mL → ÷6.
The shortcut works because: Drop Factor ÷ 60 min/hr = the divisor. For 15 gtt/mL: 60÷15 = 4, so divide mL/hr by 4.
Counting drops: count for 15 seconds and multiply by 4. This is more accurate than counting for a full minute because patient position can change during counting.
If the IV is infusing by gravity, patient arm position matters. Raising the arm above the IV bag can stop the flow. Lowering the arm below the bag increases flow rate.
Time-taping the IV bag: mark hourly volumes on tape attached to the bag. This provides quick visual verification that the infusion is on track.
If an infusion is behind schedule, NEVER speed it up without a provider order. Rapid infusion can cause fluid overload, especially in cardiac/renal/pediatric patients.
diagnostics: Identify the IV order: solution, volume, and time
Check the drop factor on the tubing package
Convert time to minutes if using the drip rate formula
Count drops for 15 seconds and multiply by 4 to verify
management: Universal formula: gtt/min = (Volume mL × Drop Factor) ÷ Time in minutes
Shortcut for 60 gtt/mL: gtt/min = mL/hr (they are always equal)
Shortcut for 20 gtt/mL: gtt/min = mL/hr ÷ 3
Shortcut for 15 gtt/mL: gtt/min = mL/hr ÷ 4
Shortcut for 10 gtt/mL: gtt/min = mL/hr ÷ 6
Always verify by counting actual drops after adjusting the roller clamp
nursingActions: Select appropriate tubing for the infusion type
Calculate the drip rate before starting the infusion
Set the roller clamp and count drops for 15 seconds × 4 to verify
Recheck the drip rate at least every 1-2 hours
Label IV tubing with date and time of setup
Time-tape the IV bag to track infusion progress
Document the flow rate and IV site assessment

med-math-safe-dose-range.title: Safe Dose Range Verification

cellular.title: The Nurse as the Last Line of Defense
cellular.content: Before administering any medication, the nurse must verify that the ordered dose falls within the safe therapeutic range. This is especially critical for high-alert medications, pediatric patients, patients with renal or hepatic impairment, and elderly patients. The safe dose range is found in the drug reference and is typically expressed as a dose range (e.g., 10-20 mg/kg/day), a maximum single dose, and a maximum daily dose. The nurse calculates the patient-specific safe range based on the patient's weight, then compares the ordered dose. If the ordered dose falls below the minimum (potentially subtherapeutic) or above the maximum (potentially toxic), the nurse must hold the medication and notify the prescriber. This verification process is a core nursing competency tested on all licensing exams.

riskFactors: Failure to check the drug reference before administering
Not accounting for renal/hepatic dose adjustments
Assuming the prescriber's order is always correct
Not calculating the safe range for each individual patient
Using adult dose ranges for pediatric patients
Polypharmacy with multiple drugs affecting the same system

left: Minimum therapeutic dose
Maximum therapeutic dose
Maximum single dose
Maximum daily dose
right: Ordered dose within range → Administer
Ordered dose below range → Clarify (subtherapeutic?)
Ordered dose above range → Hold and notify
Dose adjustment needed? (renal, hepatic, age)

medications.name: Gentamicin (Aminoglycoside)
medications.action: Therapeutic range: 5-7 mg/kg/day for standard dosing. Trough level must be <2 mcg/mL to avoid nephrotoxicity. Peak: 5-10 mcg/mL. Example: 70 kg patient → 350-490 mg/day

medications.name: Digoxin (Cardiac Glycoside)
medications.action: Therapeutic serum level: 0.5-2.0 ng/mL (many sources now recommend 0.5-1.0 ng/mL). Hold if pulse <60 bpm (adult) or <100 bpm (infant). Toxicity symptoms: visual changes (yellow-green halos), nausea, bradycardia, dysrhythmias

medications.name: Vancomycin
medications.action: Trough level target: 15-20 mcg/mL for serious infections. Draw trough 30 minutes before the 4th or 5th dose (steady state). Red Man Syndrome occurs with rapid infusion  -  infuse over at least 60 minutes.

pearls: The nurse's obligation: You are legally and ethically required to verify dose safety before administration. 'The doctor ordered it' is NOT a defense for administering an unsafe dose.
Safe dose range verification is MANDATORY for all pediatric patients, all weight-based medications, and all narrow therapeutic index drugs.
When holding a medication for safety concerns, always document: (1) the medication held, (2) the reason (with calculation), (3) the prescriber notified, (4) the prescriber's response.
Narrow therapeutic index drugs (digoxin, lithium, warfarin, aminoglycosides, vancomycin, theophylline, phenytoin) require serum drug levels for monitoring.
Renal impairment affects drug clearance  -  many drugs require dose reduction or extended intervals when GFR is decreased.
When in doubt: HOLD the medication, check the reference, calculate the range, and CALL the prescriber. It is always safer to verify than to guess.
diagnostics: Look up the drug in a current drug reference
Identify the recommended dose range (mg/kg or flat dose)
Identify the maximum single dose and maximum daily dose
Note any dose adjustments for renal/hepatic impairment
Calculate the patient-specific safe range based on weight
management: Step 1: Find the recommended mg/kg dose range in the drug reference
Step 2: Calculate the LOW end: patient weight (kg) × minimum mg/kg = minimum dose
Step 3: Calculate the HIGH end: patient weight (kg) × maximum mg/kg = maximum dose
Step 4: Compare the ordered dose to the calculated range
Step 5: If within range → administer. If outside range → hold and notify prescriber
Step 6: Document your calculation and the action taken
nursingActions: Perform safe dose range calculations for all weight-based medications
Verify all pediatric doses against the drug reference safe range
Hold medications that exceed the maximum safe dose
Notify the prescriber with the calculated safe range when clarifying an order
Document the reason for holding and the prescriber notification
Re-verify doses when patient weight changes significantly

endometriosis.title: Endometriosis

cellular.title: Ectopic Endometrial Tissue
cellular.content: Endometrial tissue grows outside the uterus, most commonly on the ovaries, fallopian tubes, and pelvic peritoneum. This ectopic tissue responds to cyclic hormonal stimulation (estrogen and progesterone), undergoing proliferation, secretion, and bleeding with each menstrual cycle. Because there is no outlet for the trapped blood, it causes chronic inflammation, adhesion formation, and fibrosis. Repeated cycles of bleeding and scarring lead to pelvic pain, dysmenorrhea, dyspareunia, and potential infertility due to tubal distortion and ovarian damage.

left: Cyclic pelvic pain worsening with menses
Dysmenorrhea unresponsive to NSAIDs
Dyspareunia (painful intercourse)
Chronic pelvic pain
right: Infertility
Heavy or irregular menstrual bleeding
Painful bowel movements during menses
Fatigue

medications.name: Leuprolide (Lupron)
medications.action: Suppresses estrogen production by downregulating pituitary GnRH receptors, creating a hypoestrogenic state

medications.name: Norethindrone/Ethinyl Estradiol
medications.action: Suppresses ovulation and reduces endometrial proliferation

pearls: Endometriosis is estrogen-dependent, so symptoms improve with menopause or hormonal suppression
Laparoscopy is the gold standard for definitive diagnosis
Infertility affects up to 50% of patients with endometriosis
diagnostics: Expect pelvic ultrasound to evaluate for endometriomas
Monitor pain patterns in relation to menstrual cycle
Expect laparoscopy for definitive diagnosis and staging
Monitor CA-125 levels if ordered (may be elevated)
Expect MRI for deep infiltrating endometriosis
management: Administer NSAIDs as ordered for pain management
Administer hormonal therapy as prescribed (oral contraceptives, GnRH agonists)
Prepare patient for laparoscopic excision or ablation if ordered
Apply heat to lower abdomen or back for comfort
Educate on importance of follow-up and symptom tracking
nursingActions: Assess pain characteristics using a standardized pain scale
Monitor for medication side effects (GnRH agonists cause menopausal symptoms)
Educate patient on relationship between estrogen and symptom flares
Provide emotional support for chronic pain and fertility concerns
Report uncontrolled pain or new symptoms to provider
Document menstrual cycle patterns and pain episodes

polycystic-ovary-syndrome.title: Polycystic Ovary Syndrome (PCOS)

cellular.title: Hormonal Dysregulation
cellular.content: PCOS involves a complex hormonal imbalance characterized by excess androgen production from the ovaries and adrenal glands. Insulin resistance leads to hyperinsulinemia, which stimulates ovarian theca cells to produce excess androgens. Elevated androgens disrupt the hypothalamic-pituitary-ovarian axis, impairing follicular development and preventing ovulation. Multiple immature follicles accumulate in the ovaries (polycystic appearance). Chronic anovulation leads to unopposed estrogen exposure on the endometrium, increasing the risk of endometrial hyperplasia and cancer.

left: Irregular or absent menstrual periods
Hirsutism (excess facial and body hair)
Acne (hormonal, along jawline)
Weight gain, especially central obesity
right: Acanthosis nigricans (darkened skin folds)
Thinning scalp hair (androgenic alopecia)
Difficulty conceiving (anovulatory infertility)
Mood disturbances (anxiety, depression)

medications.name: Metformin
medications.action: Decreases hepatic glucose production and increases peripheral insulin sensitivity, reducing hyperinsulinemia and indirectly lowering androgen levels

medications.name: Spironolactone
medications.action: Blocks androgen receptors and inhibits androgen synthesis, reducing hirsutism and acne

pearls: PCOS is a leading cause of anovulatory infertility in reproductive-age women
Weight loss of 5-10% can restore ovulation and improve metabolic markers
Screen PCOS patients regularly for type 2 diabetes, dyslipidemia, and depression
diagnostics: Expect serum testosterone and DHEA-S levels (elevated androgens)
Monitor fasting glucose and insulin levels for insulin resistance
Expect pelvic ultrasound showing multiple small peripheral follicles
Monitor lipid panel for metabolic syndrome
Expect LH-to-FSH ratio assessment
Monitor for signs of endometrial hyperplasia
management: Encourage weight loss through diet and exercise (5-10% reduction improves symptoms)
Administer metformin as ordered for insulin resistance
Administer combined oral contraceptives as prescribed to regulate cycles
Administer spironolactone as ordered for hirsutism
Educate on long-term metabolic and cardiovascular risks
nursingActions: Assess for signs of hyperandrogenism (acne, hirsutism, alopecia)
Monitor blood glucose and HbA1c as ordered
Educate on lifestyle modifications for weight management
Provide emotional support for body image concerns
Report irregular bleeding patterns or signs of endometrial hyperplasia
Monitor for metformin GI side effects and educate to take with food

ovarian-cysts.title: Ovarian Cysts

cellular.title: Follicular or Corpus Luteum Dysfunction
cellular.content: Ovarian cysts are fluid-filled sacs that develop on or within the ovary. Functional cysts are the most common type and arise from normal ovulatory processes. Follicular cysts form when a mature follicle fails to rupture and release the ovum, continuing to enlarge. Corpus luteum cysts form when the corpus luteum fails to regress after ovulation and fills with fluid or blood. Cyst enlargement causes ovarian capsule distension, resulting in pelvic pain. Rupture causes sudden, sharp pain and potential intraperitoneal bleeding. Ovarian torsion occurs when a cyst causes the ovary to twist on its vascular pedicle, compromising blood supply and causing ischemic necrosis.

left: Unilateral pelvic pain or pressure
Dull aching or sharp pain
Bloating or abdominal fullness
Menstrual irregularities
right: Sudden severe pain (rupture or torsion)
Nausea and vomiting (torsion)
Referred shoulder pain (diaphragm irritation from bleeding)
Guarding and rebound tenderness (rupture)

medications.name: Ibuprofen
medications.action: Inhibits cyclooxygenase enzymes, reducing prostaglandin synthesis and inflammation

medications.name: Combined Oral Contraceptive
medications.action: Suppresses ovulation, preventing new functional cyst formation

pearls: Most functional cysts resolve spontaneously within 8-12 weeks without intervention
Sudden severe unilateral pain with nausea and vomiting is torsion until proven otherwise, requiring emergent surgical evaluation
Postmenopausal cysts require closer evaluation as malignancy risk is higher
diagnostics: Expect pelvic ultrasound (transvaginal) to characterize cyst size and type
Monitor serial ultrasounds for cyst resolution or growth
Expect serum hCG to rule out ectopic pregnancy
Monitor CBC if rupture suspected (hemoglobin drop)
Expect tumor markers (CA-125) if malignancy concern in postmenopausal patients
management: Observe and monitor functional cysts (most resolve in 8-12 weeks)
Administer analgesics as ordered for pain management
Administer oral contraceptives as prescribed to prevent new cyst formation
Prepare for surgical intervention if torsion, rupture with hemodynamic instability, or persistent complex cyst
Maintain NPO status if surgical intervention anticipated
nursingActions: Assess pain location, intensity, and onset characteristics
Monitor vital signs for signs of hemorrhage (tachycardia, hypotension) if rupture suspected
Report sudden, severe unilateral pelvic pain immediately (may indicate torsion or rupture)
Educate patient on signs requiring emergency care
Monitor intake and output if surgery planned
Provide emotional support and education about condition

uterine-fibroids.title: Uterine Fibroids (Leiomyomas)

cellular.title: Benign Myometrial Neoplasm
cellular.content: Uterine fibroids are benign smooth muscle tumors arising from the myometrium. They are estrogen and progesterone dependent, growing during reproductive years and shrinking after menopause. Fibroids cause symptoms based on their size and location: submucosal fibroids distort the endometrial cavity causing heavy menstrual bleeding, intramural fibroids enlarge the uterus causing pressure symptoms, and subserosal fibroids cause pelvic pressure on adjacent organs. Heavy menstrual bleeding results from increased endometrial surface area, disrupted uterine contractility, and impaired hemostasis. Chronic blood loss leads to iron-deficiency anemia.

riskFactors: African American descent (3-4 times higher risk)

left: Heavy menstrual bleeding (menorrhagia)
Prolonged menstrual periods
Pelvic pressure or fullness
Enlarged, irregular uterus on palpation
right: Urinary frequency (bladder compression)
Constipation (rectal compression)
Iron-deficiency anemia symptoms (fatigue, pallor)
Lower back pain

medications.name: Leuprolide (Lupron)
medications.action: Creates hypoestrogenic state by downregulating pituitary GnRH receptors, causing fibroid shrinkage

medications.name: Tranexamic Acid
medications.action: Inhibits plasminogen activation, stabilizing clots and reducing menstrual blood loss

pearls: Fibroids are estrogen-dependent and typically shrink after menopause
Heavy menstrual bleeding is the most common symptom and leading indication for hysterectomy
Fibroids are benign and rarely undergo malignant transformation
diagnostics: Expect pelvic ultrasound to determine fibroid number, size, and location
Monitor CBC for iron-deficiency anemia from chronic blood loss
Expect MRI for surgical planning or complex cases
Monitor menstrual pattern changes and bleeding volume
Expect endometrial biopsy to rule out malignancy in abnormal bleeding
management: Administer iron supplementation as ordered for anemia
Administer GnRH agonists as prescribed to shrink fibroids preoperatively
Prepare patient for myomectomy or hysterectomy as indicated
Administer tranexamic acid as ordered for heavy menstrual bleeding
Educate on uterine artery embolization as a minimally invasive option
nursingActions: Assess menstrual bleeding patterns (number of pads/tampons, clot passage, duration)
Monitor hemoglobin and signs of anemia (fatigue, pallor, tachycardia)
Educate patient on iron supplementation (take with vitamin C, avoid dairy)
Report signs of acute hemorrhage or hemodynamic instability
Provide preoperative and postoperative education if surgery planned
Assess for urinary frequency or constipation from fibroid pressure effects

ovarian-cancer.title: Ovarian Cancer

cellular.title: Silent Peritoneal Malignancy
cellular.content: Ovarian cancer arises from ovarian epithelial cells, germ cells, or stromal cells, with epithelial ovarian carcinoma being the most common and lethal form. It is often called the 'silent killer' because early symptoms are vague and nonspecific. The cancer spreads primarily through direct peritoneal seeding, leading to widespread intra-abdominal dissemination. Malignant cells produce excess fluid causing ascites. BRCA1 and BRCA2 gene mutations significantly increase lifetime risk. Ovarian cancer is frequently diagnosed at advanced stages (Stage III or IV) due to the lack of effective screening methods and the deep pelvic location of the ovaries.

riskFactors: Family history of ovarian or breast cancer

left: Persistent abdominal bloating
Early satiety
Pelvic or abdominal pain
Urinary frequency or urgency
right: Unintentional weight loss or weight gain (ascites)
Abdominal distension (ascites)
Changes in bowel habits
Fatigue and malaise

medications.name: Carboplatin
medications.action: Forms DNA cross-links that inhibit DNA synthesis and transcription in cancer cells

medications.name: Paclitaxel (Taxol)
medications.action: Stabilizes microtubules, preventing cell division in M phase of the cell cycle

pearls: Ovarian cancer presents with vague symptoms, so persistent bloating, early satiety, and pelvic pain lasting more than 2-3 weeks warrant evaluation
CA-125 is used for treatment monitoring, not screening in the general population
BRCA mutation carriers may consider prophylactic bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy
diagnostics: Expect CA-125 level (often elevated but not diagnostic alone)
Expect pelvic and transvaginal ultrasound
Monitor CT or MRI for staging
Expect surgical staging with debulking for tissue diagnosis
Monitor for ascites development on imaging
management: Prepare patient for surgical debulking (cytoreductive surgery)
Administer chemotherapy as ordered (platinum-taxane regimen)
Manage ascites with paracentesis as ordered
Monitor nutritional status and support caloric intake
Coordinate referral to genetic counseling for BRCA testing
nursingActions: Monitor abdominal girth for ascites progression
Assess nutritional status and manage nausea from chemotherapy
Monitor for signs of bowel obstruction (nausea, vomiting, absent bowel sounds)
Provide emotional support for advanced-stage diagnosis
Report new or worsening abdominal distension
Educate on chemotherapy side effects and infection prevention
Assess pain and administer analgesics as ordered

pelvic-inflammatory-disease.title: Pelvic Inflammatory Disease (PID)

cellular.title: Ascending Polymicrobial Infection
cellular.content: Pelvic inflammatory disease is an ascending infection from the lower genital tract (cervix and vagina) to the upper genital tract (uterus, fallopian tubes, ovaries). It is most commonly caused by Neisseria gonorrhoeae and Chlamydia trachomatis, though polymicrobial infection is typical. Bacteria ascend through the cervical os, causing endometritis, salpingitis, and peritonitis. Inflammation damages the tubal epithelium, causing adhesion formation and tubal scarring. This scarring increases the risk of ectopic pregnancy, chronic pelvic pain, and infertility. Tubo-ovarian abscess is a serious complication that may require surgical drainage.

left: Lower abdominal pain (bilateral)
Cervical motion tenderness (chandelier sign)
Mucopurulent cervical discharge
Fever and chills
right: Nausea and vomiting
Abnormal vaginal bleeding
Dyspareunia (painful intercourse)
Adnexal tenderness on exam

medications.name: Ceftriaxone
medications.action: Inhibits bacterial cell wall synthesis, effective against Neisseria gonorrhoeae

medications.name: Doxycycline
medications.action: Inhibits bacterial protein synthesis, effective against Chlamydia trachomatis

pearls: Cervical motion tenderness (chandelier sign) is a hallmark finding on bimanual exam
All sexual partners within 60 days must be treated to prevent reinfection
Each episode of PID significantly increases the risk of ectopic pregnancy and infertility
diagnostics: Expect endocervical swabs for gonorrhea and chlamydia testing
Monitor CBC (elevated WBC indicates infection)
Expect erythrocyte sedimentation rate and C-reactive protein (elevated)
Expect pelvic ultrasound to evaluate for tubo-ovarian abscess
Monitor temperature trends
Expect pregnancy test to rule out ectopic pregnancy
management: Administer broad-spectrum antibiotics as ordered (cover gonorrhea, chlamydia, and anaerobes)
Treat sexual partners to prevent reinfection
Position in semi-Fowler's to promote pelvic drainage
Administer analgesics as ordered for pain management
Hospitalize if tubo-ovarian abscess, pregnancy, or failed outpatient therapy
nursingActions: Assess pain characteristics and pelvic tenderness
Monitor vital signs for fever trends
Administer antibiotics as ordered and on schedule
Educate on completing full antibiotic course even if symptoms improve
Report signs of peritonitis (rigid abdomen, rebound tenderness, high fever)
Educate on STI prevention including condom use
Ensure partner notification and treatment

benign-prostatic-hyperplasia.title: Benign Prostatic Hyperplasia (BPH)

cellular.title: Prostatic Glandular Enlargement
cellular.content: BPH is a non-malignant enlargement of the prostate gland caused by hyperplasia of stromal and glandular cells in the transitional zone surrounding the prostatic urethra. Age-related changes in the androgen-estrogen balance, combined with the action of dihydrotestosterone (DHT), stimulate prostatic growth. The enlarged prostate compresses the urethra, increasing resistance to urinary outflow. This causes bladder outlet obstruction leading to urinary hesitancy, weak stream, incomplete emptying, and urinary retention. Chronic obstruction leads to detrusor muscle hypertrophy, trabeculation, and eventually bladder decompensation. Prolonged obstruction can cause hydronephrosis and renal impairment.

left: Urinary frequency and urgency
Nocturia (waking to void multiple times)
Hesitancy (difficulty initiating stream)
Weak urinary stream
right: Incomplete bladder emptying
Post-void dribbling
Straining to void
Urinary retention (acute or chronic)

medications.name: Tamsulosin (Flomax)
medications.action: Relaxes smooth muscle of the prostate and bladder neck, reducing urethral resistance

medications.name: Finasteride (Proscar)
medications.action: Blocks conversion of testosterone to dihydrotestosterone (DHT), reducing prostate size over months

pearls: BPH is NOT a risk factor for prostate cancer, but both can coexist
Avoid anticholinergics, antihistamines, and decongestants as they worsen urinary retention
Post-TURP: Expect hematuria initially, monitor continuous bladder irrigation, and report bright red bleeding or clot retention
diagnostics: Expect prostate-specific antigen (PSA) to rule out malignancy
Monitor post-void residual volume by bladder scan
Expect urinalysis to rule out infection
Monitor renal function (BUN, creatinine) for obstructive nephropathy
Expect digital rectal exam findings (smooth, symmetrical enlargement)
Expect uroflowmetry to measure urinary flow rate
management: Administer alpha-adrenergic blockers as ordered (tamsulosin)
Administer 5-alpha reductase inhibitors as ordered (finasteride)
Educate on timed voiding and double voiding techniques
Avoid medications that worsen retention (anticholinergics, decongestants)
Prepare for transurethral resection of the prostate (TURP) if medical therapy fails
nursingActions: Assess urinary elimination patterns (frequency, urgency, nocturia, stream force)
Monitor post-void residual volumes as ordered
Report urinary retention or inability to void
Educate on medication timing (tamsulosin 30 minutes after same meal daily)
Advise to avoid caffeine and alcohol, especially before bedtime
Monitor for orthostatic hypotension with alpha-blocker therapy
Post-TURP: Monitor for hemorrhage and continuous bladder irrigation patency

testicular-cancer.title: Testicular Cancer

cellular.title: Germ Cell Malignancy
cellular.content: Testicular cancer most commonly arises from germ cells, classified as seminomas or non-seminomas. Seminomas are the most common single type, growing slowly and being highly radiosensitive. Non-seminomas (embryonal carcinoma, yolk sac tumor, choriocarcinoma, teratoma) are more aggressive. Cryptorchidism (undescended testis) is the strongest risk factor, as the abnormal thermal environment impairs normal germ cell development and increases malignant transformation risk. Cancer presents as a painless, firm testicular mass. Tumor markers (AFP, beta-hCG, LDH) are elevated based on tumor type and aid in diagnosis, staging, and treatment monitoring. Testicular cancer has excellent cure rates even in advanced stages.

riskFactors: Personal history of testicular cancer in contralateral testis

left: Painless, firm testicular mass (most common presentation)
Testicular heaviness or dragging sensation
Dull ache in lower abdomen or groin
Gynecomastia (hCG-secreting tumors)
right: Testicular swelling or enlargement
Hydrocele (reactive fluid collection)
Back pain (retroperitoneal lymph node involvement)
Dyspnea or cough (pulmonary metastases)

medications.name: Cisplatin
medications.action: Cross-links DNA strands, inhibiting DNA replication in rapidly dividing cancer cells

medications.name: Bleomycin
medications.action: Causes DNA strand breaks and inhibits DNA synthesis

pearls: A painless testicular mass in a young male is cancer until proven otherwise
Never perform transscrotal biopsy as it risks tumor seeding to inguinal lymph nodes
Testicular cancer has greater than 95% cure rate when detected early, even with metastatic disease
diagnostics: Expect testicular ultrasound for initial mass evaluation
Expect serum tumor markers (AFP, beta-hCG, LDH)
Expect CT scan of abdomen and chest for staging
Monitor tumor markers post-orchiectomy for treatment response
Expect radical inguinal orchiectomy for tissue diagnosis (NOT transscrotal biopsy)
management: Prepare for radical inguinal orchiectomy (primary treatment)
Administer chemotherapy as ordered (BEP regimen: bleomycin, etoposide, cisplatin)
Prepare for retroperitoneal lymph node dissection if indicated
Offer sperm banking before treatment
Educate on testicular self-examination for contralateral testis
nursingActions: Provide emotional support for body image and fertility concerns
Educate on sperm banking options before initiating treatment
Monitor for chemotherapy side effects (bleomycin pulmonary toxicity)
Assess for signs of metastasis (back pain, dyspnea, lymphadenopathy)
Report any respiratory changes during bleomycin therapy immediately
Teach monthly testicular self-examination of remaining testis
Provide post-orchiectomy wound care education

erectile-dysfunction.title: Erectile Dysfunction

cellular.title: Impaired Vascular and Neurogenic Response
cellular.content: Erectile dysfunction (ED) is the inability to achieve or maintain an erection sufficient for sexual intercourse. Normal erection requires intact neural signaling (parasympathetic nervous system), adequate arterial blood flow, functional smooth muscle relaxation, and venous occlusion within the corpora cavernosa. Nitric oxide (NO) released from endothelial cells activates cyclic GMP, causing smooth muscle relaxation and increased penile blood flow. ED results from vascular disease (atherosclerosis reduces arterial inflow), neurogenic damage (diabetes, spinal cord injury), hormonal deficiency (low testosterone), or psychogenic factors. ED is often an early marker of systemic cardiovascular disease.

left: Inability to achieve erection
Inability to maintain erection
Reduced sexual desire
Anxiety related to sexual performance
right: Signs of cardiovascular disease (hypertension, dyslipidemia)
Peripheral neuropathy signs (numbness in extremities)
Depression or mood changes
Relationship distress

medications.name: Sildenafil (Viagra)
medications.action: Inhibits phosphodiesterase-5, preventing breakdown of cyclic GMP, which enhances smooth muscle relaxation and blood flow to the penis during sexual stimulation

medications.name: Tadalafil (Cialis)
medications.action: Same mechanism as sildenafil but with longer duration of action (up to 36 hours)

pearls: ED may be the earliest manifestation of systemic atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease
PDE5 inhibitors are absolutely contraindicated with nitrates in any form due to risk of fatal hypotension
Always assess for psychogenic causes, as performance anxiety and depression are common contributing factors
diagnostics: Expect cardiovascular risk assessment (lipid panel, fasting glucose, blood pressure)
Monitor testosterone levels
Expect medication review for contributory drugs
Monitor psychosocial assessment for depression and anxiety
Expect vascular studies if peripheral vascular disease suspected
management: Administer PDE5 inhibitors as ordered (first-line pharmacotherapy)
Encourage lifestyle modifications (exercise, smoking cessation, weight loss)
Adjust contributing medications in consultation with provider
Consider testosterone replacement if hypogonadism confirmed
Refer for psychosexual counseling if psychogenic component identified
nursingActions: Assess sexual health history in a nonjudgmental manner
Educate on PDE5 inhibitor use and contraindications
Screen for depression and relationship distress
Report ED in younger men as potential early cardiovascular disease marker
Educate on avoiding nitrate use with PDE5 inhibitors (life-threatening hypotension)
Assess for medication side effects contributing to ED

sexually-transmitted-infections.title: Sexually Transmitted Infections (STIs)

cellular.title: Pathogen-Specific Tissue Invasion
cellular.content: STIs encompass bacterial, viral, and parasitic infections transmitted through sexual contact. Chlamydia trachomatis is the most commonly reported bacterial STI, causing mucopurulent cervicitis and urethritis; untreated, it ascends to cause PID. Neisseria gonorrhoeae causes purulent urethritis and cervicitis with risk of disseminated gonococcal infection. Treponema pallidum (syphilis) progresses through stages: primary (chancre), secondary (widespread rash, condylomata lata), latent, and tertiary (gummas, neurosyphilis, cardiovascular syphilis). Herpes simplex virus (HSV-2) establishes latency in sacral ganglia with recurrent painful vesicular outbreaks. Many STIs are asymptomatic initially, facilitating transmission and delayed treatment.

riskFactors: Men who have sex with men (MSM)

left: Chlamydia: often asymptomatic, mucopurulent discharge
Gonorrhea: purulent urethral or cervical discharge, dysuria
Syphilis primary: painless chancre (hard, clean ulcer)
HSV: painful vesicular lesions, grouped on erythematous base
right: Syphilis secondary: diffuse maculopapular rash (palms and soles), condylomata lata
Trichomoniasis: frothy, yellow-green malodorous vaginal discharge
HPV: genital warts (condylomata acuminata)
Systemic: fever, lymphadenopathy, malaise (disseminated infection)

medications.name: Ceftriaxone + Doxycycline
medications.action: Ceftriaxone treats gonorrhea (cell wall synthesis inhibitor); doxycycline treats chlamydia (protein synthesis inhibitor)

medications.name: Benzathine Penicillin G
medications.action: Bactericidal against Treponema pallidum, disrupting cell wall synthesis

pearls: Many STIs are asymptomatic, so routine screening in high-risk populations is essential for early detection
Always screen for co-infection when one STI is identified
Jarisch-Herxheimer reaction (fever, myalgia within 24 hours of syphilis treatment) is expected and self-limiting, not an allergy
diagnostics: Expect nucleic acid amplification testing (NAAT) for chlamydia and gonorrhea
Monitor RPR or VDRL for syphilis screening, confirmed with FTA-ABS
Expect HIV testing with fourth-generation antigen/antibody assay
Monitor viral cultures or PCR for herpes simplex
Expect wet mount or NAAT for Trichomonas vaginalis
Monitor for co-infection with multiple STIs simultaneously
management: Administer antibiotics as ordered based on pathogen identified
Treat all sexual partners (expedited partner therapy when appropriate)
Educate on barrier methods (condoms) for prevention
Report notifiable diseases to public health as required
Administer recommended vaccinations (HPV, hepatitis B)
Screen for concurrent STIs when one is identified
nursingActions: Obtain specimens using proper collection technique before antibiotic administration
Educate on completing full antibiotic course
Ensure partner notification and treatment
Provide nonjudgmental counseling on safer sexual practices
Report notifiable STIs to public health authorities
Educate on abstaining from sexual contact until treatment is completed and symptoms resolve
Screen for intimate partner violence during STI evaluation

menopause.title: Menopause

cellular.title: Ovarian Follicular Depletion
cellular.content: Menopause is the permanent cessation of menstruation resulting from the loss of ovarian follicular activity. As follicular reserves deplete, the ovaries produce progressively less estrogen and progesterone. Declining inhibin B levels reduce negative feedback on the pituitary, causing elevated FSH and LH. Estrogen deficiency causes vasomotor instability (hot flashes, night sweats), urogenital atrophy (vaginal dryness, dyspareunia, urinary symptoms), and accelerated bone loss. Decreased estrogen also alters lipid metabolism, increasing LDL cholesterol and cardiovascular disease risk. Menopause is confirmed after 12 consecutive months of amenorrhea in a woman over 45 without other pathologic causes.

riskFactors: Chemotherapy or radiation to the pelvis

left: Hot flashes and night sweats
Irregular menses progressing to amenorrhea
Sleep disturbances and insomnia
Mood changes (irritability, anxiety, depression)
right: Vaginal dryness and dyspareunia
Urinary frequency and urgency
Decreased libido
Joint pain and muscle aches

medications.name: Conjugated Estrogen/Medroxyprogesterone (Prempro)
medications.action: Replaces estrogen to reduce vasomotor symptoms; progesterone protects endometrium from unopposed estrogen stimulation

medications.name: Vaginal Estrogen (Estrace Cream)
medications.action: Restores estrogen to vaginal and urethral tissues, reversing atrophic changes

pearls: HRT should be used at the lowest effective dose for the shortest duration necessary
Any postmenopausal bleeding must be evaluated to rule out endometrial cancer
Bone loss accelerates in the first 5-7 years after menopause, making early intervention critical
diagnostics: Expect elevated FSH level (confirms ovarian failure)
Monitor lipid panel for cardiovascular risk changes
Expect bone density scan (DEXA) for osteoporosis screening
Monitor thyroid function to rule out thyroid disease as cause of symptoms
Expect endometrial biopsy if postmenopausal bleeding occurs
management: Administer hormone replacement therapy (HRT) as prescribed for moderate to severe vasomotor symptoms
Educate on non-hormonal alternatives (SSRIs, gabapentin) for hot flashes
Encourage weight-bearing exercise for bone health
Recommend calcium and vitamin D supplementation as ordered
Educate on vaginal moisturizers and lubricants for urogenital atrophy
nursingActions: Assess vasomotor symptom frequency and severity
Educate on HRT benefits and risks (individualized risk assessment)
Monitor for abnormal vaginal bleeding on HRT (report immediately)
Encourage regular cardiovascular screening and bone density testing
Educate on lifestyle modifications (exercise, diet, stress management)
Provide emotional support for mood changes and sleep disturbances
Assess for depression and refer as appropriate

infertility.title: Infertility

cellular.title: Impaired Conception Mechanisms
cellular.content: Infertility is defined as the inability to conceive after 12 months of unprotected intercourse (or 6 months if the woman is over 35). Female factors include ovulatory dysfunction (anovulation from PCOS, hypothalamic dysfunction), tubal damage (PID, endometriosis causing adhesions), uterine abnormalities (fibroids, polyps), and cervical factors. Male factors include impaired spermatogenesis (varicocele, cryptorchidism, toxic exposures), obstructive azoospermia, and ejaculatory dysfunction. Hormonal imbalances affecting the hypothalamic-pituitary-gonadal axis in either partner disrupt gametogenesis. Age is a significant factor, as oocyte quality and quantity decline with advancing maternal age, particularly after age 35.

riskFactors: Exposure to environmental toxins or radiation

left: Inability to conceive after 12 months of unprotected intercourse
Irregular or absent menstrual cycles (anovulation)
History of painful menses (endometriosis)
History of pelvic infections
right: Male: abnormal semen analysis parameters
Male: testicular pain or swelling (varicocele)
Emotional distress, anxiety, and depression
Relationship strain

medications.name: Clomiphene Citrate (Clomid)
medications.action: Blocks estrogen receptors at the hypothalamus, increasing GnRH and FSH release to stimulate ovarian follicle development and ovulation

medications.name: Letrozole (Femara)
medications.action: Inhibits aromatase enzyme, reducing estrogen production, which triggers increased FSH release and follicular development

pearls: Both partners must be evaluated simultaneously, as male factors contribute to approximately 40% of infertility cases
Ovarian hyperstimulation syndrome is a potentially life-threatening complication of ovulation induction requiring close monitoring
Age is the single strongest predictor of female fertility, with significant decline after age 35
diagnostics: Expect semen analysis for male partner evaluation
Monitor basal body temperature charting for ovulation confirmation
Expect hormone panel (FSH, LH, estradiol, progesterone, thyroid, prolactin)
Expect hysterosalpingography (HSG) to assess tubal patency
Monitor ovulation with serial ultrasounds
Expect anti-Mullerian hormone (AMH) for ovarian reserve assessment
management: Administer clomiphene citrate as ordered for ovulation induction
Prepare for intrauterine insemination (IUI) if indicated
Prepare for in vitro fertilization (IVF) if indicated
Address modifiable risk factors (weight, smoking, alcohol, stress)
Refer for surgical correction of anatomic abnormalities (varicocele repair, tubal surgery)
nursingActions: Provide emotional support and acknowledge grief and frustration
Educate on optimal timing of intercourse (fertility window)
Teach basal body temperature and ovulation predictor kit use
Monitor for ovarian hyperstimulation syndrome (OHSS) with fertility medications
Report signs of OHSS (rapid weight gain, abdominal distension, dyspnea)
Coordinate multidisciplinary care with reproductive endocrinology
Assess coping strategies and refer for counseling as needed

delayed-puberty.title: Delayed Puberty

cellular.title: Hypothalamic-Pituitary-Gonadal Axis Dysfunction
cellular.content: Delayed puberty is defined as the absence of breast development by age 13 in females or testicular enlargement by age 14 in males. The underlying pathophysiology centers on disruption of the hypothalamic-pituitary-gonadal (HPG) axis. Normally, the hypothalamus begins pulsatile secretion of gonadotropin-releasing hormone (GnRH) at puberty, stimulating the anterior pituitary to release luteinizing hormone (LH) and follicle-stimulating hormone (FSH), which in turn stimulate gonadal sex steroid production (estrogen or testosterone). Delayed puberty is classified as hypogonadotropic hypogonadism (low LH/FSH due to hypothalamic or pituitary failure) or hypergonadotropic hypogonadism (elevated LH/FSH due to primary gonadal failure). Constitutional delay is the most common cause and represents a physiologic variant where the HPG axis activates later than average but functions normally. Pathological causes include Kallmann syndrome (defective GnRH neuron migration with anosmia), Turner syndrome (45,X karyotype causing ovarian dysgenesis), and Klinefelter syndrome (47,XXY causing testicular dysfunction). Chronic illness, malnutrition, excessive exercise, and hypothalamic-pituitary tumors can also suppress GnRH pulsatility through disruption of kisspeptin signaling pathways.

riskFactors: Family history of late puberty (constitutional delay)
Chronic illness (celiac disease, inflammatory bowel disease, cystic fibrosis)

left: Absence of breast development by age 13 in females
Absence of testicular enlargement by age 14 in males
No menarche by age 15-16
Short stature relative to peers
right: Delayed bone age on radiograph
Absent or minimal pubic and axillary hair
Psychosocial distress and social isolation
Anosmia (suggests Kallmann syndrome)

medications.name: Conjugated Estrogens (Premarin)
medications.action: Provides exogenous estrogen to stimulate development of secondary sexual characteristics including breast development, uterine growth, and epiphyseal maturation

medications.name: Testosterone (IM or Transdermal)
medications.action: Provides exogenous testosterone to stimulate male secondary sexual characteristics including genital growth, voice deepening, muscle mass, and bone maturation

pearls: Constitutional delay is the most common cause and has a positive family history of late puberty in parents or siblings
Bone age is delayed in constitutional delay but matches chronological age in gonadal failure
Anosmia combined with delayed puberty is a hallmark of Kallmann syndrome and warrants MRI evaluation of olfactory bulbs
diagnostics: Expect serum LH and FSH levels to differentiate hypogonadotropic from hypergonadotropic causes
Expect bone age radiograph (left hand and wrist) to assess skeletal maturity
Monitor growth velocity and plot on standardized growth charts
Expect karyotype analysis if gonadal dysgenesis suspected
Expect brain MRI if central cause suspected (hypothalamic or pituitary pathology)
Expect serum estradiol or testosterone levels to assess gonadal function
Monitor thyroid function tests and prolactin levels to rule out contributing endocrine disorders
management: Administer sex steroid replacement therapy as prescribed to induce secondary sexual characteristics
Coordinate with endocrinology for individualized hormone induction protocols
Administer calcium and vitamin D supplementation as ordered for bone health
Prepare for genetic counseling referral if chromosomal abnormality identified
Educate on expected timeline of pubertal changes with treatment
nursingActions: Assess Tanner staging at each visit to monitor pubertal progression
Monitor growth velocity and track on appropriate growth charts
Assess psychosocial impact including peer relationships, self-esteem, and body image
Provide emotional support and age-appropriate education about pubertal development
Report failure to progress on hormone therapy to provider
Educate family on importance of adherence to hormone replacement regimen
Screen for signs of depression or social withdrawal related to delayed development

precocious-puberty.title: Precocious Puberty

cellular.title: Premature HPG Axis Activation
cellular.content: Precocious puberty is defined as the onset of secondary sexual characteristics before age 8 in females or age 9 in males. It is classified as central (GnRH-dependent) or peripheral (GnRH-independent). Central precocious puberty (CPP) results from premature activation of the hypothalamic-pituitary-gonadal axis, with early pulsatile GnRH secretion triggering LH and FSH release, which stimulates gonadal sex steroid production. The normal inhibitory mechanisms that keep the HPG axis quiescent during childhood - including gamma-aminobutyric acid (GABA) tone and makorin ring finger protein 3 (MKRN3) - fail prematurely. Kisspeptin neurons in the arcuate nucleus activate GnRH neurons earlier than expected. CPP is idiopathic in 80-90% of girls but often has an identifiable CNS cause in boys (hypothalamic hamartoma, CNS tumors, hydrocephalus). Peripheral precocious puberty (PPP) is GnRH-independent and results from autonomous sex steroid production from adrenal tumors, ovarian cysts, congenital adrenal hyperplasia, or McCune-Albright syndrome (activating GNAS1 mutation causing constitutive cAMP signaling). The key clinical consequence is accelerated linear growth with premature epiphyseal fusion, resulting in initial tall stature but ultimately compromised adult height.

riskFactors: Female sex (central precocious puberty is more common in girls)
CNS pathology (tumors, hamartomas, hydrocephalus, trauma, radiation)
Exposure to exogenous sex steroids (environmental estrogens, contaminated products)

left: Breast development before age 8 in girls
Testicular enlargement before age 9 in boys
Pubic or axillary hair development
Growth acceleration (tall for age initially)
right: Advanced bone age on radiograph
Acne and body odor
Mood changes and emotional lability
Menarche at early age (girls with central precocious puberty)

medications.name: Leuprolide (Lupron Depot-Ped)
medications.action: Continuous administration desensitizes pituitary GnRH receptors, suppressing LH and FSH release and halting gonadal stimulation. Initial transient increase in gonadotropins is followed by sustained suppression.

medications.name: Histrelin (Supprelin LA)
medications.action: Subcutaneous implant provides continuous GnRH agonist release for 12 months, achieving sustained HPG axis suppression without repeated injections

pearls: Central precocious puberty in boys always warrants brain MRI as CNS pathology is found in up to 50% of cases
The goal of GnRH agonist therapy is to preserve adult height potential by slowing epiphyseal fusion
Peripheral precocious puberty does not respond to GnRH agonist therapy because it is GnRH-independent
diagnostics: Expect serum LH and FSH levels (elevated in central, suppressed in peripheral)
Expect GnRH stimulation test (pubertal LH response confirms central precocious puberty)
Expect bone age radiograph showing skeletal age advancement beyond chronological age
Expect brain MRI in all boys and girls under age 6 with central precocious puberty to exclude CNS lesions
Monitor serum estradiol or testosterone levels
Expect adrenal androgens (DHEA-S, 17-hydroxyprogesterone) if peripheral cause suspected
Expect pelvic ultrasound to evaluate ovarian and uterine maturation
management: Administer GnRH agonist therapy as prescribed for central precocious puberty to suppress the HPG axis
Coordinate with pediatric endocrinology for ongoing management
Monitor growth velocity and bone age progression during treatment
Prepare for surgical referral if CNS lesion or hormone-secreting tumor identified
Educate family on treatment goals: slowing pubertal progression to preserve adult height
nursingActions: Assess Tanner staging at each visit to monitor pubertal progression or suppression
Monitor height velocity and plot on growth charts
Administer GnRH agonist injections according to prescribed schedule
Assess for injection site reactions and rotate sites
Provide age-appropriate education to the child about body changes
Provide psychosocial support for emotional and behavioral challenges
Report breakthrough pubertal signs during treatment to provider
Educate parents on importance of treatment adherence for height preservation

dysmenorrhea.title: Dysmenorrhea

cellular.title: Prostaglandin-Mediated Myometrial Hypercontractility
cellular.content: Dysmenorrhea is painful menstruation caused by excessive uterine contractions. Primary dysmenorrhea occurs in the absence of pelvic pathology and is driven by prostaglandin overproduction. During the luteal phase, declining progesterone triggers endometrial cell membrane phospholipid breakdown via phospholipase A2, releasing arachidonic acid. Cyclooxygenase-2 (COX-2) converts arachidonic acid to prostaglandins, primarily prostaglandin F2-alpha (PGF2α) and prostaglandin E2 (PGE2). PGF2α is a potent myometrial stimulant that causes sustained uterine contractions exceeding 150-180 mmHg of intrauterine pressure. These intense contractions compress myometrial blood vessels, causing ischemia-reperfusion injury to the uterine muscle. The resulting tissue hypoxia activates pain receptors (nociceptors) and generates reactive oxygen species that amplify inflammation. PGE2 sensitizes peripheral nerve endings to pain, lowering the pain threshold. Vasopressin also contributes by enhancing myometrial contractility and vasoconstriction. Secondary dysmenorrhea results from identifiable pelvic pathology such as endometriosis, adenomyosis (endometrial glands invading the myometrium), uterine fibroids, or pelvic inflammatory disease. In adenomyosis, ectopic endometrial tissue within the myometrium responds to cyclic hormonal changes, causing localized prostaglandin release, muscle disruption, and chronic uterine enlargement with painful, heavy periods.

left: Cramping suprapubic or lower abdominal pain beginning with menses onset
Pain radiating to lower back and inner thighs
Pain lasting 48-72 hours, worst on day 1-2
Primary: onset within 1-2 years of menarche
right: Nausea, vomiting, and diarrhea (systemic prostaglandin effects)
Headache and fatigue
Secondary: progressive worsening over time
Secondary: associated dyspareunia, heavy bleeding, or intermenstrual pain

medications.name: Ibuprofen
medications.action: Inhibits cyclooxygenase-1 and cyclooxygenase-2 enzymes, blocking conversion of arachidonic acid to prostaglandins PGF2α and PGE2, reducing myometrial contractility, uterine ischemia, and peripheral pain sensitization

medications.name: Levonorgestrel IUD (Mirena)
medications.action: Releases levonorgestrel locally into the uterine cavity, causing endometrial decidualization and atrophy, reducing prostaglandin production, and suppressing endometrial proliferation

pearls: Primary dysmenorrhea is caused by prostaglandin overproduction and typically responds to NSAIDs when initiated before pain onset
Dysmenorrhea that progressively worsens, begins after age 25, or is associated with dyspareunia should raise suspicion for secondary causes, especially endometriosis
Heat therapy at 40°C has been shown to be as effective as ibuprofen for primary dysmenorrhea pain relief
diagnostics: Expect thorough menstrual and pain history to distinguish primary from secondary dysmenorrhea
Expect pelvic examination to assess for masses, tenderness, or uterine enlargement
Expect pelvic ultrasound if secondary cause suspected (fibroids, adenomyosis, ovarian pathology)
Monitor response to NSAID therapy as a diagnostic indicator (primary dysmenorrhea typically responds well)
Expect laparoscopy if endometriosis suspected and imaging is inconclusive
Expect MRI for adenomyosis evaluation if ultrasound findings are equivocal
management: Administer NSAIDs as first-line treatment, initiated 1-2 days before expected menses and continued for 2-3 days
Administer hormonal contraceptives as prescribed (oral, patch, ring, or IUD) to reduce prostaglandin production
Apply continuous low-level topical heat (40°C) to the lower abdomen for pain relief
Encourage regular aerobic exercise to reduce prostaglandin levels and improve blood flow
Prepare for levonorgestrel IUD placement as ordered for secondary dysmenorrhea or NSAID-refractory cases
Educate on complementary therapies (TENS, omega-3 fatty acids, magnesium)
nursingActions: Assess pain using a standardized scale including location, quality, timing, and aggravating/relieving factors
Educate on proper NSAID timing (initiate before pain onset for maximal prostaglandin synthesis inhibition)
Monitor for NSAID side effects including GI irritation, renal effects, and bleeding risk
Assess for red flags suggesting secondary dysmenorrhea (progressive worsening, dyspareunia, abnormal bleeding patterns, new onset after age 25)
Report symptoms unresponsive to NSAIDs and hormonal therapy to provider for further workup
Educate on non-pharmacological comfort measures (heat, positioning, relaxation techniques)
Provide education differentiating normal menstrual discomfort from symptoms requiring evaluation

abnormal-uterine-bleeding.title: Abnormal Uterine Bleeding (AUB)

cellular.title: Endometrial Dysfunction and PALM-COEIN Classification
cellular.content: Abnormal uterine bleeding encompasses any deviation from normal menstrual parameters in frequency, regularity, duration, or volume. The FIGO PALM-COEIN classification system organizes causes into structural (PALM) and non-structural (COEIN) categories. PALM represents Polyp, Adenomyosis, Leiomyoma (fibroids), and Malignancy/hyperplasia - all identifiable structural lesions. COEIN represents Coagulopathy, Ovulatory dysfunction, Endometrial causes, Iatrogenic factors, and Not yet classified. Normal endometrial physiology depends on coordinated estrogen-driven proliferation, progesterone-mediated secretory transformation, and organized withdrawal bleeding when hormone levels decline. In ovulatory dysfunction (the most common non-structural cause), chronic anovulation produces sustained unopposed estrogen exposure without progesterone opposition. This causes disordered endometrial proliferation, fragile vasculature formation, and irregular shedding. The endometrium becomes thickened and unstable, breaking down irregularly and unpredictably. Without progesterone-induced stromal compaction and organized spiral arteriole vasoconstriction, hemostasis is impaired and bleeding is prolonged and heavy. In coagulopathies (von Willebrand disease being the most common inherited cause), defective platelet adhesion or clotting factor deficiency impairs normal hemostatic mechanisms at the endometrial level, causing heavy menstrual bleeding from menarche. Endometrial causes involve primary disorders of local hemostasis, including deficiencies in endothelin-1, prostaglandin F2α (vasoconstrictors), and excess tissue plasminogen activator (fibrinolytic), leading to heavy but regular bleeding.

left: Heavy menstrual bleeding (soaking through pad or tampon in less than 2 hours)
Menstrual periods lasting longer than 7 days
Intermenstrual bleeding or spotting
Passage of blood clots larger than a quarter
right: Iron-deficiency anemia symptoms (fatigue, pallor, dyspnea on exertion, tachycardia)
Irregular menstrual cycle frequency
Postcoital bleeding
Flooding episodes or blood staining through clothing

medications.name: Tranexamic Acid (Lysteda)
medications.action: Competitively inhibits plasminogen activation to plasmin at the endometrial surface, stabilizing fibrin clots and preventing premature clot dissolution, thereby reducing menstrual blood loss

medications.name: Medroxyprogesterone Acetate (Provera)
medications.action: Provides progesterone opposition to estrogen-stimulated endometrium, inducing secretory transformation and organized withdrawal bleeding, stabilizing the fragile anovulatory endometrium

pearls: PALM-COEIN is the standardized FIGO classification: Polyp, Adenomyosis, Leiomyoma, Malignancy (structural) and Coagulopathy, Ovulatory dysfunction, Endometrial, Iatrogenic, Not yet classified (non-structural)
Heavy menstrual bleeding from menarche should prompt evaluation for an underlying coagulopathy, especially von Willebrand disease, which affects up to 13% of women with menorrhagia
Any postmenopausal bleeding requires endometrial biopsy to rule out endometrial cancer until proven otherwise
diagnostics: Expect detailed menstrual history with pictorial blood assessment chart (PBAC) scoring
Expect pregnancy test to rule out pregnancy-related bleeding
Monitor CBC for iron-deficiency anemia (low hemoglobin, low MCV, low ferritin)
Expect thyroid function tests (TSH)
Expect coagulation studies (PT, PTT, von Willebrand panel) especially in adolescents with heavy menstrual bleeding from menarche
Expect transvaginal ultrasound to evaluate endometrial thickness and structural pathology
Expect endometrial biopsy in women over 45 or with risk factors for endometrial hyperplasia
Monitor serum ferritin to assess iron stores
management: Administer hormonal therapy as prescribed based on underlying cause (combined oral contraceptives, progestins, levonorgestrel IUD)
Administer tranexamic acid as ordered for acute heavy menstrual bleeding
Administer IV conjugated estrogen as ordered for acute severe uterine hemorrhage
Replace iron stores with oral iron supplementation and dietary counseling
Prepare for endometrial biopsy or hysteroscopy as indicated
Prepare for surgical management if medical therapy fails (endometrial ablation, hysterectomy)
Administer blood transfusion as ordered for hemodynamically significant anemia
nursingActions: Assess bleeding patterns including frequency, duration, volume (pad/tampon count), and clot passage
Monitor vital signs for hemodynamic instability in acute hemorrhage (tachycardia, hypotension, orthostatic changes)
Monitor hemoglobin, hematocrit, and iron studies as ordered
Report signs of hemodynamic instability or bleeding not responsive to initial treatment
Educate on iron supplementation (take with vitamin C for absorption, expect dark stools, avoid with dairy and antacids)
Educate on hormonal therapy expected effects and potential side effects
Assess for signs of endometrial hyperplasia or malignancy (postmenopausal bleeding, persistent irregular bleeding in perimenopausal women)
Provide emotional support for the impact of chronic heavy bleeding on quality of life

vaginitis.title: Vaginitis

cellular.title: Vaginal Microbiome Disruption
cellular.content: Vaginitis is inflammation of the vaginal mucosa resulting from infection, irritation, or hormonal changes. The healthy vaginal microbiome is dominated by Lactobacillus species, which ferment glycogen (stored in estrogen-stimulated vaginal epithelium) to produce lactic acid and hydrogen peroxide, maintaining vaginal pH at 3.8-4.5. This acidic environment suppresses pathogenic organisms. Bacterial vaginosis (BV) results from a shift in the vaginal microbiome from Lactobacillus dominance to an overgrowth of anaerobic bacteria, particularly Gardnerella vaginalis, Atopobium vaginae, Prevotella, and Mobiluncus species. These organisms form a polymicrobial biofilm on the vaginal epithelium that is resistant to antibiotic penetration and produces amines (putrescine, cadaverine, trimethylamine) responsible for the characteristic fishy odor, especially when exposed to alkaline substances. The vaginal pH rises above 4.5 as Lactobacillus populations decline. Vulvovaginal candidiasis (VVC) results from overgrowth of Candida species, most commonly Candida albicans. Candida transitions from commensal yeast form to pathogenic hyphal form, penetrating the vaginal epithelium and triggering an intense inflammatory response mediated by neutrophils and pro-inflammatory cytokines. This produces the characteristic thick, white, cottage cheese-like discharge with intense pruritus. Trichomoniasis is caused by Trichomonas vaginalis, a flagellated protozoan that adheres to vaginal epithelial cells via surface adhesins, causing direct cytotoxic damage and triggering a robust neutrophilic inflammatory response. The intense inflammation produces a frothy, yellow-green, malodorous discharge and vaginal erythema.

riskFactors: Antibiotic use (disrupts Lactobacillus, predisposes to BV and candidiasis)
Multiple or new sexual partners (BV, trichomoniasis)

left: BV: thin, grayish-white, homogeneous discharge with fishy odor
BV: vaginal pH greater than 4.5
VVC: thick, white, cottage cheese-like discharge
VVC: intense vulvovaginal pruritus and burning
right: Trichomoniasis: frothy, yellow-green, malodorous discharge
Trichomoniasis: strawberry cervix (punctate hemorrhages) on examination
Vulvar erythema, edema, and excoriation (VVC)
Dysuria and dyspareunia (all types)

medications.name: Metronidazole (Flagyl)
medications.action: Enters bacterial and protozoal cells where nitroreductases reduce the nitro group, generating cytotoxic intermediates that damage DNA, disrupting nucleic acid synthesis and causing cell death in anaerobic organisms and protozoa

medications.name: Fluconazole (Diflucan)
medications.action: Inhibits lanosterol 14-alpha-demethylase (CYP51), a cytochrome P450 enzyme essential for ergosterol synthesis in the fungal cell membrane, causing increased membrane permeability and fungal cell death

pearls: BV is the most common cause of vaginal discharge in reproductive-age women and is characterized by fishy odor, elevated pH, and clue cells
Vulvovaginal candidiasis maintains a normal vaginal pH (3.8-4.5), which differentiates it from BV and trichomoniasis
Trichomoniasis is a sexually transmitted infection requiring partner treatment, while BV and uncomplicated candidiasis are not classified as STIs
diagnostics: Expect vaginal pH testing (greater than 4.5 in BV and trichomoniasis, normal 3.8-4.5 in candidiasis)
Expect wet mount microscopy (clue cells in BV, budding yeast and pseudohyphae in candidiasis, motile trichomonads in trichomoniasis)
Expect whiff test (positive in BV: fishy odor with KOH addition to vaginal discharge)
Expect KOH preparation to visualize fungal elements
Expect nucleic acid amplification test (NAAT) for Trichomonas if wet mount is negative
