Monitor for concurrent STI co-infections (gonorrhea, chlamydia) with trichomoniasis
Expect Amsel criteria assessment for BV diagnosis (3 of 4: homogeneous discharge, pH >4.5, positive whiff test, clue cells)
management: Administer metronidazole (oral or vaginal) as prescribed for BV and trichomoniasis
Administer fluconazole or topical azole as prescribed for vulvovaginal candidiasis
Treat sexual partners concurrently for trichomoniasis to prevent reinfection
Educate on avoiding douching and vaginal irritants
Educate on completing full course of prescribed antibiotics
Recommend cotton underwear and avoidance of tight-fitting synthetic garments
nursingActions: Obtain detailed history including discharge characteristics, odor, pruritus, sexual history, and recent antibiotic use
Collect vaginal specimens using proper technique for wet mount, pH testing, and cultures
Assess vulvar and vaginal mucosa for erythema, edema, and excoriation
Educate on medication administration technique for vaginal preparations
Educate on alcohol avoidance with metronidazole therapy (disulfiram-like reaction)
Report recurrent infections (4 or more VVC episodes per year or recurrent BV) to provider for long-term management
Provide STI prevention education including condom use for trichomoniasis
Educate on vaginal hygiene practices (avoid douching, scented products, and tight synthetic clothing)

bacterial-vaginosis.title: Bacterial Vaginosis

cellular.title: Vaginal Microbiome Disruption and Biofilm Pathogenesis
cellular.content: Bacterial vaginosis (BV) represents a polymicrobial dysbiosis of the vaginal ecosystem rather than a true infection. The healthy vaginal microbiome is dominated by Lactobacillus species (primarily L. crispatus, L. jensenii, and L. iners), which metabolize glycogen deposited in estrogen-stimulated squamous epithelial cells to produce lactic acid and hydrogen peroxide, maintaining an acidic pH of 3.8-4.5 that suppresses pathogenic organisms. In BV, a dramatic ecological shift occurs: Lactobacillus populations decline precipitously while anaerobic and facultative organisms proliferate, including Gardnerella vaginalis, Atopobium vaginae, Prevotella bivia, Mobiluncus species, Sneathia, and Megasphaera. Gardnerella vaginalis initiates the process by adhering to the vaginal epithelium via pili and producing sialidase and vaginolysin, which degrade the protective mucus layer and damage epithelial cells. This allows formation of a dense polymicrobial biofilm on the vaginal epithelial surface that is highly resistant to antibiotic penetration and host immune defenses. Atopobium vaginae synergizes with Gardnerella within the biofilm, contributing to recurrence rates exceeding 50% within 12 months. The anaerobic overgrowth produces volatile amines - putrescine, cadaverine, and trimethylamine - through decarboxylation of amino acids, generating the characteristic fishy odor that intensifies with alkaline exposure (semen, KOH). The vaginal pH rises above 4.5 as lactic acid production ceases. Clue cells form when Gardnerella and other bacteria heavily coat vaginal epithelial cells, obscuring their borders on microscopy. The shift from Lactobacillus dominance to a diverse anaerobic community represents a fundamental disruption of vaginal innate immunity.

riskFactors: Douching (disrupts vaginal pH and Lactobacillus colonization)
African American ethnicity (higher prevalence linked to microbiome community state types)

left: Thin, homogeneous, grayish-white discharge coating vaginal walls
Fishy odor, especially after intercourse or with alkaline exposure
Vaginal pH greater than 4.5
Clue cells on wet mount microscopy
right: Absence of significant vulvovaginal inflammation or erythema
Positive whiff test with KOH addition
Increased discharge volume
Dysuria or mild vaginal irritation may be present

medications.name: Metronidazole (Flagyl)
medications.action: Enters anaerobic bacterial cells where nitroreductases reduce the nitro group, generating cytotoxic free radical intermediates that damage DNA helix structure, disrupting nucleic acid synthesis and causing anaerobic cell death while sparing aerobic Lactobacillus species

medications.name: Clindamycin (Cleocin)
medications.action: Binds to the 50S ribosomal subunit of susceptible anaerobic bacteria, inhibiting protein synthesis by blocking peptide bond formation and bacterial translocation, resulting in bacteriostatic activity against BV-associated anaerobes

pearls: BV is not considered a sexually transmitted infection but is associated with sexual activity and is rare in women who have never been sexually active
BV increases the risk of acquiring HIV, HSV-2, Chlamydia, and gonorrhea due to loss of the protective Lactobacillus-dominant barrier
Recurrence rates exceed 50% within 12 months due to persistent biofilm that harbors Gardnerella and Atopobium vaginae despite antibiotic treatment
Treatment of male sexual partners has not been shown to reduce recurrence in female partners in clinical trials
BV in pregnancy is associated with preterm birth, premature rupture of membranes, chorioamnionitis, and postpartum endometritis
diagnostics: Expect Amsel criteria assessment (3 of 4 required: homogeneous thin grayish-white discharge, vaginal pH >4.5, positive whiff test with KOH, clue cells on wet mount)
Expect Nugent scoring on Gram stain (score 7-10 confirms BV based on relative morphotype proportions)
Expect vaginal pH measurement using pH paper (>4.5 in BV)
Expect whiff test (addition of 10% KOH to vaginal secretions releases fishy amine odor)
Expect wet mount microscopy for clue cells (epithelial cells with stippled borders from adherent bacteria)
Monitor for concurrent STI screening as BV increases susceptibility to Chlamydia, gonorrhea, and HIV acquisition
management: Administer metronidazole 500 mg orally twice daily for 7 days as prescribed (first-line therapy)
Administer metronidazole 0.75% vaginal gel once daily for 5 days as alternative
Administer clindamycin 2% vaginal cream at bedtime for 7 days as alternative
Educate on completing full antibiotic course even if symptoms resolve
Educate on avoiding douching, scented vaginal products, and vaginal irritants
Educate on alcohol avoidance during metronidazole therapy and for 72 hours after completion
Discuss recurrence prevention strategies including vaginal probiotics (Lactobacillus-containing)
Screen and treat BV in pregnancy due to association with preterm birth and premature rupture of membranes
nursingActions: Obtain detailed history of discharge characteristics, odor timing, sexual practices, and douching habits
Collect vaginal specimens using proper swab technique for wet mount, pH testing, and Gram stain
Assess vaginal discharge for characteristic thin, homogeneous, grayish-white appearance coating vaginal walls
Educate on metronidazole disulfiram-like reaction with alcohol (nausea, vomiting, flushing, tachycardia)
Educate on vaginal clindamycin cream weakening latex condoms and diaphragms for 5 days after use
Report recurrent BV (3 or more episodes in 12 months) to provider for suppressive therapy consideration
Provide education on vaginal hygiene: avoid douching, use unscented soap externally only, wear cotton underwear
Educate pregnant patients on importance of BV treatment to reduce preterm birth risk

cervicitis.title: Cervicitis

cellular.title: Cervical Mucosal Inflammation and Ascending Infection Pathophysiology
cellular.content: Cervicitis is inflammation of the uterine cervix, predominantly affecting the columnar epithelium of the endocervical canal. The cervix serves as a critical anatomical barrier between the lower and upper reproductive tract, and its disruption creates a pathway for ascending infection. The transformation zone - where columnar epithelium meets squamous epithelium - is particularly vulnerable to infection and inflammation. Infectious cervicitis is most commonly caused by Chlamydia trachomatis and Neisseria gonorrhoeae. Chlamydia trachomatis is an obligate intracellular pathogen that infects columnar epithelial cells through a unique biphasic developmental cycle: infectious elementary bodies (EBs) attach to epithelial cells via heparan sulfate proteoglycans and are internalized into membrane-bound inclusions. Within the inclusion, EBs differentiate into metabolically active reticulate bodies (RBs) that replicate by binary fission, eventually reorganizing back into EBs that are released to infect adjacent cells. This intracellular lifecycle allows Chlamydia to evade humoral immune responses while triggering a chronic inflammatory response mediated by Th1 cytokines (IFN-gamma, TNF-alpha, IL-1). Neisseria gonorrhoeae attaches to cervical epithelial cells via type IV pili and Opa proteins, invading through both transcellular and paracellular pathways. Gonococcal lipooligosaccharide (LOS) triggers intense neutrophilic inflammation, producing the characteristic mucopurulent discharge. The organism evades immune defenses through antigenic variation of its surface pili and Opa proteins and through IgA1 protease production. Chronic cervical inflammation damages the epithelial barrier, upregulates pro-inflammatory cytokines, and increases expression of HIV co-receptors (CCR5, CXCR4) on cervical immune cells, significantly increasing susceptibility to HIV acquisition. Untreated cervicitis allows ascending infection through the endocervical canal into the endometrium and fallopian tubes, leading to pelvic inflammatory disease (PID), tubal scarring, and potential infertility or ectopic pregnancy.

riskFactors: Age under 25 years (highest prevalence of Chlamydia and gonorrhea)
Co-infection with other STIs (increases cervical vulnerability)

left: Mucopurulent endocervical discharge (yellow or green)
Endocervical friability (bleeding easily on swab contact)
Post-coital bleeding or intermenstrual spotting
Cervical erythema and edema
right: Dysuria without typical UTI findings
Dyspareunia (deep pelvic pain with intercourse)
Lower abdominal or pelvic discomfort
Often asymptomatic (up to 70-80% of chlamydial infections)

medications.name: Ceftriaxone
medications.action: Binds to penicillin-binding proteins (PBPs) on the bacterial cell wall, inhibiting transpeptidation and cross-linking of peptidoglycan polymers, resulting in defective cell wall synthesis, osmotic instability, and bactericidal activity against Neisseria gonorrhoeae

medications.name: Doxycycline
medications.action: Binds reversibly to the 30S ribosomal subunit, blocking aminoacyl-tRNA from binding to the mRNA-ribosome complex, inhibiting bacterial protein synthesis with bacteriostatic activity against Chlamydia trachomatis intracellular reticulate bodies

pearls: Up to 70-80% of chlamydial cervicitis is asymptomatic, making screening essential for sexually active women under 25 and those with risk factors
Mucopurulent cervicitis should be treated empirically before NAAT results return due to the risk of ascending infection causing PID
Cervicitis significantly increases HIV acquisition risk by 3-5 fold due to disrupted mucosal barrier and increased CCR5+ target cells
Untreated chlamydial cervicitis progresses to PID in approximately 10-15% of cases, with subsequent risks of tubal factor infertility, ectopic pregnancy, and chronic pelvic pain
diagnostics: Expect nucleic acid amplification test (NAAT) for Chlamydia trachomatis and Neisseria gonorrhoeae (gold standard, sensitivity >95%)
Expect endocervical swab for Gram stain showing >30 PMNs per high-power field
Expect speculum examination revealing mucopurulent endocervical discharge (yellow or green exudate visible at the os or on a white swab)
Monitor for friability (easily induced endocervical bleeding on swab contact)
Expect concurrent testing for Trichomonas vaginalis, Mycoplasma genitalium, and HSV if clinically indicated
Expect HIV testing and syphilis screening as part of comprehensive STI evaluation
Monitor for signs of ascending infection (PID): cervical motion tenderness, adnexal tenderness, fever
management: Administer empiric antibiotic therapy before NAAT results if mucopurulent cervicitis is present (CDC guidelines)
Administer ceftriaxone 500 mg IM single dose for gonococcal coverage as prescribed
Administer doxycycline 100 mg orally twice daily for 7 days for chlamydial coverage as prescribed
Ensure expedited partner therapy (EPT) or partner referral for STI treatment
Educate on abstaining from sexual intercourse until both patient and partner complete treatment and are symptom-free
Educate on condom use for STI prevention
Schedule test-of-cure NAAT at 4 weeks for gonorrhea (due to increasing resistance) and at 3 months for reinfection screening
Evaluate for PID if cervical motion tenderness, adnexal tenderness, or fever is present
nursingActions: Obtain comprehensive sexual history including number of partners, condom use, prior STIs, and symptoms in partners
Perform or assist with speculum examination to visualize cervix for mucopurulent discharge and friability
Collect endocervical specimens using proper NAAT collection technique (endocervical swab or self-collected vaginal swab)
Administer IM ceftriaxone using Z-track technique in the dorsogluteal or ventrogluteal muscle
Educate on importance of completing full doxycycline course (7 days) even if symptoms resolve
Educate on doxycycline administration: take with food, avoid dairy/antacids within 2 hours, use sun protection
Coordinate partner notification and treatment through health department services if needed
Report positive gonorrhea and chlamydia results to public health department as required by law
Assess for signs of PID and report immediately: fever, lower abdominal pain, cervical motion tenderness

vulvodynia.title: Vulvodynia

cellular.title: Chronic Vulvar Pain Syndrome  -  Neuroinflammatory Pathophysiology
cellular.content: Vulvodynia is a chronic vulvar pain condition lasting at least 3 months without an identifiable infectious, inflammatory, neoplastic, or neurological cause. The pathophysiology involves complex neuroinflammatory mechanisms at both peripheral and central levels. At the peripheral level, histological studies of affected vestibular tissue reveal a significant increase in intraepithelial nerve fiber density (hyperinnervation), with proliferation of nociceptive C-fibers expressing increased transient receptor potential vanilloid 1 (TRPV1) receptors and nerve growth factor (NGF) receptors. These sensitized nerve endings have lowered activation thresholds, responding to normally innocuous stimuli (allodynia) and generating amplified responses to painful stimuli (hyperalgesia). Mast cell proliferation and degranulation in the vestibular submucosa release histamine, tryptase, heparinase, and pro-inflammatory cytokines (IL-1β, TNF-α, NGF) that directly sensitize adjacent nerve fibers, creating a self-perpetuating cycle of neurogenic inflammation. The close anatomical relationship between mast cells and nerve fibers in the vestibule facilitates bidirectional communication: nociceptive fibers release substance P and calcitonin gene-related peptide (CGRP), which further activate mast cells, while mast cell mediators sensitize nerve endings. At the central level, persistent peripheral nociceptive input induces central sensitization in the dorsal horn of the spinal cord, with upregulation of N-methyl-D-aspartate (NMDA) receptors and increased excitability of second-order neurons. This central sensitization amplifies pain perception and can lead to referred pain in adjacent dermatomes. Pelvic floor muscle dysfunction (hypertonicity and myofascial trigger points in the levator ani and obturator internus muscles) frequently develops as a secondary response to chronic pain, creating additional nociceptive input and perpetuating the pain cycle. The condition is classified as either provoked (triggered by touch or pressure, most commonly localized to the vestibule as provoked vestibulodynia) or unprovoked (spontaneous, generalized vulvar pain), with provoked vestibulodynia being the most common subtype.

riskFactors: History of recurrent vulvovaginal candidiasis (epithelial inflammation may trigger nerve sensitization)
History of early or prolonged oral contraceptive use (hormonal influence on vestibular mucosa)
Pelvic floor muscle dysfunction or hypertonicity
History of anxiety, depression, or catastrophizing pain behaviors
Genetic polymorphisms affecting inflammatory cytokine production (IL-1β, IL-1RA)
History of childhood physical or sexual abuse
Comorbid chronic pain conditions (fibromyalgia, irritable bowel syndrome, interstitial cystitis)

left: Burning, stinging, or rawness of the vulva/vestibule
Allodynia (pain from light touch that is normally non-painful)
Pain with tampon insertion, speculum examination, or intercourse (provoked)
Pain localized to the vestibule (provoked vestibulodynia)
right: Hyperalgesia (amplified pain response to painful stimuli)
Pelvic floor muscle tenderness and hypertonicity on examination
Absence of visible vulvar pathology (normal-appearing tissue)
Unprovoked spontaneous vulvar burning or discomfort (generalized subtype)

medications.name: Amitriptyline (Elavil)
medications.action: Inhibits reuptake of serotonin and norepinephrine at central synapses and blocks sodium channels on peripheral nociceptive nerve fibers, modulating descending pain inhibitory pathways and reducing peripheral nerve sensitization in vulvar tissue

medications.name: Gabapentin (Neurontin)
medications.action: Binds to the alpha-2-delta subunit of voltage-gated calcium channels in dorsal root ganglia and dorsal horn neurons, reducing calcium influx and subsequent release of excitatory neurotransmitters (glutamate, substance P, CGRP), attenuating peripheral and central sensitization

pearls: Vulvodynia affects approximately 8-16% of women at some point in their lifetime, yet the average time to diagnosis is 4-5 years due to lack of awareness and visible pathology
Provoked vestibulodynia (formerly vulvar vestibulitis syndrome) is the most common subtype, characterized by pain with vestibular touch or pressure
Normal-appearing vulvar tissue on examination does not rule out vulvodynia - the pathology is at the microscopic nerve fiber and mast cell level
Multimodal treatment combining pharmacotherapy, pelvic floor physical therapy, and cognitive behavioral therapy produces the best outcomes
diagnostics: Expect cotton swab test (Q-tip test) to map areas of vulvar tenderness and allodynia, particularly at the vestibule (3-9 o'clock positions)
Expect thorough history differentiating provoked versus unprovoked pain, onset, duration, and associated factors
Expect exclusion of identifiable causes: cultures for candida, HSV testing, dermatologic evaluation for lichen sclerosus or lichen planus
Monitor pain using validated instruments (visual analog scale, vulvar pain functional questionnaire)
Expect pelvic floor muscle assessment for hypertonicity and trigger points
Expect psychosocial assessment for comorbid anxiety, depression, and sexual dysfunction impact
management: Educate on eliminating vulvar irritants: switch to cotton underwear, avoid synthetic fabrics, remove scented products
Recommend sitz baths with lukewarm water for comfort (avoid additives)
Apply topical lidocaine 5% ointment to vestibule 15-20 minutes before intercourse or as needed for pain relief
Refer for pelvic floor physical therapy with internal myofascial release and biofeedback
Administer low-dose tricyclic antidepressant (amitriptyline 10-25 mg nightly) as prescribed for neuropathic pain modulation
Administer anticonvulsant (gabapentin 300-3600 mg daily in divided doses) as prescribed for nerve pain
Recommend cognitive behavioral therapy (CBT) for pain coping strategies and addressing psychosexual impact
Apply topical estrogen cream if vestibular atrophy or hormonal contribution is suspected
nursingActions: Conduct thorough vulvar pain assessment including location, quality, triggers, duration, and impact on daily activities and sexual function
Perform cotton swab mapping of the vestibule and vulva to identify specific areas of tenderness
Educate on pain self-management strategies: ice packs wrapped in cloth, sitz baths, topical lidocaine use
Provide emotional support and validate the chronic pain experience (many patients report feeling dismissed)
Educate on pelvic floor relaxation exercises (reverse Kegels, diaphragmatic breathing)
Report worsening pain, new symptoms, or signs of depression to provider
Educate on lubricant use during intercourse (water-based, unscented, free of glycerin and parabens)
Coordinate multidisciplinary care: pelvic floor PT, pain management, psychology, gynecology

bartholinitis.title: Bartholinitis

cellular.title: Bartholin Gland Obstruction and Abscess Pathophysiology
cellular.content: The Bartholin glands (greater vestibular glands) are bilateral pea-sized glands located at the 4 and 8 o'clock positions of the vaginal vestibule, deep within the posterior labia majora. Each gland drains through a 2-2.5 cm duct that opens onto the inner surface of the labia minora at the junction of the posterior third and anterior two-thirds of the vestibular margin. The glands secrete mucoid fluid during sexual arousal, contributing to vaginal lubrication. Bartholinitis begins with obstruction of the excretory duct, most commonly due to inspissated mucus, trauma, or inflammation. When the duct becomes occluded, glandular secretions accumulate, forming a Bartholin cyst - a painless, fluctuant, unilateral labial swelling typically 1-3 cm in diameter. The cyst fluid is initially sterile. Secondary bacterial infection of the cyst transforms it into a Bartholin abscess. The infection is typically polymicrobial, involving a combination of vaginal flora (Bacteroides fragilis, Peptostreptococcus, Prevotella), enteric organisms (Escherichia coli), and occasionally sexually transmitted organisms (Neisseria gonorrhoeae, Chlamydia trachomatis). Bacterial colonization triggers a vigorous inflammatory response with neutrophil infiltration, liquefactive necrosis, and purulent material accumulation. The abscess cavity becomes walled off by a pyogenic membrane. Clinically, the abscess presents as a rapidly enlarging, exquisitely tender, erythematous, and warm unilateral labial mass with surrounding cellulitis. The intense inflammation causes significant edema that can impair ambulation and sitting. Systemic signs including fever and leukocytosis may develop with larger abscesses or surrounding tissue infection. The overlying skin may become thinned and develop a pointing area where spontaneous rupture may occur. Recurrence is common after simple incision and drainage because the duct epithelium re-obstructs, necessitating procedures that create a permanent new drainage tract (marsupialization) or maintain patency (Word catheter placement).

riskFactors: Age 20-30 years (peak incidence during reproductive years)
History of prior Bartholin cyst or abscess (high recurrence rate)

left: Unilateral labial swelling at 4 or 8 o'clock position
Bartholin cyst: painless, soft, fluctuant, non-tender labial mass
Bartholin abscess: exquisitely tender, erythematous, warm, swollen labial mass
Surrounding cellulitis with expanding erythema
right: Difficulty walking, sitting, or wearing clothing due to labial swelling
Fever and malaise with abscess formation
Spontaneous purulent drainage if abscess ruptures
Dyspareunia (painful intercourse due to labial mass)

medications.name: Amoxicillin-Clavulanate (Augmentin)
medications.action: Amoxicillin inhibits bacterial cell wall synthesis by binding to penicillin-binding proteins, while clavulanate irreversibly inhibits beta-lactamase enzymes produced by resistant organisms, restoring amoxicillin activity against beta-lactamase-producing anaerobes, E. coli, and Staphylococcus

medications.name: Ibuprofen (Advil/Motrin)
medications.action: Non-selectively inhibits cyclooxygenase-1 and cyclooxygenase-2 enzymes, reducing prostaglandin synthesis from arachidonic acid, thereby decreasing inflammation, pain signal transmission, and edema at the site of Bartholin gland infection

pearls: Bartholin gland cysts and abscesses account for approximately 2% of all gynecologic visits in reproductive-age women
Simple I&D without catheter placement has a recurrence rate of up to 50%, while Word catheter placement reduces recurrence to approximately 10-15%
In women over 40 years old, any new or persistent Bartholin gland mass should be biopsied to rule out Bartholin gland carcinoma (rare but important differential)

The Word catheter is a small balloon-tipped catheter inserted into the abscess cavity after drainage and inflated with 2-3 mL of saline; it remains for 4-6 weeks to allow epithelialization of a new drainage tract
diagnostics: Expect physical examination revealing unilateral labial mass at 4 or 8 o'clock position (cyst: non-tender, fluctuant; abscess: tender, erythematous, warm)
Expect wound culture and sensitivity of abscess fluid after drainage to guide antibiotic therapy
Expect NAAT testing for Chlamydia and gonorrhea if STI is suspected
Monitor vital signs for systemic infection indicators (fever, tachycardia)
Monitor WBC count if systemic infection is suspected
Expect biopsy consideration in women over 40 to rule out Bartholin gland carcinoma (adenocarcinoma or squamous cell carcinoma)
management: Apply warm compresses or sitz baths 3-4 times daily for 10-15 minutes to promote spontaneous drainage of small cysts
Prepare patient for incision and drainage (I&D) with Word catheter placement for abscesses (catheter remains 4-6 weeks to create epithelialized tract)
Prepare patient for marsupialization if recurrent (surgical creation of permanent drainage opening)
Administer antibiotics as prescribed for surrounding cellulitis or systemic infection (typically broad-spectrum covering anaerobes and gram-negatives)
Administer analgesics as prescribed (NSAIDs, acetaminophen, or opioids for severe abscess pain)
Educate on post-procedure wound care: sitz baths, keeping Word catheter in place, signs of recurrence
nursingActions: Assess labial mass for size, location, tenderness, erythema, warmth, fluctuance, and presence of cellulitis
Assess pain level and impact on mobility, sitting, and activities of daily living
Monitor vital signs for fever indicating systemic spread of infection
Provide warm sitz baths as ordered to promote comfort and drainage
Assist with or prepare patient for I&D procedure under local anesthesia
Provide post-I&D wound care education: keep Word catheter in place for 4-6 weeks, continue sitz baths, no tampon use or intercourse until healed
Educate on signs requiring immediate return: increasing pain, fever, expanding erythema, inability to sit or walk
Report signs of systemic infection (high fever, rigors, expanding cellulitis) to provider immediately

uterine-prolapse.title: Uterine Prolapse

cellular.title: Pelvic Floor Structural Failure and Organ Descent Pathophysiology
cellular.content: Uterine prolapse is the descent of the uterus through the pelvic floor into or beyond the vaginal canal, resulting from failure of the pelvic support structures. The pelvic floor is a complex musculofascial unit comprising three levels of support described by DeLancey: Level I (cardinal and uterosacral ligaments suspending the upper vagina and cervix from the sacrum), Level II (paravaginal attachments connecting the mid-vagina laterally to the arcus tendineus fasciae pelvis), and Level III (perineal body and urogenital diaphragm supporting the lower vagina). The levator ani muscle group (pubococcygeus, puborectalis, and iliococcygeus) forms the muscular floor of the pelvis, maintaining a tonic contraction that closes the urogenital hiatus and supports pelvic organs. Uterine prolapse results from progressive deterioration of these support systems. At the molecular level, collagen and elastin - the primary structural proteins of pelvic connective tissue - undergo qualitative and quantitative degradation. Matrix metalloproteinases (MMP-1, MMP-2, MMP-9) are upregulated in prolapsed tissue, accelerating collagen degradation while tissue inhibitors of metalloproteinases (TIMPs) are downregulated. The ratio of collagen type I (strong, tensile) to collagen type III (thin, distensible) shifts toward type III predominance, weakening tissue integrity. Elastin fibers fragment and lose their recoil capacity. Vaginal childbirth is the most significant risk factor: the fetal head descending through the birth canal causes direct mechanical injury to the levator ani muscle (avulsion from the pubic bone in up to 15-30% of vaginal deliveries), stretching and tearing of endopelvic fascia, and pudendal nerve neuropathy from compression against the ischial spine. Denervation of the levator ani leads to muscle atrophy over time. Estrogen deficiency after menopause further accelerates connective tissue deterioration, as estrogen receptors in pelvic floor tissues normally stimulate collagen synthesis, fibroblast proliferation, and tissue vascularity. Chronic intra-abdominal pressure from obesity, chronic cough, constipation, or heavy lifting provides the ongoing mechanical force that drives progressive descent through already weakened support structures. The POP-Q (Pelvic Organ Prolapse Quantification) system classifies prolapse severity: Stage 0 (no descent), Stage I (most distal portion >1 cm above the hymen), Stage II (most distal portion within 1 cm of the hymen), Stage III (most distal portion >1 cm beyond the hymen but not complete eversion), Stage IV (complete vaginal vault eversion or procidentia). Uterine prolapse frequently coexists with anterior vaginal wall prolapse (cystocele) and posterior vaginal wall prolapse (rectocele) due to shared pelvic support mechanisms.

riskFactors: Vaginal childbirth (especially prolonged labor, macrosomia, operative vaginal delivery)
Multiparity (risk increases with each vaginal delivery)
Advanced age and postmenopausal estrogen deficiency
Connective tissue disorders (Ehlers-Danlos syndrome, Marfan syndrome)
Prior pelvic surgery (hysterectomy disrupts Level I support)
Family history of pelvic organ prolapse

left: Sensation of vaginal fullness, heaviness, or pressure worsening throughout the day and with standing
Visible or palpable bulge at or beyond the vaginal introitus
Low back pain or pelvic dragging sensation
Urinary symptoms: frequency, urgency, stress incontinence, incomplete emptying
right: Difficulty with defecation, need to splint (digitally reduce prolapse to evacuate stool)
Dyspareunia or sensation of vaginal looseness
Vaginal mucosal irritation, dryness, and ulceration (especially with complete procidentia)
Symptoms improve when lying supine and worsen with prolonged standing or exertion

medications.name: Conjugated Estrogen Vaginal Cream (Premarin Vaginal)
medications.action: Binds to intracellular estrogen receptors in vaginal and periurethral epithelial cells, stimulating proliferation of the vaginal epithelium from a thin atrophic state to a thicker, more resilient glycogen-rich epithelium, increasing collagen synthesis in the subepithelial connective tissue, enhancing tissue vascularity, and restoring vaginal pH to the premenopausal acidic range

medications.name: Polyethylene Glycol 3350 (MiraLAX)
medications.action: Acts as an osmotic agent in the intestinal lumen, retaining water through hydrogen bonding and increasing fecal water content, producing softer stools that require less straining effort to evacuate, thereby reducing episodic increases in intra-abdominal pressure that worsen prolapse

pearls: Uterine prolapse affects up to 50% of parous women to some degree, though only 10-20% are symptomatic enough to seek treatment
The POP-Q system is the standardized, objective measurement system for prolapse staging, replacing older subjective grading systems
Pessaries successfully manage symptoms in approximately 70-90% of women who are properly fitted, serving as both a bridge to surgery and a long-term treatment option
Kegel exercises performed correctly and consistently for at least 3 months can improve prolapse symptoms by one POP-Q stage in mild to moderate cases
diagnostics: Expect POP-Q (Pelvic Organ Prolapse Quantification) staging assessment during Valsalva maneuver on examination
Expect speculum examination with split speculum (posterior blade only) to assess anterior and posterior compartments independently
Expect assessment for concurrent cystocele (anterior wall prolapse), rectocele (posterior wall prolapse), and enterocele
Monitor post-void residual volume to assess for urinary retention from urethral kinking
Expect urodynamic studies if urinary incontinence is present to differentiate stress, urge, and overflow components
Expect renal ultrasound if complete prolapse with concern for ureteral obstruction
management: Teach pelvic floor muscle exercises (Kegels): identify correct muscles by stopping urine midstream, then perform 10 repetitions of 10-second holds, 3 times daily
Fit and manage pessary device (ring or Gellhorn type) for non-surgical management of symptomatic prolapse
Apply vaginal estrogen cream as prescribed to improve mucosal tissue integrity and reduce pessary-related erosion
Encourage weight loss if obese to reduce chronic intra-abdominal pressure
Treat chronic cough and constipation to eliminate modifiable risk factors
Prepare patient for surgical repair if conservative measures fail: vaginal hysterectomy with vault suspension, sacrocolpopexy, or colpocleisis in select elderly patients
Educate on avoiding heavy lifting (>20 lbs) and Valsalva straining
nursingActions: Assess degree of prolapse by inspecting the introitus with the patient bearing down (Valsalva) in lithotomy and standing positions
Assess impact on quality of life: urinary symptoms (incontinence, frequency, incomplete emptying), bowel symptoms (constipation, splinting), sexual dysfunction
Teach Kegel exercises with proper technique: contract pelvic floor muscles as if stopping urine flow, hold 10 seconds, relax 10 seconds, avoid contracting abdominal/gluteal muscles
Educate on pessary self-care: daily removal and cleaning (or provider-managed every 3 months), recognizing signs of erosion (bleeding, discharge, pain)
Monitor for pessary complications: vaginal erosion, ulceration, foul-smelling discharge, incarceration
Educate on lifestyle modifications: weight management, high-fiber diet for constipation prevention, proper body mechanics for lifting
Report new or worsening urinary retention, vaginal bleeding, or irreducible prolapse to provider
Provide pre- and post-operative education for patients undergoing surgical repair including activity restrictions

benign-ovarian-cysts.title: Benign Ovarian Cysts

cellular.title: Ovarian Cyst Pathophysiology  -  Functional and Non-Functional Types
cellular.content: Ovarian cysts are fluid-filled or semi-solid sacs arising from or on the surface of the ovary. They are broadly classified as functional (physiologic) or non-functional (pathologic but benign). Functional cysts are the most common type and arise from normal ovarian follicular activity. Follicular cysts develop when a dominant ovarian follicle fails to rupture during ovulation (anovulation) and continues to enlarge due to ongoing fluid accumulation stimulated by follicle-stimulating hormone (FSH). The granulosa cells lining the cyst continue to produce estradiol, which may cause endometrial proliferation and delayed or anovulatory bleeding. Follicular cysts are typically 2-6 cm and usually resolve spontaneously within 1-3 menstrual cycles as the trapped follicular fluid is resorbed. Corpus luteum cysts form when the corpus luteum (formed after ovulation) fails to regress normally and fills with blood or fluid. The corpus luteum is a vascular structure, and hemorrhage into the central cavity creates a hemorrhagic corpus luteum cyst. These cysts produce progesterone, which may cause menstrual irregularity or a missed period, mimicking ectopic pregnancy. Corpus luteum cysts can rupture and cause significant intraperitoneal hemorrhage due to their rich blood supply. Non-functional benign cysts include dermoid cysts (mature cystic teratomas), endometriomas, and serous/mucinous cystadenomas. Dermoid cysts arise from totipotent germ cells that undergo abnormal differentiation, forming a cyst containing ectodermal derivatives (skin, hair, teeth, sebaceous material), mesodermal elements (fat, bone, cartilage), and endodermal tissue (thyroid, respiratory epithelium). They are the most common ovarian neoplasm in women under 30 and grow slowly. Torsion risk increases with cyst size >5 cm due to the weight of the cyst contents acting as a fulcrum on the ovarian pedicle. Endometriomas (chocolate cysts) contain old hemorrhagic fluid from ectopic endometrial tissue implanted on the ovarian surface that undergoes cyclic bleeding. Ovarian torsion occurs when the cyst causes the ovary and its vascular pedicle (infundibulopelvic ligament) to rotate on its axis, compressing the ovarian artery and vein. Venous and lymphatic obstruction occurs first (lower pressure), causing ovarian edema and congestion, followed by arterial obstruction leading to ischemic necrosis if not emergently detorsed. Cyst rupture releases fluid into the peritoneal cavity, causing peritoneal irritation and acute abdominal pain. Hemorrhagic cyst rupture can cause hemoperitoneum with hemodynamic instability.

riskFactors: Reproductive age (functional cysts occur during ovulatory years)
Ovulation induction medications (clomiphene, gonadotropins increase multi-follicular development)
Tamoxifen use (associated with ovarian cyst formation)

left: Dull, aching unilateral pelvic or lower abdominal pain
Pelvic fullness, bloating, or pressure
Menstrual irregularity (delayed menses with corpus luteum cyst)
Dyspareunia (pain with deep intercourse)
right: Sudden, severe unilateral pelvic pain with nausea and vomiting (torsion or rupture)
Peritoneal signs: rebound tenderness, guarding (ruptured cyst with peritoneal irritation)
Hemodynamic instability: tachycardia, hypotension (hemorrhagic cyst rupture)
Adnexal mass palpable on bimanual examination

medications.name: Ibuprofen (Advil/Motrin)
medications.action: Inhibits cyclooxygenase-1 and cyclooxygenase-2, reducing prostaglandin synthesis and decreasing inflammation, pain signal transmission, and edema associated with cyst capsule distension and peritoneal irritation

medications.name: Combined Oral Contraceptives (Ethinyl Estradiol/Levonorgestrel)
medications.action: Suppresses pituitary gonadotropin secretion (FSH and LH) through negative feedback, preventing follicular recruitment and ovulation, thereby eliminating the formation of new functional ovarian cysts

pearls: Functional ovarian cysts are extremely common and usually resolve without intervention within 1-3 menstrual cycles; reassurance is key
Ovarian torsion is a surgical emergency - do not delay intervention for imaging if clinical suspicion is high; normal Doppler flow does not exclude torsion due to dual ovarian blood supply

Dermoid cysts (mature teratomas) contain fat, hair, and teeth and have a characteristic echogenic appearance on ultrasound; they carry a 3-5% risk of torsion and should be removed if >5 cm or symptomatic
Any ovarian cyst in a postmenopausal woman warrants evaluation for malignancy with CA-125, ultrasound characterization, and possible surgical referral
diagnostics: Expect transvaginal ultrasound as first-line imaging (characterize cyst as simple vs complex, solid components, septations, vascularity)
Expect serum beta-hCG to exclude ectopic pregnancy in reproductive-age women with pelvic pain
Monitor serum CA-125 in postmenopausal women with ovarian cysts (elevated in ovarian cancer, endometriosis, PID)
Expect serial ultrasound surveillance for simple cysts <5 cm (repeat in 6-8 weeks to document resolution)
Expect CBC if ruptured hemorrhagic cyst is suspected (monitor hemoglobin for hemoperitoneum)
Expect Doppler ultrasound if ovarian torsion is suspected (absent or decreased ovarian blood flow, though normal flow does not exclude torsion)
Monitor vital signs for hemodynamic instability with ruptured hemorrhagic cysts
management: Reassure and observe for simple functional cysts <5 cm in premenopausal women (most resolve within 1-3 cycles)
Administer analgesics as prescribed for cyst-related pain (NSAIDs first-line, avoid aspirin due to bleeding risk)
Administer combined oral contraceptives as prescribed to suppress future ovulation and prevent new functional cyst formation (does not resolve existing cysts)
Prepare patient for laparoscopic cystectomy for persistent cysts >5-7 cm, symptomatic cysts, or complex cysts with concerning features
Prepare patient for emergent laparoscopy/laparotomy for suspected ovarian torsion (surgical emergency requiring detorsion within 6-8 hours to salvage ovary)
Provide hemodynamic stabilization with IV fluids and blood products for hemorrhagic cyst rupture with significant hemoperitoneum
Educate on symptoms requiring emergency care: sudden severe unilateral pelvic pain, nausea/vomiting, syncope
nursingActions: Assess pain characteristics: location (unilateral pelvic), onset (sudden with rupture/torsion vs gradual), severity, and associated symptoms
Obtain serum beta-hCG to exclude ectopic pregnancy before any imaging or intervention
Monitor vital signs closely for signs of hemorrhagic shock in suspected cyst rupture (tachycardia, hypotension, orthostatic changes)
Assess abdomen for peritoneal signs (rebound tenderness, guarding, rigidity) suggesting rupture or torsion
Administer IV fluids and analgesics as ordered for acute presentations
Educate on expected course: functional cysts typically resolve spontaneously; repeat ultrasound confirms resolution
Educate on warning signs to report immediately: sudden severe pelvic pain, dizziness, fainting, heavy vaginal bleeding
Prepare patient for surgery if torsion suspected: NPO status, IV access, pre-operative labs, consent

endometrial-polyp.title: Endometrial Polyp

cellular.title: Endometrial Glandular Overgrowth and Estrogen-Dependent Proliferation
cellular.content: Endometrial polyps are localized overgrowths of endometrial glands, stroma, and blood vessels that project from the endometrial surface into the uterine cavity. They represent a focal area of endometrial hyperplasia driven primarily by estrogen stimulation without adequate progesterone opposition. At the molecular level, polyp tissue demonstrates overexpression of estrogen receptors (ER-alpha) compared to surrounding normal endometrium, making polyp tissue more responsive to circulating estrogen. Aromatase enzyme expression is upregulated within polyp stromal cells, enabling local conversion of androgens to estrogens and creating a microenvironment of estrogen excess independent of systemic hormone levels. Additionally, polyp tissue shows relative resistance to the antiproliferative effects of progesterone due to decreased progesterone receptor (PR) expression, disrupting the normal estrogen-progesterone balance that regulates endometrial growth and shedding. Key molecular alterations include loss of PTEN (phosphatase and tensin homolog) tumor suppressor function in some polyps, removing a critical brake on the PI3K/AKT proliferative signaling pathway. Increased expression of Bcl-2 (an anti-apoptotic protein) in polyp glandular cells reduces programmed cell death, allowing continued accumulation of endometrial cells. The polyp develops a central vascular stalk (feeder vessel) that supplies blood flow to the growing tissue mass. Polyps range from a few millimeters to several centimeters and may be sessile (broad-based) or pedunculated (attached by a narrow stalk). They can be solitary or multiple. The abnormal bleeding associated with polyps results from several mechanisms: surface erosion and ulceration of the polyp as it protrudes into the uterine cavity, disruption of normal endometrial hemostasis at the polyp surface, vascular fragility of the polyp's feeder vessels, and interference with normal endometrial shedding patterns. Polyps that prolapse through the cervical os may cause intermenstrual bleeding, post-coital bleeding, and cramping. Tamoxifen, a selective estrogen receptor modulator (SERM) used in breast cancer treatment, has a paradoxical estrogen-agonist effect on the endometrium, stimulating endometrial proliferation and increasing polyp incidence by 20-35% in treated women. The overall malignant transformation rate of endometrial polyps is approximately 0.5-3%, with higher risk in postmenopausal women, women with abnormal uterine bleeding, polyps >1.5 cm, and women on tamoxifen therapy.

riskFactors: Perimenopause and postmenopause (declining progesterone levels with relative estrogen excess)
Obesity (peripheral aromatization of androgens to estrogen in adipose tissue)
Tamoxifen therapy (estrogen-agonist effect on endometrium, 20-35% incidence)
Hormone replacement therapy with estrogen (especially without adequate progestogen opposition)
Polycystic ovary syndrome (chronic anovulation, unopposed estrogen)

left: Abnormal uterine bleeding: intermenstrual spotting or heavy menstrual bleeding (menorrhagia)
Postmenopausal vaginal bleeding (any amount requires investigation)
Post-coital bleeding or spotting
Irregular menstrual cycles with unpredictable bleeding patterns
right: Infertility or recurrent pregnancy loss (polyps may interfere with implantation)
Cramping or pelvic pressure (large or prolapsing polyps)
Vaginal discharge (watery or blood-tinged)
Often asymptomatic, discovered incidentally on ultrasound

medications.name: Medroxyprogesterone Acetate (Provera)
medications.action: Binds to progesterone receptors in endometrial cells, inducing secretory transformation of the estrogen-primed endometrium, downregulating estrogen receptor expression, suppressing endometrial proliferation, and promoting organized endometrial shedding that counteracts the unopposed estrogen stimulation driving polyp formation

medications.name: Ibuprofen (Advil/Motrin)
medications.action: Inhibits cyclooxygenase enzymes, reducing endometrial prostaglandin synthesis (particularly PGE2 and PGF2α), decreasing endometrial blood flow and menstrual blood loss, and providing analgesic relief for polyp-related cramping and dysmenorrhea

pearls: Any postmenopausal vaginal bleeding requires investigation to rule out endometrial cancer - endometrial polyps are the most common benign cause but malignancy must be excluded
Saline infusion sonography (SIS) is superior to standard transvaginal ultrasound for detecting intracavitary polyps, with sensitivity approaching 95%
The malignant transformation rate of endometrial polyps is approximately 0.5-3%, with highest risk in postmenopausal women, symptomatic polyps >1.5 cm, and tamoxifen users
Hysteroscopic polypectomy is both diagnostic and therapeutic - it allows direct visualization, targeted removal, and histopathologic examination in a single procedure
diagnostics: Expect transvaginal ultrasound showing focal endometrial thickening or intracavitary mass (may show feeder vessel on Doppler)
Expect saline infusion sonography (SIS/sonohysterography) for enhanced visualization of intracavitary polyps (saline distends cavity, outlining polyp margins)
Expect hysteroscopy for direct visualization and simultaneous biopsy or removal (gold standard for diagnosis and treatment)
Expect endometrial biopsy (office Pipelle) to sample endometrium, though may miss polyps due to focal sampling
Monitor endometrial thickness on ultrasound (increased thickness >4 mm in postmenopausal women warrants investigation)
Expect histopathologic examination of removed polyp tissue to assess for atypia or malignancy
management: Observe small, asymptomatic polyps in premenopausal women (some regress spontaneously, especially <1 cm)
Prepare patient for hysteroscopic polypectomy for symptomatic polyps, polyps >1.5 cm, postmenopausal polyps, or polyps in women with risk factors for malignancy
Ensure histopathologic examination of all removed polyps to rule out atypia or endometrial cancer
Administer progestin therapy as prescribed to reduce recurrence risk in women with recurrent polyps or endometrial hyperplasia
Address underlying risk factors: weight loss for obesity, evaluate hormone therapy regimen, discuss tamoxifen alternatives with oncology
Evaluate for concurrent endometrial hyperplasia or carcinoma in postmenopausal women with bleeding
Schedule post-polypectomy follow-up ultrasound to assess for recurrence (recurrence rate 15-45%)
nursingActions: Obtain detailed menstrual history: intermenstrual bleeding, postmenopausal bleeding, menorrhagia, post-coital spotting
Educate on pad count documentation to quantify bleeding severity
Educate on saline infusion sonography procedure: mild cramping expected, take ibuprofen 30-60 minutes before, schedule in early follicular phase
Provide pre-hysteroscopy patient education: NPO requirements, anesthesia type, expected post-procedure cramping and spotting
Monitor for post-hysteroscopy complications: heavy bleeding, fever, increasing pelvic pain (signs of endometritis or perforation)
Educate postmenopausal women that any vaginal bleeding requires evaluation to rule out endometrial cancer
Coordinate pathology follow-up to ensure histology results are reviewed and communicated to patient
Report persistent or recurrent abnormal uterine bleeding after polypectomy to provider for further evaluation

galactorrhea.title: Galactorrhea

cellular.title: Prolactin Dysregulation and Mammary Secretion
cellular.content: Galactorrhea is the spontaneous or expressible milky nipple discharge unrelated to pregnancy or lactation. It results from hyperprolactinemia, which disrupts the normal hypothalamic-pituitary regulatory axis. Prolactin is secreted by lactotroph cells of the anterior pituitary gland and is uniquely regulated by tonic inhibition from dopamine released by the tuberoinfundibular dopaminergic neurons of the hypothalamus. Dopamine binds to D2 receptors on lactotrophs, suppressing prolactin gene transcription and secretion. When dopamine inhibition is disrupted - by pituitary adenomas compressing the stalk, medications blocking D2 receptors (antipsychotics, metoclopramide, domperidone), or hypothyroidism elevating TRH (which stimulates lactotrophs) - prolactin levels rise. Elevated prolactin acts on mammary epithelial cells, stimulating milk protein synthesis (casein, lactalbumin) and secretion through JAK2/STAT5 signaling. Hyperprolactinemia also suppresses GnRH pulsatility, leading to hypogonadotropic hypogonadism with amenorrhea, anovulation, and decreased libido.

riskFactors: Medications that block dopamine (antipsychotics, metoclopramide, domperidone)

left: Milky nipple discharge (bilateral in most cases)
Amenorrhea or oligomenorrhea
Headache (if pituitary mass present)
Decreased libido
right: Visual field deficits (bitemporal hemianopsia if macroadenoma compresses optic chiasm)
Infertility due to anovulation
Galactorrhea worsened by breast stimulation
Gynecomastia in males with hyperprolactinemia

medications.name: Cabergoline
medications.action: Binds D2 receptors on lactotrophs to suppress prolactin secretion and shrink prolactinomas

medications.name: Bromocriptine
medications.action: Stimulates D2 receptors on pituitary lactotrophs to inhibit prolactin release

pearls: Prolactinomas are the most common functioning pituitary adenoma - over 90% of microprolactinomas respond to dopamine agonist therapy without surgery
Always check TSH before imaging - hypothyroidism-induced hyperprolactinemia resolves with thyroid hormone replacement alone
Medication-induced hyperprolactinemia is extremely common - antipsychotics are the most frequent cause, and switching to aripiprazole (partial D2 agonist) can resolve symptoms
diagnostics: Order serum prolactin level (fasting, morning draw - elevated above upper limit confirms hyperprolactinemia)
Order TSH to rule out hypothyroidism as cause
Order pregnancy test (beta-hCG) to exclude physiologic cause
Order MRI of sella turcica with gadolinium if prolactin persistently elevated - evaluate for pituitary adenoma
Review complete medication history for dopamine-blocking agents
Order visual field testing (perimetry) if macroadenoma suspected - assess for optic chiasm compression
management: Discontinue or substitute offending medications if medication-induced
Initiate dopamine agonist therapy (cabergoline or bromocriptine) for prolactinomas
Treat underlying hypothyroidism with levothyroxine to normalize TRH and prolactin
Monitor prolactin levels serially to assess treatment response
Surgical referral (transsphenoidal resection) for macroadenomas resistant to medical therapy or causing visual field deficits
Monitor menstrual cycle restoration as indicator of treatment adequacy
nursingActions: Assess characteristics of nipple discharge - color, volume, unilateral vs bilateral, spontaneous vs expressible
Obtain thorough medication history including antipsychotics, antiemetics, antidepressants
Provide emotional support - address body image concerns and anxiety about symptoms
Educate on medication adherence for dopamine agonist therapy
Monitor for side effects of cabergoline/bromocriptine - nausea, dizziness, orthostatic hypotension
Teach signs of pituitary apoplexy (sudden severe headache, visual changes, altered consciousness) - report immediately

urethritis.title: Urethritis

cellular.title: Urethral Mucosal Inflammation and Sexually Transmitted Pathogenesis
cellular.content: Urethritis is inflammation of the urethra, classified as gonococcal (caused by Neisseria gonorrhoeae) or non-gonococcal (most commonly caused by Chlamydia trachomatis, Mycoplasma genitalium, Ureaplasma urealyticum, and Trichomonas vaginalis). Gonococcal urethritis involves Neisseria gonorrhoeae attaching to urethral columnar epithelial cells via type IV pili and Opa proteins, then invading through transcytosis. The organism's lipooligosaccharide (LOS) triggers an intense neutrophilic inflammatory response, producing the characteristic thick, purulent urethral discharge. Non-gonococcal urethritis due to Chlamydia trachomatis involves the organism's unique biphasic lifecycle: infectious elementary bodies invade epithelial cells, transform into replicating reticulate bodies within membrane-bound inclusions, then release new elementary bodies to infect adjacent cells. This intracellular lifecycle evades humoral immunity while producing a chronic inflammatory response. Mycoplasma genitalium adheres to epithelial cells via MgPa adhesin protein and causes persistent, often subclinical inflammation. Untreated urethritis can ascend to cause epididymitis in males or PID in females, leading to potential infertility.

riskFactors: New sexual partner within past 60 days
Male sex (more commonly symptomatic in males)

left: Urethral discharge (purulent/thick in gonococcal, clear/mucoid in non-gonococcal)
Dysuria (burning with urination)
Urethral itching or irritation
Frequency and urgency
right: Meatal erythema and edema
Testicular pain if epididymitis develops (ascending infection)
Many cases are asymptomatic (especially chlamydial)
Discharge may be noted on underwear

medications.name: Ceftriaxone
medications.action: Binds penicillin-binding proteins (PBPs) to inhibit bacterial cell wall synthesis, bactericidal against N. gonorrhoeae

medications.name: Doxycycline
medications.action: Inhibits bacterial protein synthesis by binding the 30S ribosomal subunit, effective against C. trachomatis and M. genitalium

pearls: Always treat for both gonorrhea and chlamydia when gonorrhea is confirmed, as coinfection rates are 20-40%
Chlamydia is the most common bacterial STI worldwide and is often asymptomatic - screening is essential for sexually active individuals under 25
Mycoplasma genitalium is increasingly recognized as a major cause of persistent or recurrent non-gonococcal urethritis resistant to standard doxycycline therapy
diagnostics: Order nucleic acid amplification test (NAAT) for N. gonorrhoeae and C. trachomatis - urine sample or urethral swab
Order M. genitalium NAAT if available (emerging important pathogen)
Urethral Gram stain showing greater than 5 WBCs per high-power field with or without gram-negative intracellular diplococci
Order urinalysis - first-void urine for leukocyte esterase (screening)
Consider Trichomonas vaginalis NAAT in persistent or recurrent cases
Order RPR/VDRL and HIV testing as part of STI workup
management: Empiric treatment before NAAT results for symptomatic patients
Gonococcal urethritis: ceftriaxone 500 mg IM single dose plus doxycycline 100 mg PO twice daily for 7 days (covers possible chlamydial coinfection)
Non-gonococcal urethritis: doxycycline 100 mg PO twice daily for 7 days
M. genitalium urethritis: azithromycin 1 g day 1 then 500 mg days 2-4 (or moxifloxacin if macrolide-resistant)
Abstain from sexual intercourse until patient and partner complete treatment (minimum 7 days)
Treat all sexual partners from the past 60 days - expedited partner therapy when appropriate
nursingActions: Obtain thorough sexual history - number of partners, condom use, history of STIs, last sexual contact
Collect first-void urine specimen for NAAT (instruct patient to hold urine for at least 1 hour before collection)
Educate on completing the full antibiotic course even if symptoms resolve
Counsel on partner notification and treatment - all partners within 60 days must be evaluated
Educate on consistent condom use for future STI prevention
Schedule follow-up NAAT in 3 months to test for reinfection (high reinfection rates)
Provide non-judgmental counseling and ensure confidentiality

urethral-stricture.title: Urethral Stricture

cellular.title: Urethral Fibrosis and Obstructive Uropathy
cellular.content: Urethral stricture is narrowing of the urethral lumen caused by scar tissue formation (spongiofibrosis) within the corpus spongiosum surrounding the urethra. The pathophysiology involves injury to the urethral epithelium from trauma, infection, instrumentation, or inflammation, triggering an abnormal wound healing cascade. Damaged urothelial cells release pro-inflammatory cytokines and transforming growth factor-beta (TGF-beta), which activates fibroblasts to proliferate and deposit excessive collagen (types I and III) in the periurethral tissue. This fibrosis contracts the urethral lumen, increasing resistance to urine flow. The resulting obstructive uropathy causes the detrusor muscle to hypertrophy in compensation, but progressive obstruction leads to detrusor decompensation, urinary retention, and potential upper tract damage (hydroureter, hydronephrosis) if untreated. Iatrogenic causes (urethral catheterization, cystoscopy, TURP) are the most common etiology in developed countries, while infectious causes (untreated gonococcal urethritis) remain significant in developing regions.

riskFactors: Hypospadias repair or prior urethral surgery

left: Weak or split urinary stream
Straining to initiate or maintain voiding
Incomplete bladder emptying sensation
Urinary frequency and nocturia
right: Urinary retention (acute or chronic)
Recurrent urinary tract infections
Post-void dribbling
Urethral discharge or bleeding

medications.name: Tamsulosin
medications.action: Relaxes smooth muscle in the bladder neck and prostatic urethra to improve urine flow

medications.name: Ciprofloxacin
medications.action: Inhibits DNA gyrase and topoisomerase IV to treat concurrent urinary tract infections associated with urethral stricture

pearls: Urethral stricture has a high recurrence rate after dilation and DVIU (50-70% within 2 years) - urethroplasty has the best long-term success rate (85-95%)
The most common cause in developed countries is iatrogenic injury from catheterization or endoscopic procedures - use the smallest catheter size possible and adequate lubrication
Urinary retention from stricture is a urologic emergency requiring prompt intervention - do NOT force a catheter through a stricture as this can create a false passage
diagnostics: Retrograde urethrogram (RUG) to visualize stricture location, length, and severity
Voiding cystourethrogram (VCUG) to assess proximal extent
Uroflowmetry - decreased peak flow rate (Qmax less than 15 mL/sec suggests obstruction)
Post-void residual volume measurement (elevated with significant obstruction)
Cystourethroscopy for direct visualization - defines stricture caliber and mucosal appearance
Urinalysis and urine culture to evaluate for concurrent UTI
management: Urethral dilation - gradual stretching with sequential dilators for short, simple strictures
Direct visual internal urethrotomy (DVIU) - endoscopic incision of stricture under direct vision
Urethroplasty (surgical reconstruction) for recurrent or complex strictures - gold standard with highest long-term success
Buccal mucosal graft urethroplasty for longer strictures
Clean intermittent self-catheterization (CISC) to maintain lumen patency after dilation or DVIU
Suprapubic catheter placement for acute urinary retention when urethral passage is impossible
nursingActions: Monitor urinary output - assess stream strength, straining, dribbling, incomplete emptying
Perform accurate intake and output measurement
Assess for signs of urinary retention - suprapubic distension, discomfort, inability to void
Provide post-procedure catheter care - maintain patency, monitor for bleeding, prevent traction
Teach clean intermittent self-catheterization technique if prescribed
Educate on adequate fluid intake and importance of follow-up uroflowmetry
Report inability to void, significant hematuria, or signs of UTI immediately

phimosis.title: Phimosis

cellular.title: Preputial Fibrosis and Foreskin Non-Retractability
cellular.content: Phimosis is the inability to retract the foreskin (prepuce) over the glans penis. It is classified as physiologic or pathologic. Physiologic phimosis is a normal developmental condition in uncircumcised males - the prepuce is adherent to the glans at birth due to natural adhesions between the inner preputial epithelium and glans epithelium. These adhesions gradually separate through intermittent erections, keratinization, and desquamation over the first several years of life. By age 3, approximately 90% of boys have a retractable foreskin. Pathologic phimosis results from scarring and fibrosis of the preputial opening, most commonly caused by balanitis xerotica obliterans (BXO), also known as lichen sclerosus. BXO is an autoimmune chronic inflammatory condition that causes progressive fibrosis and whitening of the preputial tissue. The inflammatory process involves T-lymphocyte infiltration, basement membrane thickening, and collagen homogenization in the lamina propria, creating a tight, non-elastic fibrotic ring. Recurrent balanitis (from poor hygiene, diabetes, or infection) can also cause preputial scarring and secondary phimosis. Pathologic phimosis increases the risk of recurrent infections, urinary obstruction, and squamous cell carcinoma due to chronic inflammation and smegma retention.

riskFactors: Poor hygiene leading to chronic inflammation
Age - physiologic phimosis is normal in children under 3

left: Inability to retract foreskin over the glans
White fibrotic ring at the preputial opening (BXO)
Ballooning of foreskin during urination
Dysuria or difficulty voiding
right: Pain with erection in adolescents and adults
Recurrent balanitis (erythema, discharge under foreskin)
Smegma accumulation
Paraphimosis if foreskin becomes trapped behind glans (emergency)

medications.name: Betamethasone 0.05% cream
medications.action: Reduces inflammation and fibrosis of the preputial tissue, promoting skin thinning and elasticity to allow foreskin retraction

medications.name: Clotrimazole 1% cream
medications.action: Inhibits ergosterol synthesis in fungal cell membranes, treating concurrent candidal balanitis

pearls: Never forcefully retract the foreskin in a child with physiologic phimosis - this causes microtears, bleeding, and scarring that converts physiologic phimosis to pathologic phimosis
Topical corticosteroid therapy is first-line for pathologic phimosis with a success rate of 60-90%, reducing the need for circumcision
Paraphimosis (foreskin trapped behind glans) is a urologic emergency - the constricting band causes venous congestion, edema, and potential ischemic necrosis of the glans if not reduced promptly
diagnostics: Clinical examination - assess degree of foreskin retractability using Kikiros grading scale
Differentiate physiologic (soft, non-scarred preputial opening) from pathologic (white fibrotic ring at preputial tip)
Assess for concurrent balanitis - erythema, discharge, or edema of glans or foreskin
Monitor voiding - observe for ballooning of foreskin during urination (indicates significant narrowing)
Urinalysis if UTI suspected from urinary obstruction
Consider biopsy of fibrotic tissue if BXO suspected to confirm diagnosis
management: Physiologic phimosis in children under 3: reassurance and observation - normal developmental variant requiring no intervention
Topical corticosteroid therapy (betamethasone 0.05% or clobetasol 0.05%) applied to the preputial opening twice daily for 4-8 weeks
Gentle retraction exercises in conjunction with topical steroid therapy
Preputioplasty (dorsal slit or Z-plasty) for foreskin preservation when topical therapy fails
Circumcision as definitive treatment for recurrent pathologic phimosis or BXO
Treat underlying balanitis or BXO concurrently
nursingActions: Assess foreskin retractability and document degree of phimosis
Educate parents on physiologic phimosis - reassure that forced retraction is contraindicated and harmful
Teach proper application of topical corticosteroid - thin layer to the tight preputial opening, gentle retraction stretching after steroid softens tissue
Provide post-circumcision wound care education - petroleum jelly gauze application, signs of infection
Educate on hygiene - gentle retraction of foreskin for cleaning when retractable, avoid harsh soaps
Report paraphimosis immediately (foreskin trapped behind glans - urologic emergency)

priapism.title: Priapism

cellular.title: Persistent Penile Erection and Corporal Ischemia
cellular.content: Priapism is a persistent penile erection lasting more than 4 hours that is unrelated to sexual stimulation. It is classified into ischemic (low-flow, veno-occlusive) and non-ischemic (high-flow, arterial) types. Ischemic priapism is a urologic emergency accounting for over 95% of cases. Normal erection physiology involves nitric oxide release from cavernous nerve terminals and endothelial cells, which activates guanylate cyclase to increase cyclic GMP (cGMP). cGMP relaxes corporal smooth muscle via protein kinase G-mediated calcium channel closure and potassium channel opening, allowing arterial blood to fill the sinusoidal spaces of the corpora cavernosa. Erection is terminated when phosphodiesterase 5 (PDE5) degrades cGMP, restoring smooth muscle tone and venous outflow. In ischemic priapism, venous outflow is obstructed, trapping deoxygenated blood in the corpora cavernosa. This creates a compartment syndrome-like condition - stagnant blood becomes acidotic and hypoxic, causing endothelial injury, smooth muscle edema, and progressive fibrosis. After 4-6 hours, irreversible smooth muscle necrosis begins. After 24-48 hours, extensive fibrosis leads to permanent erectile dysfunction. Sickle cell disease is the most common cause in children and African American males - sickling of red blood cells in the low-oxygen corporal sinusoids causes mechanical venous obstruction. Non-ischemic priapism results from unregulated arterial inflow after perineal or penile trauma creating a cavernosal artery-sinusoidal fistula.

riskFactors: Sickle cell disease or trait (most common cause in children)
Intracavernosal injection therapy for erectile dysfunction (alprostadil)

left: Rigid, painful erection persisting beyond 4 hours (ischemic)
Erection involves corpora cavernosa but NOT corpus spongiosum or glans (glans remains soft)
Dark blood on aspiration (ischemic)
Progressive worsening pain
right: Non-ischemic priapism is typically painless or mildly uncomfortable
History of perineal trauma in non-ischemic type
Bright red blood on aspiration (non-ischemic)
Associated sickle cell crisis symptoms (pain crisis, fever)

medications.name: Phenylephrine (intracavernosal)
medications.action: Stimulates alpha-1 receptors on corporal smooth muscle causing contraction, which compresses sinusoidal spaces and restores venous outflow to resolve ischemic priapism

medications.name: Hydroxyurea
medications.action: Increases fetal hemoglobin (HbF) production, reducing sickling of red blood cells and preventing recurrent priapism episodes in sickle cell disease

pearls: Ischemic priapism is a urologic emergency analogous to compartment syndrome - every hour of delay reduces the chance of preserving erectile function
The glans penis and corpus spongiosum remain SOFT in priapism because they have separate venous drainage - a fully rigid glans suggests a different diagnosis

Sickle cell disease accounts for approximately 65% of priapism cases in children - all children with sickle cell disease and their families should receive anticipatory guidance about priapism recognition and the importance of emergency evaluation within 4 hours
diagnostics: Clinical diagnosis based on persistent erection greater than 4 hours with pain
Corporal blood gas analysis to differentiate ischemic from non-ischemic - ischemic: dark blood with pH less than 7.25, pO2 less than 30 mmHg, pCO2 greater than 60 mmHg
Non-ischemic: bright red blood with arterial gas values
Color Doppler ultrasound - ischemic shows absent or minimal cavernosal blood flow; non-ischemic shows turbulent flow at fistula site
Complete blood count to evaluate for leukemia or sickle cell crisis
Hemoglobin electrophoresis if sickle cell disease not previously diagnosed
Reticulocyte count and peripheral blood smear
management: Ischemic priapism is a UROLOGIC EMERGENCY - immediate intervention required
First-line: corporal aspiration of stagnant blood using a large-bore needle (19-gauge) inserted into the lateral corpus cavernosum
Intracavernosal injection of phenylephrine (alpha-1 agonist) - 100-500 mcg every 5-10 minutes, maximum 1000 mcg (monitor blood pressure and heart rate)
If aspiration and phenylephrine fail, surgical shunt creation (distal: Winter or Al-Ghorab; proximal: Quackels or Barry)
Non-ischemic priapism: observation is usually safe as tissue oxygenation is maintained - selective arterial embolization if persistent
Sickle cell-associated: concurrent hydration, oxygen therapy, exchange transfusion, pain management
nursingActions: Recognize priapism as a time-sensitive emergency - prioritize rapid assessment and provider notification
Monitor vital signs closely during phenylephrine administration - watch for hypertension, bradycardia, cardiac arrhythmia
Apply ice packs to perineum for comfort (limited efficacy but may provide mild vasoconstriction)
Obtain medication history - PDE5 inhibitors, trazodone, antipsychotics, intracavernosal injections
Assess pain level and administer analgesics as ordered
Monitor for complications - penile swelling, skin changes, signs of ischemia
Provide emotional support - address embarrassment, anxiety, and fear of erectile dysfunction
Educate sickle cell patients on priapism risk and importance of seeking care within 4 hours

balanitis.title: Balanitis

cellular.title: Glans Inflammation and Preputial Microenvironment Disruption
cellular.content: Balanitis is inflammation of the glans penis, often accompanied by posthitis (inflammation of the foreskin) - collectively termed balanoposthitis. The warm, moist microenvironment beneath the prepuce creates conditions favorable for microbial overgrowth when hygiene is inadequate. Candida albicans is the most common infectious cause, particularly in diabetic patients where glycosuria provides a substrate for fungal proliferation. Candida forms biofilms on the glans epithelium, secreting aspartyl proteases that degrade host tissue proteins and triggering an inflammatory response with erythema, edema, and satellite lesions. Bacterial balanitis involves mixed flora (streptococci, staphylococci, anaerobes) invading macerated skin. Irritant balanitis results from exposure to harsh soaps, latex, or retained smegma that triggers a non-infectious inflammatory response. Zoon balanitis (plasma cell balanitis) is a chronic, benign inflammatory condition of unknown etiology, characterized by dense plasma cell infiltration and thinning of the glans epithelium in middle-aged to elderly uncircumcised men. Chronic or recurrent balanitis can lead to preputial scarring, phimosis, and in rare cases, progression to premalignant conditions (erythroplasia of Queyrat).

riskFactors: Broad-spectrum antibiotic use (disrupts normal flora, promotes Candida)
Irritant exposure (harsh soaps, latex condoms, spermicides)

left: Erythema and edema of the glans penis
White cottage cheese-like discharge (candidal)
Pruritus and burning sensation
Satellite lesions (small papules surrounding main erythematous area - characteristic of Candida)
right: Fissuring and erosions of glans surface
Foul-smelling discharge (bacterial)
Phimosis development from recurrent inflammation
Glazed, moist erythematous plaques (Zoon balanitis)

medications.name: Clotrimazole 1% cream
medications.action: Inhibits fungal cytochrome P450 enzyme lanosterol 14-alpha-demethylase, blocking ergosterol synthesis and disrupting Candida cell membrane integrity

medications.name: Fluconazole
medications.action: Inhibits fungal CYP450-dependent lanosterol 14-alpha-demethylase systemically, treating Candida infection throughout genitourinary tract

pearls: Recurrent candidal balanitis in a previously healthy male should prompt screening for diabetes mellitus - balanitis may be the initial presentation of undiagnosed type 2 diabetes

Zoon balanitis (plasma cell balanitis) presents as a shiny, moist, cayenne pepper-speckled erythematous plaque on the glans - it is benign but can mimic erythroplasia of Queyrat (carcinoma in situ), so biopsy is essential for differentiation
Circumcision is the definitive treatment for recurrent balanitis - it eliminates the preputial microenvironment that promotes microbial colonization
diagnostics: Clinical examination - assess glans for erythema, edema, discharge, satellite lesions, fissures
Fungal swab and culture if Candida suspected (KOH prep shows pseudohyphae and budding yeast)
Bacterial swab and culture if purulent discharge present
Blood glucose testing to screen for undiagnosed diabetes (recurrent candidal balanitis is often the first presentation of diabetes)
Biopsy if lesion is persistent, atypical, or unresponsive to treatment - rule out Zoon balanitis, erythroplasia of Queyrat (CIS), or squamous cell carcinoma
STI screening if sexually active with new partner
management: Candidal balanitis: topical clotrimazole 1% or miconazole 2% cream twice daily for 2 weeks
Severe or recurrent candidal balanitis: oral fluconazole 150 mg single dose
Bacterial balanitis: topical mupirocin or oral antibiotics based on culture
Irritant balanitis: remove offending agent, saline soaks, emollient application
Optimize glycemic control in diabetic patients
Circumcision for recurrent balanitis unresponsive to conservative management
Zoon balanitis: topical tacrolimus or circumcision (definitive)
nursingActions: Assess glans and foreskin - document erythema, discharge characteristics, lesion distribution, satellite lesions
Teach proper genital hygiene - gentle retraction, warm water cleansing, complete drying, avoid harsh soaps
Educate on correct topical medication application - apply thin layer to entire affected area after cleaning
Screen for diabetes if recurrent candidal balanitis - educate on blood glucose monitoring
Educate sexual partners if STI-related balanitis is suspected
Report persistent lesions unresponsive to 2 weeks of treatment - may require biopsy to rule out malignancy

cryptorchidism.title: Cryptorchidism

cellular.title: Testicular Maldescent and Germ Cell Vulnerability
cellular.content: Cryptorchidism is the failure of one or both testes to descend from the abdominal cavity into the scrotum through the inguinal canal. Normal testicular descent occurs in two phases: the transabdominal phase (8-15 weeks gestation), mediated by insulin-like factor 3 (INSL3) secreted by Leydig cells acting on the gubernaculum, causing it to swell and anchor the testis near the internal inguinal ring; and the inguinoscrotal phase (25-35 weeks gestation), mediated primarily by testosterone and calcitonin gene-related peptide (CGRP) from the genitofemoral nerve, guiding the testis through the inguinal canal into the scrotum. Disruption at either phase results in cryptorchidism. The undescended testis is exposed to core body temperature (37 C) rather than the 2-3 C lower scrotal temperature required for normal spermatogenesis. This elevated temperature causes progressive germ cell degeneration through apoptosis, reduced spermatogonial stem cell proliferation, and Sertoli cell dysfunction. Histologic changes begin by 6 months of age and become significant by 12-18 months, providing the rationale for early orchiopexy. Cryptorchidism increases the risk of testicular germ cell tumors (seminoma) by 5-10 fold, even after orchiopexy - the risk is intrinsic to the dysgenetic testis and not solely due to temperature.

riskFactors: Prematurity and low birth weight (most significant risk factor)
Maternal exposure to endocrine disruptors (pesticides, phthalates, DES)
Maternal diabetes mellitus or gestational diabetes
Genetic syndromes (Klinefelter syndrome, Prader-Willi syndrome, Kallmann syndrome)

left: Empty hemiscrotum on inspection (unilateral or bilateral)
Palpable testis in the inguinal canal (most common location)
Ipsilateral inguinal hernia (present in 90% of cryptorchid testes due to patent processus vaginalis)
Scrotal hypoplasia on affected side
right: Non-palpable testis (suggests intra-abdominal or absent testis)
Retractile testis (normal variant - can be brought to scrotum temporarily but retracts due to active cremasteric reflex)
Bilateral cryptorchidism with ambiguous genitalia (evaluate for disorder of sex development)
Infertility in adulthood if untreated bilateral cryptorchidism

medications.name: Human Chorionic Gonadotropin (hCG)
medications.action: Stimulates Leydig cell testosterone production, which may promote testicular descent through the inguinal canal in select cases

medications.name: Acetaminophen
medications.action: Inhibits central cyclooxygenase and modulates descending serotonergic pain pathways for post-operative pain control after orchiopexy

pearls: Orchiopexy should be performed by 12 months of age - germ cell degeneration from elevated temperature begins as early as 6 months and becomes significant by 12-18 months, permanently reducing fertility potential

Cryptorchidism increases testicular cancer risk 5-10 fold even after orchiopexy - the risk is intrinsic to the dysgenetic gonad, not solely due to position; orchiopexy allows surveillance by placing the testis in a palpable location
Bilateral non-palpable testes in a newborn with hypospadias is a disorder of sex development until proven otherwise - urgent karyotype and endocrine evaluation are required
