diagnostics: Physical examination - palpate inguinal canal and scrotum in warm environment with relaxed cremasteric muscle
Differentiate true cryptorchidism from retractile testis (retractile testis can be manually brought to scrotum and stays temporarily)
Ultrasound of inguinal canal and abdomen for non-palpable testis
MRI or laparoscopy for non-palpable testis not visualized on ultrasound
Serum testosterone, FSH, LH, and anti-Mullerian hormone (AMH) to assess testicular function
hCG stimulation test for bilateral non-palpable testes - testosterone response confirms functional testicular tissue (anorchia if no response)
Karyotype if bilateral cryptorchidism with hypospadias (evaluate for disorder of sex development)
management: Observation until 6 months of age (spontaneous descent occurs in most premature infants)
Referral to pediatric urology if testis has not descended by 6 months of age
Orchiopexy (surgical fixation of testis in scrotum) is the definitive treatment - recommended between 6-12 months of age
Standard orchiopexy for palpable inguinal testis - inguinal approach with mobilization and scrotal fixation
Laparoscopic orchiopexy (Fowler-Stephens procedure) for non-palpable abdominal testis - staged if high intra-abdominal position
Orchiectomy if testis is atrophic, dysgenetic, or non-viable (especially in post-pubertal patients)
Long-term surveillance for testicular malignancy - teach testicular self-examination at puberty
nursingActions: Perform careful scrotal and inguinal examination in newborns and at well-child visits
Document testicular position - scrotal, inguinal, or non-palpable for each side
Educate parents on the importance of early surgical correction (before 12 months) to preserve fertility potential
Provide pre-operative education for orchiopexy - explain procedure, expected recovery, scrotal swelling
Post-operative orchiopexy care - monitor scrotal swelling, wound site, pain management, activity restrictions
Teach testicular self-examination beginning at puberty - educate about increased malignancy risk even after orchiopexy
Monitor testicular position at follow-up visits - ascent can occur after orchiopexy (rare but possible)

orchitis.title: Orchitis

cellular.title: Testicular Inflammation and Blood-Testis Barrier Disruption
cellular.content: Orchitis is inflammation of the testis, most commonly caused by viral infection (mumps) or bacterial infection ascending from the epididymis (epididymo-orchitis). Mumps orchitis occurs in 20-30% of postpubertal males infected with mumps virus (paramyxovirus). The virus demonstrates tropism for Sertoli cells and Leydig cells of the testis, reaching the organ through viremic hematogenous spread. Viral replication within Sertoli cells disrupts the blood-testis barrier - a specialized tight junction complex (claudins, occludins, ZO-1) between adjacent Sertoli cells that separates the immunologically privileged adluminal compartment from the systemic immune system. Barrier disruption exposes haploid spermatids and spermatozoa (which express unique antigens not recognized by the immune system as self) to circulating immune cells, triggering autoimmune anti-sperm antibody formation. The resulting inflammatory infiltrate (lymphocytes, macrophages) and edema increase intratesticular pressure, compressing the seminiferous tubules and causing ischemic damage to germinal epithelium. Bacterial orchitis typically extends from epididymitis - organisms include Escherichia coli, Klebsiella, and Pseudomonas in older males and sexually transmitted organisms (N. gonorrhoeae, C. trachomatis) in younger males. The inflammatory response damages Leydig cells (reducing testosterone production) and Sertoli cells (impairing spermatogenesis), potentially causing testicular atrophy and infertility.

riskFactors: Mumps infection in postpubertal males (20-30% develop orchitis)
Urinary tract infection with vesicoureteral reflux

left: Testicular pain and tenderness (gradual onset over hours to days)
Scrotal edema and erythema
Fever and malaise (more pronounced with bacterial cause)
Reactive hydrocele (fluid around testis)
right: Parotid gland swelling preceding testicular symptoms by 4-8 days (mumps orchitis)
Elevated cremasteric reflex (PRESENT - differentiates from torsion)
Increased blood flow on Doppler ultrasound (differentiates from torsion)
Testicular atrophy in chronic or severe cases (late finding)

medications.name: Ceftriaxone
medications.action: Inhibits bacterial cell wall synthesis by binding PBPs, treating gonococcal and other bacterial causes of epididymo-orchitis

medications.name: Ibuprofen
medications.action: Inhibits cyclooxygenase (COX-1 and COX-2), reducing prostaglandin synthesis to decrease testicular inflammation, edema, and pain

pearls: The CRITICAL differentiation is orchitis versus testicular torsion - torsion is a surgical emergency requiring detorsion within 6 hours to save the testis; when in doubt, surgical exploration is mandatory
Mumps orchitis occurs 4-8 days after parotid swelling and causes testicular atrophy in 30-50% of affected testes - bilateral involvement can result in infertility
Doppler ultrasound showing INCREASED blood flow to the affected testis confirms orchitis; ABSENT blood flow suggests torsion and requires emergent surgical exploration
diagnostics: Doppler ultrasound of the scrotum - CRITICAL to differentiate orchitis from testicular torsion (orchitis shows INCREASED blood flow, torsion shows ABSENT flow)
Urinalysis and urine culture to identify bacterial pathogen
NAAT for N. gonorrhoeae and C. trachomatis in sexually active males under 35
Serum mumps IgM antibody if viral orchitis suspected
CBC showing leukocytosis with bacterial infection
Assess cremasteric reflex - PRESENT in orchitis, ABSENT in torsion
Prehn sign - elevation of testis relieves pain in orchitis but NOT in torsion (limited reliability)
management: Bacterial epididymo-orchitis in males under 35 (likely STI): ceftriaxone 500 mg IM plus doxycycline 100 mg PO twice daily for 10 days
Bacterial epididymo-orchitis in males over 35 (likely enteric organisms): levofloxacin 500 mg PO daily for 10 days
Mumps orchitis: supportive care only (no antiviral treatment available) - rest, scrotal elevation, ice application, analgesics
Scrotal support with athletic supporter or towel roll for comfort
NSAIDs for pain and inflammation
Surgical exploration if testicular torsion cannot be definitively excluded - torsion is a surgical emergency
nursingActions: Assess testicular pain onset - SUDDEN onset suggests torsion (surgical emergency), GRADUAL onset suggests orchitis/epididymitis
Apply scrotal support and elevation to reduce edema and improve comfort
Apply ice packs intermittently (20 minutes on, 20 minutes off) to reduce swelling
Monitor temperature and administer antipyretics as ordered
Administer analgesics and antibiotics as prescribed
Educate on the importance of completing full antibiotic course
Report sudden worsening of pain or new onset unilateral testicular pain immediately - may indicate torsion requiring emergent surgery
Educate on MMR vaccination to prevent mumps orchitis

prostatitis.title: Prostatitis

cellular.title: Prostatic Inflammation and Voiding Dysfunction
cellular.content: Prostatitis is inflammation of the prostate gland, classified by the NIH into four categories. Category I (acute bacterial prostatitis) is an acute infection typically caused by gram-negative enteric bacteria (E. coli, Klebsiella, Pseudomonas) or less commonly by sexually transmitted organisms. Bacteria ascend from the urethra through prostatic ducts or reach the prostate via hematogenous or lymphatic spread. The acute inflammatory response causes prostatic edema and swelling, compressing the prostatic urethra and causing obstructive voiding symptoms. Prostatic abscesses can form in severe cases. Category II (chronic bacterial prostatitis) involves persistent or recurrent bacterial infection, often maintained by bacterial biofilms on prostatic calculi. Biofilm-dwelling bacteria are resistant to antibiotic penetration and host immune responses, causing relapsing infections. Category III (chronic prostatitis/chronic pelvic pain syndrome - CP/CPPS) is the most common form (90-95% of prostatitis cases), subdivided into inflammatory (IIIA) and non-inflammatory (IIIB). The pathophysiology is multifactorial, involving prostatic inflammation, pelvic floor muscle dysfunction, neurogenic pain sensitization, and psychological factors. Elevated intraprostatic pressure from dysfunctional voiding causes intraprostatic ductal reflux of urine, triggering chemical inflammation. Category IV (asymptomatic inflammatory prostatitis) is incidentally discovered on biopsy or semen analysis.

riskFactors: Urinary tract infections (most common bacterial source)
Prostatic calculi (serve as nidus for biofilm formation)

left: Dysuria (burning with urination)
Perineal or suprapubic pain
Urinary frequency and urgency
Fever and chills (acute bacterial prostatitis)
right: Cloudy or malodorous urine
Low back pain or rectal discomfort
Painful ejaculation
Urinary retention (prostatic edema compresses urethra)

medications.name: Ciprofloxacin
medications.action: Inhibits bacterial DNA gyrase and topoisomerase IV, achieving excellent prostatic tissue penetration (prostatic-to-serum ratio of 2-3:1) due to its lipophilic nature and alkaline pH trapping in prostatic fluid

medications.name: Tamsulosin
medications.action: Selectively blocks alpha-1A receptors in prostatic smooth muscle and bladder neck, reducing urethral resistance and improving voiding symptoms

pearls: NEVER perform vigorous prostatic massage in acute bacterial prostatitis (Category I) - the inflamed, infected prostate can release bacteria into the bloodstream, causing bacteremia and potentially sepsis

Chronic prostatitis/CPPS (Category III) accounts for 90-95% of prostatitis cases and often responds best to a multimodal approach combining alpha-blockers, anti-inflammatories, pelvic floor physical therapy, and psychological support rather than antibiotics alone

Fluoroquinolones achieve the highest prostatic tissue penetration of any antibiotic class - this is why they are first-line despite FDA black box warnings for serious adverse effects; the 4-6 week treatment duration is essential because shorter courses lead to relapse from bacteria harbored in prostatic tissue
diagnostics: Urinalysis and urine culture (midstream clean-catch) - pyuria and bacteriuria in acute bacterial prostatitis
Digital rectal examination (DRE) - acutely tender, boggy, swollen prostate in Category I (AVOID vigorous massage in acute prostatitis - risk of bacteremia)
Expressed prostatic secretions (EPS) or post-prostatic massage urine (VB3) culture for chronic bacterial prostatitis
PSA may be elevated in acute prostatitis (do NOT use for cancer screening during active infection - recheck in 6-8 weeks)
CT or transrectal ultrasound if prostatic abscess suspected
Uroflowmetry to assess voiding dysfunction
4-glass test (Meares-Stamey) for chronic prostatitis classification - compares pre-massage urine, EPS, and post-massage urine cultures
management: Category I (acute bacterial): IV antibiotics initially (fluoroquinolone or TMP-SMX) with transition to oral therapy for total 4-6 weeks
Category II (chronic bacterial): oral fluoroquinolone or TMP-SMX for 4-6 weeks (prolonged course for prostatic tissue penetration)
Category III (CP/CPPS): multimodal approach - alpha-blockers for voiding symptoms, NSAIDs for inflammation, pelvic floor physical therapy, cognitive behavioral therapy
Prostatic abscess drainage (transrectal or transperineal) if abscess greater than 2 cm
Sitz baths for symptomatic relief
Stool softeners to reduce straining and perineal pressure
Adequate hydration and avoidance of caffeine, alcohol, spicy foods (irritants)
nursingActions: Assess voiding patterns - frequency, urgency, dysuria, incomplete emptying, straining
Monitor temperature and systemic signs of infection (acute prostatitis can progress to sepsis)
Educate on prolonged antibiotic course (4-6 weeks) - emphasize importance of completing full course even if symptoms improve
Provide sitz bath instructions - warm water for 15-20 minutes, 2-3 times daily for comfort
Advise on dietary modifications - avoid caffeine, alcohol, and spicy foods that can exacerbate symptoms
Monitor for urinary retention - assess suprapubic distension, post-void residual
Report inability to void, high fever, or signs of sepsis immediately
Provide emotional support for chronic prostatitis - address impact on quality of life, sexual function, and mental health

delayed-puberty-rpn.title: Delayed Puberty - RPN Fundamentals

cellular.title: Puberty Delay Overview
cellular.content: Delayed puberty occurs when secondary sexual characteristics fail to develop at the expected age. In girls, this means no breast development by age 13 or no menarche by age 15. In boys, it means no testicular enlargement by age 14. The hypothalamic-pituitary-gonadal (HPG) axis normally activates during puberty, triggering the release of sex hormones that drive physical development. When this axis fails to activate on schedule or the gonads do not respond appropriately, puberty is delayed. Constitutional delay (a normal variant where puberty starts late but progresses normally) is the most common cause.

riskFactors: Chronic illness (Crohn's disease, celiac disease, cystic fibrosis)

left: No breast development by age 13 in girls
No menarche by age 15 in girls
No testicular enlargement by age 14 in boys
right: Short stature compared to peers
Psychosocial distress and low self-esteem
Delayed bone age on X-ray

medications.name: Estradiol (low-dose)
medications.action: Initiates breast development and promotes secondary sexual characteristics in girls

medications.name: Testosterone (low-dose)
medications.action: Stimulates testicular growth, voice deepening, and muscle development in boys

pearls: Constitutional delay is the most common cause and resolves on its own
Emotional support is as important as medical treatment
Monitor growth charts closely  -  declining height velocity warrants investigation
Always document Tanner staging accurately at every assessment
diagnostics: Expect bone age X-ray to compare skeletal maturity with chronological age
Monitor growth charts for height velocity decline
Expect referral to endocrinology for hormone level evaluation
Monitor Tanner staging progression at each visit
Expect thyroid function tests to rule out hypothyroidism
management: Support emotional well-being and body image concerns
Encourage adequate nutrition and caloric intake
Administer hormone therapy as prescribed if ordered by provider
Monitor growth and development milestones at each visit
Ensure follow-up appointments with endocrinology are scheduled
nursingActions: Assess Tanner stage and document findings accurately
Monitor height and weight at each visit and plot on growth chart
Report absence of expected developmental milestones to provider
Provide age-appropriate education about puberty and development
Offer emotional support and reassurance to patient and family
Educate family that constitutional delay is the most common cause and is self-resolving
Report signs of nutritional deficiency or eating disorder behaviors

precocious-puberty-rpn.title: Precocious Puberty - RPN Fundamentals

cellular.title: Early Puberty Activation
cellular.content: Precocious puberty occurs when secondary sexual characteristics develop before age 8 in girls or before age 9 in boys. The hypothalamic-pituitary-gonadal axis activates prematurely, causing early production of sex hormones. This leads to early breast development, pubic hair growth, and menarche in girls, or testicular enlargement and pubic hair in boys. Early exposure to sex hormones accelerates bone maturation, which initially causes a growth spurt but leads to early epiphyseal closure and ultimately shorter adult height. The condition may be central (brain-mediated) or peripheral (from ovarian, testicular, or adrenal sources).

riskFactors: History of brain injury or infection

left: Breast development before age 8 in girls
Testicular enlargement before age 9 in boys
Pubic or axillary hair growth
Rapid linear growth (growth spurt)
right: Menarche before age 8 in girls
Acne and body odor
Mood changes and emotional lability
Advanced bone age on X-ray

medications.name: Leuprolide (Lupron Depot-Ped)
medications.action: Suppresses premature HPG axis activation by downregulating pituitary GnRH receptors, halting puberty progression

medications.name: Histrelin (Supprelin LA)
medications.action: Provides continuous GnRH suppression via subcutaneous implant for 12 months

pearls: Precocious puberty affects girls more often than boys
GnRH agonist treatment halts puberty progression and preserves adult height potential
Psychosocial support is critical  -  the child may feel different from peers
Always report headaches or visual changes as they may indicate a CNS tumor
diagnostics: Expect bone age X-ray (will show advanced skeletal maturity)
Monitor Tanner staging progression carefully
Expect referral to pediatric endocrinology
Monitor growth velocity (accelerated growth rate)
Expect brain MRI if central precocious puberty is suspected
management: Administer GnRH agonist injections as prescribed
Monitor for injection site reactions and medication side effects
Support the child's psychosocial adjustment
Ensure regular follow-up with endocrinology
Educate family on treatment goals and expected outcomes
nursingActions: Assess and document Tanner staging at each visit
Monitor height, weight, and growth velocity on growth charts
Administer GnRH agonist injections as ordered and monitor for side effects
Report progression of sexual development to provider
Provide age-appropriate education to the child about body changes
Assess for psychosocial distress, bullying, or behavioral changes
Educate parents about protecting the child from body-image issues
Report any headaches, visual changes, or neurological symptoms (may indicate CNS cause)

dysmenorrhea-rpn.title: Dysmenorrhea - RPN Fundamentals

cellular.title: Menstrual Pain Mechanisms
cellular.content: Dysmenorrhea is painful menstruation caused by excessive uterine contractions. Primary dysmenorrhea occurs without underlying pelvic pathology and is caused by overproduction of prostaglandins (PGF2α and PGE2) from the shedding endometrium. These prostaglandins cause intense myometrial contractions that compress uterine blood vessels, leading to tissue ischemia and pain. Secondary dysmenorrhea is caused by an underlying condition such as endometriosis, adenomyosis, or uterine fibroids. Primary dysmenorrhea typically begins 1-2 years after menarche when ovulatory cycles are established, while secondary dysmenorrhea may develop later in life.

left: Crampy, spasmodic lower abdominal pain
Pain beginning with or just before menses
Pain lasting 48-72 hours
Lower back pain
right: Nausea and vomiting
Diarrhea
Headache
Fatigue

medications.name: Ibuprofen
medications.action: Inhibits cyclooxygenase enzymes, reducing prostaglandin synthesis and decreasing myometrial contractions

medications.name: Naproxen Sodium
medications.action: Longer-acting prostaglandin synthesis inhibitor providing sustained relief from menstrual cramping

pearls: NSAIDs are most effective when started BEFORE pain begins  -  educate on timing
Heat application is an evidence-based non-pharmacological intervention for menstrual pain
Pain that progressively worsens over time may indicate secondary dysmenorrhea requiring further evaluation
Primary dysmenorrhea is common and affects up to 90% of adolescent girls
diagnostics: Expect thorough menstrual history and pain assessment
Monitor pain characteristics (timing, location, severity, duration)
Expect pelvic examination if secondary dysmenorrhea suspected
Monitor response to NSAID therapy
Expect pelvic ultrasound if secondary causes suspected
management: Administer NSAIDs as ordered  -  most effective when started before or at onset of menses
Apply heat to lower abdomen or lower back (heating pad, warm bath)
Encourage comfortable positioning (knee-to-chest position may help)
Administer hormonal contraceptives as prescribed if ordered
Encourage gentle exercise and stretching
nursingActions: Assess pain using a standardized pain scale (location, severity, timing)
Administer prescribed analgesics and monitor effectiveness
Apply non-pharmacological comfort measures (heat, positioning, relaxation)
Educate patient on timing of NSAID administration (start before menses onset for best effect)
Report severe pain unresponsive to standard treatment to provider
Report any new symptoms suggesting secondary causes (progressive worsening, intermenstrual pain, heavy bleeding)
Document menstrual cycle patterns and pain episodes
Educate on lifestyle modifications (regular exercise, adequate sleep, stress management)

abnormal-uterine-bleeding-rpn.title: Abnormal Uterine Bleeding - RPN Fundamentals

cellular.title: Disrupted Menstrual Bleeding
cellular.content: Abnormal uterine bleeding (AUB) refers to any change in menstrual bleeding pattern that deviates from the normal cycle. This includes bleeding that is heavier than normal (menorrhagia), more frequent, prolonged, or occurs between periods (metrorrhagia). AUB can result from structural causes such as polyps, fibroids, or malignancy, or from non-structural causes such as hormonal imbalances (anovulation), coagulation disorders, or medication effects. Excessive blood loss can lead to iron-deficiency anemia, causing fatigue, pallor, and tachycardia. Monitoring bleeding patterns and recognizing signs of hemodynamic compromise are essential RPN responsibilities.

riskFactors: Obesity (excess estrogen from adipose tissue conversion)
Extremes of reproductive age (adolescence and perimenopause)

left: Soaking through pad/tampon in less than 1 hour
Menstrual bleeding lasting longer than 7 days
Passing blood clots larger than a quarter
Bleeding between periods
right: Fatigue and weakness (anemia)
Pallor of skin and mucous membranes
Tachycardia and dizziness
Shortness of breath with exertion

medications.name: Ferrous Sulfate
medications.action: Replaces depleted iron stores to correct iron-deficiency anemia from chronic blood loss

medications.name: Tranexamic Acid
medications.action: Inhibits plasminogen activation, stabilizing blood clots and reducing menstrual blood loss

pearls: Soaking through more than one pad per hour is considered hemorrhagic bleeding  -  report immediately
Iron-deficiency anemia is the most common complication of chronic AUB
Always assess vital signs when bleeding is heavy  -  tachycardia may be the first sign of volume depletion
Track pad count as objective data for provider assessment
diagnostics: Expect complete blood count to assess for anemia
Monitor vital signs for signs of hemorrhage (tachycardia, hypotension)
Expect pregnancy test to rule out pregnancy-related causes
Monitor menstrual pad count and saturation
Expect thyroid function tests
Expect pelvic ultrasound if structural cause suspected
management: Monitor pad/tampon count and degree of saturation
Administer iron supplementation as ordered for anemia
Administer hormonal therapy as prescribed to regulate bleeding
Maintain accurate intake and output records
Prepare for diagnostic procedures as ordered
nursingActions: Assess and document bleeding patterns (frequency, duration, volume, clots)
Monitor pad count  -  report soaking more than one pad per hour
Monitor vital signs for signs of hypovolemia (tachycardia, hypotension, dizziness)
Assess for signs of iron-deficiency anemia (fatigue, pallor, shortness of breath, tachycardia)
Educate patient on tracking menstrual cycles and bleeding patterns
Administer iron supplements as ordered and educate on taking with vitamin C for absorption
Report heavy or prolonged bleeding to provider immediately
Provide emotional support for anxiety related to abnormal bleeding

pcos-rpn.title: Polycystic Ovary Syndrome (PCOS) - RPN Fundamentals

cellular.title: Hormonal Imbalance Overview
cellular.content: Polycystic ovary syndrome is a common hormonal disorder in women of reproductive age characterized by excess androgen production and irregular ovulation. Insulin resistance plays a central role  -  elevated insulin levels stimulate the ovaries to produce excess male hormones (androgens). These excess androgens disrupt normal follicle development, preventing ovulation and causing multiple small, immature follicles to accumulate in the ovaries. The resulting hormonal imbalance leads to irregular periods, acne, excess hair growth (hirsutism), and weight gain. PCOS also increases the risk of type 2 diabetes, cardiovascular disease, and endometrial cancer due to chronic anovulation.

left: Irregular or absent menstrual periods
Hirsutism (excess facial and body hair)
Acne (especially along jawline)
Weight gain, particularly central obesity
right: Acanthosis nigricans (dark, velvety skin in folds)
Thinning scalp hair
Difficulty conceiving
Mood changes (anxiety, depression)

medications.name: Metformin
medications.action: Decreases insulin resistance and lowers insulin levels, indirectly reducing androgen production from the ovaries

medications.name: Combined Oral Contraceptive
medications.action: Regulates menstrual cycle, reduces androgen levels, and protects the endometrium from hyperplasia

pearls: PCOS is the leading cause of infertility due to anovulation in reproductive-age women
Even modest weight loss (5-10%) can significantly improve symptoms and restore ovulation
Monitor for signs of diabetes  -  PCOS patients have high insulin resistance
Acanthosis nigricans (dark velvety skin folds) is a visual indicator of insulin resistance
diagnostics: Expect fasting blood glucose and insulin levels
Monitor for signs of hyperandrogenism (hirsutism, acne)
Expect pelvic ultrasound showing multiple small follicles
Monitor blood pressure and lipid panel for metabolic risks
Monitor menstrual cycle regularity
Expect HbA1c for diabetes screening
management: Encourage lifestyle modifications (balanced diet, regular exercise)
Administer metformin as ordered for insulin resistance
Administer oral contraceptives as prescribed for cycle regulation
Monitor blood glucose levels as ordered
Support weight management goals (5-10% weight loss improves symptoms)
nursingActions: Assess for signs of hyperandrogenism (hirsutism, acne, hair thinning)
Monitor blood glucose and report elevated levels to provider
Educate on lifestyle modifications for weight management and insulin resistance
Administer medications as prescribed and monitor for side effects
Monitor for metformin GI side effects (nausea, diarrhea) and educate to take with food
Provide emotional support for body image concerns related to hirsutism and acne
Educate on the importance of regular menstrual cycle tracking
Report irregular or absent periods lasting more than 3 months to provider
Encourage adherence to follow-up appointments for metabolic screening

pid-rpn.title: Pelvic Inflammatory Disease (PID) - RPN Fundamentals

cellular.title: Ascending Genital Tract Infection
cellular.content: Pelvic inflammatory disease is an infection of the upper female reproductive tract including the uterus, fallopian tubes, and ovaries. It is most commonly caused by sexually transmitted bacteria, particularly Chlamydia trachomatis and Neisseria gonorrhoeae, which ascend from the cervix and vagina into the upper genital tract. The infection causes inflammation and swelling of the fallopian tubes (salpingitis), uterine lining (endometritis), and surrounding tissues. If untreated, scar tissue and adhesions form in the fallopian tubes, increasing the risk of ectopic pregnancy, chronic pelvic pain, and infertility. Tubo-ovarian abscess is a serious complication requiring urgent treatment.

riskFactors: History of sexually transmitted infections (chlamydia, gonorrhea)

left: Lower abdominal pain (bilateral)
Fever and chills
Abnormal vaginal discharge (purulent, foul-smelling)
Cervical motion tenderness
right: Pain with intercourse (dyspareunia)
Irregular menstrual bleeding
Nausea and vomiting
Urinary frequency

medications.name: Ceftriaxone
medications.action: Kills bacteria by disrupting cell wall synthesis, providing coverage against Neisseria gonorrhoeae

medications.name: Doxycycline
medications.action: Inhibits bacterial protein synthesis, providing coverage against Chlamydia trachomatis

pearls: PID is a medical emergency  -  delayed treatment increases infertility risk
Always treat sexual partners to prevent reinfection (ping-pong effect)
Semi-Fowler's position promotes pelvic drainage and comfort
Chlamydia is often asymptomatic  -  partners may not know they are infected
diagnostics: Expect endocervical swabs for gonorrhea and chlamydia testing
Monitor temperature for fever
Monitor vital signs for sepsis indicators
Expect CBC (elevated WBC)
Expect pelvic ultrasound if abscess suspected
Expect pregnancy test to rule out ectopic pregnancy
management: Administer antibiotics as ordered (full course must be completed)
Administer analgesics as prescribed for pain management
Position in semi-Fowler's position to promote pelvic drainage
Ensure sexual partners are notified and treated
Maintain bedrest during acute phase as ordered
nursingActions: Assess and document pain characteristics (location, severity, onset)
Monitor temperature and report fever above 38°C (100.4°F)
Administer antibiotics as prescribed and on schedule
Educate on completing the full antibiotic course even if feeling better
Report worsening abdominal pain, high fever, or signs of sepsis immediately
Educate on STI prevention including consistent condom use
Ensure partner notification and treatment to prevent reinfection
Educate on avoiding sexual intercourse until treatment is complete and provider clears
Assess for nausea and vomiting (may indicate worsening infection)
Report signs of peritonitis (rigid abdomen, rebound tenderness, guarding)

vaginitis-rpn.title: Vaginitis - RPN Fundamentals

cellular.title: Vaginal Infection and Inflammation
cellular.content: Vaginitis is inflammation of the vagina caused by infection, hormonal changes, or irritants. The three most common infectious types are bacterial vaginosis (BV), vulvovaginal candidiasis (yeast infection), and trichomoniasis. In a healthy vagina, Lactobacillus bacteria maintain an acidic pH (3.8-4.5) that prevents overgrowth of harmful organisms. When this balance is disrupted, pathogenic organisms overgrow. BV results from a shift in vaginal flora with overgrowth of anaerobic bacteria (primarily Gardnerella vaginalis). Candidal vaginitis results from overgrowth of Candida yeast, commonly after antibiotic use. Trichomoniasis is caused by the protozoan Trichomonas vaginalis and is sexually transmitted.

riskFactors: Diabetes mellitus (elevated glucose promotes Candida growth)

left: BV: Thin, grayish-white discharge with fishy odor
Candidal: Thick, white, cottage cheese-like discharge
Trichomoniasis: Frothy, yellow-green discharge with foul odor
right: Vulvar itching and irritation
Burning with urination (dysuria)
Vulvar redness and swelling
Discomfort during intercourse (dyspareunia)

medications.name: Metronidazole (Flagyl)
medications.action: Disrupts DNA synthesis in anaerobic bacteria and protozoa, treating both BV and trichomoniasis

medications.name: Fluconazole (Diflucan)
medications.action: Inhibits fungal cell membrane synthesis, treating Candida overgrowth

pearls: Each type of vaginitis has a characteristic discharge  -  learn to differentiate them
BV: thin grayish, fishy odor; Candidal: thick white cottage cheese; Trichomoniasis: frothy yellow-green
NO alcohol with metronidazole  -  this is a critical patient safety teaching point
Trichomoniasis is the only common vaginitis that is sexually transmitted  -  treat the partner
Douching disrupts normal vaginal flora and increases infection risk  -  always educate against it
diagnostics: Expect vaginal swab for wet mount microscopy
Monitor vaginal pH (BV and trichomoniasis have elevated pH above 4.5)
Expect whiff test (fishy odor with KOH indicates BV)
Expect STI screening if trichomoniasis confirmed
Monitor for cervical inflammation on examination
management: Administer prescribed medications as ordered (specific to type of vaginitis)
Educate on proper perineal hygiene (front to back wiping)
Educate on avoiding douching and irritating products
Treat sexual partners if trichomoniasis diagnosed
Encourage wearing cotton underwear and loose-fitting clothing
nursingActions: Assess and document vaginal discharge characteristics (color, consistency, odor, amount)
Collect vaginal specimens as ordered using proper technique
Administer prescribed antifungal or antibiotic medications
Educate on completing the full course of treatment
Educate on proper perineal hygiene practices
Report persistent or worsening symptoms to provider
Educate patient to avoid douching, scented products, and tight clothing
Educate on STI prevention if trichomoniasis diagnosed
Assess for signs of secondary infection or treatment failure

bacterial-vaginosis-rpn.title: Bacterial Vaginosis - RPN Fundamentals

cellular.title: Vaginal Flora Imbalance
cellular.content: Bacterial vaginosis (BV) is the most common vaginal infection in women of reproductive age. It occurs when the protective Lactobacillus bacteria that normally dominate the vaginal environment are replaced by an overgrowth of anaerobic bacteria, primarily Gardnerella vaginalis and Atopobium vaginae. The healthy vagina maintains an acidic pH (3.8-4.5) through lactic acid produced by Lactobacillus. When these protective bacteria are depleted  -  by douching, new sexual partners, or other disruptions  -  anaerobic organisms proliferate, raising the vaginal pH above 4.5. These anaerobes produce amines that cause the characteristic fishy odor, especially when exposed to alkaline substances such as semen or potassium hydroxide (KOH). BV is not considered a sexually transmitted infection but is associated with sexual activity.

left: Thin, grayish-white vaginal discharge
Fishy odor, especially after intercourse
Vaginal pH above 4.5
right: Mild vulvar irritation
Burning with urination
Most patients are asymptomatic (up to 50%)

medications.name: Metronidazole (Flagyl)
medications.action: Disrupts DNA synthesis in anaerobic bacteria, eliminating Gardnerella and other BV-associated organisms

medications.name: Clindamycin (vaginal cream)
medications.action: Inhibits bacterial protein synthesis, effective against anaerobic organisms causing BV

pearls: BV is the most common cause of vaginal discharge in reproductive-age women
Up to 50% of women with BV are asymptomatic
Fishy odor after intercourse is a classic finding  -  semen is alkaline and releases amines
Recurrence is common  -  up to 50% recur within 12 months
NO alcohol with metronidazole  -  this is a critical safety teaching point
diagnostics: Expect vaginal swab for wet mount examination
Monitor vaginal pH (elevated above 4.5 in BV)
Expect positive whiff test (fishy odor when KOH added to discharge)
Expect microscopy showing clue cells (epithelial cells covered with bacteria)
Expect Nugent score or Amsel criteria application by provider
management: Administer metronidazole (oral or vaginal) as prescribed
Educate patient on strict alcohol avoidance during metronidazole therapy
Reinforce completion of full course of antibiotics
Educate on avoidance of douching and scented vaginal products
Encourage cotton underwear and breathable clothing
nursingActions: Assess and document vaginal discharge characteristics (thin, grayish-white, fishy odor)
Collect vaginal specimens using proper aseptic technique
Administer prescribed antibiotics on schedule
Educate on NO alcohol during metronidazole treatment and for 48 hours after completion
Educate on proper perineal hygiene (front-to-back wiping)
Report recurrent symptoms or treatment failure to provider
Educate patient that BV is not an STI but is associated with sexual activity
Assess for signs of concurrent STI and report findings

cervicitis-rpn.title: Cervicitis - RPN Fundamentals

cellular.title: Cervical Inflammation Overview
cellular.content: Cervicitis is inflammation of the cervix, most commonly caused by sexually transmitted infections such as Chlamydia trachomatis and Neisseria gonorrhoeae. The cervix is lined with columnar epithelial cells that are particularly vulnerable to bacterial invasion. When pathogens infect the cervical mucosa, they trigger an inflammatory response that produces mucopurulent discharge (yellow or green mucus with pus). If left untreated, the infection can ascend from the cervix into the uterus and fallopian tubes, leading to pelvic inflammatory disease (PID), which carries risks of infertility, ectopic pregnancy, and chronic pelvic pain. Cervicitis can also be caused by non-infectious irritants such as chemical douches or latex allergies, though infectious causes are most common.

riskFactors: History of previous cervicitis or PID

left: Mucopurulent cervical discharge (yellow-green)
Post-coital bleeding (bleeding after intercourse)
Intermenstrual spotting
right: Cervical friability (bleeds easily on contact)
Dysuria (painful urination)
Dyspareunia (pain during intercourse)
Many patients are asymptomatic

medications.name: Azithromycin
medications.action: Inhibits bacterial protein synthesis, effective against Chlamydia trachomatis

medications.name: Ceftriaxone
medications.action: Inhibits bacterial cell wall synthesis, effective against Neisseria gonorrhoeae

pearls: Many patients with cervicitis are asymptomatic  -  screening is essential for high-risk groups
Partner notification and treatment is critical to prevent reinfection
Untreated cervicitis can ascend to PID  -  monitor for fever, worsening pelvic pain, and peritoneal signs
Post-coital bleeding is a red-flag symptom that warrants cervical examination
diagnostics: Expect endocervical swab for nucleic acid amplification testing (NAAT) for chlamydia and gonorrhea
Monitor for mucopurulent cervical discharge on examination
Expect wet mount microscopy
Expect pregnancy test
Monitor temperature for signs of ascending infection
management: Administer prescribed antibiotics as ordered
Ensure sexual partners are notified and treated
Educate on abstaining from sexual intercourse until treatment is complete for patient and partner
Reinforce completion of full antibiotic course
Monitor for signs of ascending infection (PID)
nursingActions: Assess and document discharge characteristics (mucopurulent, yellow-green)
Collect cervical specimens for STI testing using proper technique
Administer prescribed antibiotics on schedule
Educate on completing full course of antibiotics even if symptoms improve
Educate on importance of partner notification and treatment
Educate on abstaining from intercourse until treatment is completed by both patient and partner
Report signs of ascending infection (increasing pelvic pain, fever, peritoneal signs) to provider immediately
Provide emotional support and non-judgmental care
Educate on STI prevention (consistent condom use, reducing number of partners)

vulvodynia-rpn.title: Vulvodynia - RPN Fundamentals

cellular.title: Chronic Vulvar Pain Overview
cellular.content: Vulvodynia is a chronic vulvar pain condition lasting at least 3 months without an identifiable cause such as infection, dermatological condition, or malignancy. The pain is thought to result from an increase in nerve fiber density and sensitivity in the vulvar tissue, combined with heightened inflammatory responses. Nerve fibers in the affected tissue become hypersensitive, causing pain signals from stimuli that would not normally be painful (allodynia) or exaggerated pain from mildly painful stimuli (hyperalgesia). The condition may be provoked (triggered by touch or pressure, such as tampon insertion or intercourse) or unprovoked (constant burning pain without a trigger). Pelvic floor muscle dysfunction often coexists, with chronic muscle tension contributing to pain.

riskFactors: History of sexual abuse or trauma

left: Burning, stinging, or rawness of the vulva
Pain with touch or pressure (provoked vulvodynia)
Pain with tampon insertion or intercourse
right: Constant unprovoked vulvar burning (unprovoked vulvodynia)
Difficulty sitting for prolonged periods
Negative impact on sexual function and quality of life
Normal-appearing vulvar tissue on examination

medications.name: Lidocaine 5% Ointment (topical)
medications.action: Blocks sodium channels in nerve fibers, providing temporary numbing of vulvar tissue to reduce pain

medications.name: Amitriptyline (low-dose)
medications.action: Modulates pain signaling by inhibiting norepinephrine and serotonin reuptake, reducing chronic nerve pain

pearls: Vulvodynia is a diagnosis of exclusion  -  infections, skin conditions, and malignancy must be ruled out first
The vulva often appears completely normal on examination despite severe pain
Emotional support is critical  -  chronic vulvar pain significantly impacts self-esteem, relationships, and mental health
Pelvic floor physiotherapy is an evidence-based first-line treatment
diagnostics: Expect cotton swab test (Q-tip test) to map areas of tenderness
Monitor pain characteristics using standardized pain scales
Expect cultures to rule out infectious causes
Expect examination to rule out dermatological conditions
Expect referral to gynecology or vulvar pain specialist
management: Apply comfort measures (cool compresses, sitz baths with plain water)
Educate on wearing cotton underwear and avoiding tight clothing
Educate on avoiding vulvar irritants (scented soaps, douches, fabric softeners)
Administer prescribed topical or oral medications as ordered
Support referral to pelvic floor physiotherapy
nursingActions: Assess pain using standardized pain tools (location, quality, intensity, triggers)
Document pain patterns and any aggravating or alleviating factors
Provide emotional support  -  chronic pain conditions significantly affect quality of life
Educate on vulvar care (gentle cleansing with water only, pat dry, cotton underwear)
Educate on avoidance of irritants (scented products, tight clothing, prolonged sitting)
Encourage use of lubricants during intercourse if applicable
Report increasing pain severity or failure to respond to treatment to provider
Support adherence to pelvic floor physiotherapy if prescribed
Assess for anxiety and depression and report findings to provider

bartholinitis-rpn.title: Bartholinitis - RPN Fundamentals

cellular.title: Bartholin Gland Inflammation
cellular.content: Bartholinitis is inflammation of the Bartholin glands, two small pea-sized glands located on either side of the vaginal opening at the 4 and 8 o'clock positions. These glands produce mucus that lubricates the vaginal vestibule. When the gland duct becomes blocked, secretions accumulate and form a painless cyst. If the cyst becomes infected  -  often by a mix of bacteria including E. coli, anaerobes, or STI organisms  -  it develops into a painful abscess. The abscess fills with pus, causing significant swelling, redness, and tenderness of the affected labium. The patient may have difficulty sitting, walking, or engaging in intercourse due to the swelling and pain.

left: Unilateral labial swelling (4 or 8 o'clock position)
Tender, warm, fluctuant mass if abscess
Pain with walking, sitting, or intercourse
right: Fever if abscess is infected
Redness and edema of the affected labium
Purulent drainage if abscess ruptures spontaneously

medications.name: Ibuprofen
medications.action: Reduces inflammation and provides analgesic effect for pain associated with Bartholin cyst/abscess

medications.name: Amoxicillin-Clavulanate (Augmentin)
medications.action: Provides broad-spectrum coverage against common organisms causing Bartholin abscess including anaerobes and E. coli

pearls: Bartholin cysts are common and often painless  -  they only require treatment when symptomatic or infected
Warm sitz baths are both therapeutic and comfort-promoting  -  encourage 3-4 times daily
Word catheter stays in place 4-6 weeks to create a new drainage tract and prevent recurrence
Report increasing pain, fever, or spreading redness immediately  -  may indicate worsening abscess or cellulitis
diagnostics: Expect physical examination showing unilateral labial swelling
Monitor for signs of abscess formation (increasing pain, warmth, fluctuance)
Expect wound culture if abscess is drained
Monitor temperature for fever indicating infection
Expect STI screening if abscess is present
management: Apply warm compresses or sitz baths 3-4 times daily for cyst
Administer analgesics as prescribed for pain management
Prepare patient for incision and drainage (I&D) if abscess present
Provide post-procedure wound care after I&D or Word catheter placement
Administer antibiotics as ordered if cellulitis or systemic infection present
nursingActions: Assess labial swelling (size, location, tenderness, warmth, fluctuance)
Monitor vital signs and temperature for signs of systemic infection
Apply warm compresses or assist with sitz baths for comfort and drainage promotion
Provide post-I&D wound care as ordered (keep area clean, monitor drainage)
Educate on sitz baths 3-4 times daily after I&D procedure
Monitor Word catheter placement if present (should remain in place 4-6 weeks)
Report signs of worsening infection (increasing pain, fever, spreading redness) to provider
Administer prescribed analgesics and antibiotics on schedule
Educate on perineal hygiene to prevent recurrence

uterine-prolapse-rpn.title: Uterine Prolapse - RPN Fundamentals

cellular.title: Pelvic Floor Support Failure
cellular.content: Uterine prolapse occurs when the uterus descends from its normal position into or beyond the vaginal canal due to weakening of the pelvic floor support structures. The pelvic floor consists of muscles (primarily the levator ani group) and connective tissue (endopelvic fascia) that hold the uterus, bladder, and rectum in place. When these structures weaken  -  from childbirth trauma, aging, chronic straining, or loss of estrogen after menopause  -  the uterus drops downward. Prolapse may be mild (the cervix descends into the upper vagina) or severe (the uterus protrudes outside the vaginal opening). Associated conditions include cystocele (bladder bulging into the vagina) and rectocele (rectum bulging into the vagina).

riskFactors: Multiple vaginal deliveries (especially traumatic or prolonged)
Family history of pelvic organ prolapse

left: Sensation of vaginal fullness or heaviness
Feeling of 'something falling out' of the vagina
Visible or palpable bulge at vaginal opening
right: Urinary incontinence or difficulty voiding
Difficulty with bowel movements (constipation)
Low back pain or pelvic pressure
Discomfort during intercourse

medications.name: Vaginal Estrogen Cream
medications.action: Restores estrogen to vaginal tissues, improving tissue thickness, elasticity, and blood flow to support pelvic structures

medications.name: Docusate Sodium (Colace)
medications.action: Draws water into stool to soften it, reducing the need to strain during defecation

pearls: Kegel exercises are the first-line conservative treatment  -  consistency is key
A pessary is a non-surgical device that supports the uterus and can be very effective
Avoiding straining (constipation, heavy lifting) is critical to prevent worsening
Prolapse often coexists with cystocele and/or rectocele  -  assess for urinary and bowel symptoms
diagnostics: Expect pelvic examination to assess degree of prolapse
Monitor for associated cystocele or rectocele
Expect assessment using POP-Q staging system by provider
Monitor urinary function (incontinence, retention)
Expect referral to urogynecology if surgical intervention considered
management: Teach and encourage pelvic floor exercises (Kegel exercises)
Assist with pessary insertion and care if prescribed
Encourage weight management and avoidance of heavy lifting
Educate on managing constipation to avoid straining
Prepare for surgical intervention if conservative measures fail
nursingActions: Assess for symptoms of prolapse (vaginal bulging, heaviness, pressure)
Teach Kegel exercises (contract pelvic floor muscles for 5-10 seconds, relax, repeat 10-15 times, 3 sets daily)
Assist with pessary fitting and educate on care (regular removal, cleaning, and reinsertion)
Monitor for pessary complications (vaginal erosion, discharge, discomfort)
Educate on avoiding heavy lifting, straining, and prolonged standing
Encourage high-fiber diet and adequate fluids to prevent constipation
Report worsening prolapse symptoms or new urinary/bowel symptoms to provider
Assess for urinary incontinence or difficulty voiding and report findings
Provide emotional support for body image and quality-of-life concerns

benign-ovarian-cysts-rpn.title: Benign Ovarian Cysts - RPN Fundamentals

cellular.title: Ovarian Cyst Formation
cellular.content: Benign ovarian cysts are fluid-filled sacs that develop on or within the ovary. The most common types are functional cysts, which arise from the normal ovulatory process. A follicular cyst forms when a follicle fails to release its egg (ovulate) and continues to grow, filling with fluid. A corpus luteum cyst forms when the corpus luteum (the structure remaining after ovulation) fails to regress and fills with fluid or blood. These functional cysts are usually small, asymptomatic, and resolve spontaneously within 1-3 menstrual cycles. Other benign cyst types include dermoid cysts (mature teratomas containing tissue such as hair, teeth, and fat) and endometriomas (blood-filled cysts from endometriosis). Complications include cyst rupture (causing acute pelvic pain and possible internal bleeding) and ovarian torsion (twisting of the ovary on its blood supply, a surgical emergency).

riskFactors: Reproductive age (most common during childbearing years)

left: Unilateral pelvic or lower abdominal pain
Pelvic pressure or fullness
Dull ache or intermittent sharp pain
right: Bloating and abdominal distension
Irregular menstrual periods
Pain during intercourse
Most small cysts are asymptomatic

medications.name: Ibuprofen
medications.action: Reduces inflammation and provides analgesic effect for pelvic pain associated with ovarian cysts

medications.name: Combined Oral Contraceptive
medications.action: Suppresses ovulation, preventing the development of new functional cysts

pearls: Most functional cysts resolve on their own within 1-3 menstrual cycles without treatment
Sudden severe unilateral pain is a red flag  -  may indicate ovarian torsion (surgical emergency) or cyst rupture
Ovarian torsion presents with sudden severe pain, nausea, and vomiting  -  report immediately
Oral contraceptives prevent new functional cysts but do not treat existing ones
diagnostics: Expect pelvic ultrasound to determine cyst size, type, and characteristics
Monitor vital signs if rupture or torsion suspected
Expect pregnancy test to rule out ectopic pregnancy
Expect CBC if hemorrhage suspected
Monitor pain characteristics and severity
management: Monitor small functional cysts expectantly (watchful waiting)
Administer analgesics as prescribed for pain management
Educate patient on signs of complications requiring emergency care
Prepare for surgical intervention if cyst is large, persistent, or symptomatic
Administer oral contraceptives if prescribed to prevent new cyst formation
nursingActions: Assess and document pain characteristics (location, severity, onset, duration)
Monitor vital signs for signs of hemorrhage if rupture suspected (tachycardia, hypotension)
Report sudden severe unilateral pelvic pain to provider immediately (may indicate torsion or rupture)
Educate patient on warning signs requiring emergency care (sudden severe pain, dizziness, fainting)
Administer prescribed analgesics and monitor effectiveness
Educate on the benign and self-resolving nature of most functional cysts
Prepare patient for pelvic ultrasound as ordered
Monitor for peritoneal signs (rebound tenderness, guarding) if rupture suspected
Provide reassurance and emotional support

endometrial-polyp-rpn.title: Endometrial Polyps - RPN Fundamentals

cellular.title: Endometrial Overgrowth
cellular.content: Endometrial polyps are localized overgrowths of the endometrial lining (the inner lining of the uterus) that project into the uterine cavity. They are composed of endometrial glands, stroma, and blood vessels. Polyps develop when areas of the endometrium respond excessively to estrogen stimulation, causing focal overgrowth. They can range in size from a few millimeters to several centimeters and may be single or multiple. Most endometrial polyps are benign, but they commonly cause abnormal uterine bleeding  -  including intermenstrual bleeding, heavy menstrual bleeding, and postmenopausal bleeding. The risk of malignant transformation is low (approximately 1-3%) but increases in postmenopausal women, those on tamoxifen therapy, and those with larger polyps.

riskFactors: Tamoxifen use (estrogen agonist effect on endometrium)

left: Intermenstrual bleeding (bleeding between periods)
Heavy menstrual bleeding (menorrhagia)
Postmenopausal bleeding
right: Prolonged menstrual periods
Spotting or staining on underwear
Infertility in some cases
Many small polyps are asymptomatic

medications.name: Ferrous Sulfate
medications.action: Replaces depleted iron stores to correct iron-deficiency anemia from chronic abnormal bleeding

medications.name: Tranexamic Acid
medications.action: Stabilizes blood clots by inhibiting plasminogen activation, reducing menstrual blood loss

pearls: Postmenopausal bleeding is ALWAYS abnormal and requires investigation  -  endometrial polyps are a common cause
Most endometrial polyps are benign but must be evaluated to rule out malignancy
Hysteroscopic polypectomy is the gold standard for both diagnosis and treatment
Monitor for anemia in patients with chronic abnormal bleeding from polyps
diagnostics: Expect transvaginal ultrasound to visualize endometrial thickening
Expect saline infusion sonography (SIS) for better polyp visualization
Expect hysteroscopy for direct visualization and possible removal
Expect endometrial biopsy to rule out malignancy
Monitor menstrual pad count and bleeding patterns
management: Monitor bleeding patterns and pad count
Prepare patient for hysteroscopy and polypectomy if indicated
Provide pre-procedure and post-procedure education and care
Administer prescribed medications for bleeding management
Monitor for signs of heavy bleeding or hemorrhage
nursingActions: Assess and document abnormal bleeding patterns (timing, amount, frequency, clots)
Monitor pad count  -  track number and saturation of pads used
Educate patient on tracking bleeding patterns and reporting changes
Prepare patient for transvaginal ultrasound or hysteroscopy procedures
Provide post-hysteroscopy care (monitor for bleeding, cramping, signs of infection)
Educate on post-procedure expectations (mild cramping and spotting are normal)
Report heavy post-procedure bleeding (soaking more than one pad per hour) to provider
Monitor for signs of anemia if chronic bleeding (fatigue, pallor, tachycardia)
Administer iron supplements as ordered for anemia

endometriosis-rpn.title: Endometriosis - RPN Fundamentals

cellular.title: Ectopic Endometrial Tissue
cellular.content: Endometriosis occurs when tissue similar to the endometrial lining of the uterus grows outside the uterus in abnormal locations such as the ovaries, fallopian tubes, pelvic peritoneum, and bowel. This misplaced tissue responds to the same hormonal changes of the menstrual cycle - it thickens, breaks down, and bleeds each month. However, because the tissue is located outside the uterus, the blood and debris have no way to exit the body. This leads to chronic inflammation, pain, scar tissue formation (adhesions), and the development of blood-filled cysts on the ovaries called endometriomas (chocolate cysts). The condition is estrogen-dependent, meaning it is fueled by estrogen and tends to improve after menopause when estrogen levels decline.

left: Cyclic pelvic pain worsening during menses
Dysmenorrhea (painful periods) that progressively worsens
Dyspareunia (pain with intercourse)
Chronic pelvic pain
right: Pain with bowel movements (dyschezia) during menses
Pain with urination during menses
Heavy menstrual bleeding
Difficulty conceiving (infertility)

medications.name: Ibuprofen
medications.action: Inhibits prostaglandin synthesis to reduce inflammation and pain associated with endometrial implants

medications.name: Norethindrone (Progestin)
medications.action: Suppresses estrogen-driven growth of endometrial implants by creating a progesterone-dominant environment and reducing menstruation

pearls: Endometriosis pain is characteristically cyclic - it worsens around the time of menstruation
Dysmenorrhea that progressively worsens over time is a hallmark and should be reported to the provider
Emotional support is essential - endometriosis is chronic, painful, and can impact fertility and relationships
NSAIDs are most effective when given BEFORE pain peaks - educate on early dosing
diagnostics: Expect thorough pain assessment and menstrual history documentation
Monitor pain characteristics (cyclic pelvic pain worsening with menses)
Expect pelvic ultrasound to evaluate for endometriomas
Expect referral to gynecology for further evaluation
Expect CA-125 blood test (may be elevated but not diagnostic)
Expect laparoscopy for definitive diagnosis
management: Administer prescribed analgesics (NSAIDs) for pain management
Administer hormonal therapy as prescribed (oral contraceptives, progestins)
Apply heat to lower abdomen for comfort during pain episodes
Encourage comfortable positioning during pain episodes
Prepare patient for surgical procedures if scheduled (laparoscopy)
Ensure follow-up appointments are scheduled with gynecology
nursingActions: Assess pain using a standardized pain scale - document location, severity, timing, and relationship to menstrual cycle
Monitor for cyclic pelvic pain that worsens with menses
Administer prescribed medications and monitor effectiveness
Apply non-pharmacological comfort measures (heat application, positioning, relaxation techniques)
Educate patient on the importance of taking NSAIDs at onset of pain or before menses for best effect
Report acute severe pelvic pain to provider immediately (may indicate ruptured endometrioma or torsion)
Provide emotional support - endometriosis is a chronic condition that can affect quality of life and fertility
Educate on the relationship between menstrual cycle and symptom patterns
Report dyspareunia (painful intercourse) findings to provider

infertility-rpn.title: Infertility - RPN Fundamentals

cellular.title: Impaired Conception
cellular.content: Infertility is defined as the inability to conceive after 12 months of regular unprotected intercourse (or 6 months if the woman is over age 35). Conception requires a complex sequence of events - ovulation of a healthy egg, transport of sperm through the cervix and uterus, fertilization in the fallopian tube, and implantation in the uterine lining. Infertility can result from problems at any step in this process. Female factors include ovulatory dysfunction (such as PCOS or hypothalamic amenorrhea), tubal damage (from PID or endometriosis), and uterine abnormalities. Male factors include problems with sperm production, motility, or delivery. In many couples, both partners contribute to the difficulty conceiving. Infertility is a deeply emotional experience and requires compassionate nursing support.

riskFactors: Male factor (low sperm count, poor motility)
History of undescended testes in males

left: Inability to conceive after 12 months of unprotected intercourse
Irregular or absent menstrual periods
History of pelvic pain or known pelvic pathology
right: Emotional distress, grief, anxiety, and depression
Relationship strain between partners
Social isolation and feelings of inadequacy

medications.name: Clomiphene Citrate (Clomid)
medications.action: Blocks estrogen receptors in the hypothalamus, causing increased release of FSH and LH to stimulate ovarian follicle development and ovulation

medications.name: Progesterone (Supplemental)
medications.action: Supports the uterine lining after ovulation to promote implantation and early pregnancy maintenance

pearls: Infertility is an emotionally devastating experience - compassionate support is a critical nursing intervention
Report any signs of ovarian hyperstimulation syndrome (OHSS) immediately - this is a potentially life-threatening complication of fertility treatment
Semen specimens must be kept warm and delivered to the lab within 30-60 minutes of collection
Visual disturbances on clomiphene citrate require immediate reporting and medication discontinuation
diagnostics: Expect basal body temperature charting to assess ovulation patterns
Expect semen analysis for male partner evaluation
Expect hormone level testing (FSH, LH, estradiol, progesterone)
Monitor ovulation predictor kit results
Expect hysterosalpingogram (HSG) to assess tubal patency
Expect transvaginal ultrasound for follicle monitoring
management: Provide emotional support throughout the evaluation and treatment process
Administer fertility medications as prescribed and on correct timing
Assist with specimen collection procedures (semen analysis)
Educate on proper timing of intercourse relative to ovulation
Ensure follow-up appointments for monitoring are scheduled
Prepare patient for procedures (HSG, IUI) as indicated
nursingActions: Assess emotional well-being at every visit - infertility causes significant grief and stress
Administer fertility medications as ordered and educate on proper timing and technique
Monitor for side effects of fertility medications (bloating, mood changes, pelvic pain)
Report signs of ovarian hyperstimulation syndrome (severe bloating, rapid weight gain, difficulty breathing, decreased urine output) to provider immediately
Assist with semen specimen collection and ensure proper handling (warm, to lab within 30-60 minutes)
Educate on basal body temperature tracking and ovulation signs
Provide privacy and dignity during all procedures and discussions
Report medication side effects to provider promptly
Connect patients with support groups and counseling resources

galactorrhea-rpn.title: Galactorrhea - RPN Fundamentals

cellular.title: Inappropriate Milk Discharge
cellular.content: Galactorrhea is the spontaneous flow of milk or milky nipple discharge unrelated to breastfeeding or childbirth. It results from elevated levels of prolactin (hyperprolactinemia), a hormone produced by the anterior pituitary gland. Prolactin is normally kept in check by dopamine, which inhibits its release. When dopamine's inhibitory effect is disrupted - by pituitary tumors (prolactinomas), certain medications (antipsychotics, metoclopramide, some antidepressants), or hypothyroidism - prolactin levels rise and stimulate breast tissue to produce milk. Galactorrhea can occur in women or men, though it is more common in women. It is usually benign but requires investigation to identify and treat the underlying cause.

riskFactors: Use of dopamine-blocking medications (antipsychotics, metoclopramide, domperidone)
Chronic breast or chest wall stimulation

left: Milky nipple discharge not related to pregnancy or breastfeeding
Discharge may be spontaneous or expressed
Bilateral discharge (both breasts) is most common
right: Irregular menstrual periods or amenorrhea
Headaches or visual changes (if pituitary cause)
Decreased libido
Infertility

medications.name: Cabergoline
medications.action: Stimulates dopamine D2 receptors in the pituitary to suppress prolactin secretion and reduce galactorrhea

medications.name: Bromocriptine
medications.action: Activates dopamine receptors to inhibit prolactin release from the anterior pituitary gland

pearls: Always review the medication list - drug-induced hyperprolactinemia is the most common cause of galactorrhea
Headaches and visual field changes require immediate reporting - they may indicate a growing pituitary tumor pressing on the optic chiasm
Galactorrhea is usually benign but the underlying cause must be identified and treated
Educate patients to avoid nipple stimulation as it can perpetuate prolactin release and worsen discharge
diagnostics: Expect prolactin blood level measurement
Expect thyroid function tests (TSH, T4) to rule out hypothyroidism
Expect comprehensive medication history review
Expect pregnancy test to rule out pregnancy
Expect MRI of the pituitary if prolactin is significantly elevated
Monitor and document nipple discharge characteristics (color, consistency, bilateral vs unilateral)
management: Document discharge characteristics accurately (color, amount, bilateral vs unilateral, spontaneous vs expressed)
Assist with medication history review to identify causative drugs
Administer dopamine agonist therapy as prescribed if ordered
Provide emotional support - nipple discharge can be distressing
Educate patient to avoid nipple stimulation as this can worsen discharge
nursingActions: Assess and document nipple discharge (color, consistency, amount, unilateral vs bilateral, spontaneous vs expressed)
Review current medication list thoroughly - many medications can cause hyperprolactinemia
Monitor for signs and symptoms of hypothyroidism (fatigue, weight gain, cold intolerance, constipation)
Educate patient to avoid excessive nipple stimulation or manipulation
Provide emotional support - galactorrhea can cause significant embarrassment and anxiety
Report any visual changes (visual field deficits, headaches) to provider immediately - may indicate pituitary mass effect
Educate on the importance of follow-up for prolactin monitoring
Report medication side effects from dopamine agonist therapy (nausea, dizziness, orthostatic hypotension)

breast-cancer-rpn.title: Breast Cancer - RPN Fundamentals

cellular.title: Malignant Breast Cell Growth
cellular.content: Breast cancer develops when cells in the breast tissue grow uncontrollably and form a malignant tumor. Most breast cancers begin in the cells lining the milk ducts (ductal carcinoma) or the lobules that produce milk (lobular carcinoma). These abnormal cells accumulate genetic mutations that allow them to divide without normal regulatory control, invade surrounding tissues, and potentially spread (metastasize) to lymph nodes and distant organs. Breast cancer can be classified by its receptor status - whether the cancer cells have receptors for estrogen (ER), progesterone (PR), or the HER2 protein - which determines treatment options. Early detection through screening and self-examination significantly improves outcomes.

riskFactors: Female sex (though males can also develop breast cancer)
Family history of breast or ovarian cancer (especially first-degree relatives)
Early menarche or late menopause (prolonged estrogen exposure)
Nulliparity or first pregnancy after age 30

left: Painless breast lump (most common presenting sign)
Skin dimpling or puckering
Peau d'orange (orange-peel skin texture)
Nipple retraction or inversion
right: Bloody or clear nipple discharge
Axillary lymph node enlargement
Breast skin redness or thickening
Change in breast size or shape

medications.name: Tamoxifen
medications.action: Blocks estrogen receptors on breast cancer cells, preventing estrogen from stimulating cancer growth

medications.name: Ondansetron (Zofran)
medications.action: Blocks serotonin receptors in the chemoreceptor trigger zone to prevent chemotherapy-induced nausea and vomiting

pearls: A painless breast lump is the most common presenting sign of breast cancer - all new lumps must be evaluated
Peau d'orange (orange-peel skin texture) is a concerning finding that should be reported immediately
After mastectomy or axillary lymph node dissection - NEVER use the affected arm for blood pressure, venipuncture, or IV lines
Emotional support is critical at every stage - diagnosis, treatment, and survivorship
diagnostics: Expect clinical breast examination findings documentation
Expect diagnostic mammography and/or breast ultrasound
Expect breast biopsy for tissue diagnosis
Monitor for lymph node enlargement (axillary)
Expect staging studies if cancer confirmed (CT, bone scan, MRI)
Monitor surgical drain output post-operatively
management: Provide pre-operative teaching for surgical procedures (lumpectomy, mastectomy)
Monitor surgical site and drain output post-operatively
Administer prescribed medications (pain management, antiemetics during chemotherapy)
Perform arm measurements to monitor for lymphedema development
Provide wound care and drain management education
Ensure follow-up appointments are scheduled
nursingActions: Teach and reinforce breast self-examination technique (monthly, 7-10 days after menses)
Educate on recognizing abnormal breast changes (new lump, skin dimpling, peau d'orange, nipple retraction, bloody nipple discharge)
Monitor post-surgical incision site for signs of infection (redness, swelling, warmth, drainage, fever)
Measure and record surgical drain output (Jackson-Pratt drain) and report excessive drainage
Assess for signs of lymphedema in the affected arm (swelling, heaviness, tightness)
Educate patient to avoid blood pressure measurements, venipuncture, and IV lines in the affected arm
Provide emotional support throughout diagnosis, treatment, and recovery
Report any new lumps, skin changes, or lymph node enlargement to provider
Monitor for chemotherapy side effects (nausea, neutropenia, fatigue, mucositis) and report findings

urethritis-rpn.title: Urethritis - RPN Fundamentals

cellular.title: Urethral Mucosal Inflammation
cellular.content: Urethritis is inflammation of the urethra, most commonly caused by sexually transmitted pathogens. The two main types are gonococcal urethritis (caused by Neisseria gonorrhoeae) and non-gonococcal urethritis (most often caused by Chlamydia trachomatis, but also Mycoplasma genitalium and Ureaplasma urealyticum). The bacteria infect the urethral mucosal epithelium, triggering an inflammatory response that causes mucosal swelling, discharge production, and pain with urination. Urethritis is more symptomatic in men (with visible urethral discharge and dysuria) than in women, where it may be asymptomatic or mimic a urinary tract infection. If untreated, the infection can ascend and cause more serious complications such as epididymitis in men or pelvic inflammatory disease in women.

riskFactors: New sexual partner in the past 60 days
Men who have sex with men

left: Dysuria (painful urination)
Urethral discharge (mucopurulent or purulent)
Urethral itching or irritation
right: Urinary frequency and urgency
Discharge may be clear, white, or yellow-green
May be asymptomatic (especially in women)

medications.name: Azithromycin
medications.action: Inhibits bacterial protein synthesis by binding to the 50S ribosomal subunit, effective against Chlamydia

medications.name: Ceftriaxone
medications.action: Inhibits bacterial cell wall synthesis, effective against Neisseria gonorrhoeae

pearls: Always treat for BOTH gonorrhea and chlamydia when gonococcal urethritis is suspected - co-infection is very common
Partner notification and treatment is essential to prevent reinfection and community spread
Provide non-judgmental care - stigma around STIs can prevent patients from seeking treatment
Report fever, scrotal pain, or pelvic pain to provider immediately - may indicate ascending infection
diagnostics: Expect nucleic acid amplification test (NAAT) for Chlamydia and Gonorrhea
Expect urethral swab or first-void urine specimen collection
Monitor for urethral discharge characteristics (color, consistency)
Expect urinalysis to rule out UTI
Expect testing for other STIs (HIV, syphilis, hepatitis B)
management: Administer prescribed antibiotics as ordered - full course must be completed
Ensure sexual partner notification and treatment
Educate on abstaining from sexual intercourse until treatment is completed and symptoms resolve
Provide STI prevention education (condom use, reducing number of partners)
Schedule test-of-cure follow-up as indicated
nursingActions: Assess and document symptoms (dysuria, urethral discharge, frequency, urgency)
Collect specimens properly - first-void urine or urethral swab as ordered
Administer antibiotics as prescribed and educate on completing full course
Educate on the importance of partner notification and treatment to prevent reinfection
Report persistent symptoms after treatment to provider (may indicate resistant organism or reinfection)
Provide non-judgmental STI education and prevention counseling
Educate on proper condom use to prevent future infections
Document discharge characteristics (color, amount, odor)
Report any signs of ascending infection (fever, scrotal pain, pelvic pain) to provider immediately

urethral-stricture-rpn.title: Urethral Stricture - RPN Fundamentals

cellular.title: Urethral Narrowing
cellular.content: A urethral stricture is a narrowing of the urethra caused by scar tissue formation (fibrosis) within or around the urethral wall. This scar tissue develops in response to injury, infection, or chronic inflammation. Common causes include prior catheterization, urethral instrumentation (cystoscopy, surgery), sexually transmitted infections (particularly gonorrhea), and traumatic injury to the perineum or pelvis. The scar tissue reduces the diameter of the urethral lumen, creating resistance to urine flow. This leads to difficulty urinating, weak stream, straining, incomplete bladder emptying, and potentially urinary retention. Strictures occur almost exclusively in males due to the longer urethral length and are most common in the bulbar urethra.

riskFactors: Prior urethral catheterization (especially prolonged or traumatic)

left: Weak or split urinary stream
Straining to initiate or maintain urination
Urinary hesitancy (difficulty starting stream)
Feeling of incomplete bladder emptying
right: Urinary retention (inability to void)
Dribbling after urination
Recurrent urinary tract infections
Decreased force of urinary stream over time

medications.name: Tamsulosin
medications.action: Relaxes smooth muscle in the bladder neck and prostatic urethra to improve urine flow

medications.name: Trimethoprim-Sulfamethoxazole
medications.action: Treats urinary tract infections that develop secondary to incomplete bladder emptying from urethral stricture

pearls: Inability to void is an emergency - report to provider immediately for urgent catheterization
Weak urinary stream that progressively worsens over time suggests stricture formation
Recurrent UTIs in a male patient should raise suspicion for urethral stricture causing urinary stasis
Monitor carefully after any urethral procedure for bleeding and ability to void
diagnostics: Expect voiding history assessment (stream strength, straining, hesitancy)
Expect uroflowmetry (urine flow rate measurement)
Expect retrograde urethrogram for stricture visualization
Monitor post-void residual volume (bladder scan)
Expect referral to urology for evaluation
Monitor intake and output carefully
management: Monitor urinary output and voiding patterns closely
Assist with urethral dilation procedures if scheduled
Provide catheter care if indwelling catheter is placed
Administer prescribed medications (analgesics, antibiotics)
Monitor for urinary retention signs and report
Ensure follow-up with urology is scheduled
nursingActions: Assess voiding pattern - document stream strength, hesitancy, straining, and completeness of emptying
Monitor for signs of urinary retention (distended bladder, suprapubic discomfort, dribbling, inability to void)
Report inability to void to provider immediately - may require catheterization
Monitor intake and output accurately
Provide post-procedure care after urethral dilation (monitor for bleeding, pain, urinary retention)
Provide catheter care if indwelling or suprapubic catheter is in place
Educate patient on signs of urinary retention to watch for at home
Report hematuria (blood in urine) after procedures to provider
Educate on adequate fluid intake to maintain urine flow

phimosis-rpn.title: Phimosis - RPN Fundamentals

cellular.title: Foreskin Retraction Difficulty
cellular.content: Phimosis is the inability to retract the foreskin (prepuce) over the glans penis. It can be physiologic or pathologic. Physiologic phimosis is normal in infants and young children - the foreskin is naturally adherent to the glans at birth and gradually becomes retractable over the first several years of life. By age 3, most boys have some degree of retractability, and by puberty, the foreskin should fully retract. Pathologic phimosis occurs when scar tissue or chronic inflammation (such as balanitis xerotica obliterans/lichen sclerosus) causes the foreskin opening to narrow and become non-retractable even in older children or adults. Complications include difficulty with hygiene, recurrent infections (balanitis), pain during erection, and in severe cases, urinary obstruction. Paraphimosis is a related emergency where a retracted foreskin becomes trapped behind the glans and cannot be returned to its normal position, causing vascular compromise.

riskFactors: Age (physiologic phimosis normal in young boys)

left: Inability to retract foreskin over the glans
Pain during erection (in older children and adults)
Ballooning of foreskin during urination
right: Difficulty with hygiene under foreskin
Recurrent balanitis (infections)
Weak or deflected urinary stream in severe cases

medications.name: Betamethasone 0.05% Cream
medications.action: Reduces inflammation and promotes thinning of the phimotic foreskin tissue, allowing gradual retractability

medications.name: Mupirocin Ointment
medications.action: Treats secondary bacterial infection of the foreskin associated with phimosis

pearls: Physiologic phimosis in infants and young boys is NORMAL - do not force the foreskin back
NEVER forcefully retract the foreskin - this causes micro-tears, scarring, and worsens the condition
Paraphimosis is a MEDICAL EMERGENCY - a trapped retracted foreskin can cause tissue death if not reduced promptly
Topical steroid therapy with gentle retraction exercises is first-line treatment before considering surgery
diagnostics: Expect physical examination to assess degree of foreskin retraction
Monitor for signs of infection (redness, swelling, discharge)
Expect assessment of urinary stream (ballooning of foreskin during voiding suggests significant phimosis)
Monitor response to topical steroid therapy
Expect referral to urology if conservative management fails
management: Apply topical corticosteroid cream as prescribed to the phimotic ring
Educate on gentle foreskin retraction exercises as directed by provider
Provide hygiene education for proper foreskin care
Prepare patient for circumcision or preputioplasty if indicated
Provide post-surgical wound care after circumcision
nursingActions: Assess degree of foreskin retractability and document findings
Apply topical steroid cream as prescribed and educate patient/parent on proper application technique
Educate on gentle foreskin retraction - NEVER force the foreskin back (can cause tearing and scarring)
Provide hygiene education - gentle cleaning under the foreskin when retractable
Monitor for signs of balanitis (redness, swelling, discharge under foreskin)
