Report paraphimosis immediately - this is a MEDICAL EMERGENCY (retracted foreskin trapped behind glans causing swelling)
Provide post-circumcision wound care (petroleum jelly gauze, monitor for bleeding, pain management)
Educate parents that physiologic phimosis in young boys is NORMAL and does not require intervention
Report ballooning of the foreskin during urination or difficulty urinating to provider

priapism-rpn.title: Priapism - RPN Fundamentals

cellular.title: Prolonged Penile Erection
cellular.content: Priapism is a prolonged, persistent penile erection lasting more than 4 hours that is unrelated to sexual stimulation or desire. It occurs when blood becomes trapped in the erectile tissue (corpora cavernosa) of the penis and cannot drain properly. The most common type is ischemic (low-flow) priapism, where venous outflow is blocked, trapping deoxygenated blood in the corpora cavernosa. Without blood flow, the tissue becomes ischemic (oxygen-deprived), and if untreated beyond 4-6 hours, permanent damage to the erectile tissue can occur, leading to fibrosis and erectile dysfunction. Priapism is a MEDICAL EMERGENCY. Common causes include sickle cell disease (the most common cause in children), medications such as PDE5 inhibitors (sildenafil), intracavernosal injections, antipsychotics, and blood disorders.

riskFactors: Sickle cell disease (most common cause in children and adolescents)
Intracavernosal injection therapy for erectile dysfunction

left: Rigid, painful erection lasting more than 4 hours
Erection unrelated to sexual desire or stimulation
Pain that worsens over time
right: Penile shaft is rigid but glans may be soft (ischemic type)
History of sickle cell disease or causative medication
Anxiety and emotional distress

medications.name: Phenylephrine (Intracavernosal)
medications.action: Causes smooth muscle contraction in the corpora cavernosa, promoting venous outflow and detumescence

medications.name: Morphine
medications.action: Provides analgesia for severe pain associated with ischemic priapism

pearls: Priapism lasting more than 4 hours is a MEDICAL EMERGENCY - delay in treatment leads to permanent erectile dysfunction
Sickle cell disease is the most common cause of priapism in children and adolescents
Always review medication history - PDE5 inhibitors and certain antipsychotics are common causes
Provide privacy and emotional support - this condition is highly distressing and embarrassing for patients
diagnostics: Expect focused history (duration of erection, associated medications, sickle cell status)
Monitor vital signs including pain assessment
Expect blood gas analysis of aspirated corporal blood
Expect complete blood count (CBC) if sickle cell or blood disorder suspected
Expect urology consultation urgently
management: This is a MEDICAL EMERGENCY - notify provider immediately upon recognition
Apply ice packs to the perineum as a temporizing measure
Monitor vital signs and pain level continuously
Prepare for potential corporal aspiration and irrigation by urology
Administer prescribed analgesics for pain management
If sickle cell related - prepare for IV hydration, oxygen therapy, and pain management
nursingActions: Recognize priapism as a MEDICAL EMERGENCY - erection lasting more than 4 hours requires immediate intervention
Notify provider and urology immediately upon identification
Assess and document duration of erection, pain level, and any identifiable cause
Review medication history thoroughly (PDE5 inhibitors, antipsychotics, trazodone)
Apply ice packs to perineum as directed
Monitor vital signs including pain assessment using pain scale
If sickle cell patient - initiate IV access, administer IV fluids and oxygen as ordered
Provide privacy and emotional support - this is an embarrassing and frightening condition
Monitor post-procedure for recurrence and ability to void
Report any signs of worsening ischemia to provider

balanitis-rpn.title: Balanitis - RPN Fundamentals

cellular.title: Glans Inflammation
cellular.content: Balanitis is inflammation of the glans penis (the head of the penis). When inflammation extends to the foreskin (prepuce), it is called balanoposthitis. The most common cause is poor hygiene combined with an intact foreskin, which creates a warm, moist environment under the prepuce where organisms can thrive. Candida albicans (yeast) is the most common infectious cause, especially in patients with diabetes mellitus. Bacterial infections, irritant contact dermatitis (from soaps, detergents, or condoms), and skin conditions can also cause balanitis. The inflammation leads to redness, swelling, tenderness, and discharge of the glans and foreskin. Recurrent balanitis can lead to scarring of the foreskin, causing or worsening phimosis.

riskFactors: Diabetes mellitus (especially uncontrolled - provides glucose for yeast growth)
Use of irritant soaps, detergents, or spermicides

left: Redness and swelling of the glans penis
Pain or tenderness of the glans
Discharge from under the foreskin (may be white, cheesy, or purulent)
right: Itching or burning sensation
Difficulty retracting the foreskin
Foul odor
Tight, swollen foreskin (balanoposthitis)

medications.name: Clotrimazole 1% Cream
medications.action: Disrupts fungal cell membrane synthesis, killing Candida organisms and resolving yeast infection

medications.name: Fluconazole
medications.action: Systemic antifungal that inhibits fungal cell membrane synthesis for more severe or recurrent Candidal balanitis

pearls: Proper hygiene is the cornerstone of balanitis prevention - gentle cleaning with warm water, avoiding harsh soaps
Diabetes is a significant risk factor - always screen for diabetes in recurrent balanitis
Recurrent balanitis can lead to phimosis from scarring - report repeated episodes to provider
White cheesy discharge under the foreskin is characteristic of Candidal balanitis
diagnostics: Expect physical examination of the glans and foreskin
Monitor for discharge characteristics (white cheesy discharge suggests Candida)
Expect wound/discharge swab for culture if bacterial cause suspected
Expect blood glucose testing to screen for diabetes
Expect referral to urology or dermatology if recurrent
management: Provide hygiene education - gentle daily cleaning with warm water, avoid harsh soaps
Apply topical antifungal or antibiotic cream as prescribed
Administer prescribed oral medications if topical therapy insufficient
Educate on keeping the area clean and dry
Manage underlying conditions (blood glucose control for diabetic patients)
nursingActions: Assess and document glans appearance (redness, swelling, discharge, lesions, odor)
Apply prescribed topical medications (antifungal or antibacterial cream) as directed
Educate on proper hygiene - retract foreskin gently, clean with warm water, dry thoroughly, and replace foreskin
Educate to avoid irritating soaps, bubble baths, and harsh detergents in the genital area
Monitor blood glucose if diabetic - uncontrolled diabetes predisposes to recurrent yeast infections
Report worsening symptoms (increasing redness, swelling, inability to retract foreskin) to provider
Report development of phimosis (inability to retract foreskin) to provider
Educate on wearing loose-fitting cotton underwear to reduce moisture
Provide emotional support - genital conditions can cause embarrassment

penile-cancer-rpn.title: Penile Cancer - RPN Fundamentals

cellular.title: Malignant Penile Growth
cellular.content: Penile cancer is a rare malignancy that most commonly presents as squamous cell carcinoma arising from the skin of the penis. The cancer typically develops on the glans penis or the inner surface of the foreskin (prepuce). It begins as abnormal cells that grow uncontrollably, forming a visible lesion that may appear as a painless lump, ulcer, or wart-like growth. The cancer can be locally destructive, invading deeper penile tissues, and can spread to regional lymph nodes in the groin (inguinal lymph nodes) and eventually to distant sites. Human papillomavirus (HPV) types 16 and 18 are strongly associated with penile cancer development. Other risk factors include phimosis (non-retractable foreskin), poor hygiene, chronic inflammation, and smoking.

riskFactors: Not being circumcised (increased phimosis and smegma risk)

left: Painless lump, ulcer, or wart-like growth on the penis
Persistent penile lesion that does not heal
Lesion on the glans or inner foreskin
right: Change in penile skin color or texture
Foul-smelling discharge from under the foreskin
Inguinal (groin) lymph node swelling
Bleeding from the lesion

medications.name: Acetaminophen with Codeine
medications.action: Provides moderate pain relief for post-surgical or cancer-related pain

medications.name: Cephalexin
medications.action: Prevents or treats wound infections following penile surgery by inhibiting bacterial cell wall synthesis

pearls: A persistent painless penile lesion that does not heal is the hallmark presentation - always report to provider
HPV vaccination is a primary prevention strategy for penile cancer - educate young males and parents
Phimosis and poor hygiene create an environment that increases penile cancer risk
Emotional support is critical - penile cancer treatment may involve partial or total penectomy with devastating psychological impact
diagnostics: Expect thorough physical examination of the penile lesion
Expect biopsy of suspicious lesions for tissue diagnosis
Monitor for inguinal lymph node enlargement
Expect imaging studies for staging (CT, MRI) if cancer confirmed
Expect referral to urology-oncology
management: Provide wound care for penile lesions as directed
Prepare patient for biopsy or surgical procedures as indicated
Provide post-surgical care (wound care, drain management, pain control)
Provide emotional support throughout diagnosis and treatment
Educate on the importance of HPV vaccination for prevention
nursingActions: Assess and document penile lesion characteristics (size, location, color, borders, drainage, odor)
Report any new or changing penile lesions to provider - especially persistent painless lesions
Assess inguinal lymph nodes for enlargement and report findings
Provide wound care to surgical sites as directed
Monitor post-surgical drain output and wound healing
Provide emotional support - diagnosis of penile cancer causes profound anxiety, fear, and body image concerns
Educate on HPV vaccination as a prevention strategy (ideally given before sexual debut)
Report signs of infection at wound sites (redness, swelling, warmth, purulent drainage, fever)
Assess for pain and administer analgesics as prescribed
Support body image and sexual health concerns with empathy and appropriate referrals

cryptorchidism-rpn.title: Cryptorchidism - RPN Fundamentals

cellular.title: Undescended Testis
cellular.content: Cryptorchidism (undescended testis) is a condition where one or both testes fail to descend from the abdomen into the scrotum during fetal development. During normal development, the testes form in the abdomen and gradually descend through the inguinal canal into the scrotum, typically completing this journey by the time of birth or within the first few months of life. The descent is guided by a structure called the gubernaculum and driven by hormones including testosterone and INSL3. When this process fails, the testis remains in the abdomen, inguinal canal, or high scrotal position. An undescended testis is exposed to higher body temperature than normal (the scrotum is 2-3 degrees cooler than body temperature), which damages the developing germ cells over time and increases the risk of infertility and testicular cancer later in life. Surgical correction (orchiopexy) is recommended between 6-12 months of age.

left: Empty or underdeveloped scrotum on one or both sides
Testis palpable in inguinal canal or not palpable at all
Asymmetric scrotal appearance
right: Scrotum appears flattened on affected side
Testis may be palpable in groin area during examination
Usually painless and discovered on routine examination

medications.name: Acetaminophen (Infant)
medications.action: Provides post-operative pain relief after orchiopexy by inhibiting central prostaglandin synthesis

medications.name: Cephalexin (Pediatric)
medications.action: Prevents post-surgical wound infection after orchiopexy

pearls: Prematurity is the biggest risk factor for cryptorchidism - always assess scrotal contents in premature infants
Orchiopexy should be performed between 6-12 months of age to minimize damage from body temperature exposure
An undescended testis increases the risk of testicular cancer later in life even after surgical correction
Parents need education that long-term follow-up is important for monitoring testicular growth and cancer risk
diagnostics: Expect physical examination of scrotum and inguinal area at birth and well-baby visits
Monitor for palpable vs non-palpable testis position
Expect referral to pediatric urology if testis not descended by 6 months
Expect ultrasound or MRI if testis is non-palpable on examination
Monitor contralateral (opposite) testis for normal position
management: Monitor testicular position at each well-baby visit
Educate parents on the condition and the importance of timely surgical correction
Prepare infant and family for orchiopexy procedure when indicated
Provide post-operative care after orchiopexy
Ensure follow-up for monitoring testicular position and growth
nursingActions: Assess scrotal contents during newborn examination and at each well-baby visit
Document testicular position (scrotal, inguinal, non-palpable) accurately
Educate parents that cryptorchidism is common, especially in premature infants
Explain that spontaneous descent may occur in the first 3-6 months but surgical correction is needed if it does not
Prepare family for orchiopexy procedure - explain it is a same-day surgery performed between 6-12 months of age
Provide post-orchiopexy care (pain management, wound care, activity restrictions)
Monitor surgical site for bleeding, swelling, and signs of infection
Report if the testis appears to have re-ascended after surgery (retractile vs re-ascent)
Educate parents on the importance of long-term follow-up for testicular growth and cancer screening

orchitis-rpn.title: Orchitis - RPN Fundamentals

cellular.title: Testicular Inflammation
cellular.content: Orchitis is inflammation of the testis, most commonly caused by viral or bacterial infection. Viral orchitis is most often associated with the mumps virus - mumps orchitis typically develops 4-8 days after parotid gland swelling and affects post-pubertal males. Bacterial orchitis usually occurs as an extension of epididymitis (epididymo-orchitis) caused by urinary tract pathogens or sexually transmitted organisms. The infection and resulting inflammation cause testicular swelling, pain, warmth, and erythema. Inflammation can damage the seminiferous tubules where sperm are produced, potentially leading to testicular atrophy and infertility. It is critical to differentiate orchitis from testicular torsion - torsion is a SURGICAL EMERGENCY where the spermatic cord twists, cutting off blood supply to the testis. Torsion presents with sudden onset severe pain, whereas orchitis typically has a more gradual onset with fever.

riskFactors: Mumps infection (especially in unvaccinated post-pubertal males)

left: Testicular swelling and tenderness (gradual onset)
Scrotal erythema and warmth
Fever (moderate to high)
right: Pain may radiate to groin or lower abdomen
Heavy or dragging sensation in the scrotum
Parotid gland swelling if mumps-related (preceding orchitis by 4-8 days)
Positive Prehn sign (pain relief with scrotal elevation)

medications.name: Doxycycline
medications.action: Inhibits bacterial protein synthesis, effective against Chlamydia and other organisms causing bacterial orchitis

medications.name: Ibuprofen
medications.action: Reduces inflammation and pain in the testis by inhibiting prostaglandin synthesis

pearls: CRITICAL: Sudden onset severe testicular pain is testicular TORSION until proven otherwise - this is a surgical emergency - report immediately
Orchitis has gradual onset with fever, while torsion has sudden onset without fever - but always report severe testicular pain urgently
Mumps orchitis occurs 4-8 days after parotid swelling in post-pubertal unvaccinated males
Scrotal elevation and ice application are key comfort measures - always wrap ice in cloth, never apply directly to skin
diagnostics: Expect testicular examination (swelling, tenderness, warmth)
Expect Doppler ultrasound to assess blood flow (differentiates orchitis from torsion)
Monitor temperature for fever
Expect urinalysis and urine culture
Expect STI testing if sexually transmitted cause suspected
Monitor for Prehn sign (elevation of the scrotum relieves pain in orchitis but not torsion)
management: Provide scrotal support (scrotal elevation with a rolled towel or athletic supporter)
Apply ice packs to the scrotum (wrapped in cloth - never directly on skin)
Administer prescribed analgesics for pain management
Administer antibiotics as ordered if bacterial cause identified
Encourage bed rest during acute phase
Monitor for signs of testicular torsion and report immediately
nursingActions: Assess and document testicular pain (onset, severity, unilateral vs bilateral, associated symptoms)
CRITICAL: Differentiate from testicular torsion - sudden onset severe unilateral pain with nausea/vomiting and absent cremasteric reflex is torsion until proven otherwise - report IMMEDIATELY
Apply scrotal support and elevation for comfort
Apply ice packs wrapped in cloth to scrotum as directed
Administer prescribed medications (antibiotics, analgesics) and monitor effectiveness
Monitor temperature and report fever to provider
Educate on the importance of completing the full antibiotic course
Assess for scrotal swelling progression and report worsening
Provide emotional support - testicular conditions can be distressing
Educate on mumps vaccination (MMR) as prevention for viral orchitis

prostatitis-rpn.title: Prostatitis - RPN Fundamentals

cellular.title: Prostatic Inflammation
cellular.content: Prostatitis is inflammation of the prostate gland, a walnut-sized organ located below the bladder that surrounds the urethra in males. The condition can be bacterial (acute or chronic) or non-bacterial (chronic pelvic pain syndrome). Acute bacterial prostatitis is caused by bacteria ascending from the urethra or bladder into the prostate gland, triggering an intense inflammatory response with swelling, pain, and systemic infection (fever, chills). The inflamed prostate swells and compresses the urethra, causing urinary symptoms such as dysuria, frequency, urgency, and potentially urinary retention. Chronic bacterial prostatitis involves recurrent or persistent bacterial infection of the prostate. Chronic pelvic pain syndrome (the most common form) involves prostatic pain and urinary symptoms without identifiable bacterial infection.

left: Dysuria (painful urination)
Perineal pain (between scrotum and rectum)
Urinary frequency and urgency
Fever and chills (acute bacterial)
right: Suprapubic or lower back pain
Pain with ejaculation
Difficulty starting or maintaining urinary stream
Malaise and myalgia (acute bacterial)

medications.name: Ciprofloxacin
medications.action: Penetrates prostatic tissue effectively to eliminate bacterial infection by inhibiting DNA gyrase

medications.name: Ibuprofen
medications.action: Reduces prostatic inflammation and provides pain relief by inhibiting prostaglandin synthesis

pearls: Acute bacterial prostatitis with urinary retention is an emergency - report inability to void immediately
Antibiotic course for prostatitis is LONG (4-6 weeks) - educate patient on the importance of completing the full course even if feeling better
Sitz baths provide effective comfort for perineal pain - 15-20 minutes in warm water, 2-3 times daily
Avoid caffeine, alcohol, and spicy foods during treatment as they can irritate the prostate and worsen symptoms
diagnostics: Expect thorough symptom assessment (dysuria, frequency, urgency, perineal pain)
Monitor temperature for fever (especially in acute bacterial prostatitis)
Expect urinalysis and urine culture
Expect digital rectal examination by provider (prostate will be tender and boggy in acute infection)
Expect prostatic secretion culture in chronic cases
Monitor for urinary retention
management: Administer prescribed antibiotics as ordered - full course must be completed (4-6 weeks for acute bacterial)
Administer prescribed analgesics and anti-inflammatory medications
Encourage sitz baths for perineal comfort (warm water, 15-20 minutes, 2-3 times daily)
Encourage adequate hydration to promote urinary flushing
Monitor for urinary retention and report inability to void
nursingActions: Assess urinary symptoms (dysuria, frequency, urgency, hesitancy, incomplete emptying)
Monitor temperature and report fever to provider
Administer antibiotics as prescribed and on schedule - educate on completing the FULL course (often 4-6 weeks)
Educate on sitz bath technique for perineal pain relief (warm water, 15-20 minutes)
Monitor for urinary retention (inability to void, suprapubic distension) and report to provider immediately
Encourage adequate fluid intake (2-3 liters per day unless contraindicated)
Assess pain and administer prescribed analgesics
Educate to avoid caffeine, alcohol, and spicy foods which can worsen symptoms
Report signs of worsening infection (increasing fever, chills, urinary retention) to provider
Provide emotional support - chronic prostatitis significantly impacts quality of life

delayed-puberty-np.title: Delayed Puberty: Molecular & Prescriptive Management

cellular.title: GnRH Pulse Generator & KNDy Neuron Regulation
cellular.content:
Puberty is initiated by reactivation of the hypothalamic GnRH pulse generator after a quiescent childhood period. Kisspeptin neurons in the arcuate nucleus (KNDy neurons  -  Kisspeptin/Neurokinin B/Dynorphin) serve as the master regulators of GnRH pulsatility. Kisspeptin binds to GPR54 (KISS1R) on GnRH neurons, stimulating pulsatile GnRH secretion into the hypophyseal portal system. GnRH activates pituitary gonadotrophs to release LH and FSH, which drive gonadal steroidogenesis and gametogenesis.

Delayed puberty is defined as the absence of breast development (thelarche) by age 13 in females or testicular enlargement (>4 mL) by age 14 in males. Constitutional delay of growth and puberty (CDGP) is the most common cause, representing a functional delay in GnRH pulse generator reactivation with an intact HPG axis. Pathological causes include hypogonadotropic hypogonadism (central deficiency) and hypergonadotropic hypogonadism (primary gonadal failure).

Hypogonadotropic hypogonadism results from GnRH deficiency due to genetic mutations in KAL1 (Kallmann syndrome with anosmia  -  disrupted olfactory bulb and GnRH neuron migration), FGFR1, PROKR2, GnRHR, or KISS1R genes. Acquired causes include hypothalamic-pituitary tumors (craniopharyngioma, prolactinoma), infiltrative disorders, radiation injury, and functional suppression from chronic illness, malnutrition, or excessive exercise (functional hypothalamic amenorrhea).

Hypergonadotropic hypogonadism reflects primary gonadal failure with elevated FSH/LH due to loss of negative feedback. Causes include Turner syndrome (45,X  -  streak gonads), Klinefelter syndrome (47,XXY  -  testicular dysgenesis), autoimmune oophoritis, gonadal dysgenesis, and gonadotoxic chemotherapy/radiation.

riskFactors: Chronic systemic illness (Crohn's disease, celiac disease, cystic fibrosis)
Congenital GnRH deficiency syndromes (KAL1, FGFR1 mutations)

left: No thelarche by age 13 (females)
No testicular enlargement by age 14 (males)
Absent growth spurt (growth velocity < 4 cm/year)
Delayed bone age (>2 SD below chronological age)
right: Anosmia or hyposmia (Kallmann syndrome)
Eunuchoid proportions (arm span > height by >5 cm)
Micropenis or cryptorchidism in males
Primary amenorrhea in females >15 years

medications.name: Transdermal Estradiol
medications.action: Binds estrogen receptors alpha and beta, inducing secondary sexual characteristics, epiphyseal maturation, and endometrial proliferation through genomic transcription activation

medications.name: Testosterone Enanthate
medications.action: Binds androgen receptors, inducing virilization including genital growth, muscle mass, deepening voice, and bone mineral density accrual via aromatization to estradiol for epiphyseal fusion

pearls: Constitutional delay is a diagnosis of exclusion  -  always rule out pathological causes before watchful waiting
Kallmann syndrome = hypogonadotropic hypogonadism + anosmia (failed GnRH neuron migration from olfactory placode)
Bone age is the single most useful initial test  -  if significantly delayed with prepubertal gonadotropins, constitutional delay is most likely
Turner syndrome patients need cardiac screening (bicuspid aortic valve, coarctation) and renal ultrasound in addition to hormone replacement
Fertility potential differs by etiology  -  constitutional delay has excellent prognosis, while primary gonadal failure requires donor gametes
diagnostics: Order basal LH, FSH, estradiol (females), testosterone (males) to classify hypo- vs hypergonadotropic
Order bone age radiograph (left hand/wrist)  -  delayed bone age suggests constitutional delay or hypogonadotropism
Order GnRH stimulation test (100 mcg IV)  -  pubertal LH response >5 IU/L suggests intact pituitary reserve
Order karyotype for suspected Turner or Klinefelter syndrome
Order pelvic ultrasound (females) to assess uterine and ovarian morphology
Order brain MRI with pituitary protocol to rule out CNS pathology (craniopharyngioma, pituitary hypoplasia)
Order prolactin level to exclude prolactinoma
Order thyroid function tests and celiac panel to exclude chronic disease
Order IGF-1 and growth hormone stimulation if growth hormone deficiency suspected
Consider genetic testing panel for isolated hypogonadotropic hypogonadism genes (KISS1R, GnRHR, KAL1, FGFR1, PROKR2)
management: Prescribe low-dose estradiol for females (transdermal 6.25-12.5 mcg/day or oral 0.25-0.5 mg daily) with gradual dose escalation over 2-3 years to mimic physiologic puberty
Add cyclic progesterone (medroxyprogesterone 5-10 mg for 10-12 days/month) once breakthrough bleeding occurs or after 2 years of estrogen
Prescribe testosterone for males (testosterone enanthate 50 mg IM monthly, increasing to 100-200 mg every 2-4 weeks over 2-3 years)
Prescribe pulsatile GnRH pump therapy for fertility induction in hypogonadotropic hypogonadism
Prescribe gonadotropin therapy (hCG + FSH) for fertility when GnRH pump unavailable
Refer for genetic counseling if chromosomal or genetic etiology identified
Monitor bone density with DEXA scan at baseline and during treatment
Coordinate with pediatric endocrinology for ongoing dose titration
Address psychosocial impact with mental health referral as needed
Manage underlying conditions (nutritional rehabilitation, disease-specific treatment)
nursingActions: Monitor Tanner staging progression at each visit to assess treatment response
Monitor growth velocity and plot on growth charts (target catch-up growth)
Assess bone age serially to track skeletal maturation
Monitor for estrogen side effects (breast tenderness, nausea, headache)
Monitor for testosterone side effects (acne, mood changes, erythrocytosis)
Educate patient and family on expected timeline of pubertal development (2-3 year induction)
Screen for psychosocial distress (peer comparison, body image, bullying)
Assess compliance with hormone replacement therapy
Monitor liver function with oral estrogen therapy
Evaluate for adrenal crisis signs if autoimmune polyendocrinopathy suspected

precocious-puberty-np.title: Precocious Puberty: Molecular Mechanisms & GnRH Agonist Therapy

cellular.title: Central vs Peripheral: Molecular Differentiation
cellular.content:
Precocious puberty is defined as the onset of secondary sexual characteristics before age 8 in females or age 9 in males. It is classified as central (GnRH-dependent, gonadotropin-mediated) or peripheral (GnRH-independent, autonomous sex steroid production).

Central precocious puberty (CPP) results from premature activation of the hypothalamic GnRH pulse generator. The most common cause in girls is idiopathic, while in boys CNS pathology is more frequently identified. Hypothalamic hamartomas are the most common identifiable CNS lesion  -  these ectopic masses contain GnRH-secreting neurons that function autonomously. Other causes include CNS tumors (gliomas, astrocytomas), hydrocephalus, post-CNS infection, and neurofibromatosis type 1. At the molecular level, gain-of-function mutations in KISS1 or KISS1R (GPR54) and loss-of-function mutations in MKRN3 (makorin ring finger protein 3, an inhibitor of GnRH secretion) have been identified in familial CPP.

Peripheral precocious puberty (PPP) involves sex steroid production independent of GnRH and gonadotropins. McCune-Albright syndrome results from postzygotic activating mutations in GNAS1, causing constitutive activation of the Gsα subunit and autonomous cAMP production in affected tissues  -  leading to autonomous ovarian follicle activation, café-au-lait macules, and polyostotic fibrous dysplasia. Other causes include congenital adrenal hyperplasia (21-hydroxylase deficiency causing excess adrenal androgens), sex-steroid-secreting tumors (granulosa cell, Leydig cell, adrenal), familial male-limited precocious puberty (testotoxicosis from activating LH receptor mutations), and exogenous sex steroid exposure.

riskFactors: Female sex (idiopathic CPP is 10x more common in girls)
Exogenous sex steroid exposure (topical testosterone, oral contraceptives)
Family history of precocious puberty (MKRN3 mutations)

left: Breast development before age 8 (girls)
Testicular enlargement before age 9 (boys)
Accelerated linear growth velocity
Advanced bone age (skeletal maturation ahead of chronological age)
right: Pubic/axillary hair development
Menarche before age 10
Acne and body odor
Café-au-lait macules with irregular borders (McCune-Albright)

medications.name: Leuprolide Depot (Lupron Depot-Ped)
medications.action: Continuous GnRH receptor stimulation causes initial gonadotropin flare followed by receptor downregulation and desensitization, suppressing LH and FSH release and halting gonadal steroid production

medications.name: Histrelin Implant (Supprelin LA)
medications.action: Provides continuous release of histrelin acetate (50 mcg/day) causing sustained GnRH receptor downregulation and gonadotropin suppression for 12 months

pearls: All boys with CPP require brain MRI  -  idiopathic CPP is rare in males (up to 60% have identifiable CNS pathology)
Girls with CPP onset before age 6 have higher rates of CNS pathology and should undergo brain MRI
MKRN3 is an imprinted gene (paternally expressed)  -  mutations cause familial CPP with autosomal dominant inheritance with paternal transmission only
McCune-Albright syndrome is always sporadic (lethal if germline)  -  caused by postzygotic mosaic activating GNAS1 mutations
GnRH agonist therapy preserves adult height potential by slowing bone age advancement relative to chronological age
Monitor predicted adult height  -  if near-adult height is acceptable, GnRH agonist may be discontinued to allow puberty to resume
diagnostics: Order basal LH, FSH, estradiol/testosterone  -  pubertal LH >0.3 IU/L (immunochemiluminometric assay) suggests CPP
Order GnRH stimulation test (leuprolide 20 mcg/kg SC)  -  peak LH >5 IU/L with LH/FSH ratio >1 confirms CPP
Order bone age radiograph  -  advanced bone age (>2 SD above chronological age) indicates significant sex steroid exposure
Order brain MRI with contrast for all boys with CPP and girls with CPP onset <6 years (higher rate of CNS pathology)
Order pelvic ultrasound (females)  -  pubertal uterine length >3.5 cm, bilateral follicle development
Order 17-hydroxyprogesterone to rule out congenital adrenal hyperplasia
Order DHEA-S and androstenedione for adrenal androgen excess
Order β-hCG if gonadotropin-independent puberty in males (hCG-secreting germ cell tumor)
Order adrenal/gonadal imaging if peripheral source suspected
Consider genetic testing (MKRN3, KISS1R, GNAS1) for familial or syndromic presentations
management: Prescribe GnRH agonist depot therapy for CPP: Leuprolide depot 11.25 mg IM every 3 months or 30 mg IM every 6 months (or 45 mg every 6 months)
Alternative: Histrelin subcutaneous implant (Supprelin LA)  -  50 mg, provides continuous GnRH agonist delivery for 12 months
Monitor treatment efficacy with suppressed LH (<1 IU/L after GnRH stimulation), regression of pubertal signs, and deceleration of bone age advancement
Prescribe aromatase inhibitors (letrozole, anastrozole) for McCune-Albright syndrome or familial male-limited precocious puberty (off-label)
Prescribe ketoconazole or anti-androgens for peripheral causes when surgery not feasible
Treat underlying etiology (neurosurgical resection for CNS tumors, glucocorticoid replacement for CAH)
Predict adult height using Bayley-Pinneau tables from bone age
Refer to pediatric endocrinology for all cases
Refer for psychological support  -  address psychosocial impact of early development
Discontinue GnRH agonist therapy at age-appropriate puberty onset (typically age 11-12 in girls) and monitor for spontaneous pubertal progression
nursingActions: Assess Tanner staging at each visit to monitor suppression or progression
Monitor height velocity  -  should decelerate to prepubertal rate on GnRH agonist therapy
Obtain bone age radiographs every 6-12 months to assess skeletal maturation rate
Monitor for GnRH agonist side effects (initial pubertal flare in first 2-4 weeks, injection site reactions, headache)
Educate family that initial hormonal flare may cause transient worsening of pubertal signs before suppression occurs
Monitor for sterile abscess at depot injection sites
Assess histrelin implant site for complications (migration, breakage, infection)
Coordinate with pediatric endocrinology for dose adjustments
Screen for psychological distress and behavioral changes
Educate families on expected treatment duration and timeline for discontinuation

dysmenorrhea-np.title: Dysmenorrhea: COX-2 Pathways & Advanced Prescribing

cellular.title: Prostaglandin Biosynthesis & Myometrial Ischemia
cellular.content:
Dysmenorrhea is classified as primary (no underlying pelvic pathology) or secondary (identifiable organic cause such as endometriosis, adenomyosis, or fibroids).

Primary dysmenorrhea results from excessive prostaglandin production by the secretory endometrium at the time of menstruation. Following progesterone withdrawal at the end of the luteal phase, membrane phospholipids are cleaved by phospholipase A2 (PLA2) to release arachidonic acid. Arachidonic acid is then converted by cyclooxygenase-2 (COX-2) to prostaglandin H2 (PGH2), which is subsequently converted to prostaglandin F2α (PGF2α) and prostaglandin E2 (PGE2) by tissue-specific synthases.

PGF2α is the primary mediator of dysmenorrhea. It causes potent myometrial smooth muscle hypercontractility, increasing intrauterine pressure above 150-180 mmHg (resting: 10 mmHg) and exceeding arterial perfusion pressure. This produces myometrial ischemia with anaerobic metabolism and lactic acid accumulation, activating pain-sensitive nerve fibers. PGF2α also sensitizes type C pain afferents to bradykinin and histamine. PGE2 contributes through vasodilation, edema, and further nociceptor sensitization. Vasopressin (AVP) independently increases myometrial contractility via V1a receptors and exacerbates uterine ischemia through vasoconstriction of uterine arterioles.

Secondary dysmenorrhea involves structural or inflammatory pathology. Endometriosis causes ectopic endometrial implants that produce prostaglandins, cytokines (IL-1β, IL-6, TNF-α), and nerve growth factor, creating peripheral and central sensitization. Adenomyosis involves ectopic endometrial glands within the myometrium, disrupting normal contractile patterns and causing diffuse uterine enlargement with heavy, painful menses.

riskFactors: Psychological stress (increases cortisol and alters pain perception)

left: Cramping suprapubic pain starting with menses
Pain radiating to lower back and thighs
Pain duration 48-72 hours
Nausea, vomiting, diarrhea (systemic prostaglandin effects)
right: Headache and fatigue (prostaglandin-mediated)
Primary: onset within 1-2 years of menarche
Secondary: progressive worsening, new onset >25 years
Dyspareunia (endometriosis), menorrhagia (adenomyosis)

medications.name: Naproxen Sodium
medications.action: Inhibits both COX-1 and COX-2 enzymes, blocking the conversion of arachidonic acid to prostaglandin H2, thereby reducing PGF2α and PGE2 synthesis in the endometrium. Also reduces peripheral nociceptor sensitization

medications.name: Levonorgestrel IUD (Mirena)
medications.action: Local progestin release causes endometrial decidualization and atrophy, reducing endometrial prostaglandin production by 50-75%. Suppresses endometrial glandular activity and reduces menstrual flow volume. In adenomyosis, reduces ectopic endometrial gland activity within the myometrium

pearls: NSAID timing is critical  -  pre-emptive dosing (1-2 days before menses) blocks prostaglandin synthesis before cascade amplification, providing superior pain relief compared to reactive dosing
COX-2 selective inhibitors (celecoxib) are equally effective for dysmenorrhea with fewer GI side effects but carry cardiovascular risk
Levonorgestrel IUD is the most effective long-term treatment for secondary dysmenorrhea from endometriosis and adenomyosis
Failure of NSAIDs + hormonal contraceptives after 3-6 months should prompt investigation for secondary causes
Vasopressin levels are elevated in women with primary dysmenorrhea  -  V1a receptor antagonists are an emerging therapeutic target
diagnostics: Order pelvic ultrasound (transvaginal preferred) to evaluate for secondary causes (endometriomas, adenomyosis, fibroids)
Order MRI pelvis if adenomyosis suspected (junctional zone thickness >12 mm is diagnostic)
Order CBC to assess for anemia from heavy menstrual bleeding
Order ESR/CRP if infection or inflammatory process suspected
Order CA-125 if endometriosis or adenomyosis suspected (may be mildly elevated)
Refer for diagnostic laparoscopy if empirical treatment fails and endometriosis is suspected
Order STI screening if secondary dysmenorrhea with risk factors for PID
Assess pain diary for cyclical pattern, onset, duration, and severity using validated scales (VAS, NRS)
management: Prescribe NSAIDs as first-line: Naproxen 500 mg loading dose then 250 mg every 6-8 hours, or Ibuprofen 400-800 mg every 6-8 hours  -  initiate 1-2 days before expected menses onset for maximum efficacy
Prescribe combined hormonal contraceptives (continuous or extended cycle) as second-line or co-therapy  -  suppress ovulation and reduce endometrial prostaglandin production

Prescribe levonorgestrel IUD (52 mg Mirena) for secondary dysmenorrhea and patients desiring long-acting contraception  -  causes endometrial decidualization and atrophy, reducing prostaglandin production by 50-75%

Prescribe GnRH agonist (leuprolide depot 3.75 mg IM monthly) for severe endometriosis-related dysmenorrhea refractory to first-line therapy  -  add-back therapy with norethindrone 5 mg/day to mitigate bone loss
Consider vasopressin V1a receptor antagonists (investigational) for dysmenorrhea with prominent vasopressin-mediated component
Prescribe progestin-only therapy (norethindrone, medroxyprogesterone) for patients with contraindications to estrogen
Refer for surgical evaluation (laparoscopic excision) if endometriosis confirmed and medical management fails
Refer for adenomyomectomy or hysterectomy if adenomyosis refractory to medical therapy in completed childbearing
nursingActions: Educate on NSAID timing  -  maximum efficacy when started 1-2 days before menses onset (pre-emptive prostaglandin inhibition)
Monitor for NSAID gastrointestinal side effects (prescribe PPI cover if prolonged use)
Assess pain response using standardized scales at follow-up visits
Educate on levonorgestrel IUD  -  irregular bleeding common in first 3-6 months, amenorrhea develops in 20-50%
Monitor for GnRH agonist menopausal side effects (hot flashes, vaginal dryness, mood changes, bone density loss)
Counsel on non-pharmacological adjuncts (heat therapy, TENS, exercise, omega-3 fatty acids)
Assess for red flags suggesting secondary causes (progressive worsening, new onset after age 25, abnormal bleeding patterns)
Coordinate referral to gynecology if empirical therapy fails after 3-6 months

aub-np.title: Abnormal Uterine Bleeding: PALM-COEIN & Advanced Management

cellular.title: Endometrial Hemostasis & PALM-COEIN Molecular Classification
cellular.content:
Abnormal uterine bleeding (AUB) is classified using the FIGO PALM-COEIN system, which divides causes into structural (PALM: Polyp, Adenomyosis, Leiomyoma, Malignancy/hyperplasia) and non-structural (COEIN: Coagulopathy, Ovulatory dysfunction, Endometrial, Iatrogenic, Not yet classified).

Normal endometrial hemostasis depends on a precise balance between vasoconstriction, platelet aggregation, coagulation, and fibrinolysis. During menstruation, spiral arteriolar vasoconstriction limits blood loss, while tissue factor (TF) activates the coagulation cascade locally. Endometrial fibrinolytic activity (tissue plasminogen activator, tPA) is regulated to allow orderly tissue shedding. The endometrium regenerates from the basalis layer under estrogen stimulation.

In AUB-E (endometrial causes), molecular defects in local hemostatic mechanisms include increased endometrial fibrinolysis (elevated tPA and reduced PAI-1), deficient endothelin-1 (ET-1) mediated vasoconstriction, and altered prostaglandin ratios (increased PGE2 and prostacyclin relative to PGF2α and thromboxane A2). This shifts the hemostatic balance toward excessive bleeding.

In AUB-O (ovulatory dysfunction), anovulation results in unopposed estrogen exposure. Without progesterone-mediated secretory transformation, the endometrium undergoes prolonged proliferation, becoming fragile and vascular. Disordered shedding produces irregular, heavy, and prolonged bleeding. Chronic unopposed estrogen also increases the risk of endometrial hyperplasia (with or without atypia) and progression to endometrial carcinoma.

AUB-C (coagulopathy) accounts for approximately 13% of heavy menstrual bleeding. Von Willebrand disease is the most common inherited bleeding disorder causing AUB, followed by platelet function defects and coagulation factor deficiencies.

riskFactors: Obesity (peripheral aromatization of androgens to estrogen)
Von Willebrand disease or inherited coagulopathies
Extreme weight loss or excessive exercise (hypothalamic amenorrhea with withdrawal bleeding)

left: Menstrual periods >7 days duration
Soaking through pad/tampon every 1-2 hours
Passage of clots >2.5 cm diameter
Intermenstrual bleeding
right: Iron-deficiency anemia (fatigue, pallor, tachycardia)
Orthostatic hypotension (acute hemorrhage)
Postcoital bleeding (cervical pathology)
Postmenopausal bleeding (endometrial pathology until proven otherwise)

medications.name: Tranexamic Acid
medications.action: Competitively inhibits plasminogen activation by binding to lysine-binding sites on plasminogen, preventing its conversion to plasmin. This stabilizes fibrin clots within the endometrium, reducing menstrual blood loss by inhibiting local fibrinolysis without systemic prothrombotic effects at therapeutic doses

medications.name: Levonorgestrel IUD (52 mg)
medications.action: Local endometrial progestin effect causes decidualization, glandular atrophy, and stromal pseudodecidual transformation. Reduces endometrial thickness, vascular density, and prostaglandin production. Suppresses endometrial VEGF expression, reducing angiogenesis

pearls: PALM-COEIN is the FIGO standard classification  -  always systematically evaluate structural and non-structural causes
Endometrial biopsy is mandatory for all women >45 with AUB and those <45 with risk factors for endometrial hyperplasia
Von Willebrand disease screening should be performed in all adolescents presenting with heavy menstrual bleeding since menarche  -  prevalence is ~13% in this population
Levonorgestrel IUD is superior to oral progestins, tranexamic acid, and NSAIDs for reducing menstrual blood loss
Acute hemorrhagic AUB management: stabilize hemodynamics first, then administer IV conjugated estrogen or high-dose oral progestin  -  follow with transition to maintenance hormonal therapy
Postmenopausal bleeding is endometrial cancer until proven otherwise  -  endometrial thickness >4 mm on TVUS warrants biopsy
diagnostics: Order CBC with iron studies (ferritin, TIBC) to assess severity of blood loss anemia
Order β-hCG to exclude pregnancy-related bleeding
Order TSH to exclude thyroid dysfunction
Order coagulation studies (PT, aPTT, VWF antigen, VWF activity, factor VIII)  -  screen for coagulopathy in all adolescents and adults with heavy menstrual bleeding since menarche
Order transvaginal ultrasound as first-line imaging for structural evaluation
Order saline infusion sonohysterography (SIS) for superior delineation of intracavitary lesions (polyps, submucosal fibroids)
Order endometrial biopsy for all women >45 years with AUB, or <45 years with risk factors for endometrial hyperplasia (obesity, chronic anovulation, unopposed estrogen, tamoxifen)
Order MRI pelvis for adenomyosis confirmation or fibroid mapping prior to surgery
Order prolactin, DHEA-S, testosterone if PCOS or adrenal pathology suspected
Consider hysteroscopy for both diagnostic and therapeutic evaluation of intracavitary pathology
management: Prescribe tranexamic acid 1300 mg PO TID for up to 5 days during menses for AUB-E (reduces blood loss by 40-50%)
Prescribe combined oral contraceptive (monophasic, 30-35 mcg ethinyl estradiol) for ovulatory dysfunction  -  regulates cycles and reduces endometrial proliferation
Prescribe levonorgestrel IUD (52 mg) as first-line long-term therapy  -  superior to oral progestins for reducing menstrual blood loss (71-95% reduction)
Prescribe high-dose oral progestin (medroxyprogesterone 20 mg TID for 7 days, or norethindrone 5 mg TID for 7 days) for acute heavy bleeding
Prescribe conjugated equine estrogen 25 mg IV every 4-6 hours for acute hemorrhagic AUB unresponsive to progestins (follow with progestin)
Prescribe cyclic progestin therapy (medroxyprogesterone 10 mg for 12-14 days/month) for anovulatory bleeding when contraception not desired
Prescribe GnRH agonist (leuprolide) or GnRH antagonist (elagolix) for fibroid-related AUB as bridge to surgery
Refer for hysteroscopic polypectomy or myomectomy for structural lesions
Refer for endometrial ablation if completed childbearing and medical management fails
Refer for hysterectomy as definitive treatment for refractory AUB with completed childbearing
Prescribe desmopressin nasal spray for Von Willebrand disease-related AUB
nursingActions: Assess menstrual bleeding patterns using pictorial blood loss assessment chart (PBAC)  -  score >100 indicates heavy menstrual bleeding
Monitor hemoglobin, hematocrit, and iron stores  -  initiate IV iron (ferric carboxymaltose) if ferritin <30 or Hb <10 with oral intolerance
Educate on tranexamic acid  -  take only during heavy bleeding days, do not combine with hormonal contraceptives containing estrogen (increased VTE risk per some guidelines)
Monitor for side effects of high-dose progestin therapy (bloating, mood changes, breakthrough bleeding)
Assess for signs of hemodynamic instability in acute hemorrhagic AUB (tachycardia, orthostatic hypotension)
Educate on endometrial biopsy indications and procedure expectations
Coordinate gynecology referral for surgical evaluation when medical management fails
Screen all adolescents presenting with heavy menstrual bleeding for underlying coagulopathy

pcos-management-np.title: PCOS: Insulin-Androgen Cascade & Prescriptive Management

cellular.title: Insulin/IGF-1/SHBG/Androgen Molecular Cascade
cellular.content:
Polycystic ovary syndrome is a complex endocrine disorder diagnosed using the Rotterdam criteria (2 of 3: oligo/anovulation, clinical or biochemical hyperandrogenism, polycystic ovarian morphology on ultrasound with exclusion of other etiologies).

The molecular pathogenesis centers on a self-amplifying insulin-androgen feedback loop. Insulin resistance (present in 50-70% of PCOS patients regardless of BMI) leads to compensatory hyperinsulinemia. Elevated insulin acts synergistically with LH on ovarian theca cells to upregulate steroidogenic enzymes  -  specifically CYP17A1 (17α-hydroxylase/17,20-lyase)  -  increasing androstenedione and testosterone production. Insulin simultaneously suppresses hepatic production of sex hormone-binding globulin (SHBG), increasing free (bioavailable) testosterone levels. Insulin also stimulates adrenal androgen production and enhances IGF-1 bioactivity by suppressing IGF-binding proteins (IGFBPs).

At the ovarian level, excess androgens disrupt folliculogenesis. Granulosa cells in developing follicles are unable to aromatize sufficient androgens to estrogens (impaired CYP19A1/aromatase activity), leading to follicular arrest at 2-9 mm. Multiple arrested antral follicles give the classic polycystic ovarian morphology (≥12 follicles per ovary or ovarian volume >10 mL). Chronic anovulation leads to absence of corpus luteum formation, resulting in progesterone deficiency and unopposed estrogen exposure on the endometrium  -  increasing the risk of endometrial hyperplasia (2.7-fold) and endometrial cancer (2.7-4-fold).

Metabolic consequences include impaired glucose tolerance (30-40% by age 40), type 2 diabetes (10% by age 40), dyslipidemia (elevated triglycerides, low HDL, increased small dense LDL), non-alcoholic fatty liver disease, and accelerated atherosclerosis. Cardiovascular risk is estimated at 2-fold increased compared to age-matched controls.

riskFactors: Intrauterine growth restriction with rapid postnatal catch-up growth
Valproic acid use (increases testosterone, causes weight gain)
Ethnic predisposition (South Asian, Hispanic, Indigenous populations)

left: Oligomenorrhea or amenorrhea
Clinical hyperandrogenism (hirsutism, acne, alopecia)
Central obesity and insulin resistance
Acanthosis nigricans
right: Polycystic ovarian morphology on ultrasound
Infertility (anovulatory)
Mood disturbances (depression, anxiety)
Obstructive sleep apnea symptoms

medications.name: Letrozole
medications.action: Inhibits CYP19A1 (aromatase) enzyme, blocking the conversion of androgens to estrogens. The resulting transient decrease in estrogen releases the hypothalamus from negative feedback, increasing GnRH pulse frequency and FSH secretion, promoting follicular development and ovulation

medications.name: Spironolactone
medications.action: Competitive antagonist at the androgen receptor, blocking testosterone and DHT binding. Also inhibits 5α-reductase (reduces conversion of testosterone to the more potent DHT) and inhibits adrenal and ovarian steroidogenesis at higher doses

pearls: Rotterdam criteria require exclusion of other etiologies (thyroid dysfunction, CAH, Cushing syndrome, androgen-secreting tumors, hyperprolactinemia) before diagnosing PCOS
Letrozole is now first-line for ovulation induction in PCOS  -  NICHD trial showed 27.5% live birth rate vs 19.1% for clomiphene
PCOS patients have 2.7-fold increased risk of endometrial cancer due to chronic unopposed estrogen  -  ensure progestin withdrawal every 1-3 months if not on OCP
Insulin resistance drives the pathology regardless of BMI  -  lean PCOS patients also benefit from metformin and lifestyle modifications
Anti-Müllerian hormone (AMH) correlates with antral follicle count and may replace ultrasound for diagnosis in some guidelines
Cardiovascular risk assessment should begin at diagnosis  -  PCOS is associated with accelerated atherosclerosis, even in young women
diagnostics: Order total and free testosterone (elevated free testosterone is the most sensitive biochemical marker)
Order DHEA-S (mildly elevated in 20-30%  -  adrenal component)
Order 17-hydroxyprogesterone (early morning, follicular phase) to exclude non-classic congenital adrenal hyperplasia (>200 ng/dL warrants ACTH stimulation test)
Order SHBG (characteristically low due to hyperinsulinemia)
Order fasting glucose with fasting insulin (calculate HOMA-IR >2.5 suggests insulin resistance)
Order 2-hour 75g oral glucose tolerance test (preferred over fasting glucose  -  detects impaired glucose tolerance earlier)
Order lipid panel (screen for metabolic syndrome)
Order transvaginal ultrasound (≥12 follicles of 2-9 mm per ovary or ovarian volume >10 mL using updated Rotterdam criteria)
Order TSH and prolactin to exclude thyroid dysfunction and hyperprolactinemia
Order AMH (anti-Müllerian hormone)  -  elevated in PCOS and may supplement ultrasound for diagnosis
Order liver function tests if considering hepatically-metabolized medications
Screen for obstructive sleep apnea (increased prevalence in PCOS, especially with obesity)
Screen for depression and anxiety (PHQ-9, GAD-7)  -  3-fold increased prevalence
management: Prescribe combined oral contraceptive (low androgenic progestin preferred  -  norgestimate, desogestrel, drospirenone) as first-line for menstrual regulation and anti-androgen therapy
Prescribe metformin 500 mg titrated to 1500-2000 mg/day for insulin resistance (extended-release formulation reduces GI side effects)
Prescribe spironolactone 50-200 mg/day for hirsutism and acne (wait 6 months for clinical effect on hirsutism)
Prescribe letrozole 2.5-7.5 mg/day (cycle days 3-7) as first-line for ovulation induction (superior to clomiphene per NICHD trial)
Prescribe clomiphene citrate 50-150 mg/day (cycle days 3-7) as alternative for ovulation induction
Prescribe topical eflornithine cream for facial hirsutism (adjunct to systemic therapy)
Prescribe GLP-1 receptor agonists (liraglutide, semaglutide) for obese PCOS patients for weight loss and insulin sensitization (emerging evidence)
Prescribe inositol (myo-inositol 4g/day) as adjunct insulin sensitizer (lower evidence grade but favorable safety profile)
Refer for bariatric surgery evaluation if BMI >40 or BMI >35 with metabolic comorbidities
Order endometrial ultrasound surveillance every 6-12 months if chronic anovulation without progestin protection  -  endometrial biopsy if thickness >7 mm
Counsel on fertility options: letrozole > clomiphene > gonadotropins > IVF (stepwise approach)
Coordinate cardiovascular risk stratification (10-year ASCVD risk calculator, annual metabolic screening)
nursingActions: Monitor metabolic panel annually (fasting glucose or OGTT, lipid panel, blood pressure)
Monitor HbA1c every 6-12 months in patients with impaired glucose tolerance or on metformin
Assess Ferriman-Gallwey score for hirsutism at baseline and follow-up (score ≥8 = hirsutism in most populations)
Monitor liver function and potassium with spironolactone therapy
Educate on reliable contraception during spironolactone use (teratogenic  -  feminization of male fetus)
Monitor for metformin GI side effects and vitamin B12 deficiency with long-term use
Educate on lifestyle modifications (150 min/week moderate exercise, Mediterranean or DASH diet, 5-10% weight loss target)
Screen for depression and eating disorders at each visit
Educate on ovulation induction monitoring requirements (follicle tracking to prevent ovarian hyperstimulation)
Coordinate multidisciplinary care (endocrinology, dermatology, psychology, reproductive endocrinology)

pid-np.title: Pelvic Inflammatory Disease: CDC Guidelines & TOA Management

cellular.title: Ascending Polymicrobial Infection & Tubal Pathology
cellular.content:
Pelvic inflammatory disease (PID) is an ascending polymicrobial infection of the upper female genital tract, involving the endometrium (endometritis), fallopian tubes (salpingitis), ovaries (oophoritis), and pelvic peritoneum (peritonitis). The primary causative organisms are Neisseria gonorrhoeae and Chlamydia trachomatis, though the infection is typically polymicrobial, involving anaerobes (Bacteroides, Prevotella, Peptostreptococcus), facultative gram-negative rods (E. coli), genital mycoplasmas (M. genitalium, M. hominis), and Gardnerella vaginalis.

The pathogenesis involves bacterial ascent from the lower genital tract through the cervical os into the endometrial cavity and fallopian tubes. This process is facilitated by cervical mucus disruption during menstruation, endocervical instrumentation, or bacterial mucinase production. N. gonorrhoeae directly damages ciliated tubal epithelium through lipooligosaccharide (LOS)-mediated inflammatory cascades, activating complement, recruiting neutrophils, and triggering TNF-α and IL-1β release. C. trachomatis induces a delayed-type hypersensitivity response through chlamydial heat shock protein 60 (cHSP60), which shares molecular homology with human HSP60  -  this molecular mimicry drives chronic inflammatory damage and autoimmune tubal destruction even after the organism is cleared.

Fitz-Hugh-Curtis syndrome occurs when infection spreads from the fallopian tubes to the liver capsule (Glisson capsule) via peritoneal or lymphatic routes, causing perihepatitis with characteristic violin-string adhesions between the liver and anterior abdominal wall. It presents with right upper quadrant pain that may mimic cholecystitis.

Tubo-ovarian abscess (TOA) develops when fallopian tube infection leads to tubal obstruction (pyosalpinx), extension to the ovary, and walled-off collection of purulent material involving the tube, ovary, and adjacent structures. TOAs contain a mixed aerobic-anaerobic bacterial community within a biofilm matrix. Rupture of a TOA is a surgical emergency causing peritonitis and sepsis.

riskFactors: History of previous PID (increases risk of recurrence and sequelae)
Recent uterine instrumentation (IUD insertion within 3 weeks, D&C, HSG)
Cervical ectopy (adolescents  -  larger ectropion increases susceptibility)
Substance use (associated with high-risk sexual behavior)

left: Lower abdominal/pelvic pain (bilateral)
Cervical motion tenderness (chandelier sign)
Adnexal tenderness (unilateral or bilateral)
Mucopurulent cervical or vaginal discharge
right: Right upper quadrant pain (Fitz-Hugh-Curtis syndrome)
Nausea, vomiting (peritoneal irritation)
Dyspareunia and abnormal uterine bleeding

medications.name: Ceftriaxone
medications.action: Binds to penicillin-binding proteins (PBPs) on bacterial cell wall, inhibiting transpeptidase-mediated cross-linking of peptidoglycan. Bactericidal activity against N. gonorrhoeae (including most fluoroquinolone-resistant strains) and most gram-negative aerobes

medications.name: Doxycycline
medications.action: Binds to 30S ribosomal subunit, inhibiting aminoacyl-tRNA binding to the mRNA-ribosome complex, blocking protein synthesis. Bacteriostatic against C. trachomatis, M. genitalium, and other atypical organisms. Achieves excellent tissue penetration including fallopian tubes

pearls: PID is a clinical diagnosis  -  do not delay treatment waiting for lab confirmation; sequelae worsen with treatment delay
Subclinical PID (silent salpingitis) causes more tubal damage than acute PID because it goes untreated  -  accounts for many cases of unexplained infertility
Fitz-Hugh-Curtis syndrome presents as RUQ pain and may mimic cholecystitis, hepatitis, or PE  -  consider in any young woman with RUQ pain and pelvic symptoms
IUD does not need to be removed for mild-moderate PID  -  only remove if no clinical improvement after 48-72 hours of appropriate antibiotics
Mycoplasma genitalium is an increasingly recognized PID pathogen  -  consider testing and treating with moxifloxacin if resistance-guided therapy available
Each episode of PID approximately doubles the risk of infertility  -  prevention and early treatment are critical
diagnostics: Clinical diagnosis based on minimum criteria (CDC): uterine tenderness, adnexal tenderness, or cervical motion tenderness in a sexually active young woman with no other identifiable cause
Order endocervical NAAT for N. gonorrhoeae and C. trachomatis (most sensitive diagnostic test for causative organisms)
Order M. genitalium NAAT (emerging as significant PID pathogen  -  available at reference labs)
Order CBC with differential (leukocytosis >10,000 supports diagnosis)
Order ESR (>15 mm/hr) and CRP (elevated) as supportive criteria
Order transvaginal ultrasound to evaluate for TOA (complex adnexal mass with internal echoes, thick irregular walls)
Order β-hCG to exclude ectopic pregnancy
Order endometrial biopsy (endometritis on histology  -  plasma cells in endometrial stroma  -  is the most specific diagnostic criterion)
Order blood cultures if systemic sepsis suspected
Consider CT pelvis with contrast for complex presentations or suspected peritonitis
Consider diagnostic laparoscopy for equivocal cases (gold standard but rarely needed)
management: Outpatient regimen (CDC 2021): Ceftriaxone 500 mg IM x1 (or 1g IM if ≥150 kg) PLUS Doxycycline 100 mg PO BID x14 days PLUS Metronidazole 500 mg PO BID x14 days
Inpatient regimen A (CDC 2021): Cefoxitin 2g IV q6h OR Cefotetan 2g IV q12h PLUS Doxycycline 100 mg PO/IV q12h  -  transition to oral after 24-48h clinical improvement, complete 14 days total
Inpatient regimen B: Clindamycin 900 mg IV q8h PLUS Gentamicin loading dose 2 mg/kg IV then 1.5 mg/kg IV q8h  -  transition to oral doxycycline or clindamycin after improvement
Add metronidazole to all regimens for enhanced anaerobic coverage (especially if TOA or BV present)

Criteria for inpatient treatment: TOA, pregnancy, surgical emergency cannot be excluded, failure of outpatient therapy at 48-72 hours, inability to tolerate oral medications, severe illness with high fever or peritonitis
TOA management: IV antibiotics first  -  70% respond to medical therapy alone within 48-72 hours; percutaneous or surgical drainage if no improvement or abscess >8 cm
Ruptured TOA: emergent surgical intervention (laparotomy or laparoscopy) with drainage, irrigation, and possible salpingectomy/oophorectomy
Treat all sexual partners from the preceding 60 days for gonorrhea and chlamydia (expedited partner therapy where legal)
Test of cure: repeat NAAT at 3 months for gonorrhea and chlamydia

Counsel on long-term sequelae: infertility risk increases with each PID episode (8% after one, 20% after two, 40% after three), ectopic pregnancy (7-10 fold increased risk), chronic pelvic pain (18-35%)
nursingActions: Monitor temperature trends  -  defervescence expected within 48-72 hours of IV antibiotics; persistent fever suggests TOA or treatment failure
Assess for rebound tenderness and guarding (peritoneal signs suggest TOA rupture  -  surgical emergency)
Monitor hemodynamic stability (tachycardia, hypotension may indicate sepsis from ruptured TOA)
Administer IV antibiotics on schedule and monitor for adverse effects (gentamicin: nephrotoxicity, ototoxicity  -  monitor trough levels)
Position in semi-Fowler's to promote pelvic abscess drainage by gravity
Educate on completing full 14-day antibiotic course even after symptom improvement
Facilitate expedited partner treatment (provide partner prescriptions or referral)
Provide contraception counseling and barrier method education
Educate on IUD safety  -  current IUD does not need to be removed unless no clinical improvement after 48-72 hours of appropriate antibiotics
Screen for HIV, syphilis, and hepatitis B/C given STI exposure

vaginitis-np.title: Vaginitis: Vaginal Microbiome & Resistance Mechanisms

cellular.title: Vaginal Microbiome Molecular Ecology & Disruption
cellular.content:
The healthy vaginal microbiome is dominated by Lactobacillus species (L. crispatus, L. iners, L. jensenii, L. gasseri), which maintain an acidic pH (3.8-4.5) through lactic acid production from glycogen metabolism. Lactobacilli produce both D- and L-lactic acid isomers, hydrogen peroxide, and bacteriocins that collectively inhibit pathogenic bacterial colonization. L. crispatus is considered the most protective species, producing the highest levels of D-lactic acid (a more potent antimicrobial isomer) and maintaining the most stable community state type (CST-I).

Bacterial vaginosis (BV) represents a dysbiotic shift from Lactobacillus-dominant to a polymicrobial community dominated by Gardnerella vaginalis, Atopobium vaginae, Prevotella bivia, Mobiluncus species, and Megasphaera. G. vaginalis initiates BV by forming a biofilm on the vaginal epithelium  -  a structured polymicrobial community encased in an extracellular polysaccharide matrix that confers antibiotic resistance. This biofilm persists after standard metronidazole therapy in up to 50% of cases, explaining the high recurrence rate (50-80% within 12 months). BV biofilm produces sialidases and prolidases that degrade cervical mucus and vaginal epithelial protective factors, and amines (trimethylamine, putrescine, cadaverine) that produce the characteristic fishy odor and elevate vaginal pH >4.5.

Vulvovaginal candidiasis (VVC) is caused by Candida species, with C. albicans responsible for 85-90% of cases. C. albicans exhibits morphological switching between yeast (commensal) and hyphal (invasive) forms  -  hyphae penetrate vaginal epithelium, activate epithelial danger response pathways (NLRP3 inflammasome), and trigger IL-1β release and neutrophil recruitment. Recurrent VVC (≥4 episodes/year) is associated with genetic polymorphisms in mannose-binding lectin and IL-4 receptor, creating a hyperinflammatory response to low fungal burdens. Azole resistance is emerging, particularly in non-albicans species (C. glabrata accounts for 5-10% of VVC and has intrinsic reduced azole susceptibility due to upregulation of CDR efflux pumps and ERG11 mutations).

Trichomoniasis is caused by Trichomonas vaginalis, an anaerobic flagellated protozoan. It adheres to vaginal epithelial cells via adhesin proteins (AP65, AP51, AP33, AP23) and disrupts epithelial integrity through contact-dependent cytolysis and protease secretion. T. vaginalis phagocytoses Lactobacilli, further disrupting the microbiome. Metronidazole resistance (2-5% of isolates) results from mutations in oxygen-scavenging enzymes (ferredoxin, flavin reductase) that reduce intracellular activation of the drug.

riskFactors: BV: multiple sexual partners, douching, new sexual partner, lack of condom use, African American race, cigarette smoking, vaginal cleansing products
VVC: antibiotic use (disrupts Lactobacillus), pregnancy (elevated estrogen increases glycogen), uncontrolled diabetes (elevated glucose), immunosuppression, high-dose estrogen contraceptives
Trichomoniasis: unprotected sexual intercourse, multiple sexual partners, history of other STIs, incarceration, substance use
General: intrauterine devices (BV), receptive oral sex (BV), tight non-cotton underwear (VVC)

left: BV: thin, grayish-white, homogeneous discharge with fishy odor
VVC: thick, white, cottage-cheese-like discharge with vulvar pruritus
Trichomoniasis: frothy, yellow-green, malodorous discharge
Vulvar erythema and edema (VVC, trichomoniasis)
right: BV: no significant inflammation (discharge without erythema)
VVC: satellite lesions, vulvar fissures, dysuria
Trichomoniasis: strawberry cervix (colpitis macularis  -  punctate hemorrhages)
Dyspareunia (all types)

medications.name: Metronidazole
medications.action: Enters anaerobic and microaerophilic organisms where its nitro group is reduced by ferredoxin or flavodoxin electron transport proteins, forming cytotoxic nitro radical anions and hydroxylamine derivatives that damage DNA (strand breakage, helix destabilization) and inhibit nucleic acid synthesis. Active against obligate anaerobes and protozoa but not aerobes or Candida

medications.name: Fluconazole
medications.action: Inhibits fungal CYP51 (lanosterol 14α-demethylase), blocking the conversion of lanosterol to ergosterol in the fungal cell membrane. Ergosterol depletion increases membrane permeability, disrupting cell wall integrity and causing fungistatic effect against Candida species

pearls: L. crispatus dominance (CST-I) is the most protective vaginal community state  -  produces high levels of D-lactic acid, hydrogen peroxide, and bacteriocins
Gardnerella biofilm is the key driver of BV recurrence  -  standard antibiotic therapy reduces planktonic bacteria but biofilm persists in up to 50% of cases
Non-albicans Candida (especially C. glabrata) should be suspected in recurrent VVC unresponsive to azoles  -  culture with species identification and susceptibility testing is essential
Trichomoniasis is associated with 2-fold increased risk of HIV acquisition due to epithelial disruption and inflammatory cell recruitment
BV in pregnancy is associated with preterm birth, PPROM, and low birth weight  -  screening and treatment recommended in symptomatic pregnant women
Boric acid 600 mg vaginal suppositories are first-line for non-albicans VVC and an effective adjunct for recurrent BV  -  toxic if ingested orally, so counsel patients carefully
diagnostics: Order vaginal pH testing: BV >4.5, Trichomoniasis >4.5, VVC normal (4.0-4.5)

Order wet mount saline preparation: BV (clue cells  -  vaginal epithelial cells stippled with adherent bacteria), Trichomoniasis (motile trichomonads with jerky motility), VVC (pseudohyphae and budding yeast on KOH preparation)
Order whiff test (amine/KOH test): BV positive (fishy odor with KOH  -  release of volatile amines)
Apply Amsel criteria for BV diagnosis (3 of 4): thin homogeneous discharge, pH >4.5, positive whiff test, clue cells on wet mount
Order Nugent score (Gram stain scoring 0-10) for research or equivocal cases: 7-10 = BV, 4-6 = intermediate, 0-3 = normal
Order T. vaginalis NAAT (most sensitive diagnostic test  -  95-100% sensitivity vs 60% for wet mount)
Order Candida culture with species identification and antifungal susceptibility testing for recurrent VVC (R/O non-albicans species)
Order molecular panel testing (Affirm VPIII or BD MAX) for simultaneous detection of BV, VVC, and trichomoniasis
Screen for concurrent STIs (gonorrhea, chlamydia, HIV, syphilis) in patients with trichomoniasis or BV
management: BV first-line: Metronidazole 500 mg PO BID x7 days OR Metronidazole 0.75% vaginal gel daily x5 days OR Clindamycin 2% vaginal cream at bedtime x7 days
BV alternative: Secnidazole 2g PO single dose (FDA-approved, improved compliance)

Recurrent BV (≥3 episodes/12 months): Metronidazole 0.75% vaginal gel twice weekly for 4-6 months suppressive therapy, then PRN; consider boric acid 600 mg vaginal suppository daily x21 days followed by suppressive twice weekly; consider TOC lactobacillus-based probiotics (L. crispatus CTV-05  -  limited but emerging evidence)
VVC uncomplicated: Fluconazole 150 mg PO single dose OR topical azole (miconazole, clotrimazole) x3-7 days
Recurrent VVC (≥4 episodes/year): Fluconazole 150 mg PO every 72 hours x3 doses (induction) then 150 mg PO weekly x6 months (maintenance suppression)
Non-albicans VVC (C. glabrata): Boric acid 600 mg vaginal suppository daily x14-21 days (first-line); Amphotericin B vaginal suppository if boric acid fails; Flucytosine 5% vaginal cream (compounded)
Trichomoniasis: Metronidazole 500 mg PO BID x7 days (preferred over single 2g dose due to higher cure rates) OR Tinidazole 2g PO single dose
Metronidazole-resistant trichomoniasis: Tinidazole 2g PO daily x7 days; if persistent, consult CDC STD Clinical Consultation Network for high-dose regimens
Treat sexual partners for trichomoniasis (expedited partner therapy)  -  retest at 3 months
BV in pregnancy: treat with oral metronidazole 500 mg BID x7 days or oral clindamycin 300 mg BID x7 days (intravaginal clindamycin may increase preterm birth risk if used late in pregnancy)
nursingActions: Educate on completing full antibiotic course even after symptom resolution
Educate on metronidazole-alcohol interaction (disulfiram-like reaction  -  avoid alcohol during treatment and for 24 hours after metronidazole, 72 hours after tinidazole)
Educate on proper vaginal gel/cream application technique (insert at bedtime, remain recumbent)
Counsel that BV is not an STI but is associated with sexual activity  -  consistent condom use reduces recurrence
Educate VVC patients that over-the-counter antifungals are appropriate only for infrequent, mild episodes  -  recurrent symptoms require provider evaluation and species identification
Monitor for treatment side effects (metronidazole: metallic taste, nausea; fluconazole: headache, hepatotoxicity with prolonged use; boric acid: vaginal burning)
Educate on vaginal microbiome health (avoid douching, scented products, unnecessary antibiotics)
Counsel trichomoniasis patients on STI screening for partners and retesting at 3 months
Assess for BV-associated complications in pregnancy (preterm birth, premature rupture of membranes, chorioamnionitis)

bacterial-vaginosis-np.title: Bacterial Vaginosis: Molecular Microbiome Ecology & Advanced Management

cellular.title: Vaginal Community State Types & Biofilm Resistance Mechanisms
cellular.content:
Bacterial vaginosis (BV) represents a polymicrobial dysbiosis of the vaginal microbiome characterized by depletion of hydrogen peroxide-producing Lactobacillus species and overgrowth of anaerobic and facultative bacteria. The healthy vaginal microbiome is classified into Community State Types (CSTs): CST-I (L. crispatus dominant  -  most protective), CST-II (L. gasseri), CST-III (L. iners  -  transitional, less protective), CST-V (L. jensenii), and CST-IV (diverse anaerobes  -  BV-associated). Transition from Lactobacillus-dominant CSTs to CST-IV is the molecular hallmark of BV.

Gardnerella vaginalis is the primary architect of BV pathogenesis through its unique biofilm-forming capacity. G. vaginalis produces sialidase, an enzyme that cleaves sialic acid residues from the protective mucin layer (MUC1, MUC4) coating vaginal epithelial cells, exposing adhesion receptors and facilitating bacterial attachment. The biofilm is a structured polymicrobial community embedded in an extracellular polysaccharide matrix that provides physical protection against antibiotics and host immune defenses. Within this biofilm, Atopobium vaginae acts synergistically with Gardnerella  -  A. vaginae has intrinsic metronidazole resistance (lacks nitroreductase enzymes required for prodrug activation) and perpetuates biofilm integrity after antibiotic treatment.

The anaerobic shift in BV alters vaginal biochemistry. Prevotella bivia and Mobiluncus species produce short-chain fatty acids (succinate, acetate) that replace the protective D- and L-lactic acid normally produced by Lactobacillus. This raises vaginal pH from the normal 3.8-4.5 to >4.5, further inhibiting Lactobacillus growth and perpetuating dysbiosis. Bacterial decarboxylases convert amino acids to volatile amines (trimethylamine, putrescine, cadaverine), producing the characteristic fishy odor that is accentuated by alkaline conditions (KOH whiff test). The loss of Lactobacillus-derived hydrogen peroxide and bacteriocins removes a critical antimicrobial barrier, increasing susceptibility to STI acquisition including HIV, HSV-2, and ascending infections.

riskFactors: Sexual activity (new or multiple partners  -  sexual transmission of biofilm)
L. iners-dominant microbiome (CST-III  -  less stable, prone to BV transition)
Cigarette smoking (benzo[a]pyrene metabolites are toxic to Lactobacillus)
Intrauterine device use (foreign body effect on microbiome)
Receptive oral sex (oral anaerobes introduced to vaginal environment)
African American ethnicity (higher prevalence of CST-IV communities)
Recent antibiotic use (broad-spectrum antibiotics deplete Lactobacillus)
Lack of condom use (semen alkalinizes vaginal pH  -  pH 7.2-8.0)

left: Thin, homogeneous grayish-white vaginal discharge
Fishy odor (accentuated post-coitus and during menses due to alkaline pH)
Vaginal pH >4.5 on pH paper testing
Clue cells on saline wet mount (>20% of epithelial cells)
right: Minimal vulvovaginal inflammation (discharge without significant erythema or edema)
Positive whiff test with 10% KOH application
Absence of significant pruritus (distinguishes from VVC)
May be asymptomatic in up to 50% of cases

medications.name: Metronidazole
medications.action: Enters anaerobic organisms where the nitro group is reduced by ferredoxin/flavodoxin electron transport proteins, generating cytotoxic nitro radical anions and hydroxylamine derivatives that cause DNA strand breakage and helix destabilization. Selectively toxic to obligate anaerobes (Gardnerella, Prevotella, Mobiluncus) while sparing aerobic Lactobacillus. A. vaginae has intrinsic resistance due to lack of nitroreductase enzymes

medications.name: Secnidazole
medications.action: Same mechanism as metronidazole but with significantly longer half-life (17-29 hours vs 6-8 hours for metronidazole), allowing single-dose oral administration. Enhanced tissue penetration and sustained bactericidal concentrations provide extended anaerobic coverage

pearls: L. crispatus (CST-I) produces D-lactic acid, hydrogen peroxide, and bacteriocins  -  the most protective vaginal microbiome configuration against BV and STIs
Gardnerella sialidase degrades the protective mucin layer, exposing epithelial adhesion sites and enabling biofilm formation  -  a potential therapeutic target
A. vaginae is intrinsically metronidazole-resistant and serves as a biofilm co-architect with Gardnerella  -  explains treatment failure when A. vaginae burden is high
BV increases HIV acquisition risk 60% through disruption of the epithelial barrier, recruitment of CCR5+ target cells, and loss of antimicrobial Lactobacillus metabolites
Vaginal microbiome transplant (VMT) from L. crispatus-dominant donors represents the most promising approach for durable BV remission  -  analogous to fecal microbiota transplant for C. difficile
Male sexual partners harbor BV-associated bacteria (Gardnerella, Prevotella) in the penile biofilm  -  concurrent partner treatment is an emerging strategy to reduce recurrence
diagnostics: Apply Amsel criteria (3 of 4 required): thin homogeneous grayish-white discharge, vaginal pH >4.5, positive whiff test (amine odor with 10% KOH), clue cells on saline wet mount (>20% of epithelial cells)
Order Nugent score (Gram stain): 0-3 = normal Lactobacillus morphotype, 4-6 = intermediate, 7-10 = BV (Gardnerella/Mobiluncus morphotypes predominate)
Order molecular diagnostic panels (BD MAX BV, Aptima BV)  -  detect G. vaginalis, A. vaginae, and Lactobacillus relative abundance with higher sensitivity than clinical criteria
Order NAAT for concurrent STI screening (Chlamydia, Gonorrhea, Trichomonas)  -  BV increases STI acquisition risk
Order vaginal culture with sensitivity only for treatment-resistant cases
Assess for clue cells microscopically  -  vaginal epithelial cells with borders obscured by adherent coccobacilli (granular stippled appearance)
management: First-line: Metronidazole 500 mg PO BID x7 days (targets anaerobes via nitro-group reduction to cytotoxic radicals causing DNA strand breakage)
Alternative first-line: Metronidazole 0.75% vaginal gel 5g intravaginally daily x5 days (comparable efficacy, fewer systemic side effects)
